The SAC framework optimizes a stochastic policy while a primal- dual multiplier adaptively enforces operational safety constraints, such as frequency and area control error limits, through primal-dual updates. This mechanism ensures that control actions remain within secure ranges without compromising dynamic performance. Extensive simulations under diverse disturbance scenarios demonstrate that TLSAC outperforms baseline reinforcement learning algorithms by achieving smoother control actions, reduced frequency and voltage deviations, and effective coordination between synchronous and inverter-based resources. Thus, the proposed method offers a promising solution for frequency regulation in future power grids with high shares of renewable energy.

title: Design and Development of a Multi-Extinguisher Autonomous Fire Fighting Robot
authors: BV Saagarika, Divya E, Gaganashree D, Shreya S, Prof. Jayashree N
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a multi-extinguisher autonomous fire-fighting robot that integrates machine learning, computer vision, and embedded systems for real-time fire detection and suppression. The system utilizes multiple camera modules to continuously monitor the environment and employs a deep learning-based approach to identify fire and smoke conditions. Based on the detection results, the system activates appropriate extinguishing mechanisms such as water spraying, mist generation, or mud application. A lightweight communication framework ensures efficient data exchange between system components, while a monitoring interface provides real-time system updates. The proposed system aims to reduce human involvement in hazardous firefighting environments and improve response efficiency. Experimental observations indicate reliable detection performance and acceptable response time, demonstrating the effectiveness of the system.

title: An Evaluation of Few-Shot Learning Strategies for Essential Oil Classification: Balancing Data Augmentation and Meta-Learning
authors: Ranggi Praharaningtyas Aji, Bayu Priya Mukti, Primandani Arsi, Aulia Safitri, Gilang Satria Mahardika
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Classifying essential oil quality from visual data is a challenging few-shot learning problem due to extreme data scarcity and class imbalance. Previous studies often rely on single train-test splits, leading to statistically unreliable conclusions. This paper presents a rigorous comparative analysis of three distinct strategies—Simple Transfer Learning (STL), Fine-tuned Prototypical Networks (ProtoNet), and Augmented Transfer Learning (ATL)—on a dataset of 63 essential oil images. To ensure statistical robustness, we employ Stratified 5-Fold Cross-Validation and report mean performance metrics with standard deviations. Our results demonstrate that while standard Prototypical Networks suffer from optimization difficulties in low-data regimes (81.3 % accuracy), the Augmented Transfer Learning strategy yields superior and most stable performance (91.7 % accuracy, 90.5% Macro F1-score). An ablation study confirms that aggressive data augmentation is the dominant factor in improving performance over architectural or metric-learning variations. We conclude that for industrial applications with severely limited data, resources may be better invested in developing robust data augmentation pipelines than in implementing complex meta-learning algorithms.

title: Context-Dependent Value of Ccus: Roles of Economy-Wide Carbon Caps, Power-Sector Decarbonization, and End-Use Electrification
authors: Jundai Koketsu, Aya Ishigaki, Ryuta Takashima
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Carbon Capture, Utilization and Storage (CCUS) can contribute to both emissions avoidance and carbon dioxide removal (CDR) toward carbon neutrality; however, its societal value strongly depends on the context, including the stringency of carbon constraints, the degree of power-sector decarbonization, and the progress of end-use electrification. This study develops an integrated model that jointly represents (i) the power supply chain, (ii) fossil-fuel supply, (iii) CO2 capture, transport, storage, and conversion into synthetic fuels, and (iv) sectoral production and demand calibrated from inputoutput (IO) tables, all within a single social profit maximization problem formulated as a convex quadratic program. We define the context along three axes: (i) an economy-wide carbon cap level (as a percentage of baseline emissions), (ii) power-sector decarbonization (represented by the fuel input coefficient and generation cost, which determine the carbon intensity of electricity), and (iii) end-use electrification rates (sectoral electricity shares). The results indicate that, under moderately stringent caps, post-combustion capture (PCC) from flue gas and CCS are widely adopted, whereas under extremely stringent caps, direct air capture (DAC) becomes indispensable. Moreover, the contribution of CCU (synthetic fuels) is not universal: it ranges from negligible to substantial, and increases sharply as electricity becomes low-carbon. These findings highlight that the value of CCUS, especially CCU, varies markedly across contexts, underscoring the importance of comprehensive and context-specific strategies for carbon neutrality.

title: Adaptive and Multi-object Grasping via Deformable Origami Modules
authors: Peiyi Wang, Paul A. M. Lefeuvre, Shangwei Zou, Zhenwei Ni, Daniela Rus, Cecilia Laschi
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Soft robotics grippers have shown great promise in handling fragile and geometrically complex objects, as a less complex alternative to sensor-heavy conventional gripper systems. In this work, we present a multi-finger hybrid gripper featuring passively deformable origami modules that generate constant force and torque output. All fingers are driven by a 1-DOF actuator mechanism, and equipped with parallel origami modules enabling passive shape adaptability and stable grasping force without active sensing or feedback control. More importantly, we demonstrate an interesting capability in simultaneous multi-object grasping, which allows stacked objects of varied shapes and sizes to be picked, transported and placed independently, significantly improving manipulation efficiency compared to single-object grasping. These results highlight the potential of origami-based compliant structures as scalable modules for adaptive, stable and efficient multiobject manipulation in domestic and industrial pick-and-place scenarios.

title: Design and Analysis of PCB Winding Permanent Magnet Linear Motor
authors: Jorge Ulises Ramirez Carrasco, Fuat Kucuk
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: High-precision positioning systems increasingly demand linear actuators that balance high thrust density with compact form factors and ease of manufacturing. However, traditional iron-core linear motors suffer from significant cogging forces, while existing coreless designs are often limited by complex and costly wire-winding processes. This paper proposes a novel coreless linear motor topology designed to address these manufacturing challenges. The proposed system utilizes a modular Printed Circuit Board (PCB) stator architecture combined with a flux-concentrating Halbach permanent magnet array. By employing a modular stacking strategy, the design allows for scalable thrust generation without the fabrication complexity of monolithic high-layer-count boards. The electromagnetic performance of the motor was analyzed and validated through dynamic Finite Element Analysis (FEA) under motion conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed topology achieves highly linear force characteristics with almost zero cogging torque. Furthermore, the design realizes a significant reduction in volumetric profile compared to conventional wire-wound actuators, proving that the modular PCB approach is a viable solution for scalable, high-precision, and low-profile motion systems.

title: Innovative Monitoring System Open Source Based: A Case Study of a Microgrid Lab with Digital Twin, PV and BESS Integration
authors: Riccardo Loggia, Roberto Menichelli, Lorenzo Frattale Mascioli, Andrea Golino, Cristina Moscatiello, Luigi Martirano
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: As microgrids and modern low-voltage electrical systems continue to evolve, the demand for advanced monitoring tools has grown significantly. Ensuring reliable operation now requires systems capable of overseeing all functional aspects to maintain safety, stability, and a high degree of automation. To address these needs, SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) solutions have emerged, offering diverse structures and protocols, but ultimately converging on a common purpose: monitoring and managing power flows effectively. The Digital Twin, based on a BIM (Building Information Modeling) approach, is the best tool for managing microgrids because it is built during the 3D digital design process, following the entire life cycle of the system. The purpose of this document is to analyze the possibilities and limitations of the monitoring and supervision systems on the market, introducing our completely open-source alternative. Through the analysis of a case study within a university laboratory microgrid, different scenarios will be analyzed in order to evaluate the behavior of the system under the various conditions presented and propose solutions for future software updates.

title: Maneuver Detection Based Adaptive Transition Probability Matrix for Improved IMM Estimate
authors: Bibhabasu Mondal, Viji Paul Panakkal, Rajbabu Velmurugan, A Vengadarajan, A.K. Pathak
abstract: A tracking system can efficiently track a target if the motion model used by the system match the target's motion model. The Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) algorithm is commonly employed in tracking systems for this purpose. IMM uses a set of models to represent the possible evolution of the target's state. Since the target's motion can switch between different modes, the IMM algorithm must switch accordingly. The switching between models is governed by the Transition Probability Matrix (TPM), which plays a key role in determining both the estimation accuracy and the response time of the tracker. In the conventional Interacting Multiple Model (CIMM) algorithm, the TPM is pre-defined and set heuristically. The diagonal elements of the TPM represent the probability that a model will continue in its current state, which directly affects the accuracy of the estimate. A higher value for the diagonal elements typically leads to more accurate estimates in the case of model matched filtering. However, larger diagonal elements also result in slower model switching, as the off-diagonal elements determine the speed at which models switch during changes in the target's motion. This creates a tradeoff between accuracy and responsiveness, limiting the performance of the CIMM algorithm. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a likelihood-based approach (ATPM_L) for updating the TPM. In the proposed method, the likelihood of each model is computed with respect to the current scan measurement and the TPM update factor is derived based on these likelihoods. This likelihood-based approach satisfies both accuracy and switching speed requirements of TPM in IMM algorithm. This algorithm also offers a faster model switching mechanism compared to all existing TPM update algorithms.

title: Analysis of DCE Iterations Using ResNetDCE for Low-Light Enhancement
authors: Riyadi Jimmy Iskandar, Chastine Fatichah, Anny Yuniarti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Low-light image enhancement is a critical challenge in computer-vision systems. The Deep Curve Estimation (DCE) framework provides an efficient zero-shot solution. However, its performance is closely tied to the number of iterative enhancement cycles used. This study analyzed the effect of DCE-Net iterations on the new hybrid architecture, ResNetDCE. This model uses a pre-trained ResNet18 to extract features. It also includes a modified DCE-Net for adjusting the illumination. We tested iteration counts of 3, 6, 9, and 12 iterations. We measured performance using three metrics: Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM), and Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS). We also considered the computational cost. Our tests on the LOL dataset demonstrate that 9-iterations yield the optimal balance, achieving a peak PSNR of 21.05 dB and SSIM of 0.8006, with negligible increases in the inference time and memory footprint. This study found a link between pixellevel and perceptual metrics. The LPIPS improved beyond the PSNR peak. This suggests a trade-off between pixel-fidelity and perceptual naturalness. This study offers the first evidence for selecting the optimal DCE iterations. Hybrid feature-curve architectures work well and set benchmarks for future research on low-light enhancement.

title: Preface: Conference Committees
authors: N/A

title: MBSE in Space Mission Concept Development
authors: Blake DeVaney, Maxwell Harrow, Debarati Chattopadhyay, Matthew Morris, Dannie Smith, Jason Arcido
abstract: In recent years, Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) has been in the spotlight as a powerful methodology for defining and designing highly complex aerospace systems. MBSE facilitates a systems engineer's ability to create relationships between elements of a system's design and the underlying analytical models, and simplifies the process of creating diagrams to communicate the system design to stakeholders. This paper presents an application of MBSE techniques to address interdependencies in a system design to improve system budgets, trade space analysis, and traceability through the system from stakeholder needs to performance metrics. In space mission design, interdependencies exist between all subsystems/components in a system. Some interdependencies are direct (such as mass affecting propulsion needs), and others are indirect (such as power affecting solar array mass and therefore propulsion needs). The conceptual design of space missions thus involves collaborative efforts from system and subsystem engineers, conducting trades and analysis to develop valid designs. However, traditional approaches often result in outdated subsystem evaluations due to evolving interdependencies. To resolve this, we propose an MBSE framework, ACEBox, which integrates subsystem analytical models with a unified system-level model. By providing a digital thread that exchanges information between a SysML Systems model and an array of spacecraft subsystem analysis tools, ACEBox enables digital capture of design changes that propagate through the system. This approach enables rapid, parameterized analysis of design alternatives, dynamically sizing systems based on predefined rules and rollups for critical budgets like the Master Equipment List (MEL), the Power Equipment List (PEL), the Data Budget, and the Link Budget. The single source of truth MBSE model enables the systems engineer to generate block diagrams (hierarchical and internal), showing the connections and interfaces between components linked to the architecture used for budget generation. To validate this approach, a case study is presented showcasing the performance of spacecraft design decisions against a set of mission requirements. This demonstrates the agility of using MBSE methods across a set of digitally integrated engineering design and analysis tools for system concept development, concurrently saving time and improving system performance.

title: A Thyristor-Switched Pulsed Power System With 78-ns Jitter for a Coaxial Plasma Gun
authors: Neil P. Laya, Tetiana Tymoshevska, David L. Chesny, Mark B. Moffett, Kirk J. Boehm, Kunning G. Xu
abstract: A fiber-optically controlled and thyristor-switched coaxial plasma gun (CPG) pulsed power system has been designed, built, and fired at up to 2 kV and 6.4 kA, with a 6.65-MW momentary peak power output from the capacitor. Total system firing jitter from initial digital signal to 90% peak current rise was measured at 78.15 ns, representing 0.18% of the total pulse time of 44.10 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>s. This includes both thyristor turn-on and CPG breakdown. Standard deviation of peak current over repeated firings was measured to be 21.09 A (0.33%). The system has experienced 100+ firings at 2 kV without a single misfire. A voltage profiling analysis has been performed where voltage differentials across system components, including the thyristor, were collected temporally for the entire pulse. High-speed and long-exposure photography were used to verify CPG operation and mass-injection/gas-puff system timing. This study sets the groundwork for plasma-load pulsed power systems using thyristors, as well as informing expanded voltage and current capability, enabling fine timing control and multisystem synchronization, allowing greater repeatability and reliability, and informing multithyristor array systems.

title: Tackling Expressive Feature-Modeling Constructs with Pseudo-Boolean d-DNNF Compilation
authors: Chico Sundermann, Stefan Vill, Elias Kuiter, Sebastian Krieter, Thomas Thüm, Matthias Tichy
abstract: Configurable systems typically consist of reusable assets that have dependencies between each other. To specify such dependencies, feature models are commonly used. As feature models in practice are often complex, automated reasoning is typically employed to analyze the dependencies. Here, the de facto standard is translating the feature model to conjunctive normal form (CNF) to enable employing off-the-shelf tools, such as SAT or #SAT solvers. However, modern feature-modeling dialects often contain constructs, such as cardinality constraints, that are ill-suited for conversion to CNF. This mismatch between the input of reasoning engines and the available feature-modeling dialects limits the applicability of the more expressive constructs. In this work, we shorten this gap between expressive constructs and scalable automated reasoning. Our contribution is twofold: First, we provide a pseudo-Boolean encoding for feature models, which facilitates smaller representations of commonly employed constructs compared to Boolean encoding. Second, we propose a novel method to compile pseudo-Boolean formulas to Boolean d- DNNFs. With the compiled d-DNNFs, we can resort to a plethora of efficient analyses already used in feature modeling. Our empirical evaluation shows that our proposal substantially outperforms the state-of-the-art based on CNF inputs for expressive constructs. For every considered dataset representing different feature models and feature-modeling constructs, the feature models can be significantly faster translated to pseudo-Boolean than to CNF. Overall, deriving d-DNNFs from a feature model with the targeted expressive constraints can be substantially accelerated using our pseudo-Boolean approach. For instance, the Boolean approach only scales for group cardinalities with up-to 13 features while pseudo-Boolean d-DNNF compilation can compile cardinalities with thousands of features. Furthermore, our approach is competitive on feature models with only basic constructs.

title: Computational Empathy in Affective LLMs: Architectures, Evaluation, and Clinical Deployment Challenges
authors: Zongtai Wu, Shin Chen, Jian Chen, Zeyu Gao, Hui Bao
abstract: Mental health systems face a persistent gap between growing demand for support and limited access to timely, affordable care. At the same time, general-purpose large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being used as informal sources of emotional support, despite limited consensus on what empathy in such systems actually means and how it should be evaluated in clinical contexts. In this review, we synthesise recent work on computational empathy in affective LLMs for mental health, drawing on studies published between 2022 and 2026. We trace the field's evolution from rule-based affect detection and deep emotion modelling to contemporary generative agents, and argue that current systems simulate cognitive empathy rather than genuine affective empathy. We further conceptualise computational empathy as a modular pipeline involving affect inference, contextual and relational modelling, supportive dialogue generation, and safety management. Across this pipeline, recent advances include empathetic fine-tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, explicit affective modules, memory and rapport mechanisms, multimodal sensing, and agent-based scaffolds for structured therapeutic techniques. However, evidence for clinical readiness remains limited. Current evaluation remains fragmented across technical benchmarks, human ratings, theory-grounded scoring, and safety tests, with no accepted standard linking empathy metrics to patient outcomes. Major deployment barriers include shallow understanding, generic or culturally mismatched support, anthropomorphic over-trust, dependency risks, privacy concerns, and unreliable crisis handling. We conclude that affective LLMs are best positioned as bounded, low-acuity adjuncts within hybrid-care models that preserve safety protocols and human oversight.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: Optimal Output Synchronization of Heterogeneous MASs: A Computationally Efficient Data-Driven Learning Approach
authors: Shicheng Huo, Zhipeng Wang, Chao Deng, Ya Zhang, Hao Shen
abstract: In this paper, we study the data-driven H∞ optimal control problem for output synchronization of heterogeneous multi-agent systems via a computationally efficient reinforcement learning (RL) method. Unlike the existing RL control results which solve for the optimal controller through the vectorization and Kronecker product (VKP) operations where the large size of the Kronecker product increases computational complexity, a computationally efficient data-driven RL control method is studied in this paper. First, the optimal output synchronization problem is transformed into an H∞ optimal control problem by constructing an internal model-based distributed control protocol. Then, the corresponding algebraic Riccati equation for the H∞ optimal control is reformulated as a generalized Sylvester-transpose matrix equation (GSTME) at each iteration. To solve the GSTME, the matrix form of the conjugate gradient least squares algorithms that do not require the VKP operations is proposed based on the system dynamics and measurement data of multi-agent systems, respectively. Furthermore, the initial stabilizing gain required for policy iteration learning is proposed based on a data-based solution method. Finally, the feasibility of the designed algorithms is demonstrated through a numerical simulation, and its better computational efficiency compared to existing methods is shown.

title: Event-Triggered Control for T-S Fuzzy of Multi-Area LFC Power Systems under Energy-Limited DoS Attacks
authors: Zhilin Yang, Yajuan Liu
abstract: Load Frequency Control (LFC) is imperative in ensuring the stability of power systems in terms of frequency, and its efficacy is frequently impacted by device nonlinearities and cyber attacks. This paper investigates the security control of multi-area LFC power systems under energy-limited denial-of-service (DoS) attacks. Firstly, a unified Takagi-Sugeno (T-S) fuzzy model for multi-area LFC power systems is developed by analysing the nonlinear components in the dynamic characteristics of the turbine and governor. Secondly, a DoS-dependent novel event-triggering mechanism (NETM) is proposed, and the impact of DoS attacks is incorporated into the design of the triggering mechanism. Sufficient conditions for the uniform ultimate boundedness stability of the power system are provided, and an algorithm is devised to determine the time required for a power system state trajectory to enter the stable region. The efficacy of the proposed control method is subsequently validated through the utilization of simulation examples.

title: A Vertically Stacked Dual-Junction SPAD for Wide Spectral Response
authors: Bing Zhang, Pengfei Hu, Congzhen Hu, Yiyun Xie, Chi Wang, Youze Xin, RuBin Yan, Li Geng
abstract: This article presents a wide spectral response single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) fabricated in a 110 nm CMOS image sensor (CIS) technology. The device integrates two vertically stacked avalanche multiplication regions comprising a shallow P++/ N-enrichment layer junction and a deeper n-type buried layer (NBL) / deep p-type buried layer (DPBL) junction, respectively. Measurement results show a peak photon detection probability (PDP) of 82% at 600 nm, with a broadband PDP exceeding 18% across 400–850 nm under an excess bias voltage of 5.0 V. At room temperature, the SPAD exhibits a moderate dark count rate (DCR) of ∼65 cps/μm². The temporal resolution corresponds to a full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) jitter of 69 ps at 450 nm and 110 ps at 905 nm, respectively. Owing to its vertically stacked dual-junction design, the proposed SPAD provides a compact pixel solution suitable for applications requiring operation from visible to NIR wavelengths.

title: Simpler is Better: Feature Guard and Interaction for Semantic Correspondence_supp1-3693144.mp4
authors: Songlin Du
abstract: Semantic correspondence establishes keypoint correspondences between different instances of the same category. Fusing texture and semantic features from vision foundation models like stable diffusion (SD) and DINO significantly improves matching performance. However, we found an unnoticed yet essential problem: current feature fusion enhances the edge and semantic information in SD features with fine textures and DINOv2 features with fine semantics, but it destroys the semantic and structural information in SD features with weak and coarse semantics. We propose guard features (GuFT), a simple yet efficient method, to prevent feature degradation. Moreover, matching methods designed for traditional deep neural networks can be simplified based on two key insights: 1) vision foundation models provide rich visual knowledge; and 2) GuFT yields high-quality feature descriptors. We propose a bottleneck-style non-shared aggregation and backward interaction (NABI) module to efficiently capture intra- and inter-feature relationships, instead of common self- and cross-attention. The resulting framework, SimBetter, embodies a “simpler is better” design philosophy. It achieves state-of-the-art results with lower computation on SPair-71k, AP-10K, and PF-PASCAL, excelling in geometry-aware, cross-species, cross-family, and cross-dataset tasks. SimBetter also shows excellent potential in the applications of image-video semantic correspondence and sticker editing. Code is available at https://github.com/wzhlearning/SimBetter.

title: AI-Assisted Moot Courts: Simulating Justice-Specific Questioning in Oral Arguments
authors: Kylie Zhang, Nimra Nadeem, Lucia Zheng, Dominik Stammbach, Peter Henderson
publish: Proceedings of the Symposium on Computer Science and Law

title: Social Robotic Surveillance and Manipulation: How Pervasive Data Collection, Personalisation, and Robotic Influence Could Harm Robot Users and Society
authors: Minja Axelsson, Hannele Seeck
abstract: None

title: Shift-and-Add (SAdd): Hardware-Aware Channel Characterization with Polar NRZ Stimuli_supp1-3694993.pdf
authors: Mitchell Cooke
abstract: High-bandwidth wireline transceivers rely on stochastic adaptation algorithms, such as the Least Mean Squares (LMS) method for equalizer calibration. While effective, these methods cannot provide upper-bounded latency guarantees, require tuning step-size parameters, and can be burdened by error propagation when calibrating Decision Feedback Equalizers (DFEs). However, if a high-fidelity estimate of the channel response is known, an alternative approach is to configure an equalizer to directly compensate for channel-induced distortion. To address the challenges of low-latency adaptation in wireline transceivers, we co-design a novel hardware-aware algorithm and a digital architecture for training-based channel response identification. We define and leverage a subset of polar Non Return to Zero (NRZ) stimulus that we refer to as Shift-and-Add (SAdd) sequences, which are designed to simultaneously favor an efficient hardware design and provide low noise amplification with precisely defined estimator variance properties. For deployment in practical resource-constrained wireline transceivers, we present an algorithm and architecture with low latency, upper-bounded computation time, low memory usage, and few arithmetic operations.

title: Why We Need Ubiquitous and Pervasive Computing as a Research Community More Than Ever
authors: Tanzeem Choudhury

title: Reliable Multi-View Clustering with Graph Neural Network
authors: Bao Zeng, Jielong Lu, Yanxin Xie, Shunxin Xiao, Jianmin Li, Chaohui Tang, Da-Han Wang
abstract: Deep multi-view clustering (MVC) typically leverages deep neural networks to extract consistency and complementarity information across multiple views. As a representative approach, graph neural networks (GNN) gather significant attention due to its ability to model both topology and feature simultaneously, making them highly attractive for researchers working on multi-view scenarios. However, existing GNN-based MVC algorithms often overlook the inherent heterogeneity and noise in multi-view data, which introduces uncertainty into the embedding learning process. Moreover, the message passing mechanism of GNN may further amplify this uncertainty, leading to unreliable embeddings and poor clustering performance. To address the issues, we propose a model called Reliable Multi-View Clustering with Graph Neural Network (RM-GNN). The proposed model first uses view-specific graph convolution encoders to project features of different views into unified semantic space. Then, we introduce an adaptive fusion method that effectively combines the representations obtained from each view. Furthermore, we propose a similarity-driven evidence learning module to effectively identify and distinguish unreliable samples in the embedding space. Finally, we introduce a unified transformation matrix to enforce global topological consistency. Extensive experimental results on nine benchmark datasets demonstrate that our algorithm generally outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: CIPHER 2026 Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)

title: Sensorless Fault-Tolerant Control of Single-Stator Dual-Rotor Contra-Rotating PMSM
authors: Huamin Gao, Weikang Huang, Zhuoran Zhang, Zhe Sun, Wenxin Huang, Wei Chen
abstract: This article proposes a sensorless fault-tolerant control strategy for a single-stator dual-rotor contra-rotating permanent magnet synchronous machine (CRPMSM). The proposed approach addresses the fundamental challenges arising from single current measurement and strong multiplicative coupling between the electrical states of the two counter-rotating rotors. A control-oriented state-space model is established to characterize the coupled dual-rotor electrical dynamics. Based on this model, a full-order sliding mode observer (FOSMO) is developed to reconstruct the composite back electromotive force (back-EMF) without introducing additional low-pass filtering, and its stability and convergence properties are analytically proven. A phase-locked loop is then employed to extract a control-equivalent electrical position from the estimated composite back-EMF, enabling sensorless vector control and seamless transition to fault-tolerant operation under position sensor failure. It is demonstrated that the extracted electrical angle inherently converges to an intermediate position between the two rotor electrical angles, thereby enabling natural torque sharing and synchronous operation without explicit estimation of individual rotor positions. Simulation and experimental results validate stable operation under speed transients and asymmetric load disturbances, demonstrating the suitability of the proposed strategy for high-reliability electric propulsion applications.

title: Analog Variance Noise Modulation: Detection by Extended Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filtering
authors: Nargess Sadeghzadeh-Nokhodberiz, Hadi Zayyani, Mohammad Salman, Felipe A. P. De Figueiredo, Rausley A. A. De Souza
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: This paper presents a novel framework for analog variance noise modulation (VNM) and its corresponding variance-based demodulation technique. Unlike traditional digital noise communication systems such as Kirchhoff–Law–Johnson–Noise (KLJN) schemes that encode binary data using discrete noise levels, the proposed analog VNM continuously maps a signal’s amplitude onto the variance of a white Gaussian noise (WGN) carrier. In this way, information is embedded within the statistical behavior of noise rather than its waveform, enabling inherently secure and noise-like transmission. To accurately reconstruct the transmitted signal, we develop a new state-space modeling and inference framework inspired by the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT). This model captures the temporal structure of the analog baseband signal through its harmonic components while treating the noise variance as a time-varying latent process. Building upon this structure, a Rao–Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF) is designed to estimate the underlying signal by jointly inferring its harmonic dynamics and the hidden variance states, enabling robust demodulation under stochastic noise conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed analog VNM achieves accurate signal recovery and exhibits strong concealment properties compared to classical amplitude and frequency modulation schemes. Overall, this work introduces a new class of analog noise-based communication with an inherently simple and energy-efficient transmitter, while employing a higher-complexity receiver-side inference framework for accurate signal recovery. This asymmetric architecture makes the proposed VNM particularly suitable for low-power sensing and Internet of Things (IoT) scenarios where ultra-lightweight transmitters communicate with computationally capable gateways or base stations.

title: navCLIP: A Novel CLIP-guided Drone Navigation Model
authors: Hussein Samma
abstract: Autonomous drone visual navigation in indoor environments remains a challenging problem due to the lack of reliable localization signals and the complexity of dynamic surroundings. Developing robust and cost-effective navigation solutions is therefore essential for real-world inspection and monitoring applications. This work presents a novel, low-cost visual navigation model named navCLIP, for indoor environments. navCLIP builds on a pre-trained CLIP (contrastive language–image pre-training) model for vision–text encoding and adds an MLP (multilayer perceptron) projection layer. This layer is trained via contrastive learning to align (1) an ensemble encoder (monocular depth estimation, semantic segmentation, and a scene description) with (2) discrete action texts (e.g., “move forward” and “rotate left/right”). During inference, navCLIP uses cosine similarity to select the best action for drone navigation. The proposed navCLIP model was evaluated in a realistic simulated indoor environment created with AirSim and Unreal Engine to represent the corridor area of the SDAIA–KFUPM JRCAI at KFUPM. The results indicate that navCLIP outperforms baseline models such as CNNs (Convolutional Neural Network) in terms of recognition accuracy in selecting the best action based on the collected data. Moreover, when reinforcement-learning agents navigate within the same environment, navCLIP provides better collision avoidance compared with other reinforcement-learning methods like DQN (Deep Q-Network) and enhanced-DQN agents.

title: Keynote Title
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)

title: Unique Window ESD Failure in LDMOS–SCR and Its Implication on Power-to-Fail Scalability
authors: Kranthi Nagothu, Gianluca Boselli, K. K. Shreenidhaa, Shravya N. Raj, Mayank Shrivastava
abstract: The vulnerability of parasitic SCR, integrated with laterally diffused MOS (LDMOS) device (conventional LDMOS–SCR), to trigger during MOS/switching operation was addressed using an engineered (Flipped) LDMOS–SCR configuration, which in turn resulted in a unique low current/window electrostatic discharge (ESD) failure. While the failure was found to be universal to LDMOS–SCR devices with a weaker parasitic SCR, designed as an efficient MOS switch, it was found to be specific to a window of current between trigger and holding state. Besides, it couldn’t be detected using a standard 50-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Omega $</tex-math> </inline-formula> loadline transmission line pulse (TLP). The window failure resulted in severe power-to-fail scalability issues in LDMOS–SCRs, which were noticed in system-level stress, high impedance TLP, and long pulse stress. In this work, while using systematic experiments performed by a high-resistance load-line TLP and 3-D technology computer-aided design (TCAD) simulations, we have developed detailed physical insights into the low current or window ESD failure phenomenon in these LDMOS–SCR devices. Physical insights developed in this work have resulted in device design proposals to avoid such failures and mitigate power-to-fail scalability issues during ESD stress while keeping the MOS operation and performance intact.

title: Activity Monitoring System for Long-Term Care Bathrooms Using mm-Wave FMCW Radar
authors: Joshua Visser, Hajar Abedi, George Shaker
abstract: The growing demand for long-term care (LTC) services, coupled with critical staffing shortages, highlights the need for privacy-preserving solutions to monitor resident safety, particularly in high-risk environments such as bathrooms. This study presents a comprehensive system design and evaluation of an activity monitoring system using a low-cost commercial 60 GHz mm-wave frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) radar with a single-input multiple-output (SIMO) antenna configuration, designed to recognize both dynamic and static behaviors without compromising resident privacy. The system uses deep learning to leverage spatial radar features (range-elevation and range-Doppler heatmaps), enabling accurate classification of common bathroom activities, including sitting, standing, walking, handwashing, cleaning, and falls. Over 500,000 radar frames were collected across three bathroom layouts with ten participants. The proposed system achieved classification accuracies up to 95% in training environments and maintained over 90% accuracy in new layouts. The system demonstrated consistent fall detection (95% precision), reliable occupancy and multi-person detection, and effective generalization across variable environments through transfer learning. This work addresses a critical gap in LTC monitoring by demonstrating practical, validated deployment of radar-based activity recognition in bathroom environments—one of the highest-risk areas for falls—using commercially available, low-cost hardware. The results demonstrate the system’s potential for real-world deployment, supporting timely intervention and improving safety in vulnerable care settings.

title: Bilateral Neurodynamic-Reconstructed Olfactory Model for Agricultural Product Recognition in Electronic Noses_supp1-3693348.pdf
authors: Hong Men
abstract: Electronic noses (E-noses) are intelligent identification systems inspired by biological olfactory perception mechanisms. However, data captured by conventional E-noses primarily represent mechanistic expressions of odor properties, and existing analytical methods fail to fully replicate the neurodynamic characteristics inherent in biological olfactory systems. To address the limitations of the classical KIII computational model in simulating bilateral synergistic perception, this study systematically reconstructed the topological structure and dynamic equations to establish the Bilateral Olfactory Neural Model (BONM), incorporating recent neuroanatomical findings on bilateral interaction mechanisms. Integrating the BONM module into the E-nose data processing pipeline facilitates a transition from simple pattern recognition to biomimetic perceptual processing. Self-similarity and chaotic dynamic indices, combined with small-world properties, were innovatively introduced to quantitatively validate the efficacy of BONM in simulating complex neural dynamics. Experimental odor signals from rice, eggs, soybeans, apples, and peanuts were collected using a PEN3 to construct a large-scale dataset. Results demonstrate that the BONM-integrated recognition system significantly outperforms the original KIII model in terms of feature robustness and compatibility with diverse classification algorithms, while maintaining high classification accuracy. This work provides novel insights into the structural evolution and biomimetic evaluation of olfactory models and offers an effective technical solution for developing high-performance, intelligent gas identification engineering systems.

title: Author response for "Descriptor: LYNRED Mobility Dataset Multimodal Detection Subset (LYNRED-MDS)"

title: Development of an Environmental Sensor System in the Life Mechatronics Symposium and its Use in Project-Based Learning
authors: Motoharu Fujigaki
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In this presentation, joint research activities in Cambodia conducted under the Life Mechatronics initiative will be introduced. An environmental sensor system has been developed and installed at two experimental sites. Because the system is expected to be deployed more widely in the Cambodia–Japan collaborative project, it requires expandability and low-cost implementation, needs that existing commercial products do not satisfy. A new system tailored to future research activities is therefore being developed. Furthermore, this environmental sensing system has been incorporated into project-based learning (PBL) activities, and the practical implementation of these activities will also be reported.

title: A Task-Oriented Characterization of Benchmark Datasets for Predictive Maintenance of Rolling Bearings
authors: Amanpreet Kaur, Harpreet Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Rolling-bearing predictive maintenance research relies heavily on publicly available benchmark datasets to develop and evaluate data-driven methods. However, these datasets differ fundamentally in experimental design, fault generation mechanisms, temporal structure, and labeling strategies, and are frequently applied to predictive maintenance tasks for which they violate core methodological assumptions. As a result, a significant portion of the existing literature reports performance results that are difficult to interpret, not comparable across studies, and in some cases methodologically unreliable. This paper presents a task-oriented characterization of widely used rolling-bearing benchmark datasets, including IMS, CWRU, XJTU-SY, FEMTO (PRONOSTIA), and Paderborn. The datasets are systematically analyzed and mapped to three core predictive maintenance objectives: fault diagnosis, degradation modeling, and remaining useful life (RUL) prediction based on their structural suitability. In addition, common dataset misuse patterns are identified and examined through the lens of experimental validity, highlighting threats to prognostic, construct, statistical, and ecological validity. The main contributions of this work are (i) a dataset–task compatibility framework that clarifies appropriate and inappropriate dataset usage, and (ii) a validity-driven analysis that explains why certain widely reported predictive maintenance results cannot be reliably generalized. This paper aims to serve as a corrective reference for dataset selection and experimental design in rolling-bearing predictive maintenance research.

title: SPI based Secure Communication Framework for Industrial IoT
authors: Soumyadip Motilal, Soumita Chatterjee, Ayas Kanta Swain, Kamalakanta Mahapatra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid growth and development of IoT has led to the need for safe and effective hardware based communication protocols. As any other network system, IoT systems are also prone to security breaches. To ensure data integrity and authentication, this paper proposes a novel design of a secure FPGA-based communication protocol-SPI on Xilinx Spartan-7 XC7S50-CSGA324 FPGA for Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) applications. The design involves design of a high-level synthesized (HLS) Intellectual Property (IP) of two security standards- Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) and Rivest–Shamir–Adleman (RSA) for encryption and decryption of the data. Following this, the serial interface is designed wherein the slave (sensor) transmits the encrypted data to the master (IoT Gateway). The decryption IP block then reveals the original message. The synthesis results show that the security protocols can achieve resource consumption as low as 3.79%. These security protocols have been also tested on Zybo, Pynq Z2 and Arty A7 boards. Additionally, these are also tested against various statistical strength measuring tests- NIST p-value and avalanche. The proposed secure design protocol is a stepping stone for next generation simple yet flexible secure IoT modules. The detailed experimental analysis also serves as a testimony to the fact that a highly secure protocol can be designed without requiring heavy resource usage or power overhead.

title: GNN-Based Malware Detection in IoT Networks: An Attack Graph Approach
authors: Irtika Khan, Anil Kumar Prajapati, Swaliya Khan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid expansion of Internet of Things (IoT) devices is growing exponentially, significantly increasing the global attack space, making contemporary networks more susceptible to large-scale malware attacks with high sophistication. The heterogeneous and resource-constrained characteristics of IoT settings render traditional detection strategies, which rely on flow-level statistical characteristics, to be unable to reflect the relational and structural regularities of malware propagation. To address these limitations, we introduced a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based attack graph framework that models IoT traffic as network graphs, capturing the extraction of complex behavioral dependencies among IoT devices. Traffic traces from real IoT and honeypot devices are captured in PCAP format and preprocessed to extract protocol, timing, and packet-level features. The proposed approach is evaluated using three GNN architectures: Graph Attention Network (GAT), Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN), and GraphSAGE, on the IoT-23 dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that GAT achieves highest accuracy, GraphSAGE provides consistently high detection performance, and GIN achieves competitive accuracy with precise accuracy, recall, and F1-scores, highlighting the effectiveness of GNN-based attack graphs for IoT malware detection. As a model framework, GNN provides an intelligent, robust, and scalable intrusion detection systems could be built future Internet of Things network.

title: Integrating Lean Healthcare Practices with Discrete-Event Simulation to Reduce Patient Waiting Time in Outpatient Clinics at a Sri Lankan Public Hospital
authors: Navindya Hettiarachchi, Chathumi Kavirathna, Annista Wijayanayake
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Excessive patient waiting time is a persistent operational challenge in outpatient departments (OPDs) of public hospitals, particularly in high-volume and resourceconstrained healthcare systems. This study aims to analyze and reduce patient waiting time in the OPD of a major Sri Lankan public hospital by integrating Lean management practices with discrete-event simulation (DES). Lean tools were applied to map the current-state process and identify non-value-added activities, capacity imbalance, and workflow bottlenecks. Primary data were collected through time studies conducted over three consecutive working days, capturing patient arrivals, service times, and waiting durations at registration, consultation, and pharmacy stages. A DES model was developed using Arena Simulation Software to evaluate multiple Leanbased improvement scenarios, including partial appointmentbased demand leveling, layout optimization, consultation capacity balancing during peak hours, and digital queue management at the pharmacy. Simulation experiments were conducted for both peak-demand and reduced-capacity periods. Results reveal that doctor consultation is the primary bottleneck driving excessive waiting, with pharmacy services acting as a secondary bottleneck. While isolated Lean interventions yielded limited improvements, integrated Lean scenarios achieved substantial reductions in waiting time and lead time, together with notable increases in patient throughput, without infrastructure expansion. The findings demonstrate the effectiveness of simulation-supported Lean integration as a practical decision-support approach for improving OPD efficiency in public healthcare settings.

title: Intelligent Scheduling of AI Tasks in Kubernetes
authors: Michael Abdelshaheed, Mohamed Ashour
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Kubernetes has become the de facto standard for orchestrating containerized workloads, including machine learning (ML). ML workloads exhibit long runtimes, variable compute and I/O demands, and high sensitivity to execution order, making them challenging to schedule efficiently. Kubernetes lacks mechanisms for fine-grained job ranking and does not control the exact order in which pods begin execution. Instead, Kubelet starts containers in parallel without coordination, causing pods to compete for resources simultaneously and degrade system performance. The work in this paper presents a custom ML-aware scheduling framework that addresses the above two limitations of the default scheduler. First, a ranking approach is introduced that scores jobs based on their ML-specific characteristics including estimated training duration, convergence behavior, and computational intensity. Second, a scheduler implementation is presented that enforces execution order according to the proposed ranking through pacing and execution checks. The framework is evaluated on a real Kubernetes cluster using simulated ML workloads, testing multiple pacing configurations and analyzing their impact on average Job Completion Time (JCT) and makespan. The best-performing configuration, balancing serialization and throughput, is then adopted for comparison against the default Kubernetes scheduler and a reversed-order variant across three workload intensities. The proposed work achieves up to 31.6 % improvement in average JCT and up 10.2 % improvement in makespan compared to the default scheduler. An ablation study with reversed execution order degraded JCT by up to 58 % relative to the intended ordering, confirming the importance of enforcing intelligent job sequencing. These findings highlight that controlling execution order, not just scheduling order, is a critical factor in ML workload orchestration, even in unconstrained, single-node environments.

title: Dagent: A Multi-Agent System for Device-Aware Assistance
authors: Ahmed Hassan, Ahmed Gamea, Omar Abdelgawad, Abdelaziz Serour, Eyad Hussien, Amr Khamees, Ahmed Abdalfatah, Ahmed Fares
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: The limitations of large language models (LLMs) in interacting with local computing environments have hindered their applicability in device-aware tasks. This research addresses this gap by introducing DAgent, a modular, multiagent system designed to provide personalized, context-aware assistance through integration with the user's system environment. The architecture comprises four specialized modules: the Tracer module for monitoring system activities, the RAG module for retrieval-augmented generation leveraging system logs, the Coding Agent for interacting with the environment to infer information or execute actions, and the Multi-Agent OS Assistant for orchestrating workflows. DAgent was evaluated on correctness, completeness, and clarity, scoring highly with the baseline 14 B model: 4.87,4.53, and 4.41 out of 5, respectively. Additionally, an ablation study removing the RAG module demonstrated the importance of the coding agent module, which achieved an average score of 4. 4 5 when used independently. These findings highlight DAgent's ability to excel in device-aware tasks, providing actionable, comprehensible responses and enabling secure, supervised command execution. While our evaluation was conducted on synthetic scenarios, the results indicate strong potential for deployment in real-world environments, marking a step toward intelligent, device-aware assistants that integrate language reasoning with system interaction.

title: Explainable Heterogeneous Federated Learning for Intrusion Detection in IIoT
authors: Tony Elsayed, Marwa Zamzam, Mohamed Ashour
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) is increasingly exposed to advanced cyberattacks, creating an urgent need for Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) that are accurate, interpretable, and privacy-preserving. Conventional IDS often rely on centralized data aggregation, which increases the risk of exposing sensitive information. In contrast, purely local learning avoids data sharing but struggles to generalize across heterogeneous IIoT environments. Additionally, many Machine Learning (ML)-based IDS operate as black boxes, providing limited transparency and making it difficult for operators to trust or validate alerts. These challenges highlight the need for IDS that preserve privacy, generalize effectively under nonidentical data distributions, and offer interpretable predictions. This paper introduces a Federated Learning (FL)-based IDS for IIoT that integrates Explainable AI (XAI) through SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). A comparative study of centralized, local, and FL paradigms is conducted using a multiclass Edge-IIoT-set dataset. Results show that centralized learning achieves the highest accuracy (97.7%) but requires aggregation of raw client data, while local models suffer from poor generalization under heterogeneous IIoT conditions. FL achieves competitive accuracy (95%), reduces disparities across clients, preserves privacy, and provides stable, comprehensive feature attributions. Overall, FL balances accuracy, fairness, and interpretability, making it a practical and privacy-preserving solution for IIoT intrusion detection.

title: Method for Identifying Changes Between Program Versions
authors: Mariam Arutunian, Hripsime Hovhannisyan, Arthur Malajyan, Karen Avetisyan, Hayk Aslanyan
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: This paper presents a two-stage method for detecting changes between program versions. It combines analysis based on metrics with a code clone detection algorithm. The approach uses control and data dependencies between instructions to match functions on a many-to-many basis. Additionally, it allows detecting changed instructions with high accuracy. The developed method provides reliable comparison of executable files, even in the absence of source code. Experimental evaluation on software projects has demonstrated its effectiveness in analyzing changes and detecting modifications.

title: Tensor Methods Outperform Transformers: A Comprehensive Comparative Analysis of Knowledge Graph Link Prediction Methods
authors: Manal Helal, Hammad Khawaja
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Knowledge graph link prediction has witnessed significant methodological evolution, with recent research heavily favouring transformer-based approaches. However, our comprehensive comparative analysis reveals surprising results: classical tensor factorisation methods significantly outperform modern transformer architectures across multiple domains. We systematically evaluate four major categories of link prediction methods-classical embeddings (TransE, DistMult, ComplEx), tensor factorisation (CP, Tucker Decomposition), graph neural networks, and transformer approaches-across three diverse knowledge graph datasets (UMLS, FB15K, DB15K). Contrary to prevailing trends, tensor methods achieved superior accuracy (Tensor-CP MRR: 0.2693) while transformer approaches demonstrated unexpectedly poor performance (LP-BERT MRR: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 0 3 8 9}$</tex>). Our multi-dimensional evaluation, encompassing accuracy, computational efficiency, and scalability, identifies three distinct operational tiers and provides evidence-based guidelines for method selection. These findings challenge the current research focus on transformer architectures and suggest a renewed importance for tensor-based approaches in knowledge graph completion tasks.

title: Analysis of Lightweight CNN Models for Multi-Crop Leaf Disease Classification
authors: Shivam Kumar Chauhan, Roop Pahuja
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Quick and precise plant disease detection is a key aspect in precision agriculture, particularly in resource-sensitive and field-scale settings. Whereas deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have proven to be very effective in leaf disease detection, most existing models rely on computationally intensive architectures that restrict real-time deployment. In this paper, a controlled comparative benchmark of two widely used lightweight CNNs (ResNet50 and InceptionV3) is presented for multi-crop leaf disease classification. An evidence-based preprocessing pipeline, including systematic data cleaning and Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE), is employed to optimize lesion visibility and feature consistency. Both models are trained on tomato and potato leaf disease classes from the PlantVillage dataset using transfer learning and evaluated under identical experimental conditions. The results indicate that ResNet50 converges stably with lower computational overhead, while InceptionV3 demonstrates stronger discrimination of complex disease patterns. Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) is included as an experimental interpretability tool to qualitatively analyze model attention. Although the generated heatmaps are not always precise, they provide useful insights into disease-relevant regions influencing predictions. Overall, the findings highlight the trade-offs between accuracy, efficiency, and interpretability when deploying lightweight CNN architectures for practical multi-crop disease diagnosis.

title: Nanotube Transistor Architectures: Device Physics and Scalability Perspective
authors: Dileep Kumar Gupta, Ashish Raman, Prabhat Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: When semiconductor scaling gets close to the sub-5nm range, regular MOSFETs have problems because of short-channel effects (SCEs) and the basic subthreshold swing (SS) limit of 60 mV/decade. Tunnel Field-Effect Transistors (TFETs) have become a promising alternative because they use a band-to-band tunneling (BTBT) process. This paper talks about the progress of Nanotube FET (NTFET) architectures, focusing on their hollow cylindrical shape, which gives them better electrostatic control than nanowire equivalents. We look at different types of structures, such as electrostatic doping (ED) and heterojunction integration (SiGe/Si, InGaAs/InP), and how they can improve ON-state current and make ultra-steep subthreshold slopes.

title: Comparative Evaluation of CNN Models for Maize Crop Disease Diagnosis
authors: Sainadh Singh Kshagri, C. Raju, Kuruva Raju, S. Mallikarjunaiah, Banala Saritha, G Purnachandrarao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: To ensure the security of food in the world, agriculture plays a significant role and maize stands among the most widely grown staple crops worldwide. However, maize productivity is seriously impacted by leaf diseases that result in huge yield losses every year. Early and accurate identification of these diseases will reduce the damage to the crops and to optimally utilize the agricultural resources. Conventional techniques of detection depends on manual inspection, which is impractical for large-scale agricultural. Although recent developments in Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques have been promising for plant disease diagnostics in an automated fashion, most of the studies have only considered one model or none compared the modern convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures systematically. This causes a research gap of finding the most appropriate DL model for reliable classification of maize diseases. The objective of this work is such that it classifies maize leaf images into 4 classes, namely, Northern Leaf Blight, Common Rust, Gray Leaf Spot and Healthy and compares the performances of CNN pre-trained models. The methodology is based on using transfer learning methods based on MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0 and ResNet50 models pre-trained on the Plant Village dataset that is publicly available. Fast computing Image preprocessing and fine-tuning is carried out to improve the classification performance. Key findings show closures in that ResNet50 can classify samples better than MobileNetV2 and EfficientNetB0 thanks to its simple model structure with depth and residual links that help with the process of extracting strong features. The results show the effectiveness of deep learning-based approaches can be used for an accurate maize disease detection. The proposed framework has important implications for smart and precision agriculture such as early disease diagnosis, lower crop losses, more optimal use of pesticides, and better decision support to farmers.

title: Safeguarding Knowledge in Federated Transfer Learning with Direction-Aware DP
authors: Yasas Supeksala Akurudda Liyanage Don, Thilina Ranbaduge, Ming Ding, Caslon Chua, Jun Zhang
publish: Proceedings of the International Workshop on Secure and Efficient Federated Learning
abstract: None

title: Europa Clipper Cruise Phase: From Integration and Test to Early Operations
authors: Andres F. Rivera, Erisa Stilley, Thaddeus M. Para, May Chong Chan
abstract: Jupiter's icy moon Europa is a prime target in our exploration of potentially habitable worlds beyond Earth. The Europa Clipper mission will characterize Europa's habitability as the first step in the search for potential life at the Jovian moon by conducting approximately four dozen flybys. Europa Clipper launched on October 14, 2024, aboard a SpaceX Falcon Heavy, beginning its 5.5 -year journey to the Jovian system. This paper describes the mission's transition from the implementation phase—highlighting the execution of system-level Verification and Validationin—to launch and early cruise operations. It covers key milestones such as the flight system characterization campaign, the first low data rate operational period, the first gravity assist maneuver at Mars, and the initiation of Earth-pointed inner cruise operations. The paper focuses on the major challenges during planning and implementation, the strategies adopted to address them, and the lessons learned to inform future deep space missions.

title: Autismsense: Leveraging Cnns for Early Visual Diagnosis
authors: Vasumathi A, Hiruthick B S, Indeeswaran C, Kabilan M, Karthick V
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Autism Spectrum disease (ASD) is a complicated neurodevelopmental disease that impacts social interactions, behavioral patterns, or emotional displays. The conventional approach to diagnosing ASD is highly dependent on clinical observation and tends to be subjective and time-consuming. In this regard, this study attempts to construct an automated ASD detection system utilizing deep learning techniques according to the VGG16 model and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). The autism dataset employed in this research includes essential behavioral symptoms, including gender, age, jaundice history, family background of ASD, and results of the screening test. The proposed model improves the classification accuracy of ASD using an optimized preprocessing technique, which involves data cleaning, normalization, augmentation, and efficient feature learning. The proposed system is computationally efficient, allowing for fast convergence and high accuracy even with a small amount of data. The experimental outcomes show that the system has stable training performance, improved generalization capability, and is able to distinguish well between ASD and non-ASD classes.
