title: Accurate and Efficient Equivalent Circuit Modeling for Single-Port SAW Resonators_supp1-3692761.pdf
authors: Chen Chen
abstract: An optimized equivalent circuit modeling framework for single-port surface acoustic wave (SAW) resonators is proposed in this article, addressing key limitations of conventional Butterworth–Van Dyke (BVD) and modified BVD (MBVD) models in representing spurious resonances and packaging parasitics. It incorporates: 1) simplified parasitic circuits to quantify packaging effects and 2) explicit resonance loops for spurious modes. The extraction of equivalent circuit parameters within this framework is guided by an iterative strategy that combines analytical derivation and least-squares optimization. Model validation across 15 commercial SAW resonators (303–868 MHz) shows significantly improved precision: the fit MBVD model reduces root mean squared error (RMSE) by roughly one order of magnitude, while the extended MBVD (EMBVD) further lowers RMSE by 30%–50%. The parasitic-integrated BVD (PBVD) model achieves high-accuracy packaging characterization with a relative RMSE (RRMSE) of 15.1% and root-mean-squared relative error (RMSRE) of 0.51%. In practice, EMBVD prevents oscillation mode hopping by capturing spurious resonances, while PBVD guides the optimization of devices’ packaging and PCB layout. Furthermore, the extracted equivalent circuit parameters provide clear criteria for sensor-specific resonator selection: balancing sensitivity against resolution for mass-loading sensing, or prioritizing low motional resistance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\text {m}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and static capacitance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${C}_{{0}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> to ensure oscillation stability for viscoelastic sensing. The proposed framework is also extendable to two-port SAW resonators, further broadening its applicability. This work thus facilitates the transition from precise resonator characterization to robust system design.

title: When Does Train-Time Masking Help? A Leakage-Controlled Robustness Audit of Tabular Classifiers Under Feature Missingness
authors: Sooyoung Jang, JeeSook Eun, Hyunbean Yi, Changbeom Choi
abstract: Tabular classifiers are typically benchmarked on clean train–validation–test splits drawn from the same distribution, even though deployment-time inputs routinely contain missing features that were fully observed during training, and prior robustness claims often confound train-time exposure to corrupted inputs with genuine deployment-time robustness, reuse preprocessing fitted on the full dataset, or rely on a single random split. To address this gap, we audit whether train-time stochastic masking actually improves robustness to test-time feature missingness once these confounds are controlled, using mask-augmented imputation training (MAIT)—a recipe combining stochastic feature masking, missingness indicators, and a reconstruction loss—as the audit subject. We evaluate MAIT on five OpenML benchmarks across 20 seeds with train-only preprocessing and copied MCAR, MAR, and MNAR test-set overlays, comparing against tree ensembles, an FT-Transformer-style baseline, TabPFN, and a same-backbone MLP-only control. On Covertype, MAIT degrades 0.0133 AUROC at the 30% MCAR overlay while LightGBM degrades 0.2062 and the same-backbone control isolates this gain to the masking recipe (MAIT 0.9814 nominal, 0.9682 at MCAR-30 vs. MLP-only 0.9896 and 0.9113); at the practical threshold δ = 0.005 AUROC, equivalent to a one-percentage-point Gini delta in credit-risk reporting, MAIT’s advantage is operationally meaningful on Covertype and German Credit, while tree ensembles or TabPFN lead on absolute AUROC for the remaining three datasets.We therefore recommend train-time masking when the degradation slope under missingness is itself the deployment objective, and stronger comparators when the peak nominal AUROC is the deployment objective, and present the audit protocol as a reusable template for future robustness claims under feature dropout.

title: Quantification and Visualisation of Interpersonal Synchrony using Wearable Sensors: A Case Study on Autistic and Neurotypical Children
authors: Yanke Sun, Sally E. Day, Thomas J. Gilbert, Maria Bell, Antonia F. de C. Hamilton, Jamie A. Ward
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Interpersonal synchrony (IS), a key indicator of social interactions, is traditionally assessed through video data and manual coding methods, a process that is time-consuming and subjective. This study presents an automated sensor-based framework for quantifying and visualizing IS using time series data collected from wearable sensors, demonstrated through a case study of interactions between autistic and neurotypical children in classroom settings. We evaluated time series similarity measures, including Cross-correlation (CC), Dynamic Time Warping (DTW), and Cross-Wavelet Analysis (XWA), as features for machine learning (ML) models that classify interaction levels, where ground truth labels are derived from video-coded motor coordination as a behavioral proxy for IS. Results show that these similarity-based features outperform conventional statistical features in distinguishing high and low IS using ensemble classifiers. We further compare two approaches for identifying pseudosynchrony: a surrogate data analysis for threshold estimation and a supervised learning approach for direct prediction, providing a systematic evaluation of their methodological trade-offs that has been largely overlooked in prior synchrony research. The developed visualization tools enable dynamic tracking of interaction patterns while filtering out pseudosynchrony. The proposed workflow offers a scalable, objective, and reproducible alternative to manual coding, addressing a key gap in the current literature and supporting broader applications in social, developmental, and rehabilitation research.

title: Brewster Blind Spot: Polarization-Dependent Fisher Information Collapse in Sub-THz Penetration Sensing
authors: Qiang Wu, Weiqing Huang
abstract: Jointly estimating wall permittivity and thickness from sub-terahertz penetration loss is fundamentally limited by parameter coupling, yet the role of polarization in this ill-conditioning has not been examined. This letter reveals that the TM-polarized Fisher information matrix undergoes rank-one collapse at the Brewster angle, with the condition number exhibiting an inverse-square divergence that persists for lossy building materials with tan δ ≤ 0.1, while the TE-polarized counterpart is immune and improves monotonically. The collapse drives the Cramér–Rao lower bound on wall thickness to exceed the physical wall dimension threefold. Monte Carlo maximum-likelihood estimation reaches the dual-polarization bound. To counter this blind spot, joint dual-polarization observation at a single oblique angle achieves conditioning comparable to a two-angle design, yielding 68- to 242-fold improvement across five building materials through electronic polarization switching rather than beam steering.

title: Carrier-Based PWM Strategy of Three-Level Neutral-Point-Clamped Inverter with Optimal Extension Mode
authors: Guozheng Zhang, Yufei Liu, Xin Gu, Wenhao Huang, Wei Chen
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: Neutral point voltage ripple is the main technical drawback of the three-level neutral-point-clamped (3L-NPC) inverters. Conventional hybrid PWM strategies, which combine zero sequence voltage injection PWM (ZVI-PWM) with double modulation wave PWM (DMW-PWM), can be used to suppress neutral point voltage ripple for all operation conditions. However, the switching frequency of the inverter will be significantly increased in the condition of high modulation index and low power factor. To solve the above issue, a hybrid carrier-based PWM strategy with optimal extension mode is proposed in this paper. Different from conventional hybrid PWM strategies, ZVI-PWM is extended to the neutral point voltage uncontrollable intervals. The optimal extension mode and the optimal width ratio of the extended intervals are obtained to minimize the amplitude of the neutral point voltage ripple. The coordinated control of the switching frequency and the neutral point voltage ripple is realized. The feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed modulation strategy are validated by experimental results

title: Invariant Manifold Based Model-Free Predictive Control for PMSM Motor with Adaptive Time Constant
authors: Yixiao Luo, Junqiang Luo, Jiangchun Liu, Kai Yang, Shuangxia Niu, Jincheng Yu
abstract: Most existing finite control set model-free predictive control (FCS-MFPC) methods based on ultra-local model involve complex function structures and multiple hyperparameters, which inevitably increase the complexity of system structure. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes an observer-less FCS-MFPC scheme suitable for permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) based on invariant manifold constraint. The key lies in utilizing a low-pass filter to construct constraints, thereby defining a stable convergence subspace for the online computation of unknown nonlinear terms. In this way, only one parameter, namely the time constant needs to be tuned. Meanwhile, to balance the estimation accuracy and the robustness, the time constant of the low-pass filter is online optimized using the gradient descent method for different operating scenarios. The proposed method is analyzed using Lyapunov theory to ensure system convergence. The proposed method presents a simple structure with high robustness. Finally, experimental results validate the practical effectiveness of the proposed method. Specifically, it demonstrates a 50 - 70% reduction in both steady-state torque ripple and phase current THD compared to the traditional method, alongside enhanced robustness against parameter mismatch.

title: Split-Ring Resonator-Based RF Detector for Wireless Communication Applications
authors: Ch. Raghavendra, M. Baladithya, P. Pranav, B. Bharath Reddy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This project deals with the design of a small RF detector based on a split-ring resonator (SRR). The idea behind using an SRR is to study a simple wireless sensing method that can be used in practical communication setups. Because of the SRR shape, the structure shows good electromagnetic coupling and a clear resonance response, which helps in detecting RF signals. The detector is designed on a microstrip line, and the SRR dimensions are slightly changed to store more electric field energy in the structure, which in turn improves the sensitivity. Simulation studies and basic analytical calculations are carried out to study and adjust key parameters such as the resonant frequency and resonance response. The simulated results show noticeable shifts in resonance for different RF input conditions. Since the proposed detector is compact, low-cost, and easy to integrate, it shows good potential for applications such as wireless monitoring and spectrum sensing.

title: Towards Comprehensive Semi-Formal Verification of AMBA AHB/APB Interconnects: Configuration Coverage, Mutation Testing, and Safety-Aware Design-for-Verification
authors: Reshav Jaswal, Mamta Khosla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Verification dominates effort in modern System-on-Chip (SoC) design, particularly for interconnects such as AMBA AHB and APB bridges. These interconnects are widely used but prone to subtle corner-case failures that escape simulation-only verification. This paper presents ConfigVerMet++, a semi-formal verification methodology tailored to AHB–APB interconnects. ConfigVerMet++ integrates configuration-driven coverage analysis, simulation, formal unreachability classification (UNR), counterexample-directed test generation, mutation-based robustness checks, safety-aware assertions, and design-for-verification (DFV) hooks. We apply the methodology to a representative AHB–APB subsystem implemented in SystemVerilog and show progression from baseline simulation-only functional coverage of about 75% to complete coverage closure at 100%. The paper presents detailed results: assertion proofs, UNR classification, counterexample-driven tests, mutation detection.

title: Defected Ground Structure Antenna for Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) for IoT Applications
authors: Maryam Liaqat, Matheus Henrique Barros da Silva, Maria Luysa Teles Barcelar, Antonio Celso Dantas Antonino, Maklayne Barros de Pontes Santiago, Frederico Dias Nunes
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: A low-profile miniaturized DGS Antenna with defected ground is investigated for the high efficiency RFID based IoT applications. The FR4 substrate (ε<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 4,3 and tanδ = 0.030) of thickness 1.6mm is used for fabrication with overall geometry of 22 X 21 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and ground plane defects which make it suitable for RFID applications. This prototype working at 1.94 GHz with return loss of -30 dB having bandwidth of 40 MHz form 1.92 GHz to 1.96 GHz along minimum VSWR< 1.25 and ARBW<3 dB values. The proposed antenna is in the range of ultra-high frequency (UHF) with DGS design and good agreement with S-parameters, VSWR, ARBW and SAR, demonstrating its suitability for modern wireless communication applications.

title: Investigation of Linearity Performance in p-GaN Gate Enhancement-Mode High Electron Mobility Transistor
authors: Nipun Sharma, Ashish Raman, Pankaj Kumar, Sahil Hamid, Mohit Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This work examines the linearity of a p-GaN gate HEMT using TCAD simulation. The p-GaN gate configuration is incorporated to achieve a positive threshold voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf>) needed for enhancement mode operation. The device’s I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</inf>-V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gs</inf> characteristics are first analysed to verify the V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf>, drain current (I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</inf>) behaviour and transconductance of the device. The device exhibits normally-off behaviour, achieving a positive V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> of 1.15 V and a maximum I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</inf> of 4.5 × 10⁻⁴ A/µm. Linearity performance is then analysed using the fundamental linearity parameters. The impact of drain-to-source voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ds</inf>) on device linearity is examined by determining these characteristics under various bias conditions. Based on our analysis and results, we discovered that the device exhibited optimal linearity. The research indicates that the proposed p-GaN HEMT exhibits remarkable linearity.

title: Interpretable Emotion Detection in Social Media Using Deep Learning and Transformer-Based Models
authors: Shamyla Firdoos, Muhammad Sheraz Ul Hassan, Ali Imran Cheema, Muhammad Ahmed
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The detection of emotions in social media has emerged as an important task to understand opinion of the people, improve human-computer interaction, and assist in healthcare, marketing, and education. This paper explores the use of deep learning and transformer-based models to detect multi-class emotions, focusing on post-hoc interpretability. We used GPT-based data augmentation to balance the distribution of classes using a Kaggle dataset of 34,792 text samples in 8 emotions, increasing the dataset to 64,085 samples after augmentation. Classical RNN models (RNN, LSTM, GRU, BiLSTM, BiGRU) were used as well as transformer models (BERT, ALBERT, RoBERTa, DeRoBERTa, BERTweet, etc.). Findings indicate that despite the high accuracy (0.7655) achieved by BiLSTM among the RNN models, transformer models always performed better with BERTweet being the best performing model (Accuracy = 0.8367, F1 = 0.8376) following augmentation. To introduce transparency, the LIME framework was incorporated, which can be interpreted by emphasizing the words that had an impact on each prediction. Overall, this study contributes by combining performance and interpretability, positioning BERTweet with augmentation and XAI as an effective solution for emotion detection in social media contexts. Future research will be aimed at expanding the research to multilingual and multimodal emotion detection to make the research more applicable to the real world.

title: Zero-Shot vs. Supervised: Evaluating Multilingual LLMs for 3R Content Moderation in Malaysia
authors: Ong Yew Chuan, Sharifah Sumayyah Engku Alwi, Azwa Abdul Aziz, Azilawati Rozaimee, Shafiq Angullia Tawfeik Sulleiman, Belal Al Ifan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The idea of automated moderation of Race, Religion, and Royalty (3R) content on the Malaysian social media is not easy since people use colloquial Malay, Malaysian English ("Manglish"), and mixed language when discussing a sensitive subject. We test zero-shot prompting with multilingual large language models (LLMs) on the SYN3R-TIKTOK dataset, and we compare three instruction-tuned models with supervised XLM-RoBERTa baselines across different prompts and languages. The results show that leading proprietary LLMs reach almost the same performance as supervised models, but they fall behind fine-tuned baselines by less than 3% Macro-F1, and open-weight models struggle more with informal language. The error analysis shows frequent confusion between Race and Religion, and this happens because these categories overlap in Malaysian discourse and because the task allows only one label, not because the models make random mistakes. We also find that clear and descriptive prompts reduce this confusion for smaller models. Because of these results, we propose a hybrid moderation system in which simple supervised models handle clear cases, and stronger LLMs handle content that is linguistically and socially complex.

title: Energy Efficient Mono-Crystalline Solar Cell using blended dual layer as Anti-Reflective Coating
authors: Sarabdeep Singh, Prabhat Singh, Parveen Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Increasing the efficiency of solar cells is a key concern which is addressed in this study. It presents an approach that incorporates an Anti-Reflective Coating (ARC), to enhance the potential of mono-crystalline solar cells. The ARC layer is made of titanium dioxide (TiO2) and Silicon dioxide (SiO2). It provides a better light absorption over a wide range of wavelengths by reducing surface reflection losses. Results show that optimised p-type doping of 1e18 with a work function of 4.2 eV, yields higher performance with increased photocurrent and low thermal voltages over changing bias values. Thus, provides a clear approach for enhancing the energy conversion efficiency of solar cells in commercial applications. Proposed study paves a cost-effective and scalable answer for improving performance, in applications demanding high-efficiency power harvesting during harsh environmental conditions like solar panels, solar-powered EVs, and Space, etc..

title: A Novel Epileptic Seizure Detection Technique using Hybrid Convolutional-Spiking Neural Network
authors: Prem Kumar, Viplav Singh, Nishant, Chittotosh Ganguly
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Automated detection of epileptic seizures from multi-channel EEG signals is critical for clinical diagnosis and patient monitoring. This paper introduces a novel hybrid deep learning architecture that synergistically combines the robust feature extraction capabilities of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with the temporal processing strengths of Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs). Our model first employs a deep 1D-CNN to learn hierarchical spatial-temporal features from EEG time windows. The resulting high-level feature sequence is then processed by a recurrent layer of Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) spiking neurons, which captures long-range temporal dependencies inherent in seizure dynamics. To ensure the model’s robustness and optimal performance, we implemented a systematic hyperparameter optimization strategy using the Optuna framework, automatically tuning key parameters including learning rate, architectural choices, and regularization. The proposed model was rigorously evaluated on the public CHB-MIT dataset using a dedicated 60/20/20 train/validation/test split. The final, optimized architecture achieves an accuracy of 90.97%, with a sensitivity of 90.62% and a specificity of 91.32% on the unseen test set. These results validate the efficacy of our hybrid CNNSNN approach and underscore the importance of automated hyperparameter tuning for developing state-of-the-art biomedical signal classification systems.

title: Enhancing Land Registration System Using Blockchain Technology
authors: Surendra Kumar Shukla, Vishan Kumar Gupta, Paras Jain, Pawan Kumar, Vikas Tyagi, Atul Kumar Dwivedi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Land registration in India is a process that is prerequisite in the affirmation of legal ownership and is faced with continued hindrances such as inefficiencies, fraud and record discrepancies. This study suggests a new solution which is founded on blockchain technology that optimizes the current structure by offering a decentralized and immutable registry that is skilled in handling transactions in a peer-to-peer network with a high level of efficiency and security. The application of blockchain technology based on cryptographic principles provides transparency, integrity, and tamper-resistant records, which makes it one of the best solutions to the modernization of land registry processes. Our proposed solution uses Ethereum smart contracts to enable the process of verifying and transferring ownership to be automated, thus, the feedback of the need to use middlemen is minimized, and the chances of human error are minimized. Furthermore, the off-chain storage of the ancillary documents is done with the help of Interplanetary File System (IPFS), which ensures scalability and efficient storage solutions. We demonstrate how the presented system can speed up the processes of land transactions, improve security, and validate the ownership authenticity using the framework of these decentralized technologies, and we will also solve the main weaknesses, including falsification of records and fraudster activities. The results of this study highlight the disruptive nature of blockchain technology to redefine land registration as a more efficient, transparent, and secure operation, therefore, authorizing property ownership a practice that conventional systems in the past found difficult to achieve.

title: An Ensemble Hybrid Machine Learning Framework for Network Security Enhancement
authors: Pavithra Danaveni, Prashanth Ragam, Ramya Bharathi Chalapaka
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The primary motive for conducting this research work is to design and analyze the effectiveness of the machine learning approach for the objective of classifying the network traffics as normal (0) or anomaly (1). The proposed work will implement various machine learning algorithms for the purposes of designing the intrusion detection system. The trials will be conducted based on the network traffics to analyze various statistical measures. For achieving accurate results and unbiased assessment, the dataset would be split into 85% for training and remaining 15% for testing. Error- and variance-based metrics include R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, MSE, RMSE, NRMSE, and MAD, which give insight into the prediction accuracy and stability of model performance. Besides the conventional models, a hybrid XGBoost-Random Forest approach is developed in order to enhance the detection capability by combining the strengths of Boosting with Ensemble Learning. The results clearly showed that the proposed hybrid model yields better performance in prediction compared to individual baseline algorithms. Moreover, it provides higher accuracy and much better adaptability to evolving intrusion patterns. Intrusion detection will be performed with the continuous monitoring of network activity, analyzing traffic behavior by comparing observed patterns against learned representations of normal and anomalous events. In general, these results confirm the effectiveness of machine learning techniques in IDS applications. They also point to a way in which the proposed model may provide a reliable and effective solution for network security problems using an XGBoost-RF model with a high R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> value of approximately 97%.

title: A Comprehensive Survey on Machine Learning and Deep Learning Approaches for Lung Disease Detection
authors: Mukesh Sharma, Vijay Kumar Garg
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Recent developments in artificial intelligence (AI), specifically machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL), have had a major impact on the pulmonary disease detection, diagnosis, and prognosis. This survey offers a suitable overview of the state-of-the-art AI-based methods used in the lung disease research, but it is mainly focused on lung cancer. The research review is a systematic review of thirty new studies, differentiating approaches, data modeling, quantification of performance, and explainable methods. Based on the comparative analysis, it is clear that deep learning models and convolutional neural networks in particular are superior in imaging-based detection problems and provide high diagnostic accuracy, whereas ensemble-based and classical ML are more useful in structured clinical and longitudinal data. The analysis based on visualization also proves that the notable portion of the studies focuses on detection and diagnosis, and the prediction of the prognosis, estimation of risks, identification of biomarkers, and modeling of treatment outcomes are under-explored. Other aspects of explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) as SHAP and Grad-CAM are also not widely adopted, yet they are vital to clinical trust and interpretability. As these observations indicate, some of the main gaps in research are outlined in that there is a lack of multimodal data integration, no universal benchmark, no external validation, and difficulty in practical clinical implementation. Lastly, the paper will provide research directions in the future focusing on multimodal learning, federated AI models and privacy-preserving AI models along with standardized evaluation regimens to develop reliable, interpretable and clinically deployable AI solutions to lung disease management and detection.

title: Energy-Fair and Reliable Carrier-Sense Multiple Access for LoRaWAN
authors: Chenglong Shao
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper presents ER-MAC, a novel medium access control protocol for LoRaWAN that enhances both energy fairness and communication reliability. Unlike existing carriersense multiple access (CSMA) schemes, ER-MAC adaptively adjusts the number and interval of Channel Activity Detection (CAD) operations according to the CAD energy consumption of different spreading factors (SFs), ensuring balanced sensing energy among end devices using distinct SFs. ER-MAC also employs a gateway-based announcement mechanism to mitigate the hidden-terminal problem and improve transmission reliability. Experimental results show that ER-MAC achieves more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 1 \%}$</tex> improvement in energy fairness and up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0 \%}$</tex> increase in packet reception ratio over state-of-the-art CSMA approaches.

title: Designing a Locality-Aware Index for Prefecture-Level Recipe Recommendations
authors: Yiping Ding, Tianyu Wang, Yuanyuan Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Japanese food culture is deeply rooted in local regions, yet large online recipe platforms rarely provide explicit geographic information. This paper proposes a text-based Kyodo Index (KI), which uses local dishes (Kyodo) from the Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries (MAFF) “Uchi no Kyodo Ryori” project, railway boxed lunches (Ekiben), and user-generated recipes to construct a 47-dimensional prefectural locality profile for each recipe via Japanese sentence embeddings. First, we build a unified, 12-class dish taxonomy using Rakuten Recipe data. Then, we train a pure text classifier so that the three data sources would share the same main-category labels. Next, we treat Kyodo and Ekiben dishes as anchors, compute KI scores for Rakuten recipes based on textual similarity, and combine heuristic matches between titles and anchor names to generate, for each prefecture and each dish category, a Top-10 list containing up to ten recipes.

title: Bridging the Robustness Gap: Stress-Testing Artificial Intelligence Algorithms
authors: Sergillam B. Oliveira, Eddie de Lima Filho, Lucas C. Cordeiro
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The increasing deployment of artificial intelligence (AI) elements in safety-critical systems exposes a major concern: models that prove reliable on clean data can be brittle under real-world perturbations. Besides, that may vary according to different model classes, e.g., supervised and unsupervised ones, which should guide practical applications. In that sense, numerous evaluation techniques do exist, but there is no structured scheme or results that allow for comparing and choosing between different approaches. As a response, we have conducted a study based on a methodology that applies controlled, synthetic perturbations to data, labels, and model parameters, focusing on the experimental evaluation of supervised and unsupervised algorithms. We have stress-tested 10 algorithms across 5 diverse datasets, whose outcome revealed a pronounced dichotomy: unsupervised models may suffer near-structural collapse, whereas supervised models can retain partial functionality due to the anchoring effect of labels. In conclusion, these results suggest systematic stress testing as a prerequisite for reliable and safer AI systems.

title: Exact 1-Bit Precoding for MIMO System by Deep Learning-Based Branching Variable Selection
authors: Seiryu Sugiyama, Shunta Arai, Satoshi Takabe
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: 1-bit precoding is a promising technique in massive MIMO systems. It addresses the challenge of high power consumption by using low-resolution digital-to-analog converters. However, precoder optimization is a non-convex combinatorial problem, implying that obtaining an optimal solution via branch-and-bound (BnB) is computationally demanding, with efficiency highly dependent on branching rules. To overcome this, we propose a machine learning-based branching variable selection method to accelerate BnB for the 1-bit precoding problem. We design a model combining a graph convolutional network and multi-layer perceptron with feature-wise linear modulation layers to score branching variables efficiently by imitating the vanilla full strong (VFS) rule. Numerical experiments show that our method outperforms VFS and other baselines, achieving fewer explored nodes and the shortest total solving time.

title: Introducing Optimization Techniques into Formal Verification for Faster Convergence and Reduced Runtime
authors: Adepu Saiteja, Mamta Khosla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper presents a set of optimization techniques aimed at improving convergence speed and reducing runtime in formal verification. Although formal verification is becoming more common in industrial design flows, its adoption is often hindered by inefficient implementation practices and long proof convergence times. To address these challenges, we first introduce a structured and practical verification methodology that guides engineers through the entire verification flow—from property creation and RTL preparation to error analysis and proof closure. This approach is designed to be systematic, repeatable, and suitable for real-world designs. Building on this methodology, the main contribution of this work is a set of optimization techniques developed to accelerate proof convergence and reduce solver complexity. These techniques include targeted adding constraints, parametrizing the modules and reducing parameter size, black boxing the standard modules that adapt during the verification process. Rather than relying on conventional methods, the proposed approach focuses on limiting unnecessary state-space exploration while maintaining proof completeness. Results from representative design examples show that the proposed techniques significantly improve proof success rates and achieve meaningful reductions in overall verification time. Together, these contributions offer a practical and scalable solution that improves the efficiency, reliability, and industrial usability of formal verification.

title: Deep Learning Based Framework for Automated Gray Scale Image Colorization
authors: Shuvrajit Dey, Debajyoti Nath, Swanirbhar Majumder, Sanjib Debnath, Jayanta Pal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Colorization of black-and-white images, which was a tiresome process manually done earlier is completely automated today owing to the power of Convolutional Neural Networks(CNNs). This paper introduces a deep learning-based model that can breathe new colors into grayscale images with intelligent predictions about the missing color information. It performs the computation in the LAB color space and produces a colorized image with realistic colors. A GUI-friendly interface has been developed to make this technology easily accessible to everyone. A user only needs to upload grayscale images, click a button, and watch as the model works magic out, producing brilliant colorized versions. Applications for this technology are diverse, ranging from reconstructing old photographs and historical films to improving medical imaging. This approach significantly reduces the time and effort to its maximum usage in different businesses, hence making it a valuable work in general. This indicates deep learning's ability to transform the image processing activities so that the world may become a colorized version of monochrome.

title: AI Powered IoT System for Speed Regulation and Road Sign Based Accident Prevention
authors: Praveensanthoshkumar G, Harinisree K, Indhumathi S, Mayooria G
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: An innovative system for enhancing road safety has been developed on the Raspberry Pi platform that will utilize AI for road sign recognition, real-time driver behavior detection, and ultimately improve vehicle safety. It continues to track both the driver and their surrounding environment as it continuously detects and interprets critical traffic signs such as: speed limits, warning signs, mandatory instruction signs, etc. When a speed limit is detected, the system automatically adjusts vehicle speed so that the driver will remain within safe operating limits — preventing possible accidents due to over speed. In addition to monitoring the surrounding environment, the system is designed to include driver behavior detection based on various sources, including video-based algorithms and biometric sensors, to detect driver signs of fatigue such as: eye closure, yawning, tilting of the head, etc. When the system detects early indicators of drowsiness, it immediately alerts the driver through in-vehicle alerts to help capture their attention. If the driver continues to demonstrate persistent drowsiness or abnormal behavior, the system escalates the response by sending real-time notifications to registered family members for their awareness. Improved intervening has been created from the implementation of new vehicle systems through the collection of information and communication with the driver; which will lead to improved driver safety through increased awareness of his/her health and well-being, especially for long distance and night time travelers. With the health and well-being of drivers as the main priority, this program supports driver wellness and eliminates the chances of accidents occurring as a result of fatigue-related impairments. The use of embedded systems and innovative AI technology will help support the implementation of these programs as per SDG 9 (Industries, innovation, and infrastructure) via the creation of smart transportation solutions based on technology. Additionally, by creating an environment in which drivers are safe and reducing roadway fatalities through intelligent mobility, this program supports Sustainable Development Goal 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities). Therefore, the goal of the proposed activity is to provide a more proactive approach to addressing the problem of transportation-related accidents by leveraging AI technology to create safer, more sustainable means of transportation.

title: Graceful Degradation of Spacecraft Capabilities with Simulated Component Failure
authors: Josef Biberstein, Sertac Karaman, Olivier De Weck, David Sternberg, Lorraine Fesq, Ksenia Kolcio, Maurice Prather
abstract: Development of fault detection, identification, and recovery systems for space missions requires engineers to consider interactions among all spacecraft subsystems. These systems must be robust and rigorously tested so as to not cause harm to the spacecraft. Combined with the complexity of the interactions that must be considered, this leads to conservative approaches even in modern spacecraft where the system includes redundant components, yet the response to most faults is to transition to safe mode and wait for intervention from the ground. However, a single fault may not disable a spacecraft to an extent which precludes continued operation, especially in spacecraft with redundant components. In this case, transitioning to safe mode and waiting for human intervention leads to mission downtime which impacts spacecraft performance and could be avoided. In this work, we prototype a new framework for fault detection, identification, and recovery that introduces graceful degradation of spacecraft capability in the presence of component failure. To demonstrate our framework, we focus on the attitude control subsystem. We simulate using the syssim library, a software package that enables the modular modeling of arbitrary dynamical systems with a robust and expressive API for defining faults to be injected at runtime. The MONSID library - a model-based health assessment software based on the technique of constraint suspension developed by Okean Solutions - is used in the loop for fault detection and diagnosis. We evaluate the performance of this ensemble on a non-trivial cascading fault in a set of sensors associated with an attitude control system. Graceful degradation is demonstrated across groups of redundant components that allow the spacecraft to continue operation even when a single component, such as a reaction wheel or gyroscope, has failed.

title: Built to Die: Bioinspired Locomoting Robot with Programmed End-of-Life Biodegradation
authors: Neera H. Raychaudhuri, Seola Lee, Mady Corrigan, Hanna Sigurdson, Franklin Ho, Peisheng He, Katia Bertoldi, Robert J. Wood
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: In a world where engineering innovation is growing in parallel with pollution and climate changes caused by its byproducts, researchers face an increasing urgency to develop new materials and systems that are environmentally friendly. While many robots have historically been designed to perform challenging or dangerous tasks in remote environments, deployed robots will contribute to pollution if they are lost or not retrieved in full. In this work, we propose a design scheme for replacing synthetic components of a known soft robot with entirely biodegradable alternatives to demonstrate the opportunities for systems that can be deployed without concern for robot failure or retrieval. Additionally, our robot demonstrates programmable end-of-life biodegradation, in this case when in contact with water. Our robust yet biodegradable robot remains operable for days under standard conditions. Upon contact with the programmed stimulus, water, the robot ceases operation and begins degrading, allowing future on-board payloads to be selectively deposited into a water-rich area. This robot demonstrates critical components for our long-term vision of future robot designs that can be rapidly prototyped and deployed to complete tasks autonomously without consideration for post-operation collection and failure contingencies.

title: Automated Crack Detection in Concrete Structures Using Image Processing and Deep Learning Techniques
authors: N. Mohanapriya, B. Varsha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Concrete crack detection is an essential utility that guards the structural health since surface cracks are early warning of structural damages. The existing deep learning techniques are very accurate at detecting and segmenting cracks but their functionality is limited since it only works with two-dimensional images, and it cannot provide any accurate measurements of cracks in the real world. The study presents Geo Crack Net++ that integrates the technology of two-dimensional and three-dimensional deep learning to relate visual crack detection with engineering level crack measurements. The suggested framework is made up of two closely integrated steps. The first stage uses convolutional neural networks to classify and create semantic segmentation which enables crack detection and pixel-level crack mask generation from concrete surface images. The second step applies the structure-from-motion to construct three dimensional space by matching the number of images and model three-dimensional models of the segmented crack areas which makes it possible to measure crack length and width at the real-world dimensions. The suggested system achieved a crack. With an Intersection over Union value of 0.852 and a classification score of 98.2, the system fared better than the other top 2D segmentation models. Geo Crack Net++ attains better crack length estimation accuracy of 3.6 mm to 12.4 mm and also better crack width estimation accuracy of 0.41 to 1.35 mm. The results prove that semantic segmentation in combination with three-dimensional geometrical reconstruction allows automated crack inspection systems to be more accurate. Geo Crack Net++ system offers a reliable system capable of identifying cracks and monitoring their locations as it gives accurate measurements that suit it in the civil infrastructure monitoring in real-life conditions.

title: Workspace Expansion in a Tendon-Driven Soft Joint through Base-Rotation Mechanism
authors: Claudia Sánchez, Jaime González-Menocal, Lisbeth Mena, Jorge Muñoz, Concepción A. Monje
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Tendon-driven soft joints provide high compliance and mechanical adaptability, but their morphology often restricts curvature and limits the attainable workspace. This work introduces a geometric strategy to expand the reachable workspace by integrating a discrete rotation mechanism into the joint. The mechanism enables reorientation of the bending plane without altering the soft structure, unlocking configurations previously constrained by morphology-induced blocking. An extended kinematic model is formulated to incorporate the base rotation angle into the constant-curvature representation, and the predicted workspace expansion is evaluated both numerically and experimentally. The prototype with discrete base rotation achieves up to a 60% increase in effective inclination range under controlled tendon actuation, while maintaining the intrinsic blocking behavior of the soft link. These results demonstrate that base rotation provides a practical solution to enhance the versatility and functional range of tendon-driven soft robotic systems.

title: Hybrid Spatial-Temporal Attention Networks for Plant Disease Classification: a Benchmarking Study of Deep Learning Architectures
authors: Amit Kumar Behera, Ankit Kumar, Suresh Kumar Gaddi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The accurate plant disease identification strategies can be critical for crop productivity and food security. This paper benchmarks several deep learning architectures for plant disease classification on the PlantVillage dataset with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 1, 4 8 6}$</tex> images and 38 disease categories. The individual models assessed include typical CNNs (VGG19, ResNet50, DenseNet121, and EfficientNetB0/B4), a tailored CNN baseline, hybrids (CNN+Attention and CNN+LSTM), a CNN-LSTM-Temporal Attention architecture, and a transformer-like MAE-ViT. ResNet50 yields the highest accuracy of 99.39%. The best result in terms of accuracy (98.88%) was achieved by the proposed CNN+Attention model based on a spatial attention module applied after the CNN feature extractor and aimed at learning to attend to relevant regions in the image for disease. The training time for this model was also reported to be the lowest at 66.1 minutes. The CNN+LSTM model, which feed spatial feature maps to a bidirectional LSTM to capture multi-region dependencies, acquires 98.59% in 68.6 minutes. The CNN-LSTM-Temporal Attention variant adds this with a temporal attention layer that runs over the full sequence of LSTM outputs, achieving 98.15% in 82.9 minutes. MAE-ViT manages 89.37% only, showing that transformer designs need big and varying datasets to stay fit against structured agricultural images. Results demonstrate that hybrid CNN architectures deliver competitive accuracy with reduced computational overhead, with CNN+Attention offering the optimum accuracy-efficiency tradeoff for real-time and on-the-edge agricultural monitoring.

title: A Topology-Driven Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithm for Offshore Wind Farm Layout Optimization
authors: Yixin Shao, Baohang Zhang, Wenzhu Gu, Zhenyu Lei, Shangce Gao
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The design of offshore wind farms requires optimizing turbine layouts and electrical cabling simultaneously to maximize annual energy production (AEP) and minimize costs. This paper proposes a novel topology-driven multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (T-MOEA) for offshore wind farm layout optimization (WFLO). The T-MOEA introduces spatial clustering and network-aware mutation operators that account for aerodynamic wake interactions and cable capacity constraints in one integrated framework. Using a bi-objective formulation (AEP vs. total cable cost), the proposed method efficiently searches for turbine placement and routing configurations that balance energy yield and infrastructure expense. We benchmark T-MOEA on a real offshore wind farm and compare it against five state-of-the-art algorithms. Results show that T-MOEA converges faster and achieves a superior Pareto front, improving the hypervolume (HV) by up to 0.65 and reducing inverted generational distance (IGD) by 50% relative to alternatives. The obtained layouts demonstrate a clear trade-off between higher energy output and lower cabling cost, giving practical insight into offshore design decisions. In conclusion, the topology-driven approach significantly enhances optimization performance for wind farm layout design.

title: Carbon-I: Expanding the Frontiers of Carbon Cycle Science
authors: Andrew K. Thorpe, Christian Frankenberg, Yunjin Kim, William E. Frazier, Timothy M. Linn, Lockheed Martin, Anna M. Michalak, Valerie J. Scott, Sharmila Padmanabhan, K. Dana Chadwick, Philip G. Brodrick, David R. Thompson, Robert O. Green, Matthew W. Smith, Christine L. Bradley
abstract: Earth's dynamic carbon cycle-unique among known planets—drives the growth of our food, sustains the oxygen we breathe, and generated the fossil fuels that powered the Industrial Revolution. The Carbon Investigation (Carbon-I) mission provides unprecedented clarity on this vital cycle by delivering high-resolution measurements of the main carbon-bearing molecules in the atmosphere: methane <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{CH}_{4}\right)$</tex>, carbon dioxide <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{CO}_{2}\right)$</tex>, and carbon monoxide (CO). Carbon-I directly addresses multiple NASA Decadal Survey objectives by closing the tropical data gap, focusing on natural emission sources (wetlands, permafrost, agriculture), measuring fire emissions, and providing robust source attribution across scales. The Carbon-I instrument builds on more than 40 years of imaging spectrometer technology development at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory. This Dyson imaging spectrometer provides a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 100 ~\text{km}$</tex> swath that delivers monthly global land coverage at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 400 ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 10$</tex> times finer sampling for high-priority areas. This spatial detail is combined with high-resolution atmospheric spectroscopy in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.04-2.37 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> shortwave infrared range to capture absorption lines of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CH}_{4}, \text{CO}_{2}, \text{CO}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}$</tex> (nitrous oxide) at a 0.7 nm spectral sampling interval combined with a spectral response function of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 2.5 ~\text{nm}$</tex> full width at half maximum. This ensures unambiguous discrimination of gas absorption lines and clear separation from surface albedo variations. Carbon-I is a NASA Earth System Explorer Mission Step 2 concept. The Carbon-I instrument flies on the LM400 bus, a proven midsized spacecraft architecture with significant flight heritage for all subsystems and components, including three-axis stabilization and low-jitter capabilities for high spatial sampling science needs. The payload data processor transmitter stores up to 8 Tbit of data, which is downlinked via a commercial Ka-band communication network, providing 1.46 Tb daily data <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\geq 100$</tex> million spectra per day). Carbon-I delivers actionable trace gas results at the local to regional scale to a diverse set of stakeholders and scientific communities. Beyond its core objectives, Carbon-I also contributes to water-cycle research by measuring <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} / \text{HDO}$</tex> to quantify evapotranspiration and study tropical land-atmosphere interactions. A Science Enhancement Option expands Carbon-I's scope to enhanced geologic mapping (critical minerals and rare earth elements), vegetation health (lignocellulose features for fire risk), and pollution monitoring (agricultural plastics, oil spills).

title: Short-Term Load Forecasting for the Cambodian Power System Using a Meta-Learning Ensemble of Random Forest and XGBoost Models
authors: Nop Phorn Nara, Sothea Has, Long Bun
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: This project aims to develop a short-term load forecasting framework for the Cambodian national grid load demand using a rolling-year hyperparameter tuning strategy with the meta-learning ensemble method integrated from Random Forest (RF) and eXtreme Gradient Boost (XGBoost) models. The studied data were the target six-year total hourly load demands from the years 2019 to 2024, which were formally requested from Electricité Du Cambodge (EDC), combined with meteorological variables, engineered features, and a calendar working indicator. The proposed methodology in this research was for each target test year <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y(t) \in[2020, 2021, 2022, 2023]$</tex>; the RF and XGBoost models were individually tuned using their respective hyperparameter grids by training and validating the data up to the year <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y(t-1) \in$</tex> [2019, 2020, 2021, 2022]. Then, the best-tuned parameter was used to train the models again on training and validating data up to the year <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y(t)$</tex> and make predictions for the years <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y(t+1) \in[2021,2022$</tex>, 2023, 2024]. After that, we built a new meta-stacked dataset from the year 2021 to 2024 containing two input features of predicted load demand values from the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{R F}$</tex> model (rf-pred) and predicted values from the XGBoost model (xgb-pred) combined with the target original load demand. This new dataset was split into a training dataset (the year 2021 to 2023) and a testing dataset (the year 2024) to train and test with the linear regression model (meta-LR). Root Mean Square (RMSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) were used to indicate each model's performance. The obtained results indicated that the MAPEs of RF and XGBoost were 5.18 % and 5.05 %, respectively, while the proposed meta-learning (meta-LR) outperformed with 3.86 %. These experiments would assist EDC in committing to the generation scheduling, ensuring grid stability, optimizing operational efficiency, and effectively integrating renewable energy sources.

title: Design of a Gamified Mobile Application for Preventive Student Mental Health Support
authors: Mega Kartika Sari, Merryana Lestari, Agustinus Fritz Wijaya, Donny Avianto, Robin William Awi, Harun Bless Kanaan Simamora
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Student mental health has emerged as an increasingly important issue, shaped by the escalating academic and social pressures associated with higher education. The limitations on counseling services and the inadequate awareness among students regarding their psychological well-being highlight the significance of accessible, preventative support systems. Advances in mobile technology have enabled the development of accessible and user-centered digital mental health support systems. This study aims to develop a gamified mobile application to improve students' mental well-being and to conduct an initial quantitative assessment of the application's design. This research utilizes a Design Science Research (DSR) methodology, comprising the phases of problem identification, artifact development, and initial design assessment. The prototype integrates selfassessment, gamified emotion regulation activities, and threshold-based reflective feedback. An early quantitative evaluation involving 137 students was conducted using descriptive frequency-based analysis to assess perceived feature relevance, engagement, and privacy concerns. Over 78 % of respondents perceived self-assessment and gamified emotion regulation activities as relevant, while 91.9% emphasized the importance of privacy protection. The threshold-based categorization mechanism was positively received as a structured reflective support tool. The system does not provide diagnostic or predictive functionality and is positioned strictly as a structured self-monitoring tool. These findings indicate that the gamification-inspired design and threshold-based structured reflective support approach could be further refined into an information technology-based mental health support system for students.

title: AI-Powered E-Triage Systems: Enhancing Healthcare Efficiency Through Intelligent Clinical Decision Support
authors: Mohamed Elsersy, Ahmed Abdelwahab
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI)-powered e-triage systems are transforming patient intake and clinical decision-making by automating workflows and improving diagnostic precision. These systems address critical challenges in emergency and outpatient settings, where rapid assessment and prioritization are crucial for delivering optimal care. This paper introduces an integrated architecture comprising four core components: a Data Ingestion Module that consolidates patient information from electronic health records (EHRs), wearable devices, and intake forms; an AI Triage Engine employing machine learning algorithms to evaluate patient urgency and predict risk; a Documentation Generator utilizing natural language processing (NLP) for automated admission reporting; and a Prescription Assistant that recommends evidence-based treatments aligned with clinical guidelines. Real-world deployments in hospitals across the UAE and GCC have demonstrated substantial operational improvements, including a 45 % reduction in documentation time and an 82 % increase in patient prioritization accuracy. These advancements not only enhance patient outcomes through early detection of critical conditions but also alleviate clinician workload, reducing burnout and cognitive strain. Key performance indicators (KPIs) such as diagnostic accuracy, patient throughput, and prescription error rates validate the system's effectiveness and scalability. Despite these benefits, challenges remain in ensuring data privacy compliance, mitigating algorithmic bias, and achieving seamless integration with existing EHR platforms. Future directions include incorporating real-time analytics, expanding wearable device interoperability, enabling multilingual support, and adopting human-in-the-loop models to maintain ethical and clinical robustness. By addressing these barriers, AI-powered e-triage systems can become a cornerstone of modern healthcare, delivering adaptive, efficient, and patient-centric care at scale.

title: Vision-Language Guided Pseudo-Labeling for Multi-Label Waste Classification
authors: Billy Eden William Asrul, Indrabayu, Syafruddin Syarif
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Cost efficient multi-label waste recognition in canals is hindered by clutter, occlusion, and illumination variability, while exhaustive annotation remains costly. This paper proposes TGS a vision-language-guided pseudo-label curation framework that integrates a ConvNeXt-Tiny (Convolutional Neural Network Next Tiny) classifier with Grounding DINO (text-conditioned detection) and the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for spatial verification. In TGS, a ConvNeXt-Tiny teacher trained on a small labeled subset produces image-level confidences on unlabeled images; selected labels are converted into text prompts and verified by Grounding DINO to generate prompt-consistent regions, which are refined into pixel-level masks by SAM. The core novelty lies in SAM-based mask verification, which gates pseudo-label retention using pixel-level evidence rather than confidence-only or box-level cues. Pseudo-labels are retained only when region and mask evidence satisfy quality filters, then combined with human labels to train a student model within a Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL) loop. Experiments use a canal dataset from Makassar, Indonesia (855 images, 18 classes, 2,662 instances); after quality control, 769 images are retained, with a fixed 171image test set and labeled fractions of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \%, 10 \%$</tex>, and 20 % sampled from a 598-image development pool. At <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 \%}$</tex> labeled data <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(86 / 512 / 171)$</tex>, TGS achieves micro-averaged F1-score (micro-F1) 0.9393 with pseudo-label coverage 0.9199, improving reliability over naïve self-training (micro-F1 0.9376, coverage 0.9512) and outperforming FixMatch (micro-F1 0.9036). At 20 % labeled data, micro-F1 reaches 0.9581, approaching the fully supervised upper bound (0.9896). Future work will audit pseudo-label quality on a manually annotated subset, adopt class adaptive thresholds, and benchmark efficiency and robustness for real- time deployment.

title: Contraction Vine Robots for Contact-Aided Steering
authors: Sehyun Park, Kaitlyn Lavarias, Sukjun Kim, Tania K. Morimoto
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: The steering ability of vine robots becomes increasingly crucial when navigating highly tortuous and constrained environments. In this study, we enhance the passive steering ability of vine robots by introducing shape changes induced by pressure differences through a bilayer tube design with slits, which we call the Contraction Vine Robot (CVR). Upon pressurization, the robot undergoes localized radial expansion and longitudinal contraction. By applying pressure oscillation, repeated changes in robot shape and length enables the robot tip to slide from the obstacle and continue to navigate. We established both static and geometric models to describe how radial expansion and longitudinal contraction occur under internal pressure and validated these models against experimental data. We also investigated the steering capabilities of the proposed robot design in various obstacle conditions and compared the results with a conventional vine robot. The results showed that compared to the conventional vine robot, our robot exhibited substantially superior performance along bumpy, tortuous paths.

title: Evaluation of Machine Learning Algorithms and Ensemble Methods for Prediction of Students' Graduation
authors: Soha A. Bahanshal, Vaibhav Verdhan, Bayong Kim, Bushra A. Al-Anesi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Graduation rates at six-year colleges are becoming a more essential indicator for incoming fresh students and for university rankings. Predicting student graduation is extremely beneficial to schools and has a huge potential for targeted intervention. It is important for educational institutions since it enables the development of strategic plans that will assist or improve students' performance in achieving their degrees on time (GOT). A first step and a helping hand in extracting useful information from these data and gaining insights into the prediction of students' progress and performance is offered by machine learning techniques. Data analysis and visualization techniques are applied to understand and interpret the data. The data used for the analysis contains students who have graduated in 6 years in the academic year 2017-2018 for science majors. This analysis can be used to predict the graduation of students in the next academic year. Different Predictive modeling such as logistic regression, decision trees, support vector machines, Random Forest, Naïve Bayes, and KNeighborsClassifier are applied to predict whether a student will graduate. These classifiers were evaluated with k folds of 5. The performance of these classifiers was compared based on accuracy measurement. The results indicated that Ensemble Classifier achieves better accuracy, about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 1. 1 2 \%}$</tex>. This GOT prediction model would hopefully be useful to university administration and academics in developing measures for assisting and boosting students' academic performance and ensuring they graduate on time.

title: Earth's Magnetopause Positions in Solar Wind Density-Velocity Space from 1963-2025
authors: Scott Carpenter, Remy Xie, Matthew Qi, Skyler Mao, Sophia Hu, Daniel Chen, Elena Yu, Mingyuan William Tang, Ranya Zhang, Emma Yu, Seabert Mao, Siuwa Andy Yang, Mingze Michael Tang, Zehan Luca He, William Sun, David Wu, Brayden Quo, Cherry Chen, Ziqing Angela Tang
abstract: In our prior work, we developed and successfully benchmarked the Portable Magnetopause (MP) Model against spacecraft-measured MP-position data for (1) magnetic fields and MPs at the dipole-bearing planets, and for (2) coronal mass ejection (CME) buffeting of Earth's MP. Here, we use the Portable MP Model to plot scaled spacecraft measures of solar-wind plasma density (independent x-axis) and velocity (independent y axis) to visualize the time-duration MP standoff distances (dependent z-axis contours) for Earth. The Portable MP Model accurately computes MP position for a variety of cases that include (i) planetary MPs in the ‘farfield,’ such as those for Earth, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune; and (ii) planetary MPs in the ‘nearfield,’ such as for Mercury, for proposed artificial MPs around Mars and other solar bodies, and for MPs generated by current rings placed at Lagrange-1 co-orbits. Additionally, the Portable MP Model is a firstprinciples model rather than a statistical model, so its terms are human understandable and interpretable. MP-standoff positions are plotted/visualized. Simplification of the model's math to basic algebra opens research into MP science to a wide audience: space-science and engineering professionals, math and science students from sixth grade to university graduate levels, and the industrious public.

title: Decentralized Radio Access Network Virtualization via Distributed Ledger Technology
authors: Ahmed Sarwar Mohammed
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: This paper explores the application of distributed ledger technology to facilitate dynamic resource allocation in virtualized radio access networks (RANs). A novel architecture is presented that leverages blockchain to automate the exchange of network functions among diverse stakeholders. This approach addresses challenges related to trust and scalability in open RAN environments, enabling autonomous and secure sharing of infrastructure resources. The proposed system employs auction-based mechanisms and network virtualization to allow entities to transact RAN resources, such as virtual network functions (VNFs), in a decentralized manner. Simulation results demonstrate the potential of this framework to enhance flexibility and efficiency in next-generation mobile networks.

title: GPU-Accelerated Merkle Tree Construction Using CUDA: A Parallel SHA-256 Framework for Cryptographic Applications
authors: Mithun S K, Avandhica S R, Arun M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Building the Merkle tree is a problem which seems simple until one attempt to make it fast. At small scales it is fine on the CPU, but the moment leaf counts climb into the tens of thousands - which is routine in any active blockchain or certificate log - the sequential SHA-256 hashing becomes a genuine bottleneck. This paper describes a CUDA implementation that addresses that gap by hashing all leaves at once on a GPU using real SHA-256, not a placeholder. The design uses shared memory tiling inside both the leaf and tree-level kernels, with Thrust handling device buffer management and CUDA Events measuring every stage precisely. Running on an NVIDIA Tesla T4 with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 8, 6 7 4}$</tex> leaves extracted from an actual IEEE paper, it finishes in under 2 ms compared to 78 ms on CPU - a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$68.7 \times$</tex> speedup - and processes 13.8 million leaves per second. Both kernels hit <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 \%}$</tex> theoretical occupancy. The GPU output was checked byte-for-byte against Python hashlib to confirm the SHA-256 implementation is correct, including the two-block padding case that catches out a lot of GPU hash implementations.

title: Arabic Calligraphy Classification: A Comparative Study of Modern CNN Models
authors: Ahlam Zain Alzaylaee, Hanen Himdi, Basma Alharbi, Nuha Zamzami
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Arabic calligraphy, with its rich diversity of styles, represents a cornerstone of Islamic cultural heritage and poses unique challenges for automated recognition owing to its high visual similarity and artistic variations. In this study, we proposed an explainable deep learning model for the classification of Arabic scripts based on a well-balanced dataset of 729 images from the HICMA dataset. By using contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) pre-processing, we were able to enhance the stylistic features used for training and compared four of the most popular models: ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0, ConvNeXt-Tiny, and DenseNet-121. ConvNeXt-Tiny proved to be the best model, achieving a macro-averaged F1 score (macro-F1) of 94% on both the internal and external datasets. To interpret the model's predictions, we relied on gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM) heat maps, which were able to identify characteristic features, such as the linear strokes of Naskh and the rounded flourishes of Diwani scripts. These findings confirm that state-of-the-art modern convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can be used effectively to tackle the complexities of Arabic calligraphy, providing a clear roadmap for the improvement of optical character recognition (OCR) technology and the digital protection of cultural heritage.

title: Reliable Maintenance Policy for Distributed Affine Formation Control of UAVs
authors: Hongyu Zhou, Zhonggang Li, Raj Thilak Rajan
abstract: Distributed affine formation control (AFC) enables unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) swarms to achieve coordinated motion while maintaining a desired geometric configuration, thereby enhancing their maneuverability. However, conventional leader-follower-based distributed AFC remains vulnerable to dynamic changes in the network topology, where UAVs may be temporarily unavailable due to maintenance in real-world applications. In this work, we propose a reliable maintenance policy that enables individual UAVs to detach from the swarm without compromising the formation stability. Our policy introduces an agent homogenization strategy that replaces the conventional leader UAVs with virtual leaders, thereby ensuring all operational UAVs are followers and thus eligible for maintenance. A relative affine localization (RAL) technique is employed, which allows the remaining UAVs to estimate the relative positions of missing neighbors by leveraging the formation geometry. Our proposed framework is validated through a series of experiments with a swarm of Crazyflie quadrotors in an indoor environment, which demonstrate the effectiveness of our policy that allows individual UAVs to be removed and returned in sequence while the rest of the swarm maintains its target configuration with high accuracy. Our proposed maintenance policy enables robust and long-duration deployments of UAV swarms in inaccessible and harsh environments.

title: A Classification and Prediction Method for DDoS Attacks Using Machine Learning
authors: D.Dhayalan, P.Jyotheeswari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: A fresh dissection of DDoS attacks has unveiled them as synchronized efforts to overwhelm systems and thereby to exclude authorized users from access. We studied detection methods by means of the recently revised UNWS, np, 15 datasets to take into account the current threat landscape. The system built in Python employs machine learning classifiers instead of traditional tools for the purpose of attack type identification. Confusion matrices illustrated the model's effectiveness when it was tested. Only two methods, namely, Random Forest and XGBoost, were tested; both were independently built and evaluated. The combination of accuracy and recall was 89% for the primary classification problem. Subsequently, XGBoost surpassed the competitor in a comparable test and obtained nearly 90% in both recall and precision. The results indicate that the DDoS trends appearing in the chosen dataset can be effectively recognized by ensemble methods.

title: Optimizing Face Detection in Low-Resolution Images under Blur Condition Using Lucy Richardson Algorithm
authors: S. Hemalatha, V. Sowmitha, P. Nithya, V. Dharunraj, K. Dhinesh, S. Jayasurya
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Aim: The purpose behind this project is to optimize face detection performance in low-resolution images with blur and increase the clarity of the image using the Lucy Richardson deconvolution algorithm. Materials and Methods: The experimental evaluation is conducted using two groups. Group 1 is a classical deblurring process that uses traditional methods to deblur the image using the CNN Algorithm. According to the experiment data, Group 1 results in achieving a face detection rate of about 60.9%, a precision rate of 61.9%, recall of 58.9% and an F1 score of 59.9%, which gives a4 low-resolution image, and it takes a large amount of time to deblur the image. Group 2 uses modern methods to restore the image quality, using a Lucy-Richardson algorithm with NLM, TV regularization, Haar-cascade and GFPGAN, which helps to provide a better optimized image. Result: Performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score were used to measure the performance of the existing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C N N$</tex>-based approach and the proposed Lucy-Richardson-based approach for image restoration in blur conditions. The accuracy of the existing approach was as low as 56.2% in high blur conditions, whereas the proposed approach achieved a maximum of 92.3% accuracy in low blur conditions and 85.2% accuracy in high blur conditions with a statistical significance value of 0.001 (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p<0.05$</tex>). Conclusion: The proposed system manages to obtain back the blurred images and maintain the stability against the Lucy-Richardson-based deconvolution is an efficient and reliable preprocessing solution that helps to improve the performance of face detection in the conditions of real-life surveillance and forensics.

title: An AI-Based Li-Fi Innovative Framework for Optical Security Optimization in Smart Cities
authors: M. A. Hadi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Recent developments in the area of wireless communication have resulted in the introduction of a new type of communication technology called Li-Fi (Light Fidelity). As a new wireless communication method, it has also created new challenges with respect to its security. For instance, the security of data transmitted via Li-Fi can be compromised through a variety of means that traditional wireless-based data security solutions are unable to address. Because of these reasons, we have developed a comprehensive conceptual framework for enhancing the security of Li-Fi, which we refer to as the Innovative Framework for Optical Security Enhancement (IfoSec). Ifo-Sec provides a comprehensive platform for addressing the security-related issues associated with Li-Fi. It provides a multi-layered, integrated security architecture including a physical layer (dynamic beamforming), an optical layer (light) and an application layer (encryption methods).
