The Ifo-Sec architecture provides an opportunity for the integration of dynamic beamforming with adaptive cryptographic methods and the application of machine learning-based anomaly detection to enhance the security of Li-Fi. The Ifo-Sec architecture addresses several critical areas related to Li-Fi security, such as the need for better integration of the physical and cryptographic layers; challenges associated with the mobility of Li-Fi; and the performance penalty of the traditional cryptographic methods. The empirical results demonstrate that Ifo-Sec offers a superior Eavesdropping Protection Rate (EPR) of 98.7%, with only a marginal Additional Data Throughput Burden (B_DTh) of 4.3%, which is better than those of all existing hybrid security architectures and cryptographic-only architectures. This research presents an opportunity to provide a foundation for developing secure and high-performance optical wireless networks as part of the development of next generation wireless networks.

title: Accelerating the Post-Quantum Transition: A GPU-Based Performance Comparison of ML-KEM and ML-DSA
authors: Sangeetha K, Shreya Khodanpur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: As National Institute for Standards and Technology (NIST) moves towards completing its Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) standards (FIPS 203 ML-KEM & FIPS 204 ML-DSA), it leaves the cloud security industry with a complex, yet unavoidable challenge. These new lattice-based algorithms are designed to combat attacks from quantum computers, but they do come with a price. The performance degradation will be significant as well as impacting the overall infrastructure.To understand the impact of NIST's new algorithms we performed a benchmarking study to see how each of them compare side-by-side over multiple NIST security levels with an emphasis on the resources they consume and time taken to perform each operation.As expected, we found that both algorithms have a large amount of overhead due to their computational processes (specifically using Number Theoretic Transform and rejection sampling) which, while not necessarily apparent to end-users, are a factor users should be aware of.One thing we did not do in our testing is to only test in an ideal laboratory environment rather we also created a hybrid test system consisting of an AMD Ryzen 9 7950X and an NVIDIA H100 (to closely resemble a real-world modern cloud setup). Although there was a lot of variance in the results, ML-KEM yielded the best overall effectiveness, particularly due to its lower resource utilization (compared to ML-DSA) and optimized for high-volume key exchange very well. The challenge is still figuring out how to implement and implement correctly; that will require further research in the future.

title: Composite Habitat: An Incremental Development Path from Propellant Tank to Crewed Habitat
authors: Matthew Ziglar, Michael Elsperman
abstract: This paper presents an evolutionary path for the development of a composite habitat aimed at enhancing space exploration capabilities. The proposed strategy adopts a phased approach campaign, beginning with simple structure and progressively advancing to more complex systems, culminating in a Mars surface habitat. Throughout this evolutionary path, the paper emphasizes the importance of incremental development, risk management, and technology integration. By breaking down the complex campaign into manageable components, teams can gather feedback and make necessary adjustments, thereby enhancing the quality and reliability of the final product. This iterative process not only fosters innovation but also allows for the seamless incorporation of emerging technologies, ensuring that the habitat benefits from the latest advancements. Moreover, the strategy underscores the significance of partnership and collaboration among various stakeholders, including government agencies (especially NASA and its Moon to Mars architecture), private companies, and research institutions. Such collaborations facilitate resource sharing and knowledge exchange, allowing each entity to leverage their strengths to enhance the overall campaign objectives. The initial phase focuses on the creation of a propellant tank, which serves as the foundational building block, leveraging lightweight composite materials that offer increased strength and stiffness with reduced mass compared to aerospace metals. This foundational phase is critical for establishing the Technology Readiness Level (TRL) and Manufacturing Readiness Level (MRL) necessary for subsequent developments. The second phase involves the design and construction of a composite logistics module, which builds upon the knowledge gained from the propellant tank. It incorporates additional functionalities such as internal and external logistics, docking capabilities, and crew accommodations that enable the logistics missions. The composite logistics module serves as a vital development step in preparing for more complex modules. The third phase introduces a crewed Mars fly-by mission, akin to Apollo 8 or Artemis II, which aims to validate critical technologies and systems required for long-duration space travel. This mission will test the composite habitat's ability to support crew life, including life support systems, logistics, and crew accommodations, while providing invaluable operational experience in deep space. The final phase culminates in the development of a longduration crewed surface habitat, primarily targeting Mars but also applicable to lunar missions. This habitat integrates advanced systems for sustainable living, including dust mitigation, airlocks, and resupply capabilities, ensuring the safety and well-being of astronauts in a partial gravity environment. A long-term vision is also crucial for guiding the incremental composite module development efforts, ensuring that each phase aligns with overarching goals and objectives. This strategic framework ties into NASA's Moon to Mars architecture and enables the team to develop each phase to add immediate value. Instead of point solution optimized to one phase, each module is designed with the campaign end state in mind. Ultimately, the evolutionary path of a composite habitat aims to establish a human presence on Mars while remaining applicable to lunar missions. By strategically using composite structures and systematically developing each phase, the campaign strategy lays a robust foundation for future space missions and sustainable habitation on other celestial bodies.

title: ECORE: Energy-Conscious Optimized Routing for Deep Learning Models at the Edge
authors: Daghash K. Alqahtani, Maria A. Rodriguez, Muhammad Aamir Cheema, Hamid Rezatofighi, Adel N. Toosi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Edge computing enables data processing closer to the source, significantly reducing latency, an essential requirement for real-time vision-based analytics such as object detection in surveillance and smart city environments. However, these tasks place substantial demands on resource-constrained edge devices, making the joint optimization of energy consumption and detection accuracy critical. To address this challenge, we propose ECORE , a framework that integrates multiple dynamic routing strategies, including a novel estimation-based techniques and an innovative greedy selection algorithm, to direct image processing requests to the most suitable edge device–model pair. ECORE dynamically balances energy efficiency and detection performance based on object characteristics. We evaluate our framework through extensive experiments on real-world datasets, comparing against widely used baseline techniques. The evaluation leverages established object detection models (YOLO, SSD, EfficientDet) and diverse edge platforms, including Jetson Orin Nano, Raspberry Pi 4 and 5, and TPU accelerators. Results demonstrate that our proposed context-aware routing strategies can reduce energy consumption and latency by 35% and 49%, respectively, while incurring only a 2% loss in detection accuracy compared to accuracy-centric methods.

title: iPCB-Guard: A Confidence-Calibrated, Edge-Optimized Inline PCB Inspection Framework with Cost-Aware Robotic Segregation
authors: Aman Reddy J, J. Aniketh Reddy, M. Chandralekha, Rahul Raj M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs) play a vital role in the manufacture of electronics and electrical systems and any undetected fault can result in tremendous losses in terms of scrap, rework and back-end failures. The current systems are either rule-based systems or reject-only systems, which are not flexible, there is a high rate of false positive outcomes and poor compatibility with real-time production operations. Moreover, the majority of the deep learning-based solutions rely on offline investigation, hence they do not support practical implementation in line. The paper suggests a deep learning-based inline PCB inspection and defect-sensitive robotic segregation workflow, which has been modeled and implemented at the software phase and real-time defect classification, as well as autonomous segregation of defective PCBs using a robotic arm. It employs a convolutional neural network, which is based on transfer learning and has a ResNet-18 backbone to provide effective PCB defect classification. The model is operated on an edge computing platform and is combined with a robotic arm that is used to sort defective PCBs based on the type of defect, enabling the separation of them (e.g., rework or reject). Operational reliability is enhanced with decision thresholding which is confidence-based. The findings represent the specific results of correct classification outputs, which have low inference latency, and attest the appropriateness of the proposed system as a scalable and efficient PCB quality control solution.

title: Physics-Data Driven Weak Point Identification for Voltage Stability in Receiving-End Power Grids
authors: Chaoming Zheng, Yangqing Dan, Zhouzhenyan Hong, Zhiheng Shen, Feifei Sun, Xiahui Zhang, Hongji Yang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Aiming at the issues of weak dynamic reactive power support and poor voltage stability in receiving-end power grids, this paper proposes a physics-data fusion-driven method for identifying weak points. First, an augmented Jacobian matrix considering the regulation characteristics of high-voltage direct current and dynamic reactive power sources is constructed to derive the generalized static voltage control sensitivity. Second, a dynamic clustering algorithm based on multi-objective modularity is employed to accurately partition the physical boundaries of the local grid. Finally, the extended voltage sensitivity factor is extracted as a physical prior feature and integrated into a hybrid neural network incorporating convolutional neural networks, long short-term memory and attention mechanism to capture the spatiotemporal features of generalized measurement data. Simulations on the IEEE 39-bus system demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior identification accuracy, generalization capability, and physical interpretability even with small sample sizes.

title: A Comparison Analysis of Traditional Versus AI-Driven Tourism Ecosystems
authors: Marwan Aljijakli, Nadine Akkari
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The tourism sector has gone through a paradigm shift from traditional to AI-based systems that use emerging technologies to transform customers' journeys and operating service models. Currently, the static distribution of information and the reactive delivery of services in traditional tourism ecosystems have progressively been replaced by digital platforms and intelligent systems that utilize machine learning with predictive analytics. Three key aspects of these ecosystems are critically compared in this paper, including operational performance, costs, customer experience, and sustainability. While AI-driven ecosystems are capable of scalability and realtime adaptability, hyper-personalization is crucial not just for data forecasting but also for environmental footprint. Yet the evolution poses some issues, such as data privacy and algorithmic biases that can lead to inefficient human experience. The comparative analysis recommends hybrid environments enabling artificial intelligence augmentation in combination with human monitoring to minimize ethical challenges and maximize digital advantages. Future trends are toward the convergence of smart tourism that addresses autonomous agents under responsible AI frameworks aiming for sustainability and ethical deployment of AI-driven solutions.

title: Attention Feature Fusion with Cluster Contrastive Learning for Snoring and Breath-Holding Detection Using Seismic Sensing
authors: Yingjian Song, Jiayu Chen, Zixuan Zeng, Yida Zhang, Zaid Farooq Pitafi, Bradley G. Phillips, Xiang Zhang, Fei Dou, Wenzhan Song
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Snoring and breath-stopping are key symptoms of sleep apnea. Most existing studies primarily focus on wearable devices or smartphone-based systems. Wearable devices can be uncomfortable, while smartphone-based systems often require specific angles, distances, or positions, making them sensitive to environmental changes. This paper proposes a contactless and engagement-free system for snoring and breath-stopping detection using a seismic sensor. Distinguishing between snoring, breath-stopping, and normal breathing from raw data alone is challenging. Snoring features typically reside in a higher frequency range than breath-stopping and normal breathing, with breath-stopping features appearing in a lower frequency range. Calculating the differential and integral of raw data can enhance features in low and high frequencies, respectively. We introduce AFFCL, an attention feature fusion and contrastive learning framework to leverage information from differential and integral signals. AFFCL generates both shared and exclusive features from the differential and integral signals and employs an attention mechanism for feature fusion. Additionally, cluster-level supervised contrastive learning in AFFCL further enhances system performance. Our system has been performed 5-fold cross-validation on 44 people, which achieves an average accuracy of 93.40% and an F1 score of 92.42%. The accuracy for detecting breath-stopping, snoring, and normal breathing are 89.54%, 94.60%, and 96.06%, respectively. Evaluation results demonstrate that our system effectively identifies breath-stopping and snoring.

title: Coordinated Planning of Grid-Forming and Grid-Following SVGs for Static and Dynamic Voltage Support
authors: Xu Jiang, Ding Wang, Xiaozhe Song, Qinhao Chen, Yun Liu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: High penetration of renewable energy significantly reduces system strength, posing challenges to various forms of stability issues. Static var generators (SVGs) are effective in overcoming these challenges by providing voltage support capabilities. While both Grid-Following (GFL) and Grid-Forming (GFM) SVGs can offer static voltage support to mitigate voltage violation and instability risks, the latter can further offer dynamic voltage support by enhancing small-signal stability, at the cost of a higher investment. To achieve an optimal trade-off between the voltage support capabilities and economic efficiency, this paper proposes a two-stage coordinated planning framework for GFM/GFL SVG configurations, which decouples the problem into two distinct phases. Stage I optimally places SVGs disregarding their control modes to secure the static voltage stability margin based on the minimum singular value of the power flow Jacobian matrix. Stage II optimally allocates GFL or GFM control modes to each device to maintain small-signal stability governed by the generalized short-circuit ratio (gSCR) constraints while optimizing the economic efficiency. A support function-based iterative algorithm is developed to address the eigenvalue-constrained planning model. Numerical simulations on the modified IEEE 39-bus system demonstrate that the proposed method effectively coordinates the technical advantages of GFM with the cost benefits of GFL. The results confirm a 17.1% reduction in investment costs compared to all-GFM scenarios while strictly satisfying system strength requirements.

title: Comparative Study on Dual Active Bridge Based Quasi-Two-Stage Partial Power Processing Topologies
authors: Wenjie Bi, Zhengmei Lu, Miao Zhu, Pengfeng Lin
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper proposes a family of quasi-two-stage DAB partial power transfer topologies suitable for wide-range voltage regulation scenarios. By splitting the intermediate DC bus, these topologies integrate the basic voltage regulation circuit with the DAB full-bridge/half-bridge structure, deriving two types of basic connection architectures and multiple sub-topologies. The paper systematically analyzes the operating modes, voltage and current stresses, partial power transfer characteristics, and voltage regulation range of each topology, and proposes topology selection criteria based on the typical characteristics of each converter in the topology family. These topologies achieve partial power transfer to reduce overall losses, while flexibly regulating the peak-to-peak value of the high-frequency voltage between DAB bridges, ensuring full operating condition zero-voltage switching (ZVS) under a wide voltage regulation range. Finally, a typical topology is selected to verify the effectiveness of the proposed topology and modulation strategy through a simulation model, and the efficiency advantage of the selected topology over the traditional two-stage converter in a wide voltage range is verified.

title: Research on the Safety Evaluation of the Power Industry Chain for New Electricity System under the New International Situation
authors: Wenjing Ruan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This research systematically examines the security of China's new-type electric power system industry chain amidst evolving geopolitical and economic landscapes. Through mapping the industry chain structure, developing a security evaluation index system, and proposing strategic measures to enhance stability, competitiveness, and coordination, the study provides valuable insights for policymakers, industries, and enterprises. Given the dynamic nature of the international situation, continuous refinement of security assessments is necessary to reflect changes in geopolitical tensions and global trade patterns. Future work should focus on further delineating the evolving panorama of the industry chain, incorporating specific enterprise characteristics into the security evaluation index system, and refining risk assessment precision. This research underscores the importance of adaptability and innovation in safeguarding national economic stability and achieving green development goals, ensuring that China's new- type electric power system industry chain remains resilient and competitive in a complex global environment.

title: HIL Simulation of Multi-Objective Power Quality Mitigation for High-Penetration Renewables and New Loads
authors: Xiaoming Wang, Wenguang Zhao, Bin Xu, Hao Zheng, Yadong Jiao, Mingxing Zhu, Fangfang Zhang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The complex grid integration characteristics of new "dual-high" sources and loads are expected to continue degrading medium- and high-voltage power quality in power systems. The chain Static Var Generator (SVG), capable of implementing multiple mitigation strategies, represents a critical solution to this challenge. To address potential applicability issues arising from limitations in strategy development and validation for medium- to high-voltage applications, this paper constructs a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulation platform for simulating various power quality disturbances and verifying their mitigation. First, the relationship between the grid-connected dynamic characteristics of typical new-type source-load systems and power quality problems is established, clarifying the control requirements and strategic principles for chain SVG. Second, based on the need for controller-in-the-loop verification, the framework of the HIL simulation platform, the aggregated model equivalence method for multi-new-type source-load cluster scenarios, and the operational state setting requirements are outlined. Finally, using an actual controller as an example, targeted validation of multiple control strategies is performed in a renewable energy transmission scenario. The results demonstrate that the constructed HIL platform can comprehensively capture the dynamic response characteristics of various power quality control strategies, providing strong support for full-link verification of complex chain SVG control schemes.

title: Scenario Generation of Wind-Solar Output Under Multiple Weather Types Based on an Improved Generative Adversarial Network
authors: Tao Wang, Zhongdan Zhang, Min Xu, Lei Ni, Jun Zhang, Wangwang Bai, Xiang Wang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address the key problem of low accuracy in generating scenic power scenes under multiple types of weather, this paper proposes an improved generative adversarial network (GAN) scenic power scene generation method. Firstly, this paper describes the basic principle of GAN. Subsequently, a conditional Wasserstein generative adversarial network with gradient penalty (cWGAN-GP) is further developed, which includes the introduction of conditional batch normalization (CBN) to strengthen the control effect of the conditions, and the construction of internal and external condition vectors. Finally, using real data to verify the effectiveness of the proposed method in generating scenarios of wind and light output under multiple weather types. The results show improved cWGAN-GP case generation method has an ability of accurately capturing the statistical properties of the outputs under multiple weather types in terms of probability distribution and autocorrelation, and provide reliable support for dispatch planning.

title: A Hybrid Carrier-Based PWM With Optimized Output Quality and Reduced Common-Mode Voltage for Coupled Ten-Switch Three-Phase Three-Level Inverter
authors: Xiaojun Deng, Xiaonan Zhu, Zongheng Wu, Yang Chen, Hongliang Wang, Xi Gui, Mingyang Han, Botao Linghu, Yun Yang, Xiumei Yue
abstract: Coupled ten-switch three-phase three-level inverter (TP-TLI) has attracted significant attention in industry due to reduced switch count and compact structure. However, existing carrier-based pulse-width modulation (CBPWM) strategies require at least one phase to operate under two-level (2L) at any modulation index. This article presents a hybrid CBPWM (HCBPWM) to improve the performance. The duty ratio relationships of high level for the three phases are analyzed to reveal the formation mechanism of undesired switching states, which are prevented by hybrid-level modulation (HLM) based on volt-second balance. In HCBPWM, the maximum and minimum phases are modulated under three-level (3L), while the middle phase is modulated using HLM, including HL, 3L, or 2L. This method improves output voltage and current quality, naturally reduces the common-mode voltage (CMV) by 33.3% without additional effort, and maintains the same DC-link voltage utilization as traditional CBPWM. Moreover, a zero-sequence voltage (ZSV) injection method, considering the neutral-point (NP) currents and NP voltage (NPV) deviation, is presented to balance the NPV under various modulation indexes and power factors. Experiments on a 3 kVA prototype verify the feasibility of the proposed HCBPWM, showing up to 53.64% and 74.44% reduction in total harmonic distortions (THDs) for phase voltage and phase current, respectively.

title: MGRT-Net: Multi-Gain Radiometric Response Transfer Network for On-Orbit Relative Radiometric Calibration of Micro Nighttime Remote Sensing Satellites
authors: Li T. Li, Jia Y. Cao, Yong H. Jiang, Cheng J. Wang, Xin Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Inconsistent radiometric response characteristics of nighttime sensor detectors can interfere with weak nighttime surface radiation, resulting in radiometric inconsistencies in captured images. This degrades image quality and compromises information extraction from such image products. Relative radiometric calibration involves calibrating response differences among imaging detectors to eliminate radiometric inconsistencies, which is particularly critical for nighttime sensors. However, existing on-orbit calibration methods require onboard calibration devices, reliable attitude control, and dedicated calibration imaging tasks. The limited hardware capabilities and attitude control capacity of micro nighttime satellites pose significant challenges for radiometric calibration. This study proposes a deep learning–based on-orbit relative radiometric calibration method for nighttime sensors to address these limitations, representing an effort to apply deep learning techniques to relative radiometric calibration of remote sensing satellites. A Multi-Gain Radiometric Response Transfer Network (MGRT-Net) was developed to characterize the radiometric response relationships of nighttime sensor across gain settings. A daytime low-gain radiometric calibration reference was established using a uniform calibration method. The calibration reference is transferred to nighttime images to calibrate the radiometric parameters of the nighttime sensor in high-gain mode. Using Luojia1-01 nighttime images, MGRT-Net accurately simulated radiometric responses across different gain settings and effectively transferred daytime low-gain radiometric calibration references to nighttime images. After MGRT-Net–based transfer correction, radiometric response errors among sensor detectors in nighttime high-gain images were effectively eliminated. Under low- and high- brightness, the streaking metrics exceeded 0.027% (76.5% improvement) and 0.028% (75.6% improvement), respectively, outperforming existing methods. Compared with analytical calibration methods, the proposed method requires no prior model or additional satellite requirements and demonstrates strong adaptability. This study provides a reference for micro-remote sensing satellites constrained by manufacturing costs and validates the applicability of deep learning methods for relative radiometric calibration.

title: Bearing-Only Tracking of Underwater Multiple Acoustic Sources Using Underwater Vehicles Under Latency and Packet Loss
authors: Tianming Gao, Paolo Di Lillo, Gianluca Antonelli, Tianyi Guo, Alessandro Marino, Jing Yan, Xinping Guan
abstract: Bearing-only tracking (BOT) of underwater acoustic sources is crucial for numerous applications in the underwater domain. A promising approach involves reconfigurable underwater wireless sensor networks (UWSNs) composed of a group of underwater vehicles that enable target state estimation via data fusion while enhancing observability. However, the uncertainty of measurement-target association and unstable acoustic communications pose significant challenges. This article presents a selective association probabilistic multi-hypothesis tracking (SA-PMHT) solution tailored for BOT of underwater targets under latency and packet loss. Specifically, a selective association mechanism is designed to determine measurement-target associations by synthesizing candidate measurements for each target. Then, a pseudo-linear equation is constructed to establish regression matrices for targets, and a sliding-window least-squares estimator is embedded for iterative state estimation. We demonstrate that the SA-PMHT tracking framework is a pseudo-posterior variant of the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm. Meanwhile, theoretical analyses are provided for its computational complexity and sufficient conditions for target observability. Finally, both simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness of our solution.

title: Bearing-Only Tracking of Underwater Multiple Acoustic Sources Using Underwater Vehicles Under Latency and Packet Loss_supp1-3684218.mp4
authors: Tianming Gao
abstract: Bearing-only tracking (BOT) of underwater acoustic sources is crucial for numerous applications in the underwater domain. A promising approach involves reconfigurable underwater wireless sensor networks (UWSNs) composed of a group of underwater vehicles that enable target state estimation via data fusion while enhancing observability. However, the uncertainty of measurement-target association and unstable acoustic communications pose significant challenges. This article presents a selective association probabilistic multi-hypothesis tracking (SA-PMHT) solution tailored for BOT of underwater targets under latency and packet loss. Specifically, a selective association mechanism is designed to determine measurement-target associations by synthesizing candidate measurements for each target. Then, a pseudo-linear equation is constructed to establish regression matrices for targets, and a sliding-window least-squares estimator is embedded for iterative state estimation. We demonstrate that the SA-PMHT tracking framework is a pseudo-posterior variant of the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm. Meanwhile, theoretical analyses are provided for its computational complexity and sufficient conditions for target observability. Finally, both simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness of our solution.

title: FOF-X: Towards Real-time Detailed Human Reconstruction from a Single Image
authors: Qiao Feng, Yuanwang Yang, Yebin Liu, Yu-Kun Lai, Jingyu Yang, Kun Li
abstract: We introduce FOF-X for real-time reconstruction of detailed human geometry from a single image. Balancing real-time speed against high-quality results is a persistent challenge, mainly due to the high computational demands of existing 3D representations. To address this, we propose Fourier Occupancy Field (FOF), an efficient 3D representation by learning the Fourier series. The core of FOF is to factorize a 3D occupancy field into a 2D vector field, retaining topology and spatial relationships within the 3D domain while facilitating compatibility with 2D convolutional neural networks. Such a representation bridges the gap between 3D and 2D domains, enabling the integration of human parametric models as priors and enhancing the reconstruction robustness. Based on FOF, we design a new reconstruction framework, FOF-X, to avoid the performance degradation caused by texture and lighting. This enables our real-time reconstruction system to better handle the domain gap between training images and real images. Additionally, in FOF-X, we enhance the inter-conversion algorithms between FOF and mesh representations with a Laplacian constraint and an automaton-based discontinuity matcher, improving both quality and robustness. We validate the strengths of our approach on different datasets and real-captured data, where FOF-X achieves new state-of-the-art results.

title: Indistinguishability Analysis of JPEG Image Encryption Schemes
authors: Yuan Yuan, Lingfeng Qu, Ji Zhang, Ningxiong Mao, Hadi Amirpour
abstract: JPEG images are widely used for communication and storage, making secure encryption essential for privacy protection. Existing JPEG encryption studies primarily rely on empirical metrics such as visual distortion, key space, or correlation, while overlooking the formal indistinguishability against chosen-plaintext attacks (IND-CPA) property. This work provides the first systematic analysis of existing JPEG encryption schemes from the IND-CPA perspective. A new metric, termed feature change rate, is introduced to quantify the preservation of residual features. Both theoretical and experimental results demonstrate that preserving residual features compromises IND-CPA. Furthermore, the relationship between feature change rate and key estimation success under CPA is established, indicating that smaller feature changes result in higher attack accuracy. Based on these findings, we propose a set of design principles for constructing secure and practical JPEG encryption schemes. Finally, we outline a feature-changing encryption strategy that enhances IND-CPA security while maintaining JPEG compatibility and compression efficiency.

title: A Streamlined Fault Detection and Selectivity Method based on a Normalized Current Difference Criterion and Apparent Impedance for Onshore Wind Farm Collector Grids
authors: Raphael T. Sakai, Silvio G. Di Santo, Nelson Kagan, Celestino P. T. Kalueyo, Carlos F. M. Almeida
abstract: The large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) has substantially altered the magnitude, phase-angle behavior, and transient evolution of fault currents in modern power systems. These changes pose significant challenges to conventional protection schemes in wind farm collector grids. In such systems, the limited short-circuit current contribution from converter-interfaced wind turbines, together with variations in apparent impedance, can compromise relay performance. In this context, this paper proposes a streamlined two-stage feeder protection approach for onshore wind farm collector grids. In the first stage, faults are detected using a normalized current-difference criterion computed from pre-fault and fault current samples. In the second stage, internal and external faults are discriminated using the apparent positive-sequence fault impedance evaluated in the R-X plane. The method was assessed through 24,120 PSCAD-simulated fault scenarios based on a realistic wind farm model. All simulated fault cases were successfully detected, and all analyzed cases were correctly classified as internal or external under the studied conditions.Additional validation using nine real fault records confirmed the practical feasibility of the proposed approach and showed faster fault detection than the installed feeder relays in the analyzed events. Overall, the results indicate that the method enables fast, reliable protection decisions in wind-farm collector grids while maintaining low computational complexity.

title: FSA-DFNet: A Diffusion-Enhanced Soft Sensor Prediction Model for BTP with Hybrid Frequency-Spatial Attention Mechanism
authors: Yuhan Gong, Chonghao Wang, Xin Yu, Ruikun Gong, Mohamad Tarmizi Abu Seman
abstract: Sintering, as a critical process in iron and steelmaking, has a direct impact on the quality of sintered ore as well as the output and quality of blast furnace ironmaking. The burn-through point (BTP) is a key state parameter for assessing sinter quality, yet it remains difficult to accurately predict in actual production. Given the characteristics of the sintering process—such as multivariable dynamics, large time delays, nonlinearity, and strong coupling—this paper develops an intelligent BTP prediction model based on a hybrid attention FNet architecture.Its diffusion-enhanced preprocessing module can simultaneously collect various sensor time-series data (such as temperature, pressure, etc.) from the windbox, as well as process parameters like ignition temperature and bed thickness.By utilizing an iterative denoising mechanism, it mitigates noise in the windbox data and accomplishes predictive modeling and reconstruction of missing or anomalous sensor data. By integrating an FNet feature fusion network into a hybrid frequency-domain and spatial-domain attention module, the proposed model reduces computational complexity and achieves a 30% improvement in predictive performance over CNN-LSTM models. With an overall data fitting degree of 0.948, the model effectively addresses the scarcity of key abnormal operating condition samples in industrial scenarios and significantly enhances robustness and generalization capability under extreme operating conditions.

title: NeuroSymX: A Transparent Neuro-Symbolic AI Framework for Causality-Aware and Human-Interpretable Decision Systems
authors: M. Ilampari, M.N. Faruk, Rahul Berry, Ankita Verma, Jarabala Ranga, Yogesh Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The growing demand for trustworthy artificial intelligence (AI) has accelerated research into models that are not only accurate but also interpretable and aligned with causal reasoning. In this regard, we present NeuroSymX, a hybrid neuro-symbolic AI system that combines deep learning and symbolic reasoning to derive transparent, causally consistent and human interpretable decisions. In contrast to classic black box neural networks that are difficult to justify make predictions, or adhere to domain specific rules, NeuroSymX entails the combination of Transformer-LSTM-based neural networks and Prolog-based symbolic logic engines. Causal graphs and ontology-consistent rules are also implemented into the framework based on external ontologies like ConceptNet. Extensive testing done on ten benchmark datasets such as CLEVR, UCI, eICU, and COMPAS show that NeuroSymX has an accuracy of between 85.4-94.9, with fidelity, and causal consistency scores always outperforming both explainable neural and symbolic-only models. NeuroSymX also scored the best in terms of user trust in comparative analysis with an average of more than 4.5 out of 5. Ablation studies also confirmed the relevance of each component and the complete model performed better in every case. Notably, the model allows counterfactual and abductive reasoning, allowing end-users to query the decisions using the question- why and the question- what-if. This makes NeuroSymX an effective solution to sensitive areas like healthcare, legal compliance and finance. The findings confirm the idea that the synergy between symbolic logic and sub-symbolic learning can be used to bridge the gap between AI-based accuracy and transparency, which creates the opportunity to implement explainable and ethical AI at scale.

title: Automated Consistency Checks of Contract Data Requirement Lists and Model-Based Systems Engineering Models Using Natural Language Processing
authors: Matthew Giorgio, Jeremy Marshall, John Roberts, Aleksandra Khusid, Jyotirmay Gadewadikar
abstract: Maintaining consistency between model-based systems engineering (MBSE) models and their corresponding Contract Data Requirement List (CDRL) deliverables is crucial in largescale systems engineering projects. This is especially important when CDRLs are not automatically generated fully and directly from the MBSE tools. Traditionally, consistency check task are manual, labor-intensive, and prone to errors, as it involves line-by-line reviewing of documents against their associated models, which are usually in vastly different formats. This paper introduces an automated tool leveraging machine learning techniques to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of consistency checks between CDRLs and Cameo diagrams. By utilizing Python and its libraries, the tool parses the Cameo models and corresponding CDRLs, employing sequence matching to identify and evaluate document-model mappings of requirements. In addition to this evaluation, the tool also identifies missing information between the CDRL and the models through low matching scores. The implementation of this tool significantly reduces the time required for consistency checks, as demonstrated by a quantitative time study showing a reduction from 22.5 minutes to just 4 seconds for 162 comparisons, with 100 % accuracy. Contractors can often deliver large increments of CDRLs near to their due dates, leaving the Government little time to perform effective reviews without the assistance of such a tool. The tool's limited dependencies facilitate broader applicability across different projects. This research not only addresses existing gaps in automated consistency checks but also provides a foundation for future advancements in the field. The findings underscore the potential for substantial time savings and improved accuracy, while highlighting the necessity of human oversight in handling sensitive information. Future research may further refine and automate these processes, enhancing their utility across diverse programs.

title: Research on Typical Wind and Solar Power Output Scenarios Generation Method Based on Quadratic Clustering Algorithm
authors: Yuchen Zhang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper describes a method for generating typical scenarios through quadratic clustering combining density-based spatial clustering of application with noise (DBSCAN) and the K-means algorithm. This method uses DBSCAN to stratify the daily net load duration curve, then performs dimensionality reduction on the high-dimensional time series data, and combines the K-means algorithm to implement final clustering within each layer, generating representative typical scenarios sets. We evaluated the performance of the proposed method in simulating high-load and extreme scenarios via a case study. This research provides a reliable scenario construction approach for power systems with renewable energy.

title: Using AI Predictive Analytics Model to Enhance the Safety of People with Special Needs in Sports Crowds
authors: Hassanin M. Al-Barhamtoshy
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Ensuring the safety of individuals with special needs-such as those with physical disabilities, sensory impairments, or cognitive disorders-in crowded sports environments presents a significant challenge. This paper introduces an AI-based predictive analytics framework aimed at improving safety for at-risk attendees by anticipating crowd behaviors, pinpointing potential hazards, and facilitating proactive measures. The goal is to introduce an all-in-one software solution for improving the safety for persons with special needs in crowded sports sites. Built on top of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML), this system analyses real-time data inputs - density, noise and speed of movement - to predict potential safety risks. The main goal is to create an automated decision-support system for early warning and access to a precise set of safety guidelines and procedures in sports stadia. Therefore, dataset building and generating based on standard AI simulation criteria. As real-time sensor information was not abundant as required, a solid synthetic dataset was created to mimic real-world applications. This dataset emulates the inputs commonly mined from IoT sensors, stadium cameras, and environmental monitors. Accordingly, utilizing realtime data sourced from surveillance systems, wearable devices, and past incident records, the model employs machine learning techniques to forecast possible dangers (like overcrowding, falls, or medical emergencies) and deliver tailored safety notifications. Testing through simulated crowd situations has shown a 100% accuracy rate in risk assessment, allowing venue managers to allocate resources effectively (such as repositioning staff or establishing evacuation routes). This method addresses a crucial need in inclusive crowd management, enhancing safety for all participants.

title: Unbalance Compensation for Autonomous AC Microgrid
authors: Daming Zhang, Xiaoyan Zhu, John Fletcher
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Unbalance situation usually occurs in a three-phase four-wire power system due to the unbalanced loads across its different phases. In this case, the unbalance compensation is necessary for the stable operation of power generation units. In order to restore balance, in this paper, a functional and flexible unbalance compensation system has been used in the AC microgrid which operates at constant frequency and contains one grid-forming generator (inverter) and three grid-supporting inverters. The unbalance compensation works with energy fed back to the grid in order to reduce energy waste. For this purpose, three separate rectifiers each of which is followed by a medium-frequency transformer isolated DC/DC converter are controlled to transfer different levels of power. The outputs of the three DC/DC converters are joined together to be the input for the subsequent DC/AC inverter which can output balanced power back to the grid. In order to increase the transient stability, a LCCL filter has been used for each rectifier.

title: State Space Quantification in Reinforcement Learning
authors: Rodney Sanchez, Paul T. Schrader, Jamison Heard
abstract: Neural Network architecture design is currently understood as an art, where the network's hyperparameters are primarily formulated through a synthesis of scientific intuition and investigative methodologies. This process is further exacerbated in reinforcement learning (RL), where informative representations (e.g., t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding or t-SNE plots) are often unavailable to validate the efficiency of an agent's feature extractor. In the context of RL, this amplifies the misunderstanding of the provided algorithm's performance as either a byproduct of learning more descriptive “states” or policy improvements. As a potential solution, we introduce a metric to robustly quantify the agent's internal states by observing the rate of change in the Modern Hopfield Pooling (MHP) associative space. We validate this method's ability to comprehend environmental complexity by testing it on increasingly challenging environments, starting with CartPole and progressing to Breakout. Beyond environment complexity, the method is tested in a variety of convolutional architectures to understand how feature extractors adjust the agent's internal state. Specifically, we test the depth of convolution, filter size, and the inclusion of pooling layers to encapsulate how the agent's internal representation changes. Our results demonstrate that MHP can explain how the complexity of environmental features and the architecture of feature extractors influence the agent's internal state. Overall, the MHP method provides an informative quantitative measure of how an environmental feature or feature extractor forms the agent's internal representation, particularly under complex conditions such as temporal difference learning, or outside the constraints of independent and identically distributed state space representations.

title: Wafer-scale GPU Memory Pool with In-Package Optics for Enhanced Capacity and Bandwidth
authors: Khakim Akhunov, Aakash Patel, Robert Kleijnen, Julien Ryckaert, James Myers, Dwaipayan Biswas
abstract: The escalating demands of modern AI applications push the limits of conventional GPU memory systems, where high-bandwidth memory (HBM) architectures face critical challenges in scalability and bandwidth efficiency. We propose a wafer-scale system integrating photonic interconnects to decouple GPU compute from memory, addressing capacity and bandwidth limitations of conventional HBM-based architectures. We develop an analytical framework, HBM-pool, blending in-package optical I/O (OIO) parameters with GPU memory traffic for high-density nodes. The system-technology co-optimization (STCO) study shows that 300 mm wafer-scale disaggregation with OIO can achieve a 4× increase in memory capacity and reduce bandwidth bottlenecks up to 8× compared to a 2.5D electrically packaged node, enabling larger training/inference and improving latency.

title: Large Signal Stability and Parametric Analysis of Hybrid System with Grid-Following Converter and Grid-Forming Converter
authors: Lu Chen, Boyuan Zhao, Peng Su, Lei Chen, Hu Fan, Jiaheng Zheng
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper theoretically investigates the large signal synchronization stability of the hybrid system with grid-following and grid-forming converter with droop controller. Firstly, a simplified second-order model is established, and then the expression of the equilibrium points is obtained, and the region of attraction (ROA) for large-signal stability is analytically determined via the invariant manifold approach. Parametric analysis reveals that: (1) increasing GFL-PLL proportional gain or GFM droop coefficient, or decreasing GFL-PLL integral gain, expands the stability domain; (2) the GFM droop coefficient exerts the dominant influence when controller parameters vary simultaneously; and (3) rising line impedance degrades large signal stability, with the GFL-side impedance having a more pronounced effect. The proposed framework provides theoretical insights into stability boundaries for hybrid converter systems. The electromagnetic transient simulations have verified the theoretical analysis.

title: LFO Modal Identification and PSS Parameter Tuning Based on CEEMD-Prony Method
authors: Zhouyue Hu, Shuyi Li, Zhaoru Han
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: In scenarios involving long-distance and heavy-load power transmission, power systems are susceptible to small disturbances. These disturbances can induce relative rotor swings among generators, thereby leading to low-frequency oscillations (LFO), which threaten the grid's security and stability. To tackle the difficulties related to the identification of non-stationary and noise-corrupted LFO signals, this paper proposes a combined CEEMD-Prony method for oscillation analysis and controller tuning. First, Complementary Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition (CEEMD) is applied to denoise and reconstruct the signals, effectively extracting the principal components of the oscillation modes. Next, the Prony method is utilized to calculate key modal metrics, including frequency and damping ratio, acting as the foundation for constructing an equivalent system model. Based on this model, the parameters of the Power System Stabilizer (PSS) are tuned utilizing the principles of pole placement and phase compensation. Simulation results obtained from Power System Analysis Software Package (PSASP) demonstrate that the proposed method can stably extract dominant parameters even in noisy environments, thereby effectively guiding PSS design to enhance system damping and mitigate LFO.

title: SLAP: Slapband-Based Autonomous Perching Drone with Failure Recovery for Vertical Tree Trunks
authors: Julia Di, Kenneth A. W. Hoffmann, Tony G. Chen, Tian-Ao Ren, Mark Cutkosky
abstract: Perching allows unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) to reduce energy consumption, remain anchored for surface sampling operations, or stably survey their surroundings. Previous efforts for perching on vertical surfaces have predominantly focused on lightweight mechanical design solutions with relatively scant system-level integration. Furthermore, perching strategies for vertical surfaces commonly require high-speed, aggressive landing operations that are dangerous for a surveyor drone with sensitive electronics onboard. This work presents the preliminary investigation of a perching approach suitable for larger drones that both gently perches on vertical tree trunks and reacts and recovers from perch failures. The system in this work, called SLAP, consists of vision-based perch site detector, an IMU (inertial-measurement-unit)-based perch failure detector, an attitude controller for soft perching, an optical close-range detection system, and a fast active elastic gripper with microspines made from commercially-available slapbands. We validated this approach on a modified 1.2 kg commercial quadrotor with component and system analysis. Initial human-in-the-loop autonomous indoor flight experiments achieved a 75% perch success rate on a real oak tree segment across 20 flights, and 100 % perch failure recovery across 2 flights with induced failures. Videos and code of flight tests can be found at our project website: https://slap-perching.github.io/.

title: Design and Implementation of the NASA Psyche Discovery Mission Science Data Center
authors: Ernest Cisneros, James F. Bell, Lauren Auchterlonie, Madeline Marquardt, Michael Walworth, Nathan A. Cluff, Raymond C. Espiritu, Jodie Ream, Daniel D. Wenkert
abstract: The NASA Psyche Discovery Mission is currently cruising towards a rendezvous with the asteroid (16) Psyche, the largest M-class asteroid in the main asteroid belt [1]. The spacecraft instrument suite consists of a magnetometer, gamma ray and neutron spectrometers, multispectral imagers, and radio science experiments, all designed to unravel the history of (16) Psyche [2], [3]. The resulting data products generated by the mission (e.g., images; spectroscopic, magnetic, and gravity field data; shape models; geologic maps, etc.) will be delivered to the Small Bodies Node of the NASA Planetary Data System (PDS) for long-term archiving [4]. The Psyche mission's Science Data Center (SDC), part of the JPL Psyche Mission System, is the point of contact for all data sharing and archiving activities. Here we describe the design and implementation of the SDC, which was guided by many factors: supporting mission requirements related to data warehousing; performing data dissemination and archiving; adhering to federal, NASA, and ASU cybersecurity guidelines; and following industry best practices. Given the long baseline of the mission (launch in October 2023 and arrival at the asteroid in mid-2029), the system needs to be easily maintainable and upgradable during the mission's lifetime. The SDC is located on the Tempe campus of ASU, with connections to the mission's Ground Data System at JPL and is available to the Psyche team via a web portal utilizing purpose-built tools. The SDC leverages heritage tools, concepts, and lessons learned from previous ASU instrument operations, such as for the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Cameras on LRO, the Mastcam cameras on the Mars Science Laboratory rover, and the Mastcam-Z cameras on the Mars 2020 rover. We describe the heritage, principles, and requirements that guided the design and initial development of the Psyche Science Data Center, including real-world examples of the SDCs data portal, RESTful interface, in-house scripts, and early data products. The design of the SDC is centered around a relational database, with a schema to model the many files that are ingested and tracked, as well as their relationships to the PDS bundles being aggregated and delivered. The SDC disseminates data products to the Psyche Team for science investigations through a web-based portal, which also includes a RESTful interface that allows team members to upload, download and search data using in-house scripts. The three instrument teams make heavy use of the RESTful interface for uploading their PDS products. The web portal also makes mosaics and individual image products available to the team using Web Mapping Service technology. Most of the tools developed for the SDC are written in Python, using virtual environments to minimize the need to configure and maintain Python at the system level. These adhere to the UNIX philosophy of software development: make each program do one thing well, expect output (when generated) to become input to another tool, and test early and refactor as needed.

title: A Novel Sheath-Core Detection System for Wire-Stripping of Power Distribution Live-line Operation Robots
authors: Yuepeng Wang, Yifei Meng, Panpan Zhao, Weichen Cui, Bingchao Du, Zeyang Yu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: [Background] Circular wire-stripping is a common method to remove sheath layers by power distribution live-line operation robots (PDLOR) at 10 kV or higher voltages. During wire-stripping, the cutter of the PDLOR has to be controlled accurately to ensure the metal conductor or core ("core") remain un-damaged while the sheath layers ("sheath" or "insulation") are completely removed. [Methods] Due to difference in optical properties for different layers, the reflected light from the cable stripping process received by infrared sensors can be used to differentiate sheath layers vs metal core and control the stripping process. In the tested PDLOR, a matrix of fiber optic sensors, each of which is capable of both transmitting infrared detection light and receiving the reflected light signals, is installed alongside the cutter. Compared with the conventional scheme of using discrete infrared emitters and receivers as skin-core detection sensors in live-line operation robots, matrix fiber optic sensors significantly improves detection sensitivity at the hardware level. Meanwhile, its strong resistance to electromagnetic interference further enhances the reliability of the detection device. During the wire stripping process, the reflected light intensity signals, in the form of sampled ADC values, are collected and processed in real time. A special method has been established such that the ADC values can be analyzed and used to adjust the core detection threshold in real time to control the stripping process to accommodate (self-adjust) complex PDLOR field operation in different scenarios. [Results] The wire stripping device based on the matrix fiber optic sensors combined with the corresponding control logic can achieve efficient and reliable sheath-core differentiation. [Conclusion] The novel detection system solves a number of practical application problems with traditional infrared sensor, and provides a sheath-core detection solution with high sensitivity, high accuracy, and also with great resistance to electromagnetic interference, which greatly improves the wire stripping quality of PDLOR systems.

title: GMEAC Framework for Condition Assessment and Risk Early Warning of Substation RP & IAS Equipment
authors: Ke Shi, Jing Niu, Weixiang Qiao, Jiwei Jin, Yi Qi
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: At present, the maintenance mode and equipment condition assessment methods of substation Relay Protection and Integrated Automation System (RP & IAS) have defects such as lagging identification of hidden defects, insufficient accuracy of risk early warning, and weak decision support capability, which are difficult to meet the engineering requirements of predictive maintenance of equipment. To address the above problems, this paper proposes a Generative Multi-Expert Agent Collaborative (GMEAC) decision-making framework. It deeply integrates the distributed collaborative architecture of multi-agent systems with the reasoning and reflection capabilities of generative agents, and constructs a collaborative decision-making system including 5 heterogeneous domain expert agents for protection logic, hardware health, communication performance, secondary circuit, and external correlation, as well as a top-level generative agent. The framework simulates the human expert consultation process to realize full-dimensional condition assessment and advanced risk early warning of RP & IAS equipment. Comparative experimental results based on simulation and measured datasets show that the proposed method achieves an average condition assessment accuracy of 95.85% and an average risk warning lead time of 42.62 h under 5 typical equipment degradation scenarios. All performance indicators are significantly better than the three comparison models, which can provide effective technical support for intelligent operation and maintenance and risk prevention and control of substation secondary equipment.

title: Analysis of Cost-Effective Lunar Positioning Constellation with Heterogeneous Satellites
authors: Sae Ogoshi, Yosuke Kawabata, Kenshiro Oguri, Satoshi Ikari, Ryu Funase, Shinichi Nakasuka
abstract: This study proposes a hybrid navigation system that may achieve cost reduction while maintaining positioning accuracy for lunar navigation constellations. Currently proposed Lunar Navigation Satellite System (LNSS) concepts require all satellites to carry expensive GNSS receivers with large high-gain antennas. Our approach strategically divides the constellation into large satellites with GNSS capability and small satellites using only satellite-to-satellite tracking (SST). Through simulations evaluating GNSS+SST performance with 60 -second and 1 -second measurement intervals, we analyzed all 254 possible configurations with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1-7$</tex> large satellites out of 8 total. Results suggest that configurations with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1-2$</tex> large satellites can meet the 50 m position accuracy requirement under the assumed conditions, while configurations with 3 or more large satellites show performance variation depending on satellite placement. Additional evaluation of optical navigation revealed performance degradation compared to the GNSS+SST baseline under current implementation. Economic analysis using the USCM8 cost model indicates potential reductions of up to 61 % for initial deployment and 27 % for 10 -year operations compared to an all-GNSS baseline, though these estimates do not account for integration complexity or operational overhead. This analysis assumes ideal clock behavior, employs simplified simulation models, and relies on single runs per configuration rather than Monte Carlo statistics. These preliminary findings suggest that hybrid architectures may warrant further investigation as a pathway toward economically sustainable lunar positioning infrastructure.

title: Comparative Statistical Lifetime Modeling of Red AlGaInP LEDs Under Multi-Stress Accelerated Life Testing: Lognormal Versus Weibull Analysis
authors: Ahowd S. Bahashwan, Mohammed Abdulmajid, Omar Kittaneh
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative lifetime modeling study of red AlGaInP LEDs under multi-stress accelerated life testing (ALT) with Type-I right-censored data. Weibull and Log-normal distributions were fitted using maximum likelihood estimation and evaluated via Cramér-von Mises, Anderson-Darling, and Jarque-Bera tests.Results indicate that the Lognormal distribution provides more consistent statistical performance across stress conditions. Based on this selection, Intel and Peck life-stress formulations were compared, where the Intel-Lognormal model demonstrated improved predictive accuracy. The study emphasizes the importance of rigorous distribution and acceleration model validation in ALT-based LED reliability analysis. The findings support sustainability-aware reliability modeling for long-life solid-state lighting systems.

title: Techno-Economic Analysis With Environmental Performance Considerations For Fluorescent-To-LED Lighting Retrofits
authors: Sultan Sh. Alanzi
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Lighting systems represent a significant and persistent component of electrical demand in buildings, making them a practical target for energy efficiency improvements. This paper presents a comprehensive techno-economic assessment framework for fluorescent-to-LED lighting retrofits, developed to support evaluation across different buildings and market conditions. The proposed framework quantifies connected load reduction, annual energy savings, and key economic performance indicators, including simple and discounted payback periods. Scenario-based assumptions are incorporated to account for uncertainty in cost parameters and component lifetimes to enable scalable and transferable analysis. The framework is applied to a representative building as a case study, where conservative, moderate, and optimistic scenarios are evaluated. The results demonstrate that LED retrofitting achieves substantial reductions in electrical demand and annual energy consumption, leading to significant operating cost savings and short payback periods across all examined scenarios. In addition to the economic evaluation, the framework incorporates environmental performance indicators to facilitate future carbon impact assessment when emission data are available.

title: Exact Modal Decomposition of Axial Currents in Bulk and Hollow Conducting Cylinders
authors: N. Isernia
abstract: The modal decomposition of eddy current problems is commonly treated numerically via integral formulations or via the imposition of arbitrary homogeneous boundary conditions at some distance from the sources in case of differential formulations. In this paper the eigenvalues and eigenvectors for axial currents in an infinitely long conducting cylinder surrounded by vacuum are computed analytically, rigorously accounting for the solution of the outer magnetostatic problem. The analytically derived eigenvalues and eigenvectors are compared against the results of a specifically developed numerical model based on an integral formulation. The two approaches mutually confirm each other’s validity. The procedure highlighted is general and could be extended to different geometries.

title: Reliability-Based Preventive Maintenance for a Humanoid Robot System under Dependent Competing Failure Processes
authors: Chunyan Ling, Lechang Yang, Jingzhe Lei, Suk Joo Bae
abstract: Modeling system reliability under dependent competing failure processes remains challenging due to the complex interactions among failure modes. In such systems, soft failures arise from the combined effects of continuous degradation and additional damage induced by abrupt external shocks, while hard failures are triggered by sudden and catastrophic stresses from the same shock process. In this work, a general reliability model is developed by incorporating coupling factors, permitting a formulation that is independent of specific degradation or shock models and does not rely on predefined failure mode classifications. Then, a tailored solution algorithm is designed for the proposed reliability model by decoupling the originally coupled structure and incorporating auxiliary variables and additional constraints. Building on this framework, a preventive maintenance strategy is developed to minimize the average long-run cost rate under periodic inspection. Finally, the reliability model and maintenance approach are implemented on a humanoid robot system, demonstrating the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed methodology.

title: DA-LSTM Photovoltaic Power Strong Fluctuation Scenario Prediction Based on KNN Similar Time Period Features Fusion
authors: Yuyang Wang, Wei Hu, Yunchao Sun, Boqian Yang, Yipu Li, Yaru Cai
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: A dual attention long short-term memory (DA-LSTM) short-term photovoltaic power prediction model that integrates K-nearest neighbor (KNN) similar time period features is proposed to address the volatility and periodicity of photovoltaic power, as well as the problem of traditional neural networks only analyzing the current input window and ignoring similar time period information strongly related to weather. Firstly, the KNN algorithm is used to match K similar historical weather periods in the current time period, and the preliminary predicted value of photovoltaic power is obtained through weighted processing; Secondly, the preliminary predicted value is input as a key feature into the DA-LSTM network, where the feature attention mechanism dynamically adjusts the weights of this feature and other meteorological features, and the temporal attention mechanism mines the long-range dependencies of the power sequence. The experimental results show that the model effectively enhances its ability to capture power peak moments by injecting KNN similar time period empirical features, and its prediction accuracy is better than that of a single DA-LSTM and other comparative models.

title: Dyadic Conflicting Control Design for Cyber-Physical Systems: Applications to Variable Cycle Engines
authors: Sirong Lu, Muxuan Pan, Ye-Hwa Chen, Ying Han
abstract: A novel dyadic conflicting control (DCC) design is proposed for cyber-physical systems (CPSs). It is to effectively address the combined challenges of internal safety constraints (from actuator saturation) and external safety hazards (from cyberattacks including spoofing and mixed threats). These two challenges are self-conflicting, meaning actuator saturation weakens defensive capacity, while resilient defense mechanisms often trigger saturation by demanding high control authority. Despite substantial progress in isolated treatments of input constraints or cyberattacks, unified approaches remain limited, particularly under tight performance and robustness requirements. To this end, we propose a DCC design that integrates a diffeomorphism-based transformation and an unbounded auxiliary input mechanism to manage both saturation and cyberattack related nonlinearities. The DCC guarantees both uniform boundedness and uniform ultimate boundedness. Cosimulations on a high-fidelity variable cycle engine (VCE) demonstrate the effectiveness of the DCC design. Under single-bypass mode (a key VCE operating condition), the DCC reduces settling time by 94.83% and steady-state error by 98.37% compared to standard control methods, while strictly adhering to input constraints. These results underscore DCC’s robustness and adaptability, with potential applications in other CPS domains, such as robotics and power systems, enabling secure, high-performance control in nonlinear, and resource-constrained environments.

title: Self-Packaged High-Selective Filter Based on Dual-Layer Dual-Mode Strip Patch Resonators
authors: Zhi-Jia Cai, Qing Liu, Ke Gong, Yong-Liang Zhang, Xiao-Ming Li, De-Wei Zhang, Dong-Fang Zhou
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this letter, a novel compact high-selectivity bandpass filter (BPF) with electromagnetic (EM) shielding based on the dual-layer dual-mode (DM) square strip patch resonators (SPRs) is proposed. The coupling relationships between dual-layer DMs are analyzed in detail via magnetic-field vector decomposition, and thus, the coupling coefficient ratio can be precisely controlled. The proposed filter achieves a four-pole quasi-elliptic response with two controllable finite transmission zeros (FTZs). For the demonstration, a BPF with the center frequency of 6.9 GHz is designed, fabricated, and measured. The measured results are in good agreement with the simulated results.

title: An Equilibrium Analysis of Feed-in Premiums and Forward Contracts for Renewable Energy Integration
authors: Taisei Ishikawa, Kazuya Ito, Ryuta Takashima
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: This study develops an electricity market equilibrium model with transmission constraints, a Feed-in Premium (FIP) scheme, and forward contracts, and examines how transmission capacity and renewable energy penetration affect social surplus. The model is numerically illustrated using a three-node network.Numerical results indicate that expanding transmission capacity increases social surplus; however, the effect exhibits diminishing returns, and excessive expansion may weaken the role of the forward market. In addition, a higher renewable energy share consistently improves both forward market surplus and overall social surplus, with particularly pronounced effects at intermediate levels of renewable integration. This study provides foundational insights for market design and transmission planning in the context of increasing renewable energy integration.

title: Test-Bed Development for Sensor Fusion Application for Rendezvous and Proximity Operations
authors: Cristobal Garrido, David Barnhart
abstract: Rendezvous and proximity operations are becoming increasingly relevant due to the growing number of objects orbiting and the potential capabilities they enable. However, access to space to perform those activities is prohibited due to cost, risk, and difficulty. This work presents a developed Test-bed to mimic Rendezvous and Proximity Operation scenarios. We emulate elements such as light conditions, and with full six degrees of freedom for the spacecraft mockups. This facility allows an accessible space for developing and testing hardware and techniques in space. In particular, we are interested in studying Sensor Fusion techniques using the test-bed. Here, we introduce the main components of the test-bed facility. Then, we include the sensor fusion hardware description and describe how this is evaluated using the developed facility. The goal is to demonstrate that the test bed enables the testing of different procedures for rendezvous and proximity operations, utilizing hardware that can be implemented in future inspace applications.

title: A Machine-Learning Emulator Framework for Multi-Objective Policy Scenario Simulation and Optimization
authors: Varun Viswapriyan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: We present a computational framework for multi-objective macroeconomic policy optimization that combines a machine-learning dynamic emulator, counterfactual simulation, and surrogate-assisted constrained optimization. Using data series from the FRED-MD macroeconomic dataset, the emulator learns nonlinear transition dynamics from a lagged input state representation. A Sobol low-discrepancy experimental design efficiently explores the feasible policy space and supports surrogate modeling of the policy-response surface for optimization. Multi-objective solutions are obtained via desirability-based aggregation with economic penalty constraints to exclude implausible policy configurations. In counterfactual simulations over a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 2}$</tex>-month horizon, the composite-optimal policy configuration yields consistent improvements relative to baseline, including reductions in financial stress, inflation, and unemployment (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\triangle \text{BAAFFM} \text{<apt>} \mathrm{T} \approx-\text{1. 0}, \triangle \text{C P I} \_\text{Y O Y} \approx-\text{0. 1 2}$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\triangle U N R A T E \_L \approx-0.17$</tex>). The reported optimal policy shocks should be interpreted as illustrative scenarios consistent with historical reduced-form dynamics rather than literal policy prescriptions. The proposed framework provides a scalable decision-support approach for systematically exploring policy trade-offs and identifying feasible configurations that satisfy explicit multi-objective criteria.

title: Predicting Competitive Programming Problem Tags and Ratings from Tutorials Using BERT-Based Models
authors: Albert Yulius Ramahalim, Christoffer Edbert Karuniawan, Tjeng Wawan Cenggoro
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: In competitive programming, online judges (OJs) are a form of educational technology that host a large number of algorithmic problems. Codeforces is one such OJ that annotates problems with algorithmic tags and difficulty ratings, enabling efficient search for digital learning and practice. However, such metadata is not consistently available across OJs, motivating automated prediction from problem content. Prior approaches mostly use problem statements, whose narrative can obscure the intended technique; in contrast, tutorials and solution code often describe it explicitly. We study whether tutorial text improves prediction quality by comparing BERT-based models trained on (i) statements and (ii) tutorial text plus solution code. We construct CREST, a Codeforces dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8, 9 4 1}$</tex> problems (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 6 ~ t a g s}$</tex>) with math-rich statements, tutorials, and solution code, and evaluate multilabel tag prediction and rating regression. For tag prediction, tutorial+code improves PR-AUC from 0.463 to 0.603 and micro-/macro-F1 from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.476 / 0.276$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.563 / 0.371$</tex>. For rating prediction, tutorial+code reduces MAE from 503 to 332 rating points and increases the coefficient of determination from 0.221 to 0.637. Overall, our results indicate that tutorials and solution code provide stronger predictive signals than statements for automated tagging and difficulty estimation.

title: Drone-Based Multi-Sensor Detection System for Underground Human Identification
authors: S. Balambigai, Raksith C R, Saravanakumar A, Sivachandiran S, Vinothraja S, Vedhanth S S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: In disaster and forensic investigations, the issue of determining the location of human beings who are buried under the ground or trapped in their rubble is an urgent and highly monitored problem, which is resolved quickly and with precision. Thermal imaging and manual ground surveys are traditional methods that are limited and time-consuming, particularly when it comes to subsurface identification. To overcome these shortcomings, this project suggests a Drone-based Multi-Sensor Detection System Underground Human Identification, which incorporates the Ultra-Wideband (UWB) radar, thermal imaging, and RGB cameras on a platform of an aerial drone. The suggested system uses several sensors to enhance the accuracy in detecting. The applications of UWB technology include the detection of underground anomalies through the transmission of low-power and widespectrum signals, the identification of temperature changes on the surface, and the visual mapping of the treated section through thermal and RGB cameras, respectively. The data from all sensors is processed in real time by an onboard processor, and the results are an anomaly map, which is wirelessly communicated to a mobile application dashboard to view live data. This will serve to give real time representation of a possible human presence found on a 2D/3D map of GPS position. As in the experimental results, it has been shown that the system costs less in search time when compared to the oldfashioned standalone systems, in addition to higher detection accuracy.
