The small size and aerial features of the system enable prompt implementation in the disasterstricken regions and fallen buildings, as well as in challenging environments. The solution suggested can be used in various ways in disaster rescue phases, in forensic investigations, in defense, or in archaeological surveys. Within the scope of the future, one may consider automatic classification of anomalies with the help of artificial intelligence and drone swarms to cover vast areas, as well as sharing data via clouds and enhancing coordination between rescue crews.

title: DCRU-Net: A Dual Cross-Attentive Recurrent U-Net Architecture for Full-Field Deformation Estimation of Ground-Based Radar System
authors: Islam Helmy, Andreas Schenk, Omar M. Saad, Rene Hexel, Gervase Tuxworth
abstract: This work proposes a deep learning (DL) architecture for estimating full-field deformation maps from ground-based complex radar data. Unlike existing methods that focus on estimating deformation time series at selected points, the proposed model directly estimates the entire deformation map from a single-frame complex radar observation. This capability enables faster, spatially comprehensive monitoring, which is crucial for real-time early warning systems (EWSs) in Internet of Things (IoT)-enabled environments. The primary input to the model is a 2-channel matrix formed by the real and imaginary components of the complex radar data. We propose a dual cross-attentive recurrent U-Net architecture (DCRU-Net) consisting of two networks trained simultaneously. The two networks, a primary and an auxiliary network, are based on long short-term memory (LSTM) units arranged in a U-Net architecture, where the model inputs are spatially restructured into sequences, enabling the model to learn deformation-related phase variations across the scene rather than modeling temporal dependencies as in conventional approaches. The primary network processes the 2-channel complex radar data, which is the leading network of the model. In contrast, the auxiliary network is guided by the amplitude dispersion index (ADI) to emphasize coherent scattering regions during training. A cross-attention mechanism enables interaction between the two networks, allowing the auxiliary network to guide the primary network toward physically meaningful regions and suppress clutter. The motivation behind this architecture is to predict the main features of the deformation maps while minimizing the impact of slight variations, which can result in clutter-induced distortions. The model is evaluated against benchmark methods, and the results demonstrate that our model outperforms existing methods and has better alignment with the ideal deformation maps, showing its potential for real-world radar applications.

title: Securing the Sky: A Lightweight Authentication Protocol for Emergency UAVs
authors: Saeed Ullah Jan, Fahad Algarni, Ali Delham Algarni
abstract: Utilizing drones for various disaster management tasks, such as mitigation, prevention, aid delivery, survivor search, rapid response, and recovery, establishes a communication network that integrates UAVs, ground stations (GS), and rescue personnel. However, such a network is vulnerable to security threats, including entity spoofing to gain unauthorized control, command-and-control data manipulation, and eavesdropping on sensitive information, all of which can significantly compromise the mission. Hence, a secure initial authentication between UAVs and GS is crucial in such scenarios. Authentication is one of the fundamental security measures; however, a number of schemes already proposed for this adversarial environment suffer from several shortcomings. Some rely on computationally expensive cryptography, which resource-constrained UAVs cannot afford, while others, upon analysis, are inherently vulnerable to spoofing or do not offer comprehensive key establishment. In light of these limitations, this paper proposes an efficient and verifiably secure protocol for mutual entity authentication along with session key establishment for subsequent communications. The protocol uses lightweight hash functions in its structure to ensure suitability for emergency scenarios. The security claims are formally verified using GNY logic and the ProVerif tool, demonstrating resistance against a defined threat model that includes spoofing and unauthorized access. A comparative performance analysis indicates that the proposed scheme reduces computation and communication costs by approximately 85.26% and 16%, respectively, compared to a set of contemporary schemes. Furthermore, it also performs better in terms of energy consumption and scalability.

title: IEEE Internet of Things Journal Society Information
authors: N/A

title:
High-Performance Self-Powered NUV Photodetectors Based on Trilayered SnS/h-BN/SnS
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Heterojunctions
authors: Mengjiao Dong, Shiao Feng, Xiyu Jia, Xueting Luo, Chun Sun, Yonghui Zhang, Chao Fan
abstract: Abstract—In this letter, a trilayered heterojunction of tin sulfide (SnS)/hexagonal BN (h-BN)/SnS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> and corresponding 405-nm NUV photodetectors based on such heterojunctions are proposed. They exhibit a supreme self-powered performance with a remarkable responsivity of up to 1.8 A/W, a large detectivity of 1.51 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</sup> Jones and a low dark current of 1.2 pA, which surpasses those of NUV photodetectors based on SnS/SnS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> bilayered heterojunctions and most other 2D materials, and even commercial photodetector (Thorlabs FDS100). This work offers an efficient optimization strategy for SnS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-based self-powered photodetectors, thereby paving the way for their utilization and application.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: A Secure Affine Frequency Division Multiplexing System for Next-Generation Wireless Communications
authors: Ping Wang, Zulin Wang, Yuanhan Ni, Qu Luo, Yuanfang Ma, Xiaosi Tian, Pei Xiao
abstract: Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) has garnered significant attention due to its superior performance in high-mobility scenarios, as well as multiple waveform parameters that provide greater degrees of freedom for system design. This paper proposes a novel secure affine frequency division multiplexing (SE-AFDM) system, which enhances physical-layer security by dynamically varying an AFDM pre-chirp parameter across subcarriers and over AFDM symbols. In the SE-AFDM system, the pre-chirp parameter is dynamically generated from a codebook controlled by a long-period pseudo-noise (LPPN) sequence. Instead of applying spreading in the data domain, our parameter-domain spreading approach provides additional security while maintaining reliability and high spectral efficiency. We also propose a synchronization framework to solve the problem of reliably and rapidly synchronizing the dynamic parameter in fast time-varying channels. The theoretical derivations prove that unsynchronized eavesdroppers cannot eliminate the nonlinear impact of the time-varying parameter and further provide useful guidance for codebook design. Simulation results demonstrate the security advantages of the proposed SE-AFDM system in high-mobility scenarios, and the hardware prototype validates the effectiveness of the proposed SE-AFDM system and the proposed synchronization framework.

title: HarmonyQA: A Unified Framework for Harmonization Quality Assessment
authors: Zitong Xu, Huiyu Duan, Guangji Ma, Liu Yang, Jiarui Wang, Sijing Wu, Yunhao Li, Qingbo Wu, Xiongkuo Min, Guangtao Zhai, Patrick Le Callet
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Image or video composition aims to combine the foreground object and the background content to generate a realistic composite image or video, in which harmonization algorithms play a crucial role in adjusting the color or light of the foreground object to make the entire image or video harmonious. However, existing image quality assessment (IQA) and video quality assessment (VQA) methods often fail to align with human preference in evaluating harmonization algorithms due to insensitivity to minor color or light inconsistencies. To address the issue and facilitate the advancement of harmonization, we introduce the first harmonization quality assessment database, termed HarmonyQAD, which consists of Harmony-IQAD comprising 1,800 harmonized images generated by 9 different image harmonizaion algorithms (IHAs), and HarmonyVQAD comprising 1,600 harmonized videos generated by 2 IHAs and 6 video harmonization algorithms (VHAs). All harmonized images and videos are accompanied by mean opinion scores (MOSs), collected through a meticulously designed subjective evaluation experiment. Based on the HarmonyQAD, we propose a unified framework for harmonization quality assessment, termed HarmonyQA, to predict the harmonization quality of both harmonized images and videos. Extensive experiments show that HarmonyQA achieves state-of-the-art performance on our datasets, and also achieves competing results on traditional IQA and VQA tasks. Furthermore, cross-dataset evaluation also shows that HarmonyQA exhibits better generalization ability than self-supervised learning-based IQA methods. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/IntMeGroup/HarmonyQA.

title: Optimistic Thompson Sampling for No-Regret Learning in Unknown Games
authors: Yingru Li, Liangqi Liu, Wenqiang Pu, Hao Liang, Zhi-Quan Luo
abstract: We study the problem of learning to play a repeated multi-player game with an unknown reward function and bandit feedback. The central challenge arises from the need to balance exploration and exploitation under bandit feedback while strategically responding to other players. To address this, we propose Thompson Sampling-based algorithms that leverage available information about opponents’ actions and reward structures, resulting in a significant reduction in regret bound. Building on these insights, we introduce the Optimism-then-NoRegret framework, which encompasses various game algorithms as special cases. Simulation evaluations on three distinct types of games show that our proposed algorithms consistently and substantially outperform standard baselines.

title: Distinguishing Task-Specific and General-Purpose AI in Regulation
authors: Jennifer Wang, Andrew D. Selbst, Solon Barocas, Suresh Venkatasubramanian
publish: Proceedings of the Symposium on Computer Science and Law
abstract: None

title: Local Verification of Current-State Opacity and Static-State Opacity in Distributed Discrete-Event Systems
authors: Yin Tong, Kai Cai
abstract: The integration of physical processes and cyber-networks in cyber-physical systems (CPSs) has made information security crucial, particularly in preventing unauthorized access and data leakage. Current-state opacity (CSO) in discrete-event systems (DESs) formalizes the security property that an intruder can never infer secret states based on its observation. This paper addresses CSO and its verification in distributed DESs composed of multiple local systems. Existing methods of verifying CSO in distributed DESs either rely on sufficient conditions for local verification or require costly synchronization of local systems and observers. This paper first proposes necessary and sufficient conditions for locally verifying CSO of distributed DESs, reducing the time complexity from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(2^{n^{m}})$</tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(mn)$</tex-math></inline-formula>, where <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$m$</tex-math></inline-formula> is the number of local systems and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n$</tex-math></inline-formula> is the largest state number of local systems. We also introduce static-state opacity (SSO), a new information security notion for distributed systems characterizing intruders' incapability to deduce global secrets from local ones. We show that SSO can be verified (necessarily and sufficiently) through a matrix involving CSO of local systems with a time complexity of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(m2^{n})$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Furthermore, we explore the relationship between CSO and SSO, showing scenarios where they are equivalent.

title: Affiliate Plan of the IEEE Nuclear and Plasma Sciences Society
authors: N/A

title: Nine-year observation of Global Precipitation Particle Size Distribution and Radar Reflectivity
authors: Qingpeng Wu, Wei Cheng, V. Chandrasekar, Tiantian Yu, Shuangshuang Bian, Hao Wang, Qiangyu Zeng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Understanding global precipitation microphysics is essential for quantitative precipitation estimation (QPE) and numerical weather prediction (NWP). Using nine years (2015-2023) of the Global Precipitation Measurement (GPM) mission Dual-frequency Precipitation Radar (DPR) observations over the quasi-global domain (67°S-67°N), we characterize precipitation particle size distribution (PSD) properties for rainfall and snowfall. We analyse the mass-weighted mean diameter (Dm), normalized intercept parameter (Nw), radar reflectivity (Z), and dual-frequency ratio (DFR), stratified by land/ocean, convective/stratiform type, altitude, and phase. Rainfall rates are grouped into six classes and snowfall rates into five classes to quantify intensity dependence. To interpret DPR dual-frequency signatures, we develop an observation-constrained forward-scattering framework that adopts DPR-consistent PSD and particle-shape assumptions and performs T-matrix simulations for rain, dry snow, and wet snow with varying liquid-water volume fraction (Fvw), evaluated against nine-year near-surface DFR-Z statistics. Quasi-global PSD peaks (Dm∼1.0 mm; log10Nw∼3.4) closely resemble oceanic precipitation, and convective and stratiform regimes substantially overlap in Dm (0.9-1.8 mm) and log10Nw (3.0-4.0). Vertically, Dm skewness and kurtosis decrease from 2-6 km, with distinct features near ∼10 km and ∼15 km. Rainfall DSDs become more variable at high intensities (>5 mm h-1), whereas snowfall PSDs remain comparatively stable (Dm mainly 1.0-1.5 mm; log10Nw∼3.4). Forward-scattering comparisons show that dry-snow assumptions cannot reproduce the observed DFR-Z space, while increasing Fvw yields close agreement, indicating that near-surface snowfall is consistent with wetted melted particles. These results provide observation-based constraints for improving solid-hydrometeor scattering representations in DPR snowfall retrievals and microphysical parameterizations in NWP.

title: State of Power Online Estimation for Series-Parallel Connected Lithium Titanate Battery Packs Considering Cell Inconsistencies and Branch Voltage Consistency Constraints
authors: Sidi Dong, Xuexia Zhang
abstract: Accurate estimation of the state of power (SOP) for series–parallel connected battery packs is essential for ensuring safe and reliable operation. However, most existing works focus on SOP estimation at the battery cell level, while investigations on series–parallel connected battery packs remain limited due to their increased structural complexity. To address this issue, a model-based online SOP estimation method for series–parallel connected lithium titanate battery packs is proposed. First, a centralized online modeling strategy based on the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) is developed to explicitly account for cell inconsistencies, in which the branch currents and model parameters of all battery cells are simultaneously identified online. Subsequently, a branch-voltage-oriented systematic SOP estimation algorithm that considers both cell inconsistencies and branch voltage consistency constraints is designed. The algorithm maximizes the pack power while ensuring that all battery cells operate within their safe operating areas and that the voltage consistency among parallel branches is strictly maintained. An integrated online SOP estimation framework is then proposed by combining the EnKF-based centralized model with the designed SOP estimation algorithm. The developed online model is validated under locomotive operating conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared with the unscented Kalman filter (UKF)-based centralized model, the proposed EnKF-based centralized model reduces the root mean square error (RMSE) of voltage estimation by an average of 29.24% and decreases the computational cost by 59.86%, enabling more accurate and efficient tracking of cell voltage dynamics. Furthermore, the proposed framework is applied to online SOP estimation under locomotive conditions, and the effects of cell inconsistencies and branch voltage consistency constraints on SOP are systematically analyzed.

title: High-Frequency Silicon Platelet Feedhorns for LiteBIRD
authors: Sarah A. Stevenson, Jason E. Austermann, James A. Beall, Shannon M. Duff, Nils W. Halverson, Johannes Hubmayr, Gregory C. Jaehnig
abstract: LiteBIRD is a planned space mission to observe the polarized cosmic microwave background in the frequency range 34 GHz to 448 GHz, with the goal of measuring or constraining the tensor-to-scalar ratio r. We have developed silicon platelet feedhorns for LiteBIRD, consisting of stacked metalized silicon wafers with deep-etched holes to create an array of feedhorns with profiled tapers and a coefficient of thermal contraction that is matched to the detector wafer. We present beam pattern measurements for silicon platelet feedhorn prototypes designed for 166-322 GHz, 200-388 GHz, and 356-448 GHz, corresponding to each of the planned LiteBIRD high-frequency pixel types. The measurements were carried out on a room-temperature millimeter-wave vector network analyzer and are in excellent agreement with simulation.

title: Member Get-A-Member (MGM) Program
authors: N/A

title: Computationally Efficient SDA-MPC With Capacitor Voltage Balancing for 3P-5L-ANPC Converters
authors: Zhixun Ma, Jiyuan Gong, Haichuan Niu, Xu Zhang, Zhongshu Shao, Xiang Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a computationally efficient sphere decoding algorithm model predictive control (SDA-MPC) method for the three-phase five-level active neutral-point clamped converter. The core of this approach is the dynamic incorporation of the neutral-point potential and the three-phase flying capacitor voltage balancing errors into the calculation of the hypersphere radius and the sphere centroid distance, which effectively achieves precise multi-capacitor voltage balancing. Furthermore, the strategy reconstructs the search tree, which contains redundant switching states, by imposing level transition constraints and sector partitioning constraints, thereby significantly reducing the maximum computational burden of the SDA-MPC. Experimental results fully validate the feasibility and superiority of the proposed method.

title: Member Get-A-Member (MGM) Program
authors: N/A

title: NOMA-Enabled Joint Sensing and Communication for MISO-Based Non-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Wali Ullah Khan, Jaehyuk Choi, Fahd N Al-Wesabi, Hadeel Alsolai, Xingwang Li
abstract: This letter proposes joint sensing and communication (JSAC) for a high-altitude platform station (HAPS) that serves downlink non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) users while simultaneously sensing a ground target. Unlike designs that allocate a dedicated sensing beam, we illuminate the target with the superimposed communication beams and process the echo with a single receive combiner. We formulate a joint transmit–receive design that minimizes the sensing mean-squared error (MSE) under user's minimum rate constraints. The nonconvex problem is addressed by alternating optimization, where the original joint problem is solved in two stages. First, solve the HAPS transmit beamformers problem using semidefinite programming, with the fixed combiner. Then, with the given beamforming, we update the receive combiner in closed form using the linear minimum MSE solution. The proposed NOMA-JSAC solution in HAPS guarantees a monotonic MSE decrease and converges to a stationary solution with low computational complexity. Simulation results demonstrate a clear rate–sensing trade-off versus transmit power, validating the practicality and effectiveness of the proposed NOMA-enabled HAPS JSAC design.

title: Edge Computing Energy Management with Reinforcement Learning for Smart EV Charging
authors: Sin-Hang Chang, Yao Chiang, Hung-Yu Wei
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The rapid rise of electric vehicles (EVs) and distributed energy resources (DERs) has increased the complexity of local energy management, creating a need for intelligent coordination at the edge. This paper proposes a three-layer edge computing framework that integrates photovoltaic (PV) generation, an energy storage system (ESS), and multiple EV chargers under a unified cost objective. We adopt a multiagent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) algorithm for EV charging control, while the ESS follows a rule-based dispatch strategy. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme effectively reduces total operating cost while maintaining grid stability and acceptable EV charging performance.

title: Vector Perturbation Through SNR Maximization for Uplink Overload MIMO Spatial Multiplexing
authors: Ryoki Honjo, Satoshi Denno, Yafei Hou
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper proposes vector perturbation techniques in overloaded MIMO spatial multiplexing suitable for uplinks of wireless systems where the number of transmit antennas is less than that of receive antennas. The number of the spatially multiplexed signal streams is increased to that of the receive antennas in spite of the number of the transmit antennas. The proposed vector perturbation comprises a lattice reduction-based transmit power reduction with modulo function and brute force search for the signal to noise power ratio (SNR) maximization. The performance of the overload MIMO with the proposed vector perturbation is evaluated by computer simulation in terms of the average SNR and the bit error rate (BER). The simulation results show that the proposed vector perturbation achieves better performance than the conventional vector perturbation in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times 4$</tex> overloaded MIMO channel where the number of transmit antennas and that of the receive antennas are 2 and 4.

title: Enhancing LLM Reliability and Efficiency with Query Refinement in Retrieval Augmented Generation
authors: Aya Mohamed Abd ElHamied, Ahmed A. AboelFarag, Yasser El-Sonbaty
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Large language models have achieved great success in various natural language processing tasks, yet they still face several serious challenges, such as errors and hallucinations, and high inference cost. The contribution of this work is an extension framework called Refined Query Retrieval Augmented Generation (RQ-RAG) to enhance query handling through rewriting, decomposition, and disambiguation. The algorithm works with a specifically chosen dataset of some 42,810 samples, and search results from DuckDuckGo are used to provide answers that seemingly make sense in context. We are interested in exploiting LLaMA-4 Maverick, a state-of-the-art model with 17B parameters, as their base. We demonstrate on RQ-RAG dataset how the query refinement technique impacts upon facts' accuracy. On the single-hop (ARC-C, OBQA, POPQA) and multi-hop (HotpotQA, 2Wiki, MuSiQue) experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms the baseline models and previous state-of-the-art retrievalaugmented approaches in terms of Accuracy and Match Score as well as F1 Score. The results show that query refinement can increase the reliability, robustness, and efficiency of LLM enabled systems.

title: Time-Variant Transformer for Predicting Alzheimer's Disease Progression from Longitudinal Data
authors: Habiba Assem Sharaf, Mohamed Hamed Kholief, Hany Hanafy Mahmoud Said
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Alzheimer's disease gradually affects cognitive functions such as memory, reasoning, and daily activities. Although transformer-based models have improved disease progression forecasting, they often face limitations in handling uneven visit times, incomplete records, and diverse data modalities. This study introduces a Time-Visit Transformer (TVT) tailored to predict Alzheimer's progression by explicitly modeling the timing gaps between patient follow-ups. TVT (i) encodes each visit, (ii) augments representations with Time2Vec to capture the temporal phase and scale, (iii) adjusts attention using the real-time intervals between visits delta-Time-FiLM, and (iv) incorporates site-specific context through Relational-FiLM. An attention layer then reads the sequence of visits in order and can gracefully handle missing entries. In head-to-head evaluations on longitudinal cohorts, TVT achieves an AUC-ROC of 0.9331 for conversion from cognitively normal (CN) to mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and 0.9529 for MCI to Alzheimer's disease (AD), demonstrating a time-sensitive model suitable for practical clinical use.

title: Enhnacing the Production Scheduling in a Multi-Product Powdered Mix Factory
authors: Lina Ismail, Farida Haytham Wagdy, Mayar Essam Yassin, Khaled S. El-Kilany
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Efficient production scheduling remains a key challenge in modern manufacturing, especially in multi-product systems that rely on shared resources and experience sequencedependent changeovers. This study developed a discrete event simulation model in ExtendSim to enhance production scheduling performance in a powdered mix factory in Egypt. The developed model represented real production processes, including sieving, mixing, and packaging operations considering crucial factors/parameters such as sequence-dependent changeovers, machine breakdowns, and operational constraints. Validation against actual production data showed a deviation of only 6.27 %, confirming the model's accuracy and reliability. Through extensive scenario testing, the enhanced scheduling configuration achieved a 15 % reduction in average cycle time and a 17 % increase in total throughput compared with the factory's existing scheduling method. These improvements highlight the effectiveness of discrete event simulation as a decision-support tool for production scheduling and resource management. The results demonstrate that simulation-based models can significantly enhance system performance in complex, changeover-intensive food production environment.

title: Towards Urban Digital Twins: A Framework Integrating CARLA with 3D City Models
authors: Shunsuke Kamioka, Taku Yamazaki, Takumi Miyoshi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Digital twins, which reproduce real-world urban environments in virtual space, have recently attracted increasing attention. In Japan, the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism (MLIT) is developing nationwide 3D city models through Project PLATEAU. Although PLATEAU provides detailed static information, it lacks dynamic elements such as pedestrian and vehicle motion and therefore cannot function as a fully dynamic urban digital twin on its own. Meanwhile, CARLA, a widely used open-source road-traffic simulator, offers a highly realistic simulation environment but does not provide an efficient mechanism for ingesting and updating real-world data. Existing studies have explored integrating external data into CARLA to construct digital twins; however, these approaches primarily focus on generating actors from external data and struggle to update large numbers of actors in real time, limiting their applicability to city-scale digital twins. In this paper, we propose a method for constructing a dynamic urban digital twin by importing 3D city models into CARLA and continuously updating actor states based on externally acquired vehicle information. We implemented the proposed method and demonstrated that it can reproduce large-scale urban environments while maintaining stable real-time updates for a large number of vehicles.

title: Design and Implementation of a Smart Agriculture STEAM Program in Higher Education
authors: Hirooki Aoki
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper reports on the design and implementation of a short-term STEAM education program for smart agriculture. We invited two faculty members and three undergraduate students from the Institute of Technology of Cambodia and conducted intensive training that integrated image electronic engineering and agricultural engineering. The program consisted of three modules: LED matrix control for agricultural signage, three-dimensional plant measurement using an RGB-D camera, and water quality monitoring in an IoT-based aquaponics system. Undergraduate and graduate students at our university designed and delivered the classes in English. Through project-based group work, participants gained hands-on experience with core technologies for agricultural DX and developed international communication skills.

title: CNN-Based Point Cloud Geometry Compression with Feature Extraction for Object Recognition
authors: Hideaki Kimata
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Many studies have advanced to improve performance in both compression coding and object recognition for point clouds. A point cloud comprises numerous points, resulting in large data volumes, thus efficient compression methods are desirable. However, high compression using CNNbased methods is anticipated to reduce the object recognition performance. Point cloud compression techniques suitable for object recognition are necessary. We propose a novel compression method that improves object classification performance even at low coding rates by training the network to perform feature extraction for object recognition, achieved through modifying the loss function during DNN training for point cloud geometry compression. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can enhance object classification performance.

title: An Architecture to Provide Sensory Expansion for Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing Individuals in Digital Television
authors: Juliana C. Silva, Claudio F. M. Pinheiro, Eddie B. de Lima Filho, Rômulo Fabrício, Rafaell G. Santana
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Television systems offer accessibility features for people who are deaf or hard of hearing, such as Closed Captioning or sign language, but these resources often lack personalization for different levels of hearing loss. This paper presents a system architecture that leverages smartphones connected to digital TV receivers to capture audio from the tuned service and perform real-time audio processing and tactile vibration generation. The proposed architecture enables personalized audio adaptation based on user hearing profiles and explores the use of smartphone-based haptic feedback to enrich television accessibility. Rather than focusing on clinical validation, this work aims to describe the design principles, processing pipeline, and integration mechanisms required to support future assistive television applications for deaf and hard-of-hearing users.

title: Fuzzy IoT Temperature Regulated Storage System with Consumer-Oriented Dashboard
authors: Ryan Rhay P. Vicerra, Lance Raphael T. Beltran, Marcus Allen P. Gomeri, Arthur Caleb L. Co, Rennan G. Baldovino, Ethan Bryce S. Kaw
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper presents a scalable fuzzy-based IoT temperature-regulated storage system designed to optimize conditions for temperature-sensitive products using intelligent control. The proposed system integrates IoT sensors to continuously monitor environmental parameters and applies a fuzzy logic algorithm to adjust cooling mechanisms in real time. A consumer-oriented dashboard provides users with live data visualization, alerts, and remote-control capabilities for system management. Test results show temperature stability and enhanced user engagement compared to conventional control methods.

title: The Impact of Generative AI and Large Language Models on Telemedicine Diagnostics: A Systematic Review
authors: Sarthak Srivastava, Harshvardhan Chandrakar, Harshpreet Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The combination of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) and Large Language Models (LLMs) is redefining telemedicine diagnostics by improving diagnostic accuracy, automating routine clinical tasks, and expanding access to quality healthcare for underserved and remote populations. This systematic review compiles recent evidence on the use of these technologies within telehealth environments, focusing on the diagnostic tasks they support and their clinical and operational impacts. Using a structured search and screening process, relevant literature covering language-based reasoning, multimodal image and signal analysis, and workflow augmentation was analyzed to evaluate performance, reliability, and real-world readiness. The findings indicate that current Large Language Models and multimodal systems demonstrate high accuracy in structured diagnostic contexts and can significantly reduce documentation workload. However, existing evidence remains largely preliminary and concentrated within controlled experimental settings. Major challenges hindering safe implementation include model hallucinations, limited interpretability, dataset bias, data governance and privacy concerns, and fragmented regulatory frameworks. This review highlights key research priorities such as specialty-specific validation, integration of explainable AI mechanisms, comprehensive bias auditing, and regulatory harmonization to promote responsible adoption.

title: A Smart Virtual Makeup Try-On and Recommender System Using Artificial Intelligence and Augmented Reality
authors: B. Lanitha, Anushya R, Jagasri V R, Preethi P P, Sowmiya P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The cosmetics industry is rapidly adopting artificial intelligence (AI) and Augmented Reality (AR) to improve customer experience and personalization. Unfortunately, most of the virtual makeup test applications currently in use rely on flimsy overlays and ignore important aspects such as skin conditions, lifestyle, and allergies, which results in sub-par recommendations and unhappy users. In this paper a new AI-based framework for a smart virtual makeup try-on and recommender system that combines deep learning, augmented reality, and computer vision is presented. For high resolution skin analysis (acne, pigmentation, dryness, and redness), the system uses Vision Transformers (ViT/FastViT). For contextual personalization, Tabular Transformers/MLPs are used to process user profile data. Visual and profile features are dynamically balanced by an attention-based fusion mechanism, and recommendations are transparent thanks to Explainable AI (SHAP/LIME). Immersion and interaction are made possible by a real-time AR try on module that superimposes recommended items on the user’s face. In this work, personalized recommendations, AR visualization, and skin health analysis are combined to provide safer, more reliable, and user-focused beauty technology for the cosmetics and e-commerce sectors.

title: Modification and Testing of Patch Antenna for Wireless Communication System
authors: Maklayne Barros de Pontes Santiago, Maryam Liaqat, Maria Luysa Teles Barcelar, Matheus Henrique Barros Da Silva, Frederico Dias Nunes, Antonio Celso Dantas Antonino
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Osteoporosis is a progressive bone disease characterized by reduced bone mineral density, leading to increased fracture risk. Conventional diagnostic techniques such as X-ray and dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) involve ionizing radiation and high operational costs, limiting their use for continuous monitoring. Mineral deficiencies, notably calcium and Vitamin D, precipitate a critical decline in bone mineral density (BMD), resulting in structural degradation and heightened fracture susceptibility—a condition exemplified by severe hypocalcemia observed in regional postmenopausal populations (e.g., Peshawar, Pakistan, is below 1300 mg/day WHO standard). The early diagnosis is the solution of this hypocalcemia which can be possible by Microwave Imaging (MWI). Dielectric properties of defected bones can be measured using MWI, non-conventional non-ionizing methods. Furthermore, its reliance on low-cost, miniaturized MWI as an ideal candidate for a portable, point-of-care diagnostic system. The MWI technique offers a pivotal solution for the early-stage stratification and preventative management of osteoporotic defects, mitigating future morbidity associated with pathological bone fragility. In this work, a modified dual-band microstrip patch antenna is proposed for microwave-based bone health assessment.

title: Machine learning methods for SWIPT enabled NOMA networks under imperfect SIC conditions
authors: Vegi Neelima Thulasi Aparna, A Vijayasankar, Peruri Sai Meghana, Surada Sridhar, Yaddanapudi Sarada Devi, Kota Nageswara Rao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The integration of Simultaneous Wireless Information and Power Transfer (SWIPT) with Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) has shown significant promise for achieving energy-efficient and reliable communications in Internet-of-Things (IoT) networks. However, practical system performance is often constrained by imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC), non-linear energy harvesting (EH), and channel fading, which adversely impact outage probability and overall reliability. In this study, we introduce a machine learning-driven strategy utilizing an oracle policy to minimize outage probability in SWIPT-enabled NOMA networks. This approach dynamically optimizes power allocation and splitting ratios, improving both system reliability and energy efficiency under realistic channel conditions. Simulation results indicate that the oracle-based policy surpasses traditional schemes, offering notable reductions in outage probability and valuable guidance for efficient resource management in next-generation wireless networks.

title: Nonlinear Adaptive Filtering with Robust Adaptive Exponential Functional Link Networks under the Impulsive Interference
authors: Parishmita Deka, Bijit Kumar Das
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Adaptive filtering for nonlinear system identification has been explored using many different approaches, and adaptive exponential functional link networks (AEFLN) are one of the popular choices. Although performance deteriorates under the impulsive interference with non-Gaussian characteristics. To address this limitation, this work integrates a generalized soft-root-sign (GSRS) cost function with AEFLN, resulting in a robust algorithm termed AEFLN-GSRS. Performance results demonstrate that the proposed AEFLN-GSRS approach effectively mitigates the influence of impulsive noise while maintaining desired convergence behavior and achieves improved steady-state performance and enhanced robustness compared to conventional AEFLN and robust AEFLN algorithms under Gaussian and impulsive interference.

title: Dependence of optical refractive index of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength in near-infrared waveband for warm and shallow sea_supp4-3693439.mp4
authors: Shanshan Wang
abstract: Dependence of optical refractive index (RI) of seawater on temperature, salinity, and pressure is crucial for optical fiber sensors operated in seawater. Expressions of RI of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength applicable to near-infrared waveband are developed based on a great deal of RI data measured by a semi-encapsulated microfiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer sensor. To verify the accuracy of these expressions, RI measured by infrared refractometer, theoretical calculated sensitivity and experimental measured sensitivity are used, by which the most accurate expression is determined. Based on this expression, a variable sensitivity matrix is established to demodulate temperature, salinity and pressure simultaneously with errors of 10.91 %, 6.09 % and 18.80 %, respectively. The expression proposed here will provide valuable references for theoretical and experimental study on optical fiber sensors operated in infrared waveband and push the applications of fiber optical sensors in ocean investigations.

title: Calibrating IMU Pitch Misalignment in the Field to Reduce Wheel-Inertial Odometry Elevation Drift
authors: Tushaar Jain, David Wettergreen, Michael Kaess
abstract: Rovers estimate their position (“positioning”) and map their surroundings, but these tasks are complicated by measurement errors, which accumulate. This effect is severe in the vertical channel, as drift in the rover's elevation estimate is particularly difficult to mitigate. One of the main contributors to this drift is pitch misalignment between the rover's velocity vector and its Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU). This motivates our contribution: a method for calibrating an IMU's pitch misalignment. Our method does not require an initial ground calibration, external infrastructure, a source of ground truth pose, accounting for wheel slip, or sensors beyond an IMU and wheel encoders. We simply require the rover to drive in loops on approximately planar (but not necessarily level) terrain. This can be done autonomously in a planetary environment, requiring little oversight from mission operations. Our theoretical analysis, simulation results, and physical tests prove our method's effectiveness. Additionally, we analyze the effect of terrain, IMU quality, and calibration path on the calibration. Though our idea to drive in loops is simple, our main contributions are that we prove why our method works, analyze it extensively in theory, simulation, and practice, determine what parameters do and do not matter, and provide a comprehensive argument for applying our method to any wheeled rover.

title: Dependence of optical refractive index of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength in near-infrared waveband for warm and shallow sea_supp2-3693439.xls
authors: Shanshan Wang
abstract: Dependence of optical refractive index (RI) of seawater on temperature, salinity, and pressure is crucial for optical fiber sensors operated in seawater. Expressions of RI of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength applicable to near-infrared waveband are developed based on a great deal of RI data measured by a semi-encapsulated microfiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer sensor. To verify the accuracy of these expressions, RI measured by infrared refractometer, theoretical calculated sensitivity and experimental measured sensitivity are used, by which the most accurate expression is determined. Based on this expression, a variable sensitivity matrix is established to demodulate temperature, salinity and pressure simultaneously with errors of 10.91 %, 6.09 % and 18.80 %, respectively. The expression proposed here will provide valuable references for theoretical and experimental study on optical fiber sensors operated in infrared waveband and push the applications of fiber optical sensors in ocean investigations.

title: Inter-Satellite Link Configuration for Fast Delivery in Low-Earth-Orbit Constellations
authors: Arman Mollakhani, Jerayu Tiamraj, Shu-Jie Cao, Dongning Guo
abstract: End-to-end latency in large low-Earth-orbit (LEO) constellations is dominated by propagation delay, making total delay roughly proportional to the network diameter—the longest shortest-path in hops. Current inter-satellite link (ISL) layouts have rarely been optimized to minimize network diameter while simultaneously satisfying physical and operational constraints, including maximum link distance, line-of-sight, per-satellite hardware limits, and longterm link viability over orbital periods. In this study, the selection and assignment of inter-plane ISLs is formulated as a diameterminimization problem on a Starlink-inspired Walker-Delta constellation in which each satellite is equipped with two fixed intra-plane links and may activate up to two inter-plane links. Beginning with a feasible baseline, the topology is iteratively refined by a local-search procedure that replaces or reinforces links to shrink the diameter. The resulting ISL configuration meets all geometric and hardware limits, preserves link stability across multiple orbital periods, and yields a sparse, diameter-aware graph with potential for centralized routing capabilities. Simulations demonstrate the proposed algorithm achieves low worst-case latency without compromising ISL stability, and the trade-off between hop count and long-term link stability is empirically measured for guidance of future LEO network deployments.

title: Quantum Game Coordination Mechanism for Transformer District Prosumers with Grid Constraints
authors: Jingwen Xu, Yi Chen, Qing Luo, Ruichong Li, Jiayue Wang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: High distributed photovoltaic penetration often triggers transformer overloads and voltage violations due to uncoordinated individual arbitrage. To resolve these challenges, this paper proposes a collaborative prosumer trading strategy based on quantum game theory. By mapping the strategy space into the Hilbert domain via quantum entanglement, the mechanism reshapes the classical payoff matrix to incorporate transformer capacity and carbon reduction targets. An integrated entanglement contract is designed to internalize carbon externalities and congestion risks, effectively enforcing low-carbon cooperation through economic penalties. Simulation results confirm that the proposed model achieves a social welfare of 321.84 RMB and a local consumption rate of 76.92%, representing improvements of 36.55% and 13.64% over classical games. These findings demonstrate the model's effectiveness in aligning economic incentives with grid security.

title: Asymmetrical Fault Control Strategy for Distributed Photovoltaics Considering Capacity Constraint
authors: Yuehui Hu, Xiaobin Wang, Zhaoru Han, Fang Shi
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of distributed photovoltaics (PV) in distribution networks, the new national standard of China (GB/T 29319-2024) imposes stricter requirements on Low Voltage Ride Through (LVRT) and dynamic reactive power support. During asymmetrical faults, traditional negative-sequence suppression strategies can cause severe voltage unbalance at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC), potentially leading to protection malfunctions. To address this, this paper proposes a fault ride-through and negative sequence control strategy based on inverter capacity constraint priority. A composite sequence network model of the PV-integrated distribution grid is established to elucidate the regulatory effect of PV output currents on sequence voltages. Subsequently, a multi-stage current allocation rule is formulated: prioritizing positive-sequence reactive current to comply with LVRT codes, utilizing the remaining capacity to inject negative-sequence current for mitigating grid unbalance, and dynamically curtailing active current when capacity is limited. Simulation results show that the proposed strategy adaptively allocates current under various voltage dip depths, effectively reducing the negative-sequence voltage amplitude at the PCC (by approximately 19%).

title: Research on Electric Shock Diagnosis under Insulation Defects Based on Bi-GRU-ATT
authors: Renliang Liu, Yao Liu, Zhiheng Wu, Fang Guo, Hangye Cai
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Electric shock hazards caused by insulation defects pose a serious threat to the safe operation of low-voltage direct current (LVDC) distribution systems. To address this issue, this study designs a laboratory-scale experimental platform to simulate human electric shock processes under different insulation defect levels. Pork tissue is employed as a surrogate for human biological tissue, while adjustable resistors are used to emulate various insulation degradation conditions. Residual current signals flowing through the simulated human body are collected and analyzed. A bidirectional gated recurrent unit network with an attention mechanism (Bi-GRU-ATT) is proposed to extract temporal features from the residual current signals and to classify electric shock states under different insulation conditions. Based on the diagnostic performance corresponding to different residual current intervals, a segmented protection strategy is further proposed to enable graded responses to electric shock risks. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high diagnostic accuracy and provides effective technical support for intelligent and reliable electric shock protection in LVDC systems.

title: A Multi-Agent Cognitive Isolation Framework for Defect-Driven Power Grid Document Assessment
authors: Jingjing Yang, Zhiqi Ao, Haowen Ren, Haibo Zhu, Lifu Ding, Minghao Wang, Xuejun Jiang, Qinmin Yang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Automated Document Quality Assessment (DQA) in the power system domain struggles with the "black-box" nature of traditional deep learning and the challenges of LLM-based few-shot scoring, including role confusion and stochastic drift during cold starts. To address these issues in smart grid operations, we propose CREDIT, an interpretable Defect-Driven Few-Shot Rule Induction framework via Multi-Agent Collaboration. CREDIT achieves cognitive isolation by decoupling rule generation, scoring, and diagnosis across independent agents. It introduces a Spearman correlation-guided cold-start strategy to rapidly identify the optimal optimization direction. Rather than relying on unguided reflection, the framework utilizes "abnormal relative samples" as explicit negative feedback, driving dynamic rule iteration via Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Extensive experiments on structured power grid business documents validate the framework’s effectiveness, achieving a 0.835 weighted F1-score using a small-scale sample set. Crucially, to address strict data privacy constraints in high-stakes power dispatch and compliance scenarios, we demonstrate the framework’s lightweight deployment feasibility. By executing the entire pipeline locally on a lightweight 8B model within resource-constrained edge environments, the explicitly induced rules guarantee high scoring stability and robust defect-capturing capabilities. CREDIT establishes a data-efficient and highly interpretable scoring paradigm for power grid assessment scenarios.

title: An Advanced Predictive Control Strategy for Current Harmonic Suppression in Three-Phase Three-Level Vienna Rectifier
authors: Tawfique Uzzaman, Tyrone Fernando, Herbert HoChing Iu, Xinan Zhang
abstract: The three-phase three-level Vienna rectifier (3L-VR) is widely adopted for AC/DC conversion. Nonetheless, high input current distortion and neutral point voltage (NPV) imbalance remain significant challenges. This article proposes a novel sector-optimization-based predictive control, combining finite control set virtual-voltage-vector (FS-V3-MPC)-based predictive current control with an outer loop linear quadratic regulator (LQR) for voltage regulation. FS-V3-MPC improves current tracking via synthesized virtual vectors, while the improved sector optimization minimizes computational burden. The LQR guarantees optimal DC-link voltage regulation and NPV control with robust disturbance rejection. Compared with state-of-the-art methods, the proposed approach achieves up to a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf{38}\boldsymbol{\%}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> reduction in input current THD with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf{74}\boldsymbol{\%}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> faster dynamic response. Extensive simulations and experimental results validate the effectiveness and practical applicability of the proposed approach.

title: Novel Nanocrystalline Alloy Symmetrical Magnetic Focused Eddy Current Array Sensing for Microcrack Detection
authors: Xumei Yang, Qiuping Ma, Bin Gao, Rui Chen, Jiaqi Zhou, Chen Zhu, Yukuan Kang
abstract: Nondestructive detection and characterization of micronear-surface cracks is a critical and challenging task for evaluating material performance. However, existing methods are limited by low sensitivity, poor anti-interference capability, and bad adaptability to complex surfaces. This article proposes a novel eddy current array microcrack detection method based on a symmetrical magnetic focused sensing structure. In this design, a symmetrically distributed magnetic path generates a focused and uniform eddy current region of interest (ROI), significantly enhancing the field intensity for high-sensitivity sensing. This advancement improves detection sensitivity and anti-interference capability for microcracks. Theoretical derivations and simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. In particular, experimental tests conducted on various samples with microcracks demonstrate the reliability and applicability of the approach. The proposed method is capable of detecting artificial crack defects with a width and depth of 0.2 mm at a liftoff of 4 mm, as well as natural weld cracks with minimum detectable dimensions of approximately 5 × 0.07 × 0.07 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. Furthermore, the array design can cover the entire convex weld region with a width of 10 mm, enabling single scan imaging of multioriented cracks with dimensions of 10 × 0.1 × 1 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. It is applicable to the detection of surface and near-surface cracks in both ferromagnetic and nonferromagnetic materials.

title: Instrument-embedded sampling oscilloscope for in-situ waveform characterization and its implementation in the Ocean Color Instrument of the NASA PACE mission
authors: Kim S. Jepsen, Ulrik Gliese, Omar Haddad, Matt Owens, Steve Marlow, Bradley Tse, Leland Chemerys, Gerhard Meister, Robert H. Estep, Jeremy Werdell
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: We propose and demonstrate the use of an embedded sampling oscilloscope technique for characterizing and troubleshooting certain broadly used sampled measurement systems. Good examples of these are imaging and lidar sensors where a repetitive waveform carries the output information from the system. The method makes it possible to perform in-situ, remote troubleshooting with a clarity that is difficult to obtain by other methods, and is well suited for use in, e.g., satellite-based observation platforms. The implementation is based solely on a simple and switchable firmware core, making the method inherently low Size, Weight, Power and Cost (SWaP-C). The embedded sampling oscilloscope has been implemented in the firmware of the Ocean Color Instrument (OCI) on the NASA Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, ocean Ecosystem (PACE) mission. We demonstrate its use in both pre-launch ground testing and post-launch, on-orbit characterization.

title: Data Distributional Attention Based Heterogeneous Representation Learning for PolSAR Land Cover Classification
authors: Jianlong Wang, Bingjie Zhang, Wenke Wang, Meijuan Yang, Siyun Hou, Junding Sun
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) data is typically represented as a complex matrix, and different polarization decomposition parameters result in varying data distributions. Directly applying deep learning methods to extract features from PolSAR data, without accounting for the impact of these distributional differences, leads to incomplete representations. To address this issue, a data distributional attention based heterogeneous representation learning method is proposed for PolSAR land cover classification. This method first analyzes the distribution patterns followed by different polarization decomposition parameters. It combines the eigenvector polarization decomposition method to obtain parameter vectors that follow the Gamma distribution and those that follow the Gaussian distribution. To extract features from the two distinct distribution forms, a distributional convolutional autoencoder representation learning framework is proposed. By leveraging the information entropy of different data distributions, data distribution attention can incorporate distribution characteristics into representation learning features. Secondly, a heterogeneous feature fusion strategy enables simple yet effective fuse of feature representations with different distributional characteristics. This strategy employs a multi-head self-attention mechanism to assign weights to features from different distributions, thereby filtering out those that are beneficial for classification. Moreover, it allows for the further extraction of global information from PolSAR data. Finally, to make feature fusion architecture better adapt to each stage of optimization, an H-CE loss function for combinatorial optimization is utilized. The experimental results on four standard datasets show that the proposed method has better classification results compared with the existing methods. The source code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/Bigice.Zhang/DCAE-Code/tree/main</uri>.

title: Optimal Droop Control Strategy for Coordinated Voltage Regulation and Power Sharing in Hybrid AC-MTDC Systems
authors: Hongjin Du, Tuanku Badzlin Hashfi, Rashmi Prasad, Pedro P. Vergara, Peter Palensky, Aleksandra Lekić
abstract: With the growing integration of Modular Multilevel Converters (MMCs) in Multi-Terminal Direct Current (MTDC) transmission systems, there is an increasing need for control strategies that ensure both economic efficiency and robust dynamic performance. This paper presents an enhanced Optimal Power Flow (OPF)-based framework for hybrid AC-MTDC systems, integrating a novel droop control strategy that coordinates DC voltage and AC frequency regulation. By embedding frequency control loops into the MMCs, the method enables system-wide coordination, enhancing power sharing and improving system resilience under disturbances. The proposed strategy dynamically adjusts converter operating points to minimize generation costs and DC-voltage deviations, thereby balancing economic objectives with system stability. A modified Nordic test system integrated with a four-terminal MTDC grid is used to validate the approach. Optimization is performed using Julia, while the system’s dynamic performance is evaluated through electromagnetic transient simulations with the EMTP software. Case studies across multiple scenarios demonstrate that the proposed droop control achieves significantly improved frequency and voltage robustness compared to active power control, while maintaining a lower generation cost than the adaptive droop benchmark. The results highlight the ability of the proposed strategy to deliver cost-effective operation without compromising performance, offering a promising solution for the coordinated control of future hybrid AC-DC transmission networks.

title: Consensus Adjustment Mechanism Based on Semisupervised Clustering and Cross Allocation for&#x000A0;Large-Scale Social Network Group Decision&#x000A0;Making_supp1-3686760.pdf
authors: Yanjing Guo
abstract: Consensus adjustment is essential in group decision-making (GDM) to reduce the judgment bias of decision-makers (DMs). Advances in communication technology enable greater online participation, foster complex social networks, and provide more diverse evaluation information. However, few existing studies on large-scale social network-based GDM explored consensus adjustment from the perspective of DM interactions. To address this gap, this article proposes a novel large-scale social network GDM method that incorporates consensus adjustment considering the interactions among DMs and subgroups. First, a two-stage semisupervised fuzzy C-means clustering method based on information fusion is introduced to handle the complexity of social networks. Then, to deal with the issue of inconsistent minimum adjustment amounts between individuals, subgroups, and the overall group, a two-stage cross-allocation approach is proposed, which considers the favorable allocation preferences of all DMs under the overall minimum adjustments. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through a case study that demonstrates its practical applicability and performance in achieving consensus in complex social network environments.

title: Resource Allocation in ISTN via Learning and Optimization over Riemannian Manifolds
authors: Joarder Jafor Sadique, Ahmed S. Ibrahim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Dynamic time–frequency resource allocation in integrated satellite–terrestrial networks (ISTNs) with fast-moving LEO satellites remains challenging. We aim to maximize delivered multicommodity throughput by allocating time–frequency resources across both inter-satellite links (ISLs) and satellite–UE access links. The network in each slot is represented as a time-expanded graph (TEG), whose regularized Laplacian is a symmetric positive-definite (SPD) matrix. Mapping this SPD matrix on a Riemannian manifold allows us quantify topology changes induced by allocation via manifold distances. Building on this geometry, we develop two alternative low-complexity controllers within a software-defined networking framework: (i) a Riemannian Thompson-Sampling multi-armed bandit (TS-MAB) that uses manifold distance as the reward to select satellites per slot, and (ii) a Riemannian Ant Colony Optimization (RACO) scheme that performs pheromone-guided search using the same geometric signal. Throughput is computed by solving a standard multicommodity max-flow on the resulting TEG. Simulation results show that both methods consistently raise throughput and track the exhaustive-search upper bound closely, while maintaining practical runtimes.

title: UAV-Based Through-the-Wall Radar Sensing for 3D Urban Building Layout Reconstruction Using an Enhanced U-Net
authors: Shichao Zhong, Zhongjie Ma, Xiaolu Zeng, Renjie Liu, Xiaopeng Yang
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) building layout sensing is a key capability for Internet of Things (IoT)–enabled smart city applications, including post-disaster assessment and urban security monitoring. However, acquiring reliable 3D building layouts from an external perspective remains challenging in complex urban environments due to severe signal attenuation and multipath effects. This paper proposes an IoT-enabled unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)–based through-the-wall radar (TWR) sensing framework for large-scale 3D building layout reconstruction. In the proposed framework, UAV-mounted radar sensors act as mobile IoT sensing nodes to collect multi-view sensing data. A multi-layer wall echo propagation model and an angle-weighted three-dimensional back-projection (BP) imaging algorithm are employed to generate multi-view 3D synthetic aperture radar (SAR) representations. An enhanced U-Net architecture is then developed to fuse the multi-view SAR data and reconstruct clearer 3D building layout representations. The simulation and real-world experimental results show that the proposed framework achieves improved reconstruction performance over representative existing methods under the tested conditions, indicating its potential for IoT-oriented smart city sensing.

title: Robust and Energy-efficient Multi-UAV Trajectory Planning for Data Collection: A Game-theoretic and Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Nan Qi, Hua Jiang, Sa Xiao, Daolong Wu, Fuhui Zhou, Chunguo Li, Shi Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: In an open electromagnetic environment, multi-unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) communications may suffer from intermittent data transmissions, incomplete information on jammers and geographical obstacles. This deteriorates the UAV-ground and UAV-UAV wireless communications, potentially leading to physical collisions and posing significant safety risks. While existing studies rarely account for intermittent UAV connectivity and the associated communication costs, this paper proposes an effective cooperative approach utilizing grid map exploration and experience sharing. Specifically, game-theoretic methods are employed to facilitate distributed cooperative information exchange. Although each UAV seeks to maximize its individual utility, the proposed mechanism incentivizes cooperation to achieve the collective mission. To mitigate collaboration interruptions caused by intermittent transmission, we propose an opportunistic cooperative reinforcement learning framework combined with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based predictive learning, which explicitly accounts for the dynamic communication costs of UAVs. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that our algorithm significantly outperforms existing non-cooperative methods, with a 31% improvement in converged reward compared to the non-cooperative baseline. Furthermore, it exhibits superior stability and data collection efficiency compared to established multi-agent baselines (e.g., MADDPG, MAPPO). Particularly, the system’s performance robustness regarding the LSTM prediction accuracy is rigorously evaluated, confirming its resilience against intermittent communication.

title: Self-training Based Source-free Domain Adaptation for Remote Sensing Scene Classification_supp1-3695530.pdf
authors: Xu Tang
abstract: Remote sensing scene classification (RSSC) is crucial for interpreting remote sensing (RS) images across various applications. Existing deep learning methods have promoted RSSC results to a new level. However, most of them rely on an assumption that training and test data are independently and identically distributed. While this requirement can be readily met under laboratory conditions, it proves to be exceptionally stringent in practical settings, as the collected new RS images may differ significantly in distribution from the RS images used to train the classification model. In this cross-domain case, many successful models would be disabled. To address the above challenges and enhance the practicability of the RSSC model, this paper proposes a self-training based source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) method named STSFDA. First, a multi-task source domain pre-training strategy is developed to provide a strong initialization for the SFDA process. Next, a teacher-student network is adopted to drive the SFDA process. In the teacher network, target RS scenes are divided into two groups based on confidence and then passed to the student network. The student network applies supervised learning to reliable samples and unsupervised learning to uncertain samples. A neighbor-guided pseudo-labeling strategy is also introduced to generate more reliable pseudo-labels with the consideration of neighborhood information. In addition, mixup learning and contrastive learning are applied to all target domain samples to obtain more discriminative feature representations. Experimental results demonstrate that STSFDA consistently outperforms many existing baseline models, highlighting its effectiveness and potential for future research in the RS community. Code is available at https://github.com/TangXu-Group/STSFDA.

title: COMBINER: Composed Image Retrieval Guided by Attribute-based Neighbor Relations
authors: Zixu Li, Yupeng Hu, Zhiwei Chen, Haokun Wen, Xuemeng Song, Liqiang Nie
abstract: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) represents a challenging retrieval task that targets locating specific images through multimodal inputs. Despite recent progress in CIR techniques, prior approaches often overlook cases where images appear visually alike yet differ in attributes, potentially undermining both multimodal feature fusion and similarity modeling. To mitigate this limitation, we design a unified representation of cross-modal features based on attribute prototypes. Nevertheless, the task is far from straightforward, owing to three core issues: (1) entangle ment in attribute-level semantics, (2) inconsistency across modalities, and (3) supervised signal missing. To tackle the above obstacles, we introduce a COMposed image retrieval network guided By attrIbute-based NEighbor Relations (COMBINER). Specifically, we first design an Adaptive Semantic Disentanglement module, which is capable of disentangling attribute features based on multimodal primitive features. Secondly, we propose a Unified Prototype-based Composition module, which can construct cross-modal unified prototypes (CUP) and facilitate multimodal feature composition. Finally, we introduce a Dual Relations Modeling module, which can mine pairwise and neighbor relations based on attribute similarity. Compared to traditional neighbor relations modeling CIR methods, COMBINER represents the first study addressing the phenomenon of visually similar but attribute unrelated samples. It achieves a more accurate understanding of the semantic relations among samples by employing an attribute prototype-based similarity metric. Comprehensive experiments conducted on three benchmark datasets confirm the effectiveness of our proposed COMBINER. The implementation of our method will be accessed at https://github.com/Lee-zixu/COMBINER.

title: Limitations of Nyquist Criteria in the Discretization of 2D Electromagnetic Integral Equations at High Frequency: Spectral Insights into Pollution Effects
authors: Viviana Giunzioni, Adrien Merlini, Francesco P. Andriulli
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Electromagnetic integral equations are typically solved numerically using boundary element methods (BEMs). When the frequency of the wave phenomenon under study increases, the discretization of the problem is typically chosen to maintain a fixed number of unknowns per wavelength. Under these conditions, the BEM over finite-dimensional subspaces of piecewise polynomial basis functions is commonly believed to provide a bounded solution accuracy, at least for non-trapping geometries. If proven, this would constitute a significant advantage of the BEM with respect to finite element and finite difference time domain methods, which, in contrast, are affected by numerical dispersion, that causes the number of unknowns per wavelength required to achieve a prescribed solution accuracy to increase with frequency, phenomenon known as pollution. In this work, we conduct a rigorous spectral analysis of some of the most commonly used boundary integral operators and examine the impact of the BEM discretization on the solution accuracy of widely used integral equations modeling the electromagnetic scattering from a perfectly electrically conducting cylinder. We consider both ill-conditioned and well-conditioned equations, the latter being characterized by solution operators bounded independently of frequency. Contrary to the common belief, our analysis reveals a form of pollution that affects, in different measures, equations of both kinds. After elucidating the mechanism by which the BEM discretization impacts accuracy, we propose a solution strategy that can cure the pollution problem thus evidenced. The defining strength of the proposed theoretical model lies in its capacity to deliver deep insight into the root causes of the phenomenon.

title: Distributed Triangulation Filter for Target Motion Estimation via Bearing-Only Measurements
authors: Yuhan Liu, Pei Chi, Jiang Zhao, Song Jiang, Luning Yang, Xuyang Gao, Yingxun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper introduces the novel consensus-based distributed spatial-temporal coordinated triangulation filter (CD-STCTF), a unified framework designed to overcome the fundamental challenge of achieving reliable and high-precision motion estimation of non-cooperative targets using bearing-only measurements in multi-agent systems. First, our approach centers on the spatial-temporal coordinated triangulation (STCT) model, which optimally integrates historical and spatial bearing information to transform the nonlinear problem into a robust, pseudo-linear state estimation problem. Second, this powerful geometric linearization is embedded within a consensus-on-information-matrix distributed architecture, ensuring robust operation and network consistency across multi-agent systems with sparse communication topologies. Third, a key technical feature is the incorporation of a dynamic measurement noise covariance matrix, which correctly models the distance-dependent uncertainty in angular measurements. Furthermore, we prove the global bounded stability of the CD-STCTF dynamic error. Simulation and real-world experimental results against existing distributed algorithms and the centralized Kalman filter (CKF) benchmark demonstrate that the CD-STCTF achieves superior estimation accuracy, faster convergence rates, and enhanced robustness and is comparable to the CKF, establishing its efficacy for high-performance cooperative bearing-only target motion estimation.

title: Intelligent Defect Detection and Identification for Additive Manufacturing Systems
authors: John S Poindexter, Seong Hyeon Hong
abstract: Additive manufacturing (AM) enables rapid prototyping and precision fabrication but faces persistent challenges from defect formation, particularly in high-reliability applications. Defect detection in AM remains challenging, particularly across new geometries and defect types, where existing methods often require extensive labeled data or manual tuning. This work introduces a semi-supervised framework based on a convolutional Variational Autoencoder (VAE) trained exclusively on non-defective samples. During training, the VAE learns a compressed latent representation of normal part structures, which provides a statistical baseline for comparison. At evaluation, encoded mean vectors are analyzed using a global Mahalanobis distance measure, which accounts for correlations across latent features and quantifies deviations from the non-defect distribution. Unlike reconstruction-error metrics, which often show overlap between subtle defects and normal samples, this latent-space distance offers a more robust and generalizable decision rule. The framework was evaluated on datasets of 3D-printed rectangles, cylinders, hourglasses, and pyramids, including both familiar and previously unseen geometries. Results show that non-defective embeddings form tightly clustered distributions, while defective parts consistently deviate outward, enabling reliable thresholding. The method achieved an average classification accuracy of 93% with F1 scores up to 0.95, balancing recall and precision while prioritizing detection of subtle anomalies. Visualizations using PCA and t-SNE confirmed that the latent space organizes shapes meaningfully and highlights separability between non-defects and defects. By reducing dependence on labeled defect datasets and applying a single global threshold across geometries, this framework demonstrates a scalable, dataefficient approach to quality assurance. It further provides a foundation for integration into real-time inspection pipelines for intelligent manufacturing.

title: MSA-ENet: Multi-Scale Attention Enhanced Network for Kidney Stone Detection in Ultrasound Images
authors: Kedharnadh Kamatham, Sanjana Reddy Sangam, Vaidhehi M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: MSA-ENet, a deep learning model for kidney stone detection in ultrasound images, is presented in the proposed work. The model combines ensemble learning, dual attention techniques, and multi-scale feature extraction to achieve good classification performance. It can identify stones of all sizes since it is based on EfficientNet-B0, which eliminates hierarchical feature mappings at various spatial resolutions. These maps are processed by a Multi-Scale Feature Extraction (MSFE) module which contains parallel branches and captures fine details, local patterns, and larger context. The dual attention mechanism which is channel and spatial attention pays attention to the channels of interest and spatial locations in order to detect better the features. Furthermore, MSA-ENet uses a combination of three classifiers, which boosts the display of robustness and generalization. A focal loss, which is a composite loss function, that includes cross-entropy, label smoothing, and class imbalance is used to correct the imbalance of classes and overconfidence. The model is trained on two datasets: Kidney Stone Ultrasound Dataset of Roboflow and Kidney Stone Classification and Object Detection Dataset of Kaggle and provides an accuracy of 98.25 and 98.94, respectively, with the ROC-AUC of 0.9935 and 0.9999.
