MSA-ENet showed a high level of accuracy at 94% when trained on external data and this indicates that it is generalizable. The model is more accurate, stronger and more generalized when compared to traditional CNN methods. Grad-CAM also contributes to the interpretability, which renders MSA-ENet applicable to clinical use. The study presents the possibilities of multi-scale, attention-based architectures in the analysis of medical images to improve the identification of kidney stones.

title: ARGOS: Leveraging Visual Priors for Scalable Wireless Navigation in Dynamic Environments
authors: Arko Datta, Tharaneeshwaran V U, Aravindh Sriram Kumar A G, Ayon Chakraborty
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Performance of wireless navigation systems degrade sharply in industrial environments dominated by metallic clutter and heavy multipath. The primary cause lies in how anchors are placed or selected. Methods that succeed in open spaces fail under severe non-line-of-sight (nLoS) and frequent layout changes, for instance, induced by moving forklifts or shifting shelves. We introduce Argos, a multimodal wireless digital twin that fuses visual and RF information to optimize anchor selection. Visual imagery reconstructs the 3D layout, updated continuously via existing surveillance cameras, while RF measurements capture material-specific attenuation and reflections. We show that layout priors alone are insufficient; combining them with the required RF optics yields a material-aware channel model that predicts range errors under severe nLoS. Argos adapts proactively to environmental changes, without the requirement of repeated RF calibration or retraining, sustaining sub-meter localization accuracy in dynamic scenes. We validate Argos in a 120m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> factory testbed spanning over 700 locations, where the digital twin is built from 5K RGB images and 0.4M UWB CIR samples. To our knowledge, this is the first system to exploit visual priors for adaptive orchestration of wireless navigation infrastructure.

title: Accurate Prediction of Dynamic Line Rating for Overhead Transmission Lines Based on GWO-VMD-GRU
authors: Tao Wang, Hongyun Xie, Lei Wang, Junwei Yao
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Precisely determining the dynamic current-carrying capacity is crucial for the safe capacity increase of overhead transmission lines. To mitigate the limitations of conventional predictive models, which are contingent upon manual expertise for hyperparameter selection and struggle to effectively dampen the pronounced volatility inherent in DLR data, a novel predictive methodology termed GWO-VMD-GRU has been introduced. This methodology integrates the Gray Wolf Optimization (GWO) algorithm, Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD), and the Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) model to enhance prediction accuracy. The present study employs a methodology that leverages the global optimization capabilities of the GWO algorithm to ascertain the optimal hyperparameters for VMD. Subsequent to this optimization process, the DLR data are decomposed into several stationary components via VMD. Following decomposition, each resultant component is individually modeled and its future behavior is predicted using a GRU neural network. Finally, the global predicted value is obtained by superimposing the component results. Investigations were carried out utilizing empirical data obtained from a 110 kV aerial transmission line situated in Guangzhou. The findings indicate that the predictive accuracy of the novel approach exceeds that of the conventional model by a margin of at least 5.03%. Furthermore, the model consistently outperforms all benchmarks in key metrics such as RMSE and MAPE, offering a robust framework for the secure and increased utilization of overhead lines.

title: AcouBLE: Clock-Seeding BLE chips via Acoustic Signals for Extended Energy Efficiency
authors: Mohammad Rostami, Gentian Jakllari
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: We present AcouBLE, the first system to significantly reduce the BLE tag’s RF front-end energy consumption while preserving standard BLE operation, backward compatibility, and one-to-one deployability. At its core is clock-seeding, a new paradigm that offloads the generation of the high-frequency MHz clock from the energy-constrained tag to the central (smartphone). The smartphone transmits a low-frequency audio signal that the tag transforms into a clean, ultra-low-jitter reference clock–eliminating the most energy-expensive component of a BLE connection event.Our design combines a novel digital-analog upconversion pipeline that converts a kHz audio tone into a stable 32 MHz clock with a lightweight feedback protocol to implement clock-seeding on commodity BLE chips without modifying the BLE stack or requiring privileged access on the central. A full prototype demonstrates up to 5.9X energy savings in Connected mode, while maintaining RF performance, seamless deployability, and backward compatibility.

title: AI-Enabled Automated Transcription and Clinical Data Extraction for NHS 999 Call Handling Using Wav2Vec 2.0 and Clinical BERT Models
authors: Zain Ul Abideen Ayaz
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: NHS ambulance emergency call handlers work under high pressure and are under considerable cognitive pressure. They often have to multitask, like communicate with the patient effectively, gather all necessary clinical and personal information, perform instant clinical triage while simultaneously entering the details into their respective clinical information systems. This study proposes an AI-enabled transcription and decision-support pipeline, which is designed to automate the transcription of emergency 999 calls. This paper aims to enable the NHS emergency call handlers to focus more on patient interaction and clinical assessment. This paper uses an automatic speech recognition (ASR) using a fine-tuned Wav2Vec 2.0 architecture, which is combined with BERT-based natural language understanding (NLU) models for structured data mapping and entity extraction as per NHS protocols. Model development and evaluation will use publicly available datasets from NHS Digital England, specifically the Emergency Care Data Set (ECDS) for structured clinical coding patterns and the Ambulance Data Set (ADS) for call-related operational descriptors. These datasets will be used to develop domain-specific vocabularies, train clinical entity-extraction components, and validate the mapping of free-text ASR outputs to standardized fields. ECDS and ADS were used to define the coding framework, while the ASR model was trained on synthetic emergency call audio designed to reflect key features of 999 calls. The proposed approach aims to reduce administrative load on the NHS staff, improve data input accuracy, and reduce triaging time in emergency calls. By combining AI/ML techniques with existing NHS practices, this study outlines the integration of AI in healthcare and presents a pathway towards safer, more efficient emergency call handling.

title: Towards an AI-Powered Threat Modeling Framework for Securing Robotic Pharmaceutical Laboratories
authors: Dhruhi Patel, Gahangir Hossain
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Pharmaceutical research laboratories increasingly rely on robotics, IoT-enabled instruments, cloud platforms, and AI-driven analytics to accelerate drug discovery. While these technologies enhance efficiency and scalability, they also introduce complex cybersecurity risks, including experimental manipulation, robotic interference, and intellectual property theft. Traditional threat-modeling frameworks such as STRIDE and NIST provide baseline security guidance but are inadequate for addressing AI-driven attacks, insider threats, and cyber-physical vulnerabilities in automated laboratories. This study proposes a hybrid AI-powered threat-modeling framework that integrates STRIDE, Zero Trust architecture, and AI-based anomaly detection to strengthen laboratory security. Through literature analysis, case studies, and prompt-engineering-based attack simulations, the framework demonstrates improved detection of subtle anomalies, including robotic motion deviations, assay interference, and insider misuse. The results show higher threat identification accuracy compared to classical models, offering a holistic and modern approach to securing AI-enabled pharmaceutical laboratories.

title: Commercial Orbit Transfer Services to Facilitate Lunar and Martian Exploration
authors: Matt Costello
abstract: Lunar and Martian missions require significant propulsive capability to reach operational orbits. Orbital transfer vehicles (OTVs) offer a cost-effective, efficient, and flexible solution compared to traditional launch services. Impulse Space conducted detailed trajectory and performance analyses with two OTVs: Mira, a highly propulsive spacecraft bus, and Helios, a high-energy kick stage. Two design reference missions were evaluated for each vehicle as part of a study for the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Launch Service Program (LSP) with the goal of demonstrating the current and near-term utility of OTVs to inform the development of a commercial approach to deliver payloads to exo-geosynchronous (xGEO) orbits that are not easily served by traditional launch services. The OTV capability would be procured through the VADR (Venture-Class Acquisition of Dedicated and Rideshare) launch services contract. For Mira, payload mass performance was evaluated to LLO (Low Lunar Orbit) and an ELFO (Elliptical Lunar Frozen Orbit). Results show that Mira is able to deliver 100 kg of payload to LLO through the completion of a Lunar Orbit Insertion (LOI) burn and an orbit reduction maneuver. For the ELFO mission, Mira performs a midcourse targeting maneuver to achieve the desired approach geometry for direct insertion into the ELFO, delivering 48 kg of payload mass. For many missions, it may be more effective to use Mira not only as the transfer vehicle but also as the long-term hosting platform, leveraging its robust payload support capabilities while eliminating the need for separate deployers. The design reference missions (DRMs) employed relatively direct lunar transfer orbits. Alternative approaches, such as ballistic lunar transfers, may further increase payload performance but were not covered in this study. For Helios, a heliocentric and LLO DRM were evaluated. Results from the heliocentric orbit DRM demonstrate Helios' significant utility in augmenting the performance of medium LVs to provide more access to higher energy escape orbits. The Helios LLO mission requires upgrades over the current version of the OTV in the form of onboard power generation and improvements to the thermal management of cryogenic propellants. The evaluation of the Helios LLO DRM factors in these potential upgrades and the effect that boiloff has on propellant mass during the transfer. The <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2,800 ~\text{kg}$</tex> of payload mass deliverable by Helios to LLO creates significant opportunities for future lunar missions including both orbiters and landers. These results highlight the current and near-term utility of commercial OTVs for lunar and deep-space missions, enabling new methods of payload delivery and mission architectures to the Moon, Mars, and beyond.

title: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
authors: N/A

title: Electricity Spot Market Price Risk Evaluation Model Considering the Regulation Capacity of BS Transceivers and Backup Energy Storage
authors: Hongji Yang, Qiaofeng Wu, Zhaoyang Wu, Jixiao Chen, Yiying Wu, Chao Guo
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the rapid development of renewable energy sources, their variability and randomness pose significant challenges to the stability and security of the power systems. Virtual Power Plants (VPPs) have become an effective solution by integrating and dispatching regulatory resources. As important infrastructure, Base Stations (BSs) have gradually demonstrated the value of being aggregated into VPPs to participate in the electricity market. Although BS transceivers and backup energy storage are considerable regulatory resources, their effect on stabilizing electricity price risk is not clear. Consequently, an electricity spot market price risk evaluation model is established to analyze the effect of BSs. Firstly, the constraints of BS transceivers and backup energy storage are established considering their operational characteristics. Subsequently, an electricity spot market clearing model that incorporates BS aggregators is developed to obtain the market prices. Furthermore, a capacity evaluation method based on equivalent energy storage capacity is proposed. Finally, a modified IEEE 9-bus system is used to evaluate the effectiveness of BSs in moderating electricity price fluctuations.

title: TG-CausalX: A Threshold-Gated Causal AI Framework of Explainable and Autonomous GPON Network Optimization
authors: Sreenivasa K., C Fancy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Modern broadband networks rely heavily on Gigabit Passive Optical Networks (GPON), which offer high-speed connectivity but are complex to manage due to multi-layer dependencies, bursty traffic patterns, and rapid state changes. Faults in such environments rarely occur in isolation; a single issue, such as optical signal degradation, can quickly impact higherlayer metrics like latency and Quality of Experience (QoE). Traditional approaches based on static thresholds or black-box models often fail to accurately identify root causes or support real-time decision-making. This paper presents TG-CausalX, a Threshold-Gated Causal AI framework designed for explainable and autonomous GPON network management. The system combines anomaly detection using Isolation Forest and classification via XGBoost, with a gating mechanism that activates causal reasoning only during abnormal conditions. A LiNGAM-based causal model constructs a directed acyclic graph to identify root causes, while Five-Why analysis and do-calculus enable interpretable and safe corrective actions. Experimental results demonstrate 90 % diagnostic accuracy, 92 % remediation success, and low-latency operation (14.3 ms), highlighting the effectiveness of TG-CausalX for intelligent network optimization.

title: A Framework for Scenario Generation, Training, and Evaluation of Neuro-Symbolic AI for Autonomy
authors: Hambisa Keno, Nicholas J. Pioch, Christopher Guagliano, Timothy H. Chung, Gautam Vallabha, Chace Ashcraft
abstract: Application of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) in search and rescue, emergency management, and law enforcement has gained traction with the advent of low-cost platforms and sensor payloads. The emergence of hybrid neural and symbolic AI approaches for complex reasoning is expected to further push the boundaries of these applications with decreasing levels of human intervention. However, current UAV simulation environments lack semantic context suited to this hybrid approach. To address this gap, HAMERITT (Hybrid Ai Mission Environment for RapId Training and Testing) provides a simulation-based autonomy software framework that supports the training, testing and assurance of neuro-symbolic algorithms for autonomous maneuver and perception reasoning. Developed under DARPA's Assured Neuro-Symbolic Reasoning and Learning (ANSR) program, HAMERITT includes scenario generation capabilities that offer mission-relevant contextual symbolic information that complement raw UAV sensor data feeds. Scenarios for area search and moving target pursuit include symbolic descriptions for entities of interest and their relations to scene elements, as well as spatiotemporal constraints in the form of restricted zones and time-bounded areas of interest with prior probabilities within those areas. HAMERITT also features support for training distinct algorithm threads for UAV maneuver vs. perception within an end-to-end mission. Most recent work includes improving scenario realism and scaling symbolic context generation through automated workflow. In a comprehensive evaluation using HAMERITT as the training and test environment, performance benchmarks were established for a neural-only baseline autonomy solution and top neuro-symbolic AI solutions significantly outperformed the baseline.

title: Smart Car Parking Management System Using Internet-of-Things Approach
authors: Osama S. Alghamdi, Muath A. Almeghaiwli, Abdulmalik A. Alnami, Ahmad A. Jiman
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The rising number of vehicles in Saudi Arabia, particularly in major cities like Riyadh and Jeddah, has intensified parking shortages, forcing drivers to spend more time searching for spaces and contributing to traffic congestion, fuel consumption, and pollution. This study presents a Smart Car Parking Management System (SCPMS) that leverages Internet-of-Things (IoT) technology to provide real-time monitoring of parking availability, securing parking reservations with assisted navigation to reserved parking spaces and tracking parking duration. The implemented system integrates microcontrollers, ultrasonic detection sensors, custom designed parking barriers, and a real-time database developed with a user-friendly mobile application. The total cost of implementing the SCPMS on six parking spaces was around SR 1800 (480). The modular design of the SCPMS offers practical scalability of the system to cover large parking structures. Validation experiments on the system demonstrated the detection accuracy of the sensors (95.84%), the fast activation of parking barriers (within 4 sec), and the reliable accuracy of the system in tracking parking duration (98.63″). Further work is needed to optimize the SCPMS design and test the scalability of the system in more diverse indoor and outdoor parking structures and conditions. Overall, this study presents a practical, cost-effective, reliable and scalable solution to improve parking efficiency, reduce driver frustration, and support sustainable smart city initiatives.

title: Smart Fall Risk Detection using Machine Learning
authors: D Vaishali, Akash Barath VS, R Mirsha, K Sri Krishna
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Falls are a major health danger, especially for older people and people who have trouble moving around. They can cause serious injuries and higher healthcare expenditures. This work introduces a Smart Fall Prediction System utilizing Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) methodologies, created and assessed with the accompanying dataset of wearable sensor inputs. The collection comprises time-series data obtained from inertial sensors, including accelerometers and gyroscopes, during several Activities of Daily Living (ADL) and simulated fall incidents. The suggested system includes preprocessing signals by filtering out noise and breaking them up into sliding windows. Then, it extracts features from both the time and frequency domains. We use classical ML models like Random Forest and Support Vector Machines as baseline classifiers.

title: Progress Towards a Near-Unity Fill Factor FIR Thermopile Detector for Space-Based Remote Sensing
authors: Ricardo Braga Nogueira Branco, Byeong H. Eom, Brian Pepper, Matthew E. Kenyon
abstract: High-priority science can be accomplished by using thermal detectors for thermal imaging and spectroscopy of planets, satellites, and primitive bodies throughout the Solar System. Thermopile detectors are uncooled thermal detectors that are reliable and can be packaged within a small instrument for use in cubesats, smallsats, and rovers. We demonstrate in this work the latest advancements in JPL's thermopile detector technology. Thermopile detectors do not currently have the large format sizes currently available in competing technologies such as bolometers, and do not have the design architecture optimized for far-infrared (FIR) remote sensing. The nextgeneration thermopile detector being developed at JPL will have 16x the number of pixels compared to the current state-of-theart thermopile, diffraction-limited pixels, and near-unity fill factor that will enable a compact instrument with hyperspectral imaging capability for demanding future missions for Earth science, planetary science, and Moon exploration.

title: Mitigating Single-Event Functional Interrupts in the Peripheral Circuitry of Non-Volatile Memories in Radiation Environments
authors: Daniel Puckett, Henri Malahieude, Joseph D'Amico, Josh Joffrion
abstract: Non-Volatile Memories (NVMs) are essential to computing systems, including systems present in radiation environments. Previous radiation tests on NVMs have revealed failure cases where the peripheral circuitry of an NVM is affected such that the NVM is inaccessible but no data is corrupted. We experienced this failure case, which we call lock-up, on an NVM we tested. Lock-up occurs for many microseconds at a time, which can lead to several kilobytes of data corruption. In this paper, we present a two-pronged mitigation strategy for lock-up to improve NVMs' reliability and availability and evaluate our mitigation strategy through simulations. First, we address NVMs' reliability by designing and evaluating several detection policies, which can detect when lock-up begins and ends consistently and with low overhead. Second, we address NVMs' availability by designing the Cache Window algorithm, which pre-loads data into a radiation hardened cache, enabling the processor to make forward progress while the NVM is in lock-up.

title: Electrical Carbon Emission Prediction of Urban Areas with LSTM-Kalman Filter
authors: Yushi Chen, Zifei Wang, Yanlu Huang, Zhiwei Dai, Fan Zhang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper proposes a hybrid prediction method based on Long Short-Term Memory Network (LSTM) and Kalman filtering for the prediction of urban power carbon emissions. LSTM can effectively learn time series data and capture the periodic characteristics of carbon emissions. Kalman filtering further enhances the model’s accuracy by dynamically correcting the prediction results. This experiment adopted the power consumption and meteorological data of a city from September 2024 to March 2025 as the dataset. Through correlation analysis, key features were screened out, and the performance of random forest, single LSTM and Kalman filtering models was compared.

title: High-Bandwidth Flash for KV Caches: Endurance and Performance Implications
authors: Kwanhee Kyung, Yeon Ji Moon, Juhwan Cho, Jung Ho Ahn
abstract: During Large Language Model (LLM) inference, the key-value (KV) cache is a critical bottleneck in both capacity and memory bandwidth as context lengths surge. While High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) can meet current bandwidth demands, its capacity has not kept pace with the rapidly growing footprint of the KV cache. Consequently, KV caches are frequently offloaded to CPU memory for higher capacity, yet performance remains constrained by limited interconnect bandwidth. High-Bandwidth Flash (HBF) has emerged as a promising alternative, offering sequential read bandwidth comparable to HBM4 with up to an order-of-magnitude higher capacity. Although Flash memory has limited write endurance, KV caches exhibit a favorable access pattern, being written once and read many times during inference. Our analysis demonstrates that the KV cache read-to-write ratio increases with longer context lengths, particularly with larger output sequences. This characteristic enables KV caches to be stored in HBF while maintaining an acceptable device lifetime, addressing both the capacity and bandwidth challenges of contemporary LLM systems.

title: Collaborative LLM-Based Agents for Autonomous Multi-UAV Mission Execution
authors: Jeremy Ludwig, Daniel Tuohy, Forest Finnigan
abstract: This paper investigates the use of large language model (LLM)-driven agents to coordinate multi-UAV missions, with emphasis on reliability, adaptability, and human-agent teaming. A proof-of-concept system, the Collaborative LLMbased Agents (COLA) framework, was developed to illustrate how modular language-based agents can be integrated with existing autopilot software and human supervision. The architecture uses an LLM server that uses structured prompts to convert natural language commands into JSON-formatted API calls, enabling intuitive operator control while maintaining precise execution of UAV functions. Validation and verification combined simulation, live flight trials, and automated prompt testing. Results showed that agents could coordinate search tasks, manage control handoffs, monitor telemetry, and reallocate routes dynamically, including in response to unplanned operator commands. Simulation enabled exploration of advanced behaviors, while live flights demonstrated feasibility under real-world constraints and revealed additional challenges not observed in simulation. The study contributes an illustrative architecture and initial insights into validation of LLM-based autonomy, establishing a foundation for scalable, safe, and reliable application of LLMs to aerospace autonomy.

title: Hybrid BEMT-Vortex Theory Model to Predict Amphibious Propeller Performance
authors: Md Umar Farooq, Sayani Biswas, Jagadeesh Kadiyam
abstract: Accurate modelling of propeller performance is essential for predicting thrust, torque, and power across diverse operating conditions, enabling the design of efficient and reliable rotor systems. This study presents a hybrid modelling approach that integrates Blade Element Momentum Theory (BEMT) with Vortex Theory to improve performance predictions of amphibious propellers operating in dual-media environments, namely air and water. Classical BEMT, while widely used, suffers from inherent limitations such as inaccurate handling of high axial induction factors, stall behaviour, and tip losses, particularly under transitional flow regimes. To address these shortcomings, the study proposes a modified BEMT framework incorporating high-induction factor corrections by Shen and Spera, along with the Viterna-Corrigan stall model to account for post-stall airfoil behaviour. In addition, vortex-based modelling is employed to capture tip vortex dynamics, which classical BEMT fails to represent accurately. The hybrid model combines sectional blade discretization with velocity-based transition criteria to determine the onset of stall and dynamic effects. The methodology is validated through comparison against an extensive set of experimental data from APC propellers operating under various conditions. Results indicate that the hybrid model significantly outperforms both pure BEMT and vortex-only models, especially in predicting performance across transitional regimes where air-water interaction dominates.

title: Time-Domain Linear Model-based Framework for Passive Acoustic Mapping of Cavitation Activity_supp1-3695753.pdf
authors: Tatiana Gelvez-Barrera
abstract: Passive acoustic mapping enables the spatial mapping and temporal monitoring of cavitation activity, playing a crucial role in therapeutic ultrasound applications. Most conventional beamforming methods, whether implemented in the time or frequency domains, suffer from limited axial resolution due to the absence of a reference emission onset time. While frequency-domain methods, the most efficient of which are based on the cross-spectral matrix, require long signals for accurate estimation, time-domain methods typically achieve lower spatial resolution. To address these limitations, we propose a linear model-based beamforming framework fully formulated in the time domain. The linear forward model relates a discretized spatiotemporal distribution of cavitation activity to the temporal signals recorded by a probe, explicitly accounting for time-of-flight delays dictated by the acquisition geometry. This model is then inverted using regularization techniques that exploit prior knowledge of cavitation activity in both spatial and temporal domains. Experimental results show that the proposed framework achieves enhanced or competitive cavitation map quality while using only 20% of the data typically required by frequency-domain methods. This highlights the substantial gain in data efficiency and the flexibility of our spatiotemporal regularization to adapt to diverse passive cavitation scenarios, outperforming state-of-the-art techniques.

title: Area-Level Medication Demand Prediction Via Multitask Spatial-Temporal Machine Learning
authors: Xu Wang, Jiangtao Wang, Long Chen, Bruno Arpino, Om Kurmi, Dingchang Zheng
publish: ACM Transactions on Computing for Healthcare
abstract: None

title: A Cyber-Physical Testing Framework for Evaluating Space Habitat Performance
authors: Herta Montoya, Manuel Salmeron, Motahareh Mirfarah, Sreehari Manikkan, Christian E. Silva, Shirley J. Dyke
abstract: Designing resilient and efficient space habitats is critical to support long-term human presence on the Moon or Mars. These habitat systems must not only withstand harsh and unpredictable extraterrestrial environments but also integrate autonomous fault detection and intervention capabilities. To validate smart habitat technologies and address the complexities of operational scenarios, innovative testing methods are required. This study presents the development and implementation of a thermomechanical cyberphysical testing (CPT) framework, a novel and cost-effective approach that couples real-time numerical simulations with physical components to evaluate habitat system responses under extreme dynamic conditions. Unlike conventional testing or modeling alone, this hybrid methodology captures critical emergent behaviors and interactions vital to system resilience that are otherwise inaccessible. The CPT framework is demonstrated through a series of scenarios utilizing a system-of-systems lunar habitat testbed integrated with an autonomous health management system. Scenarios are designed to replicate the thermomechanical cascading effects that would occur in a lunar habitat due to a micrometeorite impact damaging its structural protective layer during lunar night. The framework allows for real-time execution of both damage and repair interventions on the numerical subsystem, while simultaneously imposing the resulting conditions on physical components. Enabling and performing thermomechanical CPT tests for complex systems undergoing disruptions, with real-time damage and repair capabilities, can be far from straightforward. Thus, this paper outlines the experimental methods and systematic approaches necessary to design and configure tests that successfully execute challenging experiments, highlighting the challenges and solutions in recreating realistic fault scenarios. The CPT approach offers a new pathway for testing ideas and enhancing autonomous fault detection and decision-making in off-world habitats, providing crucial insights into fault propagation, systemlevel interdependencies, and the limitations of current diagnostic strategies.

title: A Cost-Efficient Fabric-Embedded Resistive Soft Sensor for Grid-Based Contact Localization
authors: Rishad Hasan, Bodee Davis, Keerthi Marri, Xiaonan Huang
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Fabric-embedded soft sensors for contact localization are gaining popularity for several robotic applications, including robot manipulation, therapeutic robots for eldercare, smart textiles, and more. However, the design and manufacturing of such sensors typically require expensive materials and specialized equipment, which limits their accessibility for research and education. A low-cost alternative was developed using conductive fabric strips in a grid pattern, separated by a polyester mesh layer, achieving competitive performance at ${\$}$ 1.14 per 100cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>—significantly lower than existing solutions. The sensor detects multi-touch input at 25 points with gentle activation forces (0.82 N), fast response times (81 ms), and good repeatability (25.6 % variation within cells). The design scales to 64 sensing points using only a standard microcontroller, making soft sensing technology practical for educational robotics, rapid prototyping, wearable devices, and robotic skin applications where low cost and customization are essential.

title: Streaming Fixed-Point FPGA Preprocessing and Feature Preparation for QCM Sensor Signals on a SoC Platform
authors: Adharul Muttaqin, Setyawan Purnomo Sakti, Valentino Delviery Butarbutar, Agus Naba, Panca Mudjirahardjo, Nanang Sulistiyanto
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: This paper presents an FPGA-based streaming preprocessing pipeline for Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) sensor signals on a Zynq-class system-on-chip (SoC). In electronic-nose applications, QCM frequency-shift streams are often noisy and offset-dependent, requiring conditioning before data-driven analysis. The proposed AXI-Stream/DMA pipeline implements baseline shifting, fixed-point exponential moving average (EMA) smoothing with a shift-based coefficient <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha=1/4$</tex>, and interval-based gradient extraction with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$L=8$</tex>, all in programmable logic with initiation interval <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$I I=1$</tex>. On-chip inference is intentionally excluded to keep the IP lightweight and independent of the downstream ML model, enabling reuse across deployments. Numerical evaluation against a software reference shows that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9-13$</tex> fractional bits preserve fidelity with low error and marginal resource increase, with gradient features being the most quantization-sensitive. The pipeline produces stable, analysis-ready features suitable for downstream feature aggregation and edge artificial-intelligence pipelines.

title: Quantum Image Processing for Medical Image Analysis: A Comprehensive Literature Survey
authors: K. L. Sujitha, Umamaheswaran S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This literature review analyses the convergence of unsupervised learning and quantum image processing for medical imaging applications. It aims to look at advancements in quantum convolutional neural networks (QC-NNs), variational quantum algorithms (VQAs), quantum-enhanced neural architectures, and hybrid quantum-classical systems with a focus on image classification and enhancement. The goal of this project is to explore possible solutions for medical image analysis, so that it can be more accurate and efficient by using unsupervised learning and quantum computing frameworks. The survey explores how quantum-assisted workflows can enhance the images from medical images, providing new avenues to diagnosis and visualization. While the NISQ era is promising, the limitations of NISQ are not permitting full quantum advantage in clinical practice at this time. To augment medical imaging with quantum-assisted unsupervised learning, this study presents research gaps and a path forward.

title: CLAD: Contrastive Representation Learning for Unsupervised Botnet Detection in IoT Network
authors: Akshaya V, Sriharini S, Swadhi R, Shreedhar J
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The limitless expansion of heterogeneous IoT infrastructures has increased systemic exposure, allowing botnets to expolit lightweight protocols and circumvent rule-based detection mechanisms. Existing signature-based intrusion detection systems and supervised learning models have a rigid rule set and are too reliant on labelled attack data, making them ineffective against previously unseen and evolving threats. This paper proposes CLAD, a contrastive learning-based anomaly detection architecture that integrates transformer-driven flow modeling to enable scalable and adaptive IoT botnet detection. After Suricata-based filtering of the known threats, network traffic is aggregated into statistical features of flow-level features which are embedded by a lightweight FT-Transformer, which has been trained by a margin-based triplet loss. This approach provides compact, benign representations while highlighting anomalous deviations in latent space, allowing label-free and distance-based anomaly scoring. Empirical evaluation on the N-BaIoT data shows that CLAD achieves up to 95.97% detection accuracy, outperforming a Siamese contrastive baseline, in terms of embedding separability, threshold stability, and generalisation to unseen attacks. The framework includes a drift-aware retraining capability, which allows the transition to changing benign traffic patterns and, in turn, provides persistent detection reliability within dynamic IoT environments and supports autonomous operation across distributed network infrastructures.

title: Smart IoT Health Monitoring Using Spectral HRV Analysis for Early Stress and Autonomic Imbalance
authors: Hasyifah Maritza Karsa, Khairul Azzam, Raniah Syifa Kamila Syarif, Khalisha Mayori Bambang, Aniqa Putri Ladisya Ardhiansyah, Dedi Rimantho
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The increasing prevalence of stress has highlighted the need for real-time, non-invasive health monitoring systems. This study presents an IoT-based wearable system that measures Heart Rate Variability (HRV) using the MAX30102 photoplethysmography (PPG) sensor and Raspberry Pi 3 for edge processing. The system integrates ultra short-term HRV analysis (60 seconds) with DASS-21 psychological assessments to evaluate stress, anxiety, and depression levels. HRV parameters including mean Heart Rate (HR), Standard Deviation of NN intervals (SDNN), Root Mean Square of Successive Differences (RMSSD), percentage of Normal-to-Normal intervals greater than 50 milliseconds (pNN50), and stress index were analysed, with classification primarily based on RMSSD values. Data from 10 participants demonstrate that lower HRV values correlate with higher psychological stress and autonomic imbalance, while higher HRV reflects stable mental states and healthy autonomic regulation. The IoT system allows real-time monitoring, ondevice processing, and secure cloud integration, ensuring timely feedback without transmitting raw data. These findings indicate that combining wearable HRV measurement with standardized psychological assessment provides a reliable, non-invasive method for early detection of stress and autonomic imbalance, supporting personalized preventive healthcare.

title: Is Simplicity Golden? a Survey of Post-Launch Adaptation in Planetary Missions
authors: Masahiro Ono, Richard Rieber, Tony Freeman, Mathieu Choukroun, Michel D. Ingham, Chloe Gentgen, David Murrow, Daniel Selva
abstract: The conventional wisdom in space systems engineering holds that simplicity is golden: systems should minimize complexity while meeting requirements. A simpler system is typically considered more robust and less prone to risk because it can be tested thoroughly and has fewer points of failure. While the core philosophy of this principle remains valid, we argue that the reality is more nuanced-particularly for planetary exploration missions, which face substantially greater uncertainties than Earth-orbiting missions. We investigated eight past and ongoing missions that encountered unexpected situations and either successfully or unsuccessfully adapted to them: Galileo, Hayabusa, Deep Space 1, Juno, SMAP, OSIRIS-Rex, InSight, and Mars 2020 Rover (Perseverance). Our study draws on a series of interviews with experts directly involved in these missions, as well as a thorough review of relevant literature. We found that it is often departures from design minimalism that enabled, or could have enabled, missions to adapt to anomalies and surprises. Specifically, we identified six key design features that enabled in-space adaptivity. These findings can help establish new design principles for making space systems adaptive by design.

title: Towards an AI-Augmented Monitoring and Control Applications Lifecycle Management System
authors: Luigi Palladino, Francesco Croce, Diogo Vicente, Joao Silva
abstract: The rate at which satellites are being placed in orbit is creating visible challenges on the management, development and maintenance of ground segment software applications. Ensuring maintainability and reliability, while scaling systems and teams is a challenge, particularly across a diverse set of specialised domains such as the satellite operations sub-segment with its critical applications such as mission control, flight dynamics, mission planning, simulation, among others. In the context of Mission Control Applications and Tools maintenance and development, EUMETSAT has defined a common Application Lifecycle Management Requirements (ALM-R) for Mission Control Applications and Tools (MCAT) covering engineering and processes specifications aimed to ensure that lifecycle of any/most of mission control applications is defined in a cohesive, coherent and-harmonized way. The requirements have then been mapped to an engineering Application Lifecycle Management System (ALM-S) made up of tools and implementation processes enabling the different stakeholders (e.g. developer, verification manager, librarian, QA team) to manage the phases of the engineering lifecycle according to their role and responsibility. The current combined ALM-R and ALM-S is the result of years of lifecycle management evolution and lessons learned which has been so far considered reasonably fulfilling the overall goal, but recently showing difficulties to scale with the increase number of ground segments to develop and maintain, complexity in terms of knowledge (technologies, design, etc..) and processes management, effective and intelligent automation, all projecting teams in the future to act with a suboptimal level of efficiency. In addition, this scenario is experiencing increasing budget constraints raising concerns on efficiency sustainability. Due to all these factors, assessment towards an ALM-R and ALM-S evolution addressing the mentioned factors has become a clear strategic necessity which we have addressed in the definition of a roadmap including indeed evolution, but also new technologies taking advantage of Artificial Intelligent (AI) technologies and paradigms. AI has been identified having a core role, specifically deployed with assistant and agentic capabilities to equip stakeholders with a configurable AI + Human-in-the-loop integration addressed by a system design capable to evolve both in terms of use-cases and technologies advancements. The approach covers both a revision of known ALM use-cases with their formalization into use-case taxonomy and in parallel their mapping to an advanced prototype implementation of a system layered architecture. The layered architecture presents at its bottom a knowledge base layer in charge to store and maintain into vector databases information artefacts from different data sources and made them available to the upper agentic layer (in charge of implementing back-end use-cases functionality) and the presentation layer providing ALM end-users with diverse UI/UX access to ALM use-cases. This paper introduces what we consider the fundamental phase of the roadmap for the ALM-R and ALM-S evolution named AI Augmented-ALM (AA-ALM) and specifically its implementation solution referred as AA-ALM-System (AA-ALM-S).

title: Real-Time Sign Language Recognition System with Web-Scraped Datasets and Image Annotation for Accessibility and Human-Computer Interaction Applications
authors: S. Umamaheswari, R. Krishanveni, G. Lingeswaran, V.S. Akshaya, A. Rajendran, B. Shuriya
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This paper proposed the methodology for developing a real-time sign language recognition system using web-scraped datasets and image annotation through Roboflow. Models like CNN and RetinaNet were trained using Python frameworks, and text-to-speech tools translated gestures into native and English languages. The system is deployed as a Streamlit-based web application with robust security measures to protect user data. The system provides high accuracy in gesture classes, low latency, and high throughput. This application can be given to the accessibility and human-computer interaction application.

title: Analysis of the Impact of Energy Storage Operation Conditions for Wide-Band Oscillation in Photovoltaic-Storage-Charging Stations
authors: Jinsong Liu, Xinyi Zhang, Shu Liu, Ningning Ma
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: With the rapid development of renewable energy and the continuous growth in the number of electric vehicles (EVs), integrated Photovoltaic-Storage-Charging stations that combine photovoltaic (PV) generation, energy storage systems, and direct current (DC) charging facilities have been widely deployed in distribution networks. The parallel integration of multiple types of power electronic devices in such systems leads to complex dynamic coupling, making them prone to wide-band oscillations in the frequency range of tens to thousands of hertz. This paper establishes a simulation model based on PSCAD/EMTDC to study the impact of the charging and discharging power of the energy storage system on system stability. The results indicate that when the energy storage system is in a charging state, increasing the charging power tends to induce wide-band oscillations in the system, though it has minimal impact on the DC charging piles. In contrast, the system exhibits stronger stability when the energy storage system is in a discharging state. The findings provide valuable insights into the mechanisms of wide-band oscillations in multi-converter coupled systems on the distribution grid side.

title: Front Cover and Back Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)

title: Comparative Analysis of Large Language Model Performance in News Content Classification
authors: Kendricko Adrio, Yaya Heryadi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: In today's fast-paced digital landscape, manually sorting through thousands of daily news articles has become an inefficient and error-prone task. While Large Language Models (LLMs) provide a powerful solution for automated text classification, media organizations often struggle to find the right balance between high accuracy and high computational costs. This study explores that trade-off by comparing three distinct architectures: the traditional Transformer, BERT, and the Llama 3.2-1B model across news datasets from HuffPost and AG News. To optimize performance without requiring massive resources, we employed Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for the larger models and used Bayesian Optimization to fine-tune our settings. Our findings reveal an interesting trend: while the Llama 3.2-1B model achieved the highest performance with an F1-score of 0.93, it only surpassed the significantly smaller BERT model by a narrow 2%. Furthermore, we observed that billion-parameter models like Llama are highly prone to “catastrophic overfitting” when working with smaller datasets. In contrast, BERT, when paired with LoRA, offered a much more stable and reliable training experience. Ultimately, this study suggests that for standard news classification, increasing model size offers diminishing returns. These insights serve as a practical guide for media organizations, helping them select resource-efficient models that deliver high-precision results without the need for excessive computing power.

title: Design and Application of an Automated Testing System for Line Protection Equipment
authors: Junhui Huang, Hui Li, Zhaoyun Zhang
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: To address the inefficiency and limited coverage of conventional testing methods for line protection and control devices in smart substations, this paper presents the design of an automated testing system. Featuring a modular hardware architecture and an integrated software platform, the system supports multi-channel acquisition and output of both digital and analog signals, and offers one-click testing and automated report generation capabilities. The system delivers a complete solution for standardized relay protection testing, significantly enhancing both testing efficiency and reliability.

title: An Intelligent Online Banking Framework with Real-Time Transaction Streaming and ML-Based Fraud Detection
authors: Manju C Nair, Sharan R, Sayshan G, R Velvizhi, P. Kabitha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The explosion in the number of online financial transactions has led to a major rise in the threat of fraudulent financial activities necessitating smart and scalable fraud detection mechanisms. This paper presents a graph-based framework for detecting anomalies in financial transactions, which combines the concepts of machine learning and network analysis to detect suspicious financial transactions. Transaction data are represented as a collection of interaction graphs linking users, devices, IP addresses and products, allowing for the representation of complex behavioral relationships that cannot easily be illustrated in tabular format. Node2Vec is used to create embeddings that are low dimensions and are based on the structure of the graph. These embeddings have been analyzed by two unsupervised algorithm techniques-Aisolation Forest and DBSCAN respectively responsible for anomaly scoring and the density-based clustering to identify the outlier nodes. A FastAPI based backend is used to support real-time transaction processing, anomaly validation, JWT-Based and Role based access controls. A real-time monitoring and analysis are allowed via an interactive (react-based) dashboard. Experimental results conducted on synthetic transaction data show the effectiveness of the proposed framework to detect anomalous patterns of transactions, which is a clear evidence of scalability and practicality.

title: Intrusion Detection System Using Deep Learning and Machine Learning
authors: Nivedha Hari, Gowri V L, Fancy C
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The risk of cyberattacks has increased significantly with the rapid expansion of cloud computing platforms, digital communication networks, and interconnected gadgets. Traditionally used intrusion detection systems usually depend on signature-based detection systems, which are not effective in identifying emerging patterns of attacks. This limitation further emphasizes the need for an intelligent system that can detect complex network dynamics. To improve the detection of cyber threats, this work introduces a hybrid model for an IDS system that integrates both deep learning and machine learning. The model identifies malicious network activities, by leveraging architectures like Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), along with machine learning algorithms such as Random Forest and Decision Trees, has been proposed. To enhance the performance of the model, data preparation techniques are employed in the system, including normalization, K-Best selection, and K-Means clustering to reduce dimensions and cluster network traffic patterns. Furthermore, the proposed system includes a web-based interface that enables the users to upload network data and analyze the framework's performance. To understand the results of intrusion detected, an AI-powered chatbot is provided to explain the types of attacks and assist the users in cognizing the results. Experiments indicate that the suggested hybrid solution is capable of high detection rates and reducing the false alarms. The system is an intelligent and scalable solution to the contemporary network security settings.

title: High-Performance MNIST Digit Classification Using Multi-Layer Perceptron with Progressive CUDA Optimization
authors: Kanampalli Sai Adarsh, Redekar Yash Jalandhar, Arun M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This paper outlines a step-by-step and even cycle approach to the implementation and optimization of a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to classify hand written digits using the MNIST dataset. This aims at bridging the gap between highlevel deep learning models and low-level system implementations by constructing the whole training pipeline. Our architecture is based on a two-layer MLP (784102410) that is trained on a variety of platforms, including PyTorch (GPU), NumPy (CPU), C (CPU), and CUDA (GPU). All implementations explicitly model forward propagation, backpropagation, and parameter updates, and therefore are easy to understand how neural networks operate and how expensive they are. This paper describes a methodical and iterative approach for the deployment and enhancement of a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) for classifying handwritten digits using the MNIST dataset. The goal is to connect high-level deep learning frameworks with low-level system implementations by building the whole training pipeline from scratch. We make a two-layer MLP architecture (784-1024-10) on a number of platforms, including PyTorch (GPU), NumPy (CPU), C (CPU), and CUDA (GPU). Each implementation clearly shows how forward propagation, backpropagation, and parameter updates work, which makes it easy to understand how neural networks work and how much they cost.

title: Intelligent ERP AI Agent with RAG and LLM Integration: A Systematic Literature Review
authors: Nandhakumar K P, Sasikala E
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: ERP systems have, for a long time now, sat at the centre of how large organisations manage their operations. That much is not in dispute. What is less settled — and what drove us to conduct this review — is whether those same systems, largely unchanged in their interface model since the 1990s, can be meaningfully transformed by combining large language models with retrieval-augmented generation. We think they can, with important caveats. Covering 47 peer-reviewed studies from 2017 through early 2025, drawn from IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, arXiv, and ScienceDirect, we trace four intersecting threads: how RAG architectures have evolved, how LLMs are finding their way into enterprise workflows, what the hallucination literature actually tells us about high-stakes deployment risks, and where agentic orchestration frameworks currently stand. The system we call IERPA — the Intelligent ERP AI Agent — sits at the convergence of all four. Technically, the pieces are ready. The harder problem is putting them together in a way that a real enterprise would trust. Hallucination in transactional settings, the near-complete absence of ERP-specific benchmarks, and unresolved accesscontrol questions at the retrieval layer remain the most critical blockers. We propose a unifying IERPA framework and identify six concrete challenges for future research.

title: Diffusion Enhanced Deepfake Detection Using Local Global Feature Integration
authors: VJ. Abhinaya, B. Elakkiya, T. Sivaprabha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The fast development of Artificial Intelligence has made useful impact on finding real images among fake images using AI functionalities to classify them in the real world applications such as Social media, news, online markets, E-commerce. These developments bring heavy ethical and societal implications, particularly in online journalism, forensics, and social media. To address these problems we created LUMINARA, a Image Analysis system based on AI to identify and classify the images which is fake. The model is created using one of the convolutional neural network EfficientNet-B4implemented in PyTorch with complementary libraries like Torchvision and PIL these are used to done preprocessing techniques on datasets to clean the dataset for better analysis. Our system is trained and tested on both AI images and real images for balanced learning and generalization. Optimization methods were employed for an improvement in accuracy and convergence. The results proved the model's performance to find the deepfake images with high accuracy and computational efficiency that is better suitable for the website. Without considering its technical value it is also usable for educational domain to improve awareness on media and transparency in digital content verification.

title: AURA: An AI-Driven Autonomous Underground Risk Assistant for Smart Mining Environments
authors: Wesal Melibari, Ahmed Abdelaal, Marwan Aljijakli, Khadijah Bawarith, Yazeed Althinayyan
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Underground mining environments present dynamic and compound safety risks arising from atmospheric hazards, structural instability, and worker physiological stress. Conventional monitoring approaches rely on fragmented sensing systems and periodic inspections, limiting real-time situational awareness and integrated risk assessment. This paper presents AURA (Autonomous Underground Risk Assistant), an AI-enabled cyber-physical framework designed to support intelligent safety monitoring in mining environments. The proposed system integrates distributed environmental IoT sensing and wearable-derived physiological monitoring through a real-time data fusion architecture. Environmental parameters including methane <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{CH}_{4}\right)$</tex>, carbon monoxide (CO), temperature, humidity, and vibration are collected via ESP32-based edge nodes and transmitted using an MQTT communication protocol. Physiological indicators such as heart rate and blood oxygen saturation (SpO2) are incorporated through API-based integration. At the core of the framework, an AI-driven hazard modeling module aggregates heterogeneous sensing data and computes a composite Hazard Index (CHI) representing the overall operational risk level. The AI layer performs multi-source risk fusion, compound hazard detection, and dynamic risk classification to support proactive safety monitoring. The resulting risk intelligence enables prioritized alert generation and real-time visualization through centralized decision-support dashboard. By combining Industrial IoT sensing, wearable health monitoring, and AI-based hazard modeling, AURA provides a scalable foundation for intelligent safety management in smart mining and Industry 4.0 environments, enabling improved situational awareness and more proactive risk mitigation in underground operations.

title: Design and Development of BLDC Motor Controller for Low Power Consumer Applications
authors: Hemant Kumar, Rintu Khanna, Santosh Sonar
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: For many years, electrical machines have been employed in commercial and industrial applications. Rapid advancements in semiconductor technology, including micro-controllers and power switching devices like MOSFETs and IGBTs, have made it possible for motor drives to drive the fast-dynamic response and high-performance control of electrical machines. This work introduces the design and development of the BLDC motor controller for low power consumer applications. The BLDC motor is powered by three-phase voltage inverters and runs by simultaneously turning on two of its three-phase windings. This maximizes the windings' potential to produce torque. The rotor position has a direct bearing on the stator windings' excitation. The rotor's location is indicated by Hall sensors. Every sixty degrees, the switching devices in the inverter are switched based on the rotor position. The inverter supplies the desired voltage & frequency output by controlling the on and off times using the most appropriate PWM technique. The SV-PWM technique and the BLDC motor speed control are simulated using the PSIM software and validated on the laboratory experimental prototype.

title: Application, Development and Analysis of Low-Speed, High-Torque Axial Flux Permanent Magnet Generator for Fluid Machinery Renewable Energies
authors: Yu-Jen Chen, Wen-Tong Chong
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: This study presents the development and experimental validation of a low-speed, high-torque axial flux permanent magnet (AFPM) generator designed for small wind turbine, small hydropower and wave energy applications. Traditional carbon-brush generators, while mature, suffer from mechanical wear, heat generation, and limited efficiency, making them unsuitable for renewable systems characterized by low and fluctuating input speeds. The proposed AFPM generator employs a slotless, brushless configuration to minimize cogging torque and mechanical losses, achieving improved low-speed efficiency and long-term reliability. Its modular architecture enables flexible scalability through serial or parallel connections, facilitating adaptation to diverse power ratings and fluid machinery renewable energies. Notably, the generator exhibits self-starting capability under low rpm in renewable energies input. Through system platform testing and simulation, the prototype achieved a stable energy conversion efficiency between 80% and 92%, with output power reaching the kilowatt level. These results demonstrate excellent performance in efficiency, stability, and expandability, providing a solid foundation for the subsequent development of integrated fluid machinery energy generation systems.

title: Cost-Effective Assessment: An Estimation of Diversity Factor for Sizing of Transformer in Selected High-Rise Residential Building
authors: Abee Jule B. Torres, Eugenia R. Zhuo
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The rapid increase of high-rise residential developments in Metro Manila has heightened the need for cost-effective and efficient power system design. Transformer sizing, typically based on demand factors prescribed by the Philippine Electrical Code, often results in oversized equipment and underutilized capacity. This study proposes using the diversity factor as an alternative approach to achieve more practical transformer ratings. Actual load demand and occupancy data from four residential buildings in Makati and Pasig City were analyzed over a four-year period (2016-2019). Statistical methods, including correlation and regression analysis, were applied to establish the relationship between occupancy rates, load demand, and diversity factor values. Results indicate that the practical diversity factor for high-rise residential buildings ranges between 1.2 and 2.3, with a strong negative correlation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(r=-0.802)$</tex> between load demand and diversity factor. By applying these values, transformer procurement costs can be reduced by an average of 22%, with potential savings up to 35%. The findings highlight the benefits of diversity factor-based transformer sizing in promoting cost-effective design and improved energy management in high-rise residential projects.

title: Simulated Annealing-Based Route Optimization for Efficient Power Outage Recovery Measures
authors: Noelyn M. De Jesus
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Modern society depend heavily on electricity, therefore any issues with the power source could have major consequences. Power outages can have an impact on businesses, homes, and public services. In some cases, they can even put lives at risk. Emergency repairs of power lines and equipment are important to make sure that power is back on as soon as possible. This is why it's important to have good route planning and timing in place for repair workers. This study aims to propose a costeffective and efficient solution for efficient power outage recovery measures, that will optimize route planning and scheduling in Batangas I Electric Cooperative, Inc. (BATELEC). The methodology used in the study involved problem formulation, application of simulated annealing algorithm to optimize repair routes and schedules, and simulation studies to assess the effectiveness of the proposed approach and compare it to manual method. As a result, simulated annealing approach was able to find solutions that were closer to the global optimum with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 \%}$</tex> improvement than the other methods, indicating its effectiveness in addressing the complex nature of power emergency repair route planning and scheduling. The proposed approach using simulated annealing can improve power emergency repair planning and enhance the overall reliability and resilience of power systems. Moreover, development of power outage reporting system integrating the proposed model could provide necessary data to improve power systems' ability to respond to disruptions.

title: Heading Estimation for Navigation of Micro Aerial Vehicle in Indoor Environment
authors: Kevin Sanjaykumar Parekh, Aryan Shukla, Laksh S, B. Anbarasu, Mahendran S, S. Akash
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This research compares heading estimation accuracy for three different types of sensors: Aeron Pollux PLX2-NS10; Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense Rev2; MPU-6050. Indoor controlled experiments were conducted by utilizing a set reference heading angle that ranged between 0° to 360°. The evaluation of heading estimation was accomplished using both the RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) and MAE (Mean Absolute Error). The Pollux PLX2-NS10 was the best performing test sensor with RMSE of 0.485° and MAE of 0.395°. Second place went to the MPU-6050 with RMSE of 0.547°, MAE of 0.485°, The Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense Rev2 had the highest error levels with RMSE of 0.604°, MAE of 0.532°.

title: Machine Learning Based Event Level Structural and Expressive Modeling of Sampled Audio for Digital Instrument Transformation
authors: Saurish Nanduri, Subham Kumar Jalan, Shashank Mishra
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Sampled audio has been used for nearly a century in music production and audio design. Despite its widespread use, sampled audio remains structurally static, typically treated as fixed recordings whose internal structures are not semantically reconfigurable. To enable new methods for analysing and utilizing sampled audio, quantitative modelling is required as a foundation for digital instrument transformation. However, there is currently limited research on event-level expressive modelling of sampled audio based on statistically validated acoustic features. Existing segmentation approaches often focus primarily on pitch, rhythm, or genre classification rather than structural expressivity. In this study, event-level segmentation is performed using onset detection, allowing the extraction of note-like acoustic units from audio signals. For each event, several temporal and expressive features are extracted, including Duration, Root Mean Square (RMS) energy, Spectral Centroid, Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR), Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC), and Event Density. Statistical validation is conducted using Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), while supervised classification is performed using a Random Forest model. In addition, regression modelling is applied to predict event duration. Experimental evaluation produced 17,770 segmented events from 25 audio recordings. Statistical analysis revealed a highly significant difference in event duration across categories (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p} \approx 10^{-76}$</tex>). The classification model achieved an accuracy of approximately 99%, with event density identified as the most influential feature. The regression model achieved an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex> value of approximately 0.35. The proposed quantitative expressive modelling framework provides a foundation for transforming static sampled audio into controllable digital units for future instrument modelling applications.

title: An Attention-Driven Bi-GRU Framework for Intelligent Judicial Case Management and Verdict Prediction
authors: P. Sindhu, J. Uma, A.M. Ravishankkar, S. Gowtham, T. Rajkumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The swift development of digital technologies has provided the possibility to modernize the judicial systems with the help of intelligent automation and data-driven decision support. This paper introduces an AI-Based Judicial Case Management and Verdict Prediction System that would help legal practitioners evaluate the records of cases and predict possible outcomes of the verdict. The suggested system consists of a formal judicial workflow system and an artificial intelligence prediction system. The neural network of Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (Bi-GRU) and attention mechanism is used to process textual accounts of legal cases and identify contextual connections between the statements of evidence and legal arguments. The model handles textual data that is obtained by textual methods of extracting legal documents through OCR and document parsing methods. It is further combined with a Large Language Model (LLM) that generates explanatory legal reasoning as to why the predicted verdict was made. It is realized based on the hybrid architecture of Flask (AI services) and Django (case management operations) that allow police officers, lawyers, judges, and public users to access the system based on roles. The experimental assessment proves that the suggested BiGRU attention model attains a prediction rate of 93.3 %, which is higher than other traditional CNN and LSTM models with the prediction rates of 85.0 % and 88.0 %, respectively. The accuracy of document extraction is also high in the platform with 96.7% of PDF documents, 98.8% of Word files and 93.4% of image-based evidence. The proposed system is much more effective in terms of judicial workflow, and it offers the interpretable AI-assisted legal decision support.

title: Robust Delay-Compensated Control of Systems with Uncertain Time Delay using a Predefined-Time Sliding Mode Strategy
authors: Salma Khatory, El Mehdi Mellouli, Houcine Chafouk, Abdelaziz Fri, Zakaria Chalh
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The autonomous vehicle lateral control is necessarily associated with time delays in sensing, computation, and actuation, and it can negatively affect the stability and tracking performance substantially. This paper explores the control of a vehicle that is carrying out a double-lane-change maneuver, and then there is an uncertain delay in the input. The strategy of predefined-time sliding mode control (PTSMC) is created to guarantee the rapid and delay-robust convergence of the tracking error. The new sliding surface using nonlinear error terms is generated so that the tracking dynamics converge to the known predefined-time when the system is in the manifold. A smooth reaching law is used to minimize chattering without sacrificing robustness in order to bring the system trajectories to the sliding surface within a given time interval. The controller gains are obtained in a systematic form as explicit functions of the intended predefined times, and they give direct relationships between the performance specifications and control parameters. The closed-loop delayed system is studied in terms of stability with arguments based on Lyapunov, and presents practically predetermined-time convergence to a small neighbourhood of the sliding surface with underbounded disturbances. The outcomes of the simulations prove the suitability of the proposed method and its ability to track the paths correctly and be stable in the conditions of different time delays.

title: Toward Agentic Software Engineering Beyond Code: Framing Vision, Values, and Vocabulary
authors: Rashina Hoda
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 International Workshop on Agentic Engineering
abstract: None

title: An Agentic System for LLM-Driven Public Transportation Analytics: A Practical Application and Case Study in Salvador-Brazil
authors: Lucas T. Borges, Fei T. Liu, Tatiane Rios, Marcos V. Ferreira, Clovis Carmo, Danilo B. Coimbra, Jorge Nery, Matheus Souza, Noe O. Garcia, Albert Bifet, Ricardo Rios
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 International Workshop on Agentic Engineering
abstract: None

title: Exploration of Optimal Conditions for a Simple Anisotropy Control on Thin-film Element Using Joule Heating
authors: Hiroaki Kikuchi, Shuto Kawasaki, Haruhi Isomura
abstract: We have proposed a simple method for controlling magnetic anisotropy in thin-film magnetic devices by combining Joule heating with permanent magnets. For thin-film magnetoimpedance elements, we confirmed the possibility of controlling anisotropy using Joule heating alone, and analyzed—based on FEM simulations—the differences in properties between Joule heating alone and Joule heating combined with permanent magnets. Furthermore, we experimentally demonstrated that by alternately applying Joule heating alone and Joule heating combined with permanent magnets, it is possible to achieve magnetoimpedance properties close to those obtained by conventional annealing under a static magnetic field. This method also has the potential to provide improved controllability.

title: Service-based RAN Control Plane Decoupling Based on the Fusion of Protocol Semantics and Topological Features
authors: Shangyuan Du, Xin Sun, Chunjing Yuan, Zecheng Fang, Lin Tian
abstract: To address the diversified scenario requirements and stringent real-time challenges of Beyond 5G/6th Generation (B5G/6G) networks, this paper focuses on the problem of insufficient scalability caused by the tight coupling of control plane (CP) functions in traditional radio access network (RAN), and proposes a service-based RAN CP decoupling method based on the fusion of protocol semantics and topological features. First, this study establishes a Context-Operation-State component model to define atomic service units, and introduces a Text Structure Fusion Architecture (TSFA) to extract the fused representation of component semantics and structural interactions. To overcome the limitations of general pre-trained models in understanding vertical domain terminology, this study constructs a communication domain specific dataset named 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) Protocol Semantic Matching (3GPP-PSM), and fine-tunes the Qwen3-Embedding model via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). Finally, the hierarchical clustering algorithm is applied to generate service units with different granularities. Comprehensive experiments conducted using metrics including the elbow method, silhouette coefficient, and latency increment demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms both the single-feature baselines and existing decoupling schemes significantly. The experimental results indicate that the proposed method not only achieves the goal of “high cohesion and low coupling” but also effectively mitigates the latency overhead induced by CP function splitting. This study provides theoretical and technical support for the softwarization and service-based architecture (SBA) of 6G networks as well as the “white-box” transformation of base stations, thereby promoting the evolution of wireless networks toward flexibility, efficiency, and intelligence.

title: National Innovation Policy: Transforming to Knowledge Economy
authors: Hossam Osman
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)

title:
Impact of Oblique Heavy Ion Irradiation on the Transient Response of Ga
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    MODFETs
authors: Gaurav Kumar, Khushwant Sehra, Anupama Anand, Manoj Saxena
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This manuscript investigates the influence of Single Event Transients triggered by heavy ion irradiation on β - (AlGa)<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>/Ga<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> MODFETs.
