title: Surrogate-Based Optimization of Open-Die Forging using Reinforcement Learning and Simulated Annealing
authors: Christian Linder, Martin Feistle, Syed Irtiza Ali
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Open-die forging processes are usually analyzed and designed using detailed finite element simulations. However, finite element models are computationally expensive, which severely restricts their use in iterative optimization and online process control. To obtain a real-time capable process control that accounts for both geometric and recrystallization requirements, a surrogate-based optimization approach using reinforcement learning is proposed and compared with simulated annealing. The workpiece state is represented as a point cloud with spatial coordinates and recrystallization values. A Pointnet++ surrogate is trained on finite element simulations at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1160^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1180^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1220^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>, and 1240° C to predict the next workpiece state for a given workpiece state, forging action and temperature. A temperature-based cross-validation scheme is used to assess generalization. The surrogate model achieves low deformation errors relative to the workpiece dimensions and recrystallization errors of about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 5 \% - 2. 2 \%}$</tex>. The surrogate model is then used to optimize forging strategies with a cost function that combines geometric and recrystallization constraints. Simulated annealing is employed as a baseline metaheuristic to approximate the global optimum and is compared to reinforcement learning. Optimization is tested for 1200° C. Simulated annealing attains the lowest total cost with 0.28, while reinforcement learning yields only a moderately higher cost of 0.42. Once trained, the reinforcement learning policy can be applied in real time, which makes the approach suited for process control in open-die forging.

title: PyPSA-Based Analysis of Power Systems with Integrated Renewable Energy Sources
authors: Md Sadman Sakib, Xin Wang
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy sources (RES), particularly solar and wind, presents both significant opportunities and operational challenges for modern power systems. While these sources contribute to reduced carbon emissions and advance sustainability objectives, their intermittent nature can affect grid stability and efficiency. This study investigates the impact of solar photovoltaic (PV) integration on the IEEE 14-bus test system using Python for Power System Analysis (PyPSA), an open-source tool for energy system modeling and optimization. Solar PV generators were implemented at buses 9 and 14, and a 24 -hour time-series simulation was conducted using real irradiance data. The analysis emphasizes voltage stability, rotor angle deviation, generator dispatch, and transmission line loading. The results indicate that the system maintains stability under moderate levels of solar penetration, with bus voltages and line flows remaining within acceptable operational limits. Although reverse power flow occurs during peak solar generation hours, it does not adversely impact overall system reliability. These findings support the technical feasibility of integrating distributed solar PV into conventional power system infrastructure.

title: Online Complaint Registration and Management
authors: M. Priya, D. Vikkiramapandian, M. Nithyakalyani, R Venkatalakshimi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This research presents a web-based Complaint Registration and Management System using HTML, CSS, Python (Flask), and SQLite to enhance urban grievance handling. Citizens submit geotagged complaints with images, while authorities receive auto-assigned tasks based on proximity for timely resolution. A live dashboard tracks complaint status, with automated email notifications and escalation logic ensuring accountability. Public verification and predictive analytics identify critical service gaps. The system improves response time by up to 60 %, enhances transparency through crowdsourced feedback, and supports efficient urban governance through real-time tracking and data-driven decision-making.

title: A Novel Laser-Ablation Resonance-Ionization Mass Spectrometer for In Situ Dating of Lunar Rocks
authors: Rico G. Fausch, F. Scott Anderson, Audrey E. Aebi, Amanda M. Alexander, Edward B. Bierhaus, Sarah E. Braden, Amy L. Fagan, Sierra N. Ferguson, Otso Gächter, Michela A. Gargano, Mary H. Hanson, Sébastien Hayoz, James W. Head, Alex M. Iseli, Katherine H. Joy, Viliam Klein, Julie M. Korsmeyer, Jonathan Levine, Steven Osterman, John F. Pernet-Fisher, Daniele Piazza, Andreas Riedo, Kevin W. Sacca, Vishaal Singh, Remo Sommer, Romain Tartèse, Tina L. Teichmann, Tom Whitaker, Peter Wurz, Marcella A. Yant
abstract: In situ geochemical and geochronological measurements on the Moon are needed to test models of late-stage lunar volcanism and to interpret crater-retention anomalies on terrains appearing geologically young. The irregular mare patch Ina is a key case: its exceptionally low crater density has been interpreted as very recent volcanic activity, but non-chronologic explanations-unusual surface mechanical properties or resurfacing-remain plausible. Resolving these hypotheses requires radiometric ages that are co-registered with petrologic and geochemical context at the scale of individual rock and mineral domains under the mass-volume-power constraints of landed payloads. We report the architecture of the Chemistry, Organics and Dating Experiment (CODEX), a compact dual-mode time-of-flight mass spectrometer developed for NASA's DIMPLE payload targeting Ina via CLPS. CODEX combines laser-ablation mass spectrometry (LAMS) for major- and trace-element mapping with laser-ablation resonance-ionization mass spectrometry (LARIMS) for interference-free <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{R b - S r}$</tex> isotope analysis in a single-reflection, off-axis reflectron analyzer. In LAMS mode, CODEX produces elemental maps over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z} \mathrm{1}-\text{2 5 0}$</tex> with mass resolution <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{m} / \Delta \mathrm{m}$</tex> of about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$300-400$</tex> (FWHM) to establish mineralogical context. In LARIMS mode, primary (prompt) ablation ions are suppressed and neutral <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{R b}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{S r}$</tex> are selectively ionized, mitigating the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{87} \text{Rb} /{ }^{87} \text{Sr}$</tex> isobar without requiring ultra-high mass resolution. Three-dimensional ion-optical modeling (SIMION) and a vented, shared ion-source geometry enable rapid electrostatic switching between modes. This two-in-one instrument is implemented in a flight-oriented opto-mechanical architecture compatible with GEVS environments and CLPS-class packaging. CODEX is designed to return co-registered elemental and isotope datasets that can constrain the timing of emplacement at Ina and inform interpretations of the Moon's late-stage thermo-magmatic evolution.

title: A Software-Based Approach to Radiation Mitigation for Planetary Missions
authors: Jeremy Nash, Vandi Verma, Evan Graser, Haoda Wang, Gerik Kubiak, Mark Maimone, Steven Myint, Jeffrey Biesiadecki, Philip Romano
abstract: The Perseverance Mars rover has demonstrated a new onboard global localization capability that has the potential to significantly improve long-distance autonomous navigation [1]. It allows the rover to precisely determine its position by comparing panoramic images from its navigation cameras with high-resolution orbital maps, and use that knowledge to reset its onboard position uncertainty model back to nearly zero meters. Leveraging a fast, commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) Snapdragon 801 processor from the Ingenuity Helicopter Base Station contained within the rover body, the system performs complex image-processing tasks to accurately determine the rover's position relative to orbital maps. This approach of diverting compute-intensive operations to a faster but nonradiation hardened CPU overcomes the limitations of the rover's 20-times slower, radiation-hardened primary processor, and is a paradigm shift in planetary robotics. We focus on the characterization, implementation and mitigation strategies required to use a non-radiation-hardened COTS processor in the harsh space environment. We describe the software-based memory checking and fault isolation techniques developed to address memory corruption potentially caused by aging or radiation effects. Furthermore, we present the performance and operational results from the deployed system on Mars, including its accuracy and robustness. The paper also discusses lessons learned during the development and flight deployment process and outline how this technology will fundamentally change future planetary navigation to support more ambitious and efficient autonomous missions by enabling continued use of commercial co-processors even in the presence of degraded memory capabilities.

title: Development of Radioisotope Stirling Generators for High Efficiency Space Power Generation
authors: Ernestina Wozniak, Matthew Stang, Salvatore Oriti, Ronald Leibach, Rebecca Buehrle, Hannah Sargeant, Ramy Mesalam, Kris Bell, Robert Tute, Michael Barron, Natasha Miller
abstract: Radioisotope power systems have been powering space missions for decades, historically using radioisotope thermoelectric generators to convert heat to usable electricity. Teams at NASA's Glenn Research Center have been studying and testing free-piston Stirling convertors, which provide significant efficiency gains for space power generation when compared to state-of-the-art thermoelectric power conversion. Stirling convertors have been validated as stand-alone units with extensive environmental and extended lifetime testing. Recently, several efforts have integrated multiple Stirling convertors in generator configurations representing the interfaces and geometry that would be present in a flight system. Three separate radioisotope Stirling generator testbeds have been designed or conceived: the Plutonium-238 Stirling Generator Testbed, an internal NASA effort; the Americium-241 Stirling Generator Testbed, an international collaboration with a team from the University of Leicester; and the Heat Source Agnostic Stirling Generator Testbed, a conceptual study. The Plutonium-238 Stirling Generator Testbed (originally named the Dynamic Radioisotope Power System Testbed) began the initial design process in 2019, with more resources for the effort added in 2021 for design, analysis, and drawing creation. First testing occurred in 2023, with follow-on testing occurring in 2024 and 2025. Initially, the Americium-241 Stirling Generator Testbed was part of a conceptual design in 2022 which showed promise and led to an international collaboration effort to build hardware and test the design in a laboratory environment. The first integration and testing of the Americium-241 Testbed occurred in 2025 at NASA Glenn Research Center, was successful, and validated thermal models of the system. Further efforts are ongoing to implement improvements to the design and test a second version in 2026. The Heat Source Agnostic Stirling Generator Testbed effort began as an idea to adapt to the changing environment surrounding fuel production and planned usage. Debates have been ongoing about the best mission options for the available radioactive material, and due to the current dynamic environment, a Stirling generator that can accommodate various heat sources, while still utilizing significantly less fuel to produce similar power output, is attractive. Recently, the Heat Source Agnostic Stirling Generator Testbed idea received funding to develop a project plan for a conceptual design study leading to hardware build, integration, and an environmental testing campaign, contingent on approval of the project plan. Design and analysis efforts for the Heat Source Agnostic Stirling Generator Testbed will show the feasibility and limitations of a Stirling generator design that can accommodate a variety of heat sources.

title: A Comparative Analysis of CNN and Vision Transformer Architectures for Visual Defect Classification Under Data Augmentation
authors: Kevin Julian Tan, Tuga Mauritsius
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Choosing the right deep learning architecture for visual inspection in the field of industrial inspection remains a challenge even with the current advancements in the field of Industry 4.0. Although a number of studies have adopted Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Vision Transformers (ViTs) have their own domain in dealing with the long range modeling of dependencies. This paper presents a comparative study on a state of the art CNN (ConvNeXt V2) and a Hierarchical Vision Transformer (Swin Transformer V2) on the large scale VisA dataset. Both models are subjected to supervised learning with and without data augmentation to analyze data efficiency and defect detection. It was observed that the standard CNN baseline, ResNet50, unexpectedly outperformed modern architectures. ResNet50 demonstrated superior robustness in the imbalanced baseline scenario (F1 0.715) and achieved the highest overall performance ceiling when trained on the augmented, balanced dataset (F1 0.849, AUROC 0.975). This leads to the conclusion that while modern Vision Transformers and ConvNeXt architectures offer theoretical advantages, mature, heavily-optimized CNNs paired with rigorous data augmentation remain the most formidable choice for industrial defect detection.

title: Cyber Resilient Provenance Tracking for Virtual Certification
authors: Malte Christian Struck, Andreas Schreiber, Alexander Weinert, Paula Ruß, Michael Felderer
abstract: The Virtual Product House (VPH) is an integration and testing center for virtually certifying aircraft components. This is important because developing an aircraft component is costly and time-consuming. If a production team discovers that a component does not meet requirements, the team must repeat the entire design, build, and test process. This repetitive development process is costly and inhibits future innovation. Provenance refers to the origin or history of an artifact, object, or piece of data. In the digital age, provenance has become increasingly important for digital content. While digital artifacts change frequently, provenance records typically do not. In aircraft development, we use provenance to track the development steps of the aircraft, as well as the digital processing workflows. We assume that traceability of aircraft data and reliability of tools and toolchains are both essential for simulation-supported aircraft certification. Based on the requirements of a virtual design process, we have extended the provenance tracking system that we developed for the VPH by applying a digital signature scheme. Our solution is adaptable to emerging security technologies, including post-quantum cryptography. We provide a high-level overview of the proposed system and discuss the design choices behind our approach. Our goal is to demonstrate the architecture, key features, and functionality of our proposed provenance tracking solution for digital aerospace design and certification processes.

title: Design, Modelling and Characterisation of a Miniature Fibre-Reinforced Soft Bending Actuator for Endoluminal Interventions
authors: Xiangyi Tan, Aoife McDonald-Bowyer, Danail Stoyanov, Agostino Stilli
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Miniaturised soft pneumatic actuators are crucial for robotic intervention within highly constrained anatomical pathways. This work presents the design and validation of a fibre-reinforced soft actuator at the centimetre scale for integration into an endoluminal robotic platform for natural-orifice interventional and diagnostic applications. A single-chamber geometry reinforced with embedded Kevlar fibre was designed to maximise curvature while preserving sealing integrity, fabricated using a multi-stage multi-stiffness silicone casting process, and validated against a high-fidelity Abaqus FEM using experimentally parametrised hyperelastic material models and embedded beam reinforcement. The semi-cylindrical actuator has an outer diameter of $18, \mathrm{~mm}$ and a length of $37.5, \mathrm{~mm}$. Single and double helix winding configurations, fibre pitch, and fibre density were investigated. The optimal 100 SH configuration achieved a bending angle of 202.9° experimentally and 297.6° in simulation, with structural robustness maintained up to $100, \mathrm{kPa}$ and radial expansion effectively constrained by the fibre reinforcement. Workspace evaluation confirmed suitability for integration into the target device envelope, demonstrating that fibre-reinforcement strategies can be effectively translated to the centimetre regime while retaining actuator performance.

title: Leveraging Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) for Digital Content Marketing Strategy: A Case Study of a Pre-Launch Skincare Brand in Indonesia
authors: Mikael Bryan Ciputra, Brianna Sanjaya, Joseph Setiawan, Antonius Felix, Akbar Lufi Zulfikar, Selly Swandari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The Indonesian skincare market, projected to attain a valuation of USD 2.76 billion, presents substantial growth opportunities while imposing considerable challenges upon emerging brands constrained by limited resources. This study documents the deployment of Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) as a Digital Content Marketing (DCM) strategy for L'Essaine, a pre-launch skincare brand targeting the 17-30 age demographic, as an applied single-case study with mixed evidence. Grounded in the SOSTAC framework for planning and the AIDA model for content development, data were gathered via in-depth interviews with brand stakeholders, competitor observations, and an online survey administered to 42 target market respondents. The AIGC implementation utilized Gemini AI, Higgsfield, ElevenLabs, and Canva to produce consistent visual and textual content over an 8-week period (November-December 2025). Key observations include a 3.9 % engagement rate, cumulative reach of 8,062, and net gain of 159 new followers, alongside a context-specific cost reduction of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 3}-\mathbf{9 4 \%}$</tex> compared with conventional production methods. This paper contributes to the AI and data-driven digital marketing track of ICCRAIDS by providing a transparent, reproducible documentation of AIGC adoption workflow, metrics reporting, and practical insights for resource-constrained startups in emerging markets, with findings limited to this single case context.

title: Breast Cancer Microarray Classification Using Ensemble Feature Selection and Bagging Histogram Gradient Boosting
authors: Akmal Zaki Asmara, Defiano Dhio Bimantara, Fitra Agung Diassyah Putra, Nanik Suciati
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The classification of breast cancer subtypes represents a challenging instance of the curse of dimensionality, where the feature space vastly exceeds available samples. To address the dimensionality issue, we employed RFE-SVM validated with hierarchical clustering analysis as the feature selector and ensemble learning pipeline utilizing Bagging Histogram Gradient Boosting. Our proposed pipeline was crossvalidated and tested on three independent microarray datasets (GSE45827, GSE7904, and GSE42568). Experimental results demonstrated that the identified feature subsets yielded excellent separability, achieving exceptional performance (1.00 accuracy and ROC-AUC) on GSE45827 and GSE42568. While the method initially exhibited sensitivity to the extreme data scarcity in GSE7904, we demonstrated that Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) successfully stabilized the decision boundaries, recovering accuracy to 97 %. These findings suggest that rigorous feature selection combined with bagging-enhanced boosting offers a highly accurate pipeline.

title: A Virtual-Variable-Length method for robust Inverse Kinematics of multi-segment continuum robots
authors: Weiting Feng, Federico Renda, Yunjie Yang, Francesco Giorgio-Serchi
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This paper proposes a new, robust method to solve the inverse kinematics (IK) of multi-segment continuum manipulators. Conventional Jacobian-based solvers, especially when initialized from neutral/rest configurations, often exhibit slow convergence and, in certain conditions, may fail to converge (deadlock). The Virtual-Variable-Length (VVL) method proposed here introduces fictitious variations of segments’ length during the solution iteration, conferring virtual axial degrees of freedom that alleviate adverse behaviors and constraints, thus enabling or accelerating convergence. Comprehensive numerical experiments were conducted to compare the VVL method against benchmark Jacobian-based and Damped Least Square IK solvers. Across more than $\mathbf{1. 8} \times \mathbf{1 0}^{6}$ randomized trials covering manipulators with two to seven segments, the proposed approach achieved up to a $20 \%$ increase in convergence success rate over the benchmark and a $\mathbf{4 0 - 8 0 \%}$ reduction in average iteration count under equivalent accuracy thresholds $\left(10^{-4}-10^{-8}\right)$. While deadlocks are not restricted to workspace boundaries and may occur at arbitrary poses, our empirical study identifies boundary-proximal configurations as a frequent cause of failed convergence and the VVL method mitigates such occurrences over a statistical sample of test cases.

title: A Training-Free Leaf Disease Lesion Segmentation Method Using HSV Color and Adaptive Thresholding
authors: Shofwatul Uyun, Haerul Anam, Baiq Titik Haerani, Latifatul Mujahidah
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Leaf disease lesion segmentation is a crucial step in plant disease analysis. Although deep learning-based methods have demonstrated strong performance, they typically require large annotated datasets and substantial computational resources, which limits their practicality in agricultural applications. Therefore, lightweight and interpretable training-free segmentation approaches remain a relevant alternative. This paper proposes a training-free leaf lesion segmentation method that integrates HSV color cues and adaptive thresholding through pixel-level logical fusion. The HSV color space is employed to extract lesion color characteristics, while adaptive thresholding enhances robustness to illumination variations. Morphological processing is further applied to refine the segmentation results. The proposed method is evaluated on a pixel-annotated leaf disease dataset consisting of 580 images using Intersection over Union (IoU) and Dice coefficient metrics. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach provides stable and consistent segmentation performance across diverse lesion appearances and lighting conditions. This method serves as an effective and interpretable baseline for leaf lesion segmentation in scenarios with limited data availability and computational resources.

title: Beyond the Bid: Navigating the Risks of Using Commercial Spacecraft for NASA Missions
authors: Rebecca Kelly, Andrew Driesman
abstract: Over the past decade, NASA science missions have shifted toward the use of more commercial spacecraft and fixed price contracts. This shift has resulted in fundamental disconnects between commercial incentives and government expectations with one key source being risk. While risk is inherent in space exploration, the introduction of lower-cost commercial options has shifted the paradigm for characterizing and defining acceptable risk in a way that often proves challenging for both parties to maintain scope and cost. At the NASA level, this is particularly evident when expectations on industry performance (pricing, schedule) clash with heritage standards for reducing risk (GEVS, gold rules) which can result in disconnections between the two entities. This occurrence, if left unchecked, can lead to increased implementation risk and limit new and emerging technology in the service of science missions. To better address potential disconnects, all parties would benefit from participating in this discussion for a deep dive into the unique characteristics and challenges associated with commercial buses and how they would be best incorporated into the NASA model. This paper explores several examples of the common NASA-toindustry disconnects and their impacts on mission risk including: unique requirements levied on production-line spacecraft (scope creep), discrepancies between expectations and performance, and NASA vs. industry standards. While this list is not all-inclusive, the authors believe these to be key contributors to the fundamental disconnects that disrupt the outcomes of partnerships between NASA and commercial providers. Furthermore, to address these concerns, this paper includes a number of potential mitigation strategies that can be employed including: utilizing experienced mission implementors to navigate the interfaces between NASA and industry, streamlining or tailoring existing standards for simpler implementation and flexibility to industry input, collecting and analyzing lessons learned from previous NASA missions to identify best practices that can be applied in the future, designing and testing processes that are clearly explained and signed off to early in the program, vetting commercial partner selection and management with an emphasis on assessing quality control and assurance, design and testing processes, and partner experience and expertise, and maintaining frequent communication and partnership-level collaboration to increase the flow of information between institutions and limit areas of ambiguity. A thorough understanding of both the risk factors and mitigation options discussed in this paper will enable NASA to effectively utilize new capabilities offered by the industrial base at historically lower prices while minimizing implementation risks associated with Agency-to-commercial interfaces. By effectively addressing the disconnects that lead to these risks, NASA will be better-equipped for future commercial partnerships that provide the opportunity to accomplish science goals faster and cheaper.

title: AI-Powered Mental Health Support Using NLP
authors: S.S. Srimun, N. Sankara Narayana, S. Pavithra, P. Dineshkumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Mental health issues such as stress and anxiety are increasingly common among students and working Professionals, yet access to timely and affordable support remains Limited. This project presents an AI-driven, multimodal mental Health Companion that detects users' emotional states through Text, voice, and video inputs. Natural Language Processing The methods of NLP are applied in order to analyze the textual data, while Voice emotion recognition makes use of audio features such as pitch and energy, while facial emotion detection is done by Computer vision techniques. The emotions detected from each modality are combined via a multimodal fusion approach These include the development of methods and techniques that improve the accuracy and reliability of outcomes. Based on the observed emotional condition, the system produces empathetic responses. and gives the patient self-care advice like relaxation. techniques and motivational guidance. The proposed system Serves as an early mental health support tool; promotes emotional awareness and well-being while being ethical and non-judicial contact. This attitude demonstrates the Potentials of multimodal artificial intelligence in applications to Mental Health.

title: Foreground Graphics-Aware Runtime DNN Scheduling on Mobile GPUs
authors: Minju Kang, Jaeho Jin, Sungmin Lee, JeongGil Ko
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: We propose GPUSched, a foreground graphics aware preemptive scheduling framework for concurrent DNN inference and seamless graphics rendering on mobile GPUs. Given that existing GPU scheduling methods relying on offline slicing are inadequate for dynamic graphics workloads, GPUSched is driven by two key components: (i) Render-State Detection via GPUPing, which leverages lightweight probing of GPU queue latency to accurately identify foreground rendering operations and (ii) Adaptive Operator Chunking, which dynamically fits DNN model chunk sizes within GPU idle times in a GPU frequency-aware manner. By combining these mechanisms GPUSched, coordinates DNN inference with graphics rendering in real time, preventing deadline violations while maximizing GPU utilization. Experiments on two commodity mobile devices show that GPUSched consistently outperforms baseline schedulers, achieving lower deadline miss rates while sustaining high rendering quality even under demanding graphics workload.

title: A Smart Bioactive Fracture Bandage for External Stabilization with Real-Time Biomechanical Load and Motion Monitoring
authors: Malliga Varshini U, Vidhya M, Vasuki S, Mohanadharshini T, Yogeshwari R, Nivetha R
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Effective fracture management requires both mechanical stabilization and continuous monitoring of biomechanical conditions during the healing process. Conventional fracture casts and bandages primarily provide immobilization but lack the capability to monitor load distribution and limb movement in real time. This study proposes a smart bioactive fracture bandage designed for external stabilization with integrated biomechanical load and motion monitoring. The proposed system incorporates natural fiber-reinforced bioactive bandage materials combined with embedded force and flex sensors to detect stress and limb movement near the fracture region. Sensor data are processed using an ESP32 microcontroller and transmitted wirelessly to a mobile interface for real-time observation. The system enables early detection of excessive loading or abnormal motion, which can prevent complications during fracture healing. The proposed approach provides a non-invasive, cost-effective, and intelligent solution for advanced fracture care and wearable rehabilitation monitoring.

title: A Dual-AI Digital Twin Framework for 7-DOF Redundant Industrial Manipulators: Integrating Generative Design and Dynamic Control
authors: Abdullah Tameem
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: As advanced manufacturing scales to meet the demands of electric vehicle and aerospace assembly, traditional 6-Degree-of-Freedom (6-DOF) manipulators face critical limitations. In dense workcells, these arms encounter kinematic singularities and lack the dexterity to navigate complex structures without collision. While 7-DOF redundant manipulators offer enhanced obstacle evasion through nullspace optimization, the added mass of a seventh actuator severely degrades high-speed dynamic performance. This paper proposes a Dual-AI Digital Twin framework that addresses this simulation-to-reality (Sim2Real) gap through two integrated phases. First, a Mechanical AI phase employs topology optimization to reduce distal link mass by up to 40 %, maximizing the stiffness-to-weight ratio essential for high acceleration. Second, the optimized geometry is bridged into a high-fidelity digital twin where a Control AI phase implements Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to autonomously resolve underdetermined inverse kinematics. The DRL agent learns to maintain precision trajectory tracking while utilizing the redundant joint for real-time obstacle avoidance-all without deterministic Jacobian inversions. This integrated framework provides a comprehensive blueprint for developing lightweight, dexterous, and high-speed redundant manipulators for nextgeneration automated production lines.

title: A Data-Driven Surrogate Modeling and Sensor/Actuator Placement Framework for Flexible Spacecraft
authors: Matthew Hilsenrath, Daniel R. Herber
abstract: Flexible spacecraft structures present significant challenges for physical and control system design due to nonlinear dynamics, mission constraints, environmental variables, and changing operational conditions. This paper presents a data-driven framework for constructing reduced-order surrogate models of a flexible spacecraft using the method of Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD), followed by optimal sensor/actuator pair placement. High-fidelity simulation data from a nonlinear flexible spacecraft model, including coupled rigid-body and elastic modes, are captured by defining a mesh of nodes over the spacecraft body. The data-driven methods are then used to construct a modal model from the time histories of these node points. Optimal sensor/actuator placement for controllability and observability is performed via a nonlinear programming technique that maximizes the singular values of the Hankel matrix. Finally, the sensor placement and dynamics modeling approach is iterated to account for changes in the dynamic system introduced by sensor/actuator physical mass. The proposed methodology enables initialization of physical modeling without requiring a direct analytical model and provides a practical solution for onboard implementation in model-based control and estimation systems. Results demonstrate optimal design methodology with substantial model-order reduction while preserving dynamic fidelity, and provide insight into effective sensor-actuator configurations for estimation and control.

title: Sentiment Analysis on Bangla Product Reviews with Emojis and Slangs
authors: Shaila Sultana, Shahidul Adham Musrur, Tanvinur Rahman Siam
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: User-generated product reviews in Bangladesh frequently contain Romanized Bangla, English expressions, emojis and informal slang, creating a highly noisy and code-mixed textual environment. Such linguistic characteristics significantly limit the effectiveness of conventional Bangla sentiment analysis models that are typically trained on formal and scriptconsistent text. This paper presents a transformer-based sentiment classification framework designed to analyze informal Bangla product reviews. A dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0, 0 0 0}$</tex> publicly available reviews was collected from Bangladeshi e-commerce and social media platforms including Facebook, Daraz and Rokomari. A tailored preprocessing pipeline was developed to address transliteration variability, slang normalization and emoji-aware sentiment preservation. Four transformer architectures-mBERT, XLMRoBERTa, BanglaBERT and BanglishBERT-were fine-tuned for three-class sentiment classification. Experimental results show that language- and domain-specific models outperform general multilingual models with BanglishBERT achieving the best overall performance with approximately 99 % classification accuracy on the held-out test set. These findings highlight the importance of domain-aware pretraining and robust preprocessing strategies for effective sentiment analysis of informal Bangla text in real-world applications.

title: Design and Performance Analysis of Underwater Wireless Optical Communication Link for Different Modulation Techniques
authors: Mohd Ashraf, Nusrat Jahan, Mainuddin, Dinesh Prasad, Khurram Karim Qureshi, Ozair Ahmad
publish: 2025 IEEE DELCON - International Conference on Recent Smart Technologies in Engineering for Sustainable Development
abstract: Underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC) is a promising option for high-speed underwater links. Its performance, however, is limited by scattering, absorption, and background noise in water. This study evaluates OOK and QPSK modulation in a UWOC system modeled in Optisystem and MATLAB. At an SNR of 15 dB, OOK reached a BER of about 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup> and supported 25-30 m of transmission. QPSK showed stronger performance, achieving a BER near 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−6</sup> and extending the range to 35-40 m in clear water. These results indicate that QPSK offers better potential for future high-speed UWOC applications.

title: A Next-Generation Multi-Disease Biomedical Diagnostic Platform Using Green AI, Edge Cloud Computing
authors: M. Sowmya, R. P. Meenaakshi Sundhari, S. Sri Ram Suriya, R. Varshini, R. Thangameena, V. Preethi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Early disease diagnosis relies greatly on medical image analysis; however, most existing systems focus on cloud-based analysis and singledisease detection, which increases latency and privacy concerns. In this study, a hybrid Edge-Cloud system is presented for a Green AI-powered multi-disease diagnosis system. For the combined classification of kidney stones, heart disease, pneumonia, and brain tumors from medical images, a light-weight convolutional neural network with transfer learning from MobileNet is employed. Although cloud-based training ensures scalability, edge-based inference reduces latency and energy consumption. With an optimized edge inference time of 0.6 seconds, the results of the experiments demonstrate an overall accuracy of 94.2 %. In real-time smart healthcare systems, the proposed system provides a scalable, secure, and energy-efficient solution.

title: Strategic Construction of Initial Datasets for Active Learning: Leveraging Self-Supervised Learning
authors: SekJin Hwang, Daeyoung Heo, JinWoo Choi, Joonsoo Choi
abstract: Deep learning has demonstrated remarkable achievements across various fields. However, performance heavily relies on the availability of large-scale labeled data. Data labeling is time-consuming and costly, which has prompted numerous studies aimed at reducing these expenses. Active learning is a prominent data-efficient learning methodology that has garnered considerable attention. Active learning methods iteratively select data that are most effective for training models, thereby gradually constructing a compact dataset. These methods typically assume a small labeled dataset at the start of training, and experiments generally use randomly composed initial labeled datasets. Although the importance of initial dataset construction is well recognized because of its impact on model training in most active learning methods, practical research in this area remains limited. In this study, a data initialization method is proposed from an active learning perspective using self-supervised learning. This method focuses on constructing a small initial dataset that maximizes learning efficiency by utilizing an unlabeled dataset. The impact on active learning is evaluated using a representative image classification dataset, in which considerable performance improvements are achieved.

title: Statistical Analysis of Electromagnetic Field Radiated by Printed Circuit Board Inside the Perforated Shielding Enclosure in Time-Domain
authors: Yuan Zhao, Jing Li, Huapeng Zhao, Jiqian Wang, Chenyang He, Guohong Du
abstract: Within a shielding enclosure with apertures, the electromagnetic field radiated by the built-in printed circuit board (PCB) will form a complex internal electromagnetic environment (EME), which potentially impacts the operational performance of the PCB itself and the others. In this article, the electromagnetic compatibility issue is studied through statistical method in time-domain. First, a near-field scanning system was developed to measure the radiated electromagnetic field, which was compared with simulated results to verify the consistency with each other. Second, the statistical characteristics of the internal EME was analyzed based on kernel density estimation and statistic parameter estimation to identify its statistical properties under three distinct scenarios: PCB is excited by different signals; PCB is located at different positions inside the enclosure; and different observation time. It was found that under the aforementioned different conditions, the time-domain magnetic field inside the perforated shielding enclosure exhibits similar statistical characteristics when the PCB is positioned at the same location. These characteristics are independent of the excitation signal and time, and all approximately follow a lognormal distribution.

title: Ameliorated Cyber Security for Power Electronic Converters Serving Asynchronous Hydro Generating Systems
authors: Rupesh Kumari
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems
abstract: In recent years, the threat of cyber-attacks on industrial process controllers and power electronics converters (PECs) has highlighted the need for enhanced research into the control systems of hydropower plants. These systems have garnered significant attention due to their critical role in energy infrastructure. The IEC Standard 62433, Industrial Automation and Control System Security, provides key recommendations to address these concerns. With PECs now being network-capable, they hold significant potential to integrate into the Internet of Energy (IoE). However, this connectivity also increases their vulnerability to cybersecurity risks. This study proposes a parallel controller strategy (PCS) to protect PECs in hydropower plants from cyber-attacks. The PCS combines a digital and an analog controller to manage large variable hydropower systems effectively. A 250 MW hydroelectric generating unit serves as the basis for this research. The simulation results are analyzed to assess the system's performance against various cyber-attacks across different operating modes of the machine. The simulation and experimental results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed technique.
