title: Distance-Aware World Model-based Reinforcement Learning for Mobile Manipulation Behaviors
authors: Xiaoxu Feng, Takato Horii
abstract: Separate control of mobile manipulation offers a promising paradigm for managing its complexity by planning for these modalities independently. However, end-to-end learning of such behaviors necessitates effective mechanisms for both embodiment selection and motion planning. For low-level motion planning, prior world model-based reinforcement learning (RL) approaches have employed the similarity of latent states as a dense reward proxy. However, using latent similarity as an implicit distance metric yields inaccurate representations and depends heavily on predefined goal states. In this work, we address the limitations of similarity-based rewards by explicitly integrating 3D spatial information into the world model and deriving more accurate rewards from that distance-aware model for policy training. For high-level embodiment selection, prior model-based methods have either utilized reachability rewards inferred from predicted task rewards or relied on heuristic composite rewards. In this study, we retain the reachability reward and demonstrate that it yields superior performance in embodiment selection compared with composite reward formulations. By combining reachability-based selection and distance-guided planning, the model improves the performance of end-to-end learning for separate mobile manipulation behaviors. Extensive evaluations were conducted across two simulation environments with both fixed and randomized target settings. For motion planning, the distance reward achieves near 100% success rates across all configurations, significantly outperforming the baseline similarity reward, which achieves only 60% success in fixed-target scenarios and drops below 20% when targets are randomized. Regarding embodiment selection, the reachability-based separate control rapidly achieves a stable result with over 85% of arm activations occurring rationally within the near-goal region. Conversely, the heuristic composite reward slowly increases to a maximum of 60% for near-goal arm selections. These findings validate that the reachability reward provides a more effective and efficient mechanism for embodiment selection.

title: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
authors: N/A

title: SketchOGD: Memory-Efficient Continual Learning
authors: Youngjae Min, Benjamin Wright, Jeremy Bernstein, Navid Azizan
abstract: When machine learning models are trained continually on a sequence of tasks, they are often liable to forget what they learned on previous tasks—a phenomenon known as catastrophic forgetting. Proposed solutions to catastrophic forgetting tend to involve storing information about past tasks, meaning that memory usage is a chief consideration in determining their practicality. This paper develops a memory-efficient solution to catastrophic forgetting using the idea of matrix sketching, in the context of a simple continual learning algorithm known as orthogonal gradient descent (OGD). OGD finds weight updates that aim to preserve performance on prior datapoints, using gradients of the model on those datapoints. However, since the memory cost of storing prior model gradients grows with the runtime of the algorithm, OGD is ill-suited to continual learning over long time horizons. To address this problem, we propose SketchOGD. SketchOGD employs an online sketching algorithm to compress model gradients as they are encountered into a matrix of a fixed, user-determined size. In contrast to existing memory-efficient variants of OGD, SketchOGD runs online without the need for advance knowledge of the total number of tasks, is simple to implement, and is more amenable to analysis. We provide theoretical guarantees on the approximation error of the relevant sketches under a novel metric suited to the downstream task of OGD. Experimentally, we find that SketchOGD tends to outperform current state-of-the-art variants of OGD given a fixed memory budget.1

title: FaaSAdapter: An Adaptive Resource Configuration Framework for Serverless Workflows at the Edge
authors: Haoyu Luo, Ming Liu, Shaojian Qiu, Xiao Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Serverless computing has emerged as a promising deployment paradigm for edge scenarios, owing to its efficient resource utilization and flexible provisioning enabled by Function-as-a-Service (FaaS). In Serverless environment, developers are required to configure resources for functions to balance cost efficiency and performance. However, determining appropriate resource allocations for the functions running at the edge is a challenge due to the dynamic nature of the environment. This challenge is further compounded when managing serverless workflows composed of multiple interconnected functions with complex dependencies. To address such an challenge, we present FaaSAdapter, an efficient runtime resource configuration framework for workflow functions, aiming at conserving computational resources at the edge while ensuring timely response to user requests. Different from existing dynamic resource configuration methods that incrementally determine resource schemes for only the immediate subsequent workflow function, FaaSAdapter predicts the execution times of all the unexecuted functions across various resource configurations and determines an optimal configuration schema for the function instances based on the current execution progress. Then, it updates the configuration schema as needed during runtime. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that FaaSAdapter ensures satisfactory response time of user requests with lowest resource consumption.

title: Decoupling Planning and Control for Resilient AGV Navigation: A Two-Tier Edge-MPC Approach
authors: Rômulo A. L. V. de Omena, Danilo F. S. Santos, Angelo Perkusich
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: The flexibility of Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV) fleets in Industry 4.0 environments is compromised by network degradation, which impacts navigational resilience. Building upon the conceptual framework introduced in previous research, this paper provides a substantive advancement through a two-tier, edge-based architecture that utilizes a predictive buffering mechanism. The core innovation lies in the systematic validation of this architecture via multi-scenario simulations—ranging from production lines to complex logistics centers—and the quantitative revelation of the intrinsic relationship between edge computational load and scene complexity. Furthermore, we provide empirical evidence of the effectiveness of a limited nearest-neighbor constraint strategy as a scalable optimization approach. Results demonstrate that the framework maintains operational persistence under network outages while enabling the use of low-cost, offering a high-fidelity blueprint for scalable and resilient smart manufacturing.

title: COMSOL–MATLAB Comparative Analysis of ISFETs
authors: Ball Mukund Mani Tripathi, Kodali Umesh Manikanta, Borugadda Jagan, Duriseti Chandrasekhar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper presents a detailed comparative analysis of planar and nanowire ion-sensitive field-effect transistors (ISFETs) using high-κ gate dielectrics, namely SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>, and HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>. The study evaluates the influence of dielectric permittivity and device geometry on electrical performance parameters such as threshold voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf>), transconductance (g<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">m</inf>), subthreshold slope (SS), and gate capacitance (C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gate</inf>). Device simulations were carried out using COMSOL Multiphysics to model the electrostatic potential and charge distribution, followed by MATLAB-based extraction of electrical characteristics.The results shows a clear improvement in the nanowire ISFET setup when you compare it to the usual planar version. Things get interesting under the same biasing conditions and with an oxide thickness of just (t<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ox</inf> = 2 nm).The nanowire design brings down the threshold voltage by about 10 to 15 percent on average. It also boosts transconductance by close to 40 percent. Looking at the different dielectrics we checked out, HfO2 stood among all. It has delivered the top gate capacitance at 0.064 F/m2. It has the smallest subthreshold slope around 49.8 mV/dec. All this points to better control from the gate and way less leakage current. The pH sensitivity improved from 52 mV/pH for SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> to 61 mV/pH for HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, approaching the theoretical Nernst limit. These results demonstrate that combining a nanowire geometry with a high-κ dielectric provides stronger electrostatic coupling, improved charge modulation, and enhanced sensing performance. The proposed approach establishes a practical pathway for designing high-sensitivity, low-power ISFET sensors for biochemical and environmental monitoring applications.

title: Kernel Reboot: Breaking the Boundaries of Neural Tangent Kernels for Neural Fields_supp1-3692624.pdf
authors: Amir Moner Mallak
abstract: Neural fields (NFs) map continuous coordinates to signals such as color or density, but fast high-quality reconstruction from sparse observations remains difficult. Classical Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) regression gives closed-form fits, yet it is fundamentally linear and cannot accumulate reusable task priors. We develop three algorithms that address these gaps. NTK-KIP learns a distilled support set of coordinates (and optional labels) so that a finite NTK can inpaint large missing regions from little observed data, yielding a compact non-linear representation instead of a raw kernel solve. MetaQuill meta-learns a shared initialization for an INR so that new scenes can be adapted by updating only a small task-specific weight offset, which provides true feature learning and a reusable prior. Finally, MetaQuill-KIP fuses both ideas: it seeds the task with a KIP-style non-linear warm start, then refines only that small offset around the meta-learned initialization. MetaQuill-KIP achieves high-PSNR reconstructions and semantically plausible inpainting under very sparse observations, while requiring only lightweight per-instance adaptation, whereas diffusion-style baselines typically depend on large pretrained generative priors and costly per-image tuning. This shows that NTK-driven neural fields can be made both non-linear and meta-learnable, narrowing the gap between analytic kernels and practical few-shot reconstruction.

title: Expansion-Contraction: A Multi-Agent Graph Traversal Pattern for Compound AI Systems
authors: Aiham Taleb, Zainab Afolabi, Joao Sousa, Mathias Seidel

title: Improving Coherence and Persistence in Agentic AI for System Optimization
authors: Pantea Karimi, Kimia Noorbakhsh, Mohammad Alizadeh, Hari Balakrishnan

title: Enhancing the Operational Efficiency of Immigration Inspection: An Empirical Study
authors: Chun-Ting You, Liang-Bi Chen, Szu-Yu Kuo
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Airport immigration inspection is crucial for national security. this study explores and recommends the deployment of an intelligent language assistance system at the arrival immigration counter, enabling travelers to communicate with immigration officers without efficiency loss due to language barriers. Finally, the study's results show that immigration officers are very willing to support and use this system.

title: Botnet Elimination via C&C-Based Collective Control of White-Hat Worms in Immune-Based Botnet Defense Systems
authors: Toshimi Nakano, Shingo Yamaguchi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: With the explosive proliferation of Internet-ofThings (IoT) devices, botnet threats typified by Mirai have become increasingly serious. As a countermeasure, an Immunebased Botnet Defense System (iBDS) has been proposed, in which white-hat worms (antibody and phagocyte) are used to suppress malicious worms. However, in conventional iBDS, each worm relies on random exploration, which results in low efficiency in discovering and eliminating malicious worms. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes a collective control method based on a Command and Control (C&C) server. The C&C server maintains the global network state as an infection ledger and computes binary Go/NoGo scores that control the movement of white-hat worms so that defensive resources can be concentrated on important nodes. Simulation results show that while the conventional method achieved a complete elimination success rate of 0%, the proposed method achieved 64%, demonstrating that C&C-based collective control is effective for botnet elimination.

title: Design of Reliable PUF Cells at Subthreshold
authors: Li-Kai Lin, Hongchin Lin, Fang-Hsing Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Scalable PUF (Physical Unclonable Function) circuits biased at subthreshold regions with low bit error rates (BER) were designed and characterized. The proposed SRAM-like PUF cells were fabricated using the 90nm CMOS process and occupied an area of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1100 \boldsymbol{F}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> /bit. By controlling the gate bias voltage of the PUF cells in subthreshold, the measurement results demonstrate good properties of randomness, stability, low bit error rate (BER), and reliability of the PUF cells. Thus, it is a good candidate for high-reliability and low-cost PUF key generation circuits.

title: A Trajectory Generation Method for Image-Based Unmanned Mobile Vehicles Using Bézier Curves
authors: Sakuya Yamaguchi, Takumi Obayashi Tatsunosuke Shiota, Yukito Taguchi, Ryuki Tsuruda, Ryosuke Oshima, Shota Koga, Nozomi Tsutsui, Tan Jin Hung, Hiromu Matsuzaki, Masahiro Kiyota, Ryuto Matsunaga, Nagisa Yoshikawa, Kento Nakai, Masahiro Nishimura, Taito Manabe, Yuichiro Shibata
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper discusses a trajectory generation method for image-based autonomous driving robots using SoC FPGAs. The UMV robot developed by the authors detects white lines on the road using a single camera and performs trajectory planning based on that information. In situations such as turning at intersections or avoiding obstacles, high-speed trajectory computation and stable navigation along an appropriate route are required. To achieve smooth trajectory generation, the system uses a Bézier curve algorithm to interpolate the feature points extracted from the images. The smoothness of the trajectory can be controlled by changing the number of computation points on the curve. In order to determine the optimal design that satisfies the real-time requirements of vehicle control under an embedded environment with limitations on performance and power consumption, we conducted a comparative evaluation from both execution time and accuracy perspectives. The results clarified that using 100 computation points is optimal for this robot.

title: One Disease, Two Worlds: An Explainable AI (XAI) Implementation for Interpretation of Risk Factors of Heart Failure Outcomes
authors: Qisthi Alhazmi Hidayaturrohman, Eisuke Hanada
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Machine learning can play an essential role in building a decision-making support system for use in clinical settings that helps quicken diagnosis and that can predict the risk of disease, in this case for heart failure. Unfortunately, a lack of trust from clinicians arises when the decision-making process of the models is not well explained, especially when the model applies to different demographics but is able to make a similar disease diagnosis. This study uses the SHAP method to explore the use of explainable artificial intelligence for model building through transfer learning with two different resources. We built a LightGBM predictive model that employs a novel cross-domain approach to improve a poor, low resource dataset by leveraging a rich, high resource dataset. This study revealed that this crossdomain approach enhanced the predictive performance of the model, with an absolute improvement of 12.86% in F1-score. Furthermore, the proposed approach still allowed the model to be interpreted with the XAI approach: 80% of the top 20 features of both cohorts that appeared in the feature importance analysis were overlap.

title: Low Power Testing: A Study of Power Aware Test Insertion Techniques
authors: Atharva Yawalkar, Vrinda Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Research on low-power in semiconductor design has always been fascinating. The chip density has significantly increased as fabrication technology has developed and moved towards smaller nano meter (nm) technologies. Testing them presents an additional constraint for the design for test (DFT) design. DFT adds a 5-20% area overhead in comparison to the chip's core functional circuitry. In digital circuits, testability logic is just as significant as functional logic. Thus, optimizing DFT circuitry can help solve the issue of controlling power consumption in digital circuits. This paper discusses various advances in power-aware DFT insertion methods like unified power format (UPF), based designs, efficient scan mapping, and various tradeoffs between compression ratio, chain length, and test time affecting Power-Performance- Area (PPA) of the integrated circuit.

title: Smart Energy Saving Socket with Automatic Power Cutoff
authors: Pasala Surya Akhil, Rayabarapu Siva Teja, Puppala Koushik Sai Venkat, Sunkara Teena Mrudula, Shaik Rezwaan, Alla Bhargav
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: In recent years, safety (i.e., electrical safety) and conservation (i.e., energy conservation) have been of great increased interest with regard to both automated systems and "smart home" technology. This paper presents a design and implementation of a Smart Energy Saving Socket with Automatic Power Cutoff based on monitoring of the load current with the intention of automatically disconnecting the electrical supply anytime the measured current falls below or equal to a predefined threshold of 0.035 A.The system employs an Arduino UNO microcontroller, an ACS712 Hall-effect current sensor, and a relay module, and operates as a standalone unit without reliance on wireless or cloud-based communication technologies to create a Smart Socket that automatically redirects electricity away from the Smart Socket when a connected device draws little to no current – thereby preventing wasteful use of energy and eliminating so-called "phantom load" or "vampire" loads during standby periods [10], [5]. This design represents an efficient and cost-effective way to manage energy consumption and promote "smart home" applications.

title: Advanced Machine Learning-Based Framework for Short-Term Renewable Energy Power Forecasting
authors: Nishant Rajput, Quish Raj, Omit Singh, Prabhat Kumar, Shailesh Pratap Singh, Pankaj Kumar Das, Kanhaiya Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Reliable short-term forecasting of renewable energy output is critical both for maintaining grid stability and for effective participation in energy markets. Both wind and solar energy are highly intermittent phenomena, making it extremely challenging for traditional forecasting models to accurately forecast these energy sources. This paper presents a critical comparison of three different machine learning models: LSTM, XGBoost, and SVR, with regard to predicting short-term outputs for both wind and solar energy. Weather patterns for these predictions are obtained from NASA’s POWER database. The performance of these models is analyzed using Mean Absolute Error, RMSE, and Coefficient of Determination on varied training periods from historical data. The findings of this paper clearly establish XGBoost as the best compromise between forecast accuracy and processing speeds, followed by SVR with relatively lower processing overheads, while LSTM models are constrained by data granularity as well as lack of foresight regarding weather patterns.

title: Transmission Gate Logic Based Counter for Biomedical Applications
authors: Varun Chaurasia, Vibhuti Baldva, Mansi Jain, Tarni Joshi, Kirti Bhargava, Disha Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper presents a compact and power efficient auto-reloadable, negative-edge triggered 8-Bit Binary Up Counter implemented using Transmission Gate Logic based D-Flip Flop using 2:1 MUX-based latch which finds its application especially in biomedical field. The proposed design follows standard binary counting principles and supports cascading for higher bit-width applications. It is optimized at transistor-level having all its blocks full-custom designed and implemented in Cadence Virtuoso using SCL-180nm Technology and simulated under 1V and 1.2V because of the low voltage input requirements in the healthcare field, wherein the measurement results show that the power dissipated is 13.47µw and 19.71µw respectively, making the design suitable for biomedical and low-power digital systems.

title: Bridging Social Science with Artificial Intelligence Fuzzy Framework Smart Pedagogy for bright minds in Decision Making Process
authors: Amit Kumar Mishra, Savithri H., Firuza Khayitova, Moxira Kasimova, Asror Sabirov, Sarvarbek Matniyazov
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The growing sophistication of social, economical, and behavioral problems requires pedagogical frameworks with the capacity to strengthen analytical thinking and decision-making skills. Conventional social science education costs frequently depend on a linear and deterministic approach to evaluation which is not responsive to uncertainty, subjectivity and cognitive diversity of human learning. In this research, it is proposed to use empirical machine learning-based fuzzy artificial intelligence structure to assist smart pedagogy in social science teaching. The architecture combines monitored ML algorithms with the fuzzy logic to capture learner thinking, interaction, and reasoning behaviors, therefore, facilitating instructive decision support. Data collected among the students of undergraduate social sciences were experimentally evaluated based on classification and prediction models with a fuzzy inference system. Findings show that the suggested ML-fuzzy smart pedagogy provides much more efficient solutions in decision making, critical thinking, and application of concepts when compared to traditional pedagogical methods. The research also initiates a new field of interdisciplinary research between social science and artificial intelligence, which is explainable and human-based learning systems.

title: Comparative Performance Analysis of Machine Learning Methods on Depression Dataset
authors: Ketaki Vinayak Gokhale, Rajeshri Shelke
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Early detection of depression can help mitigate serious outcomes in mental health. In this work, we propose a machine learning framework for classifying depression status from survey or social media datasets. The contributions are (i) designing a general preprocessing and feature-engineering pipeline for depression datasets, (ii) applying and comparing multiple classical machine learning classifiers (Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, KNN, SVM, XGBoost), and (iii) reporting detailed comparative metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC) and analysis of results. We conduct experiments on a public depression dataset (e.g. PHQ-9 dataset) and perform 10-fold cross validation. Among the classifiers, Random Forest and XGBoost deliver the highest performance, with an average accuracy exceeding 88 % and ROC-AUC above 0.91. We also analyze feature importance, misclassifications, and tradeoffs across metrics. The results suggest that ensemble methods are promising for depression classification, and that balanced sampling and careful feature engineering are vital. Future work may extend this to multimodal data (text, EEG) and deep learning architectures.

title: Terra-NTN-6G: A Multi-Layer Terrestrial–Aerial–Satellite Channel Dataset for AI-Driven 6G Networks
authors: Amandeep Kaur, Sujal Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs), including low-Earth-orbit (LEO) satellites and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), are envisioned as an essential component of sixth-generation (6G) wireless systems to enable global connectivity, high mobility support, and resilient communication services. However, due to the limited availability of AI-channel datasets, jointly modelling terrestrial, aerial, and satellite links significantly limits data-driven research in NTN-enabled 6G networks. In this paper, we introduce Terra-NTN-6G, a comprehensive multi-layer channel dataset that captures terrestrial–aerial–satellite propagation characteristics under realistic mobility, Doppler, and blockage conditions. The dataset provides configurable uplink and downlink channel state information, path-level parameters, mobility trajectories, and service-level performance labels across sub-6 GHz and mm Wave bands. Terra-NTN-6G is designed to support a wide range of AI-driven applications, including beam tracking, handover prediction, resource orchestration, and NTN-aware RAN optimization. Experimental results demonstrate that the dataset effectively supports robust AI-driven beam and link selection under high mobility and heterogeneous propagation conditions.

title: GTAssure: Ground Truth Quality Assurance Framework for Semantic Segmentation in Precision Agriculture
authors: Udaybhan Rathore, Amit Shakya, Rupesh Kumar, Shruti Phutke, Lalit Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: With the growing demand for precision agriculture to minimize food waste, optimize resource and crop management, semantic segmentation plays a critical role in enabling intelligent decision-making. However, achieving high segmentation accuracy on agricultural fields requires real, field-captured datasets. Manual annotation of these datasets is not only time-consuming and labor-intensive but also prone to errors such as missed regions, mislabels, and coarse boundaries, resulting in biased learning and unreliable model performance during training and testing. To address this, we propose GTAssure, an automated framework for ground truth quality inspection that detects and corrects annotation errors, thereby assuring dataset reliability for precision agriculture. The framework employs two correction approaches: the first addresses missed regions and misclassifications errors, while the second targets coarse annotations commonly found in complex agricultural scenes. GTAssure is evaluated across diverse agricultural tasks, including leaf disease, fruit, and crop–weed segmentation, using five challenging real-world datasets: NLB, LDSD, MinneApple, WGISD, and CWFID. The proposed framework significantly enhances models reliability and accuracy using expert-verified, refined ground truth, demonstrating consistent IoU improvements ranging from 0.68% to 5.13% across all experimental datasets, establishing a new benchmark for high-quality annotations in precision agriculture. Code will be made publicly available once the paper is accepted.

title: Evolution of ML and AI Techniques for DDoS Detection and Mitigation in SDN: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Nilesh Kumar, Amrit Lal Sangal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Software-defined networking (SDN) is a significant technology for building network infrastructures that can be centrally controlled and programmed. The flow table, on the other hand, is very easy to attack with Denial-of-Service (DoS) and Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) assaults because it doesn’t have much space and its control plane is logically centralized. A lot of research was done on machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), hybrid, and fully in-network AI-driven solutions for finding and stopping these kinds of attacks from 2017 to 2025. This analysis looks at some of the most important studies that came out between 2020 and 2025. There are papers about supervised learning models, online ensemble methods, graph-based architectures, generative frameworks, programmable data-plane strategies, and flow-table defenses that are based on learning to rank. The studies that were looked at are all put into one taxonomy and compared based on response characteristics, deployment layers, performance measures, and datasets. This shows that AI frameworks that work in real time, on two planes, and in the network are taking the place of offline systems that are based on controllers. The study finds three main problems with current research methods: they don’t protect well against zero-day and low-rate assaults, they rely too much on lab datasets, and they don’t work well with production-grade SDN systems. The paper finishes by saying that more research is needed on explainable and adaptive detection, graph- and GAN-based anomaly modeling, cross-domain benchmarking, and making AI pipelines with programmable data planes to make SDN protection that can grow and is reliable.

title: Achieving Sustainable Connectivity in Agriculture through AI-Powered Beamforming
authors: Mohamed Naeem
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Sustainable connectivity within agricultural systems ensures continuous monitoring and effective management. Disruptions in this connectivity can cause significant delays in field operations. Various factors threaten the stability of these connections, prompting many scholarly efforts to address these disruptions. However, a comprehensive solution for managing connectivity interruptions has yet to be developed. This paper proposes a novel hybrid dynamic algorithm that combines AI with classical MUSIC and least mean squares (LMS) for agricultural applications. The proposed solution utilizes the Feedforward Neural Network (FNN) to adjust the step size of the LMS adaptive beamforming algorithm, in conjunction with MUSIC direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation. It considers realistic wireless propagation models and a heterogeneous wireless network infrastructure, along with optimized beamforming management. A comparative analysis reveals that this approach outperforms prior methods, resulting in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 1 5 \%}$</tex> reduction in power consumption.

title: A Review of Resilience Research on Integrated Energy Systems Oriented towards Cyber-Physical-Social Integration
authors: Jie Hu, Sihan Liao, Yinye Shen, Guokun Cui, Jia Hu, Ziwei Li
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the profound transformation of global energy systems, Cyber-Physical-Social Systems (CPSS) have emerged as a new paradigm for future energy systems. This paper systematically reviews the research progress on Integrated Energy System (IES) resilience based on CPSS, organizing literature from four perspectives: three-layer CPSS architecture, extended "4R" resilience assessment framework, evolutionary trajectory of attack-defense games, and integration of emerging technologies. It highlights the significant impact of social behavior on energy system resilience, identifies key research gaps, and proposes future directions, providing comprehensive guidance for constructing a robust theoretical and technological system for IES resilience. Recent studies emphasize the critical role of social sentiment in crisis response, and simulation-based validations demonstrate the effectiveness of digital twin-driven strategies in extreme scenarios.

title: Linearized Bus-Splitting Configuration Models for VRE Curtailment Minimization in Power System Operations
authors: Pee Jay N. Gealone, Adonis Emmanuel DC. Tio
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The integration of variable renewable energy (VRE) sources into power grids introduces operational challenges stemming from their inherent variability and non-dispatchability. The traditional congestion management approaches rely heavily on infrastructure expansion, redispatch, and demand curtailment, which are costly alternatives. This paper presents a novel mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) framework that integrates topology reconfiguration through substation bus-splitting decisions into operational optimization to minimize VRE curtailment. Unlike conventional methods that treat network topology as static infrastructure, the proposed model treats substation configuration as a dispatchable operational asset. The framework incorporates two distinct substation configurations – double-bus and ring-bus – and systematically develops their mathematical models. The model is validated on the IEEE 6-bus and NREL-118 bus test systems using DCOPF. Results demonstrate that incorporating bus-splitting flexibility and co-optimizing it with dispatch reduces VRE curtailment in both small and large grids. Despite the real-world implications of bus splitting for system protection, reliability, and practical implementation, its potential to support effective VRE integration makes a compelling case for its integration with other congestion management solutions.

title: Fully Decentralized P2P Energy Trading in Electric-Hydrogen Integrated Energy Systems with Hybrid Renewable Energy
authors: Jinlong Yang, Pengcheng Chen, Wangzhuo Yang, Shuwang Du, Bo Chen
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Peer-to-peer (P2P) energy trading within integrated energy system (IES) facilitates benefit sharing among prosumers and effective integration of distributed renewable energy sources. This paper proposes a novel fully decentralized P2P trading framework for a coupled electric-hydrogen system. The proposed system incorporates hybrid renewable energy sources (photovoltaic and wind turbine generator), distributed generators (DG), battery energy storage systems (BESS), electrolyzers (EL), fuel cells (FC), and hydrogen storage tanks to establish a comprehensive energy management solution. The alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) algorithm is employed to achieve decentralized P2P trading optimization, where self-interested prosumers iteratively negotiate to reach optimal trading strategies for both electricity and hydrogen. The framework addresses the temporal mismatch between renewable energy generation and load demand through electricity-to-hydrogen conversion and storage, enabling long-term energy arbitrage. A IEEE 33-bus distribution network case study demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, showing convergence of dual electric-hydrogen P2P energy trading and enhanced renewable energy utilization through multi-energy synergy.

title: Research on the Loading Test Method for Propulsion Motors under Wide Temperature Ranges and Low Pressure Conditions
authors: Jinhua Liang, Haiping Xu, Fei Chen, Wei Liu, Peng Zhou, Xi Chen
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Based on an analysis of the propulsion motor’s operating characteristics and the environmental pressures it faces, a loading test platform has been developed. This platform supports dual, triple, and quadruple loading combinations, integrating alternating high/low temperature cycles, variable environmental pressure, mechanical vibration and shock from airflow disturbances, and cyclic load variations. A novel loading test methodology is then proposed, enabling single factor extreme performance testing, synchronous static coupling tests, alternating cyclic dynamic coupling tests, and comprehensive accelerated life testing. This approach allows for a more realistic simulation of multi field coupling effects under actual service conditions.

title: Influence of Turn-to-Turn and Disk-to-Disk Faults on the Frequency Response of a Transformer
authors: Elliton O. S. R. Brandão, Estácio T. W. Neto, Gustavo P. Lopes
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Diagnosing internal faults in power transformers is a highly relevant topic, as such defects can quickly progress to critical conditions, compromising the reliability of the electrical system. Among the various methods investigated, frequency response analysis (FRA) stands out for its ability to identify changes in the electromagnetic behavior of the winding, even in the early stages of failure. In this context, this work aims to investigate how different types of internal short circuits — between turns and between disks — influence the frequency response of transformers. Experimental tests were conducted on two single-phase transformers with access to multiple taps in the winding, applying short circuits between specific taps. The results showed that turn-to-turn faults caused an increase in the magnitude of the response at low and medium frequencies, as well as phase shifts at higher frequencies. For disk-to-disk faults, this increase was observed at high frequencies and significant phase shifts at medium frequencies. The statistical indicators SSD, ASLE, and SD were used and proved to be capable of identifying the presence of faults. The SDD indicator proved to be more accurate, being able to identify the frequency bands in which the main discrepancies are evident.

title: TrustHeart: Explainable and Privacy-Preserving AI Architecture for Trustworthy Cardiac Monitoring in IoMT Consumer Applications
authors: Qingyang Liu, Jingwen Tang, Jingsong Shen, Yanhui Cai, Yanhuan Huang, Nanyu Lu
abstract: Consumer wearable devices equipped with AI-powered cardiac monitoring systems face significant challenges including adversarial attacks, privacy vulnerabilities, and lack of interpretability, which undermine clinical trust. Existing privacy-preserving cardiac AI systems struggle to maintain accuracy above 92% under strict privacy guarantees (ε < 5), fail to provide interpretability while preserving privacy, and lack robustness for safety-critical deployments. This paper presents TrustHeart, a lightweight architecture integrating robust privacy, interpretability, and proactive threat detection for consumer wearables. The major contributions include: (i) an adaptive privacy budget allocation mechanism achieving ε = 4.2 with only 2.8% accuracy degradation through gradient-aware noise calibration; (ii) a privacy-aware attention mechanism achieving 0.76 Intersection over Union(IoU) alignment with clinical annotations under differential privacy guarantees; and (iii) lightweight anomaly detection algorithms detecting >93.2% of adversarial attacks with 0.9ms overhead. Experiments on MIT-BIH and CPSC2018 datasets demonstrate that TrustHeart achieves 97.6% accuracy on MIT-BIH and 86.1% on CPSC2018, F1=90.8% under Projected Gradient Descent(PGD) attacks, with 35ms inference latency on ARM Cortex-M4, 295kB model size, and <2.3mW inference power consumption. TrustHeart provides a comprehensive reference framework for trustworthy cardiac AI in consumer wearables.

title: Beyond Single-Frequency Approximation: The Dispersion Model for Ultrawideband Phase-Modulated Signals Propagating in Collisionless Cold Plasma
authors: Miao Qin, Zituo Lu, Kai Yuan, Rongxin Tang, Ziyang Zhao, Wenchong Ouyang, Xiaohua Deng
abstract: During hypersonic re-entry or high-speed flight in near-space environments, plasma sheaths enveloping vehicles induce severe communication outages—commonly known as the “blackout” problem—posing a critical bottleneck for future ultrahigh-speed aerospace communication systems. Conventional models for electromagnetic wave propagation in plasmas, such as the Appleton–Hartree formula, are typically based on monochromatic plane-wave assumptions and thus could fail to accurately characterize the behavior of modern wideband communication signals propagating through such dynamic media. Starting from the electron equation of motion, this article develops a time-varying dispersive propagation model specifically suited for ultrawideband (UWD) phase-modulated signals in collisionless cold plasma. By introducing a time-varying differential operator that captures the instantaneous dynamics of the signal envelope, the proposed model overcomes the narrowband limitations inherent in traditional frequency-domain analyses. Key findings reveal that the effective cutoff frequency for phase-modulated signals in plasma is no longer solely determined by the plasma frequency but is jointly governed by the modulation index and baseband frequency—enabling the signal penetration even when the carrier frequency falls below the classical cutoff threshold. Moreover, the plasma’s effective permittivity exhibits periodic oscillations synchronized with the baseband frequency. In addition, due to frequency-dependent dispersion, individual sideband components accumulate nonuniform phase shifts during propagation, leading to significant nonlinear phase distortion and waveform degradation at the receiver. These insights not only provide a theoretical foundation for mitigating blackout effects through optimized modulation schemes but also uncover new challenges in UWD signal transmission over dynamic plasma channels, offering critical guidance for the design of robust, high-data-rate aerospace communication systems operating in plasma-affected environments.

title: Application of Novel High-Temperature Resistant and High-Efficiency Shielding Materials in CFETR
authors: Miao Yin, Shen Qu, Qixiang Cao, Ziqiang Zhao, Fengchao Zhao, Pengfei You, Shiteng Zhang, Shengqiang Zhao
abstract: The China Fusion Engineering Test Reactor (CFETR) requires advanced radiation shielding, particularly for protecting its superconducting magnets. This study investigates a novel shielding strategy based on gadolinium hydride (GdH<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>), chosen for its high-temperature stability and exceptional efficacy in attenuating low- and intermediate-energy neutrons. To provide comprehensive shielding across the entire energy spectrum, tungsten (W) is incorporated to address high-energy neutrons and gamma rays, forming a complementary “W + GdH<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {2}}{\,}+$</tex-math> </inline-formula> W” sandwich configuration. Numerical simulations incorporating a 15% volumetric fraction of water coolant demonstrate that the optimized design reduces the peak fast neutron fluence on the magnets by up to ~70% and lowers the maximum nuclear heating by over 80%, compared to a conventional steel–water shield of equivalent thickness. This substantial performance gain enables a more compact reactor design.

title: Rod-Pipe-Climbing Soft Robot With Transitioning Locomotion
authors: Jiaxi Wu, Mingxin Wu, Chen Wang, Guangming Xie
abstract: Inspired by nature, researchers have created climbing robots with impressive performances. However, most existing soft climbing robots remain limited by their single functionality: they can either move along rods or within pipes, but typically cannot operate in both environments. Achieving smooth transitions between rods and pipes is even more challenging, restricting their deployment in practical scenarios. In this work, we propose a soft climbing robot using fabric-stacking pneumatic actuators, capable of climbing on rods, crawling inside pipes, and transitioning between rods and pipes. The robot’s body consists of two parallel fabric-stacking stretching actuators, which can stretch from an initial length of 8.8 mm to 166.4 mm, with a stretching ratio of 17.9. The robot’s foot, comprised of two zig-zag-shaped bending actuators, can grasp rods and support the inner walls of pipes by regulating air pressures. By coordinating the body with the front and hind feet, the robot can climb both vertical and curved rods and pipes. Furthermore, we demonstrate the robot’s ability to transition between two rods, two pipes, and between a pole and a tube—to the best of our knowledge, a capability not yet achieved by existing soft climbing robots.

title: Time-Dependent Response of the ITER Diagnostic Residual Gas Analyzer
authors: Brendan R. Quinlan, Jonathan E. Perry, Chris Marcus, Charles D. Smith, Theodore M. Biewer, C. Christopher Klepper
abstract: Analysis of the ITER diagnostic residual gas analyzer (DRGA) vacuum system is critical for the design and accuracy of the gas composition measurement which is used for the control of plasma heating and fuel-cycle processing. The DRGA is a diagnostic system capable of resolving hydrogen and helium isotopes as well as heavier impurity gases. The DRGA is located approximately 15 m away from the divertor and is intended to measure the composition of gases in the ITER sub-divertor region. The DRGA for the divertor analysis relies on an evacuated sampling pipe to transport the exhaust gases to the analysis station where spectrometers measure the exhaust gas composition. At the sampling pipe entrance, an in-line orifice creates molecular flow conditions in the entire length of the sampling pipe. For plasma heating control and fuel-cycle processing, it is necessary to analyze the time-dependent response resulting from changes in gas composition from injected gas and/or fueling pellets. Molflow+, which is a test particle Monte Carlo (TPMC) simulation code, is used to simulate the time-dependent response of the DRGA from injections of hydrogen gas. An innovative way to model an in-line turbomolecular pump is presented in this work. The results from a simulation code based on the physical geometry of an in-lab test article. The results from the simulation code and physical testing of the in-lab test article are compared and show good agreement. The simulation is then extended to an ITER like geometry and compared to previous work. The results of the time dependent response are shown from the sampling pipe orifice to the RGA and a plasma cell which is used for optical gas analysis (OGA) which are two distinct locations in the DRGA system, and the latter of these locations has not been simulated previously. This study provides important design validation of the ITER DRGA time-dependent response as it relates to fuel-cycle and plasma heating control.

title: Multimodal Functional Maximum Correlation for Emotion Recognition
authors: Deyang Zheng, Tianyi Zhang, Wenming Zheng, Shujian Yu
abstract: Emotional states manifest as coordinated and heterogeneous physiological responses across central and autonomic systems, posing a fundamental challenge for multimodal representation learning in affective computing. Learning such joint dynamics is further complicated by scarce and subjective affective annotations, motivating the use of self-supervised learning (SSL). However, most existing SSL approaches rely on pairwise alignment objectives, which are insufficient to characterize the joint dependencies among more than two modalities and fail to capture higher-order interactions arising from coordinated brain-autonomic responses. To move beyond this limitation, we propose Multimodal Functional Maximum Correlation (MFMC), a principled SSL framework that maximizes higher-order multimodal dependence via a Dual Total Correlation (DTC) objective. By deriving a tight sandwich bound and optimizing it with a functional maximum correlation analysis (FMCA)-based trace surrogate, MFMC directly captures joint multimodal interactions without resorting to pairwise contrastive losses. Experiments on three public affective computing benchmarks demonstrate that MFMC consistently achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance under both subject-dependent and subject-independent protocols, highlighting its robustness to inter-subject variability. In particular, MFMC achieves substantial gains on CEAP-360VR, improving subject-dependent accuracy from 78.9% to 86.8% and subject-independent accuracy from 27.5% to 33.1% using the EDA signal alone, and remains highly competitive within 0.8 percentage points of the best-performing method on the most challenging EEG subject-independent split of MAHNOB-HCI. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/DY9910/MFMC</uri>.

title: Power Allocation and Pricing Strategy in Relay-Assisted Smart Grid Communication Network with Cooperative Jamming
authors: Kai Ma, Yifu Li, Pei Liu, Yazhou Yuan, Jifeng Liang, Zhixin Liu, Bo Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: The increasing openness and heterogeneity of modern communication networks pose serious challenges to trans mission security. This paper proposes a secure transmission strategy for a dual-layer network where spectrum is shared between electrical power communication (EPC) networks and the macrocell. Cooperative relay in EPC networks emit jamming signals to degrade eavesdroppers' reception, thereby enhancing macrocell security while guaranteeing power service quality. A utility cost model is established by statistically analyzing adjustment errors under direct load control, aiming to capture the analytical relationship between load tracking deviations and communication resources. Additionally, a utility function for the macrocell is formulated, balancing secure data transmission and cooperative jamming compensation. The relay power allocation and jamming pricing problems are jointly modeled as a Stack elberg game. A robust iterative algorithm based on backward induction is proposed to obtain the Stackelberg equilibrium. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method reduces utility cost and significantly improves macrocell profitability.

title: Efficient Frequency-Domain Modeling for Lossless Recompression of Variable-Rate JPEG Images
authors: Hui Xiang, Yifan Bian, Yuqi Li, Haotian Zhang, Li Li, Dong Liu
abstract: JPEG images are ubiquitous in various digital platforms, yet their compression efficiency is limited. Several lossless recompression methods have been proposed for JPEG images to further reduce storage and transmission costs. However, existing lossless recompression methods often neglect the frequency-specific characteristics, incurring suboptimal compression performance. These methods also struggle to support JPEG images with different bitrates efficiently. To address these limitations, we propose an efficient learned method that leverages frequency characteristics while addressing variable-rate challenges. We present a frequency-based masking model, which captures the frequency-specific dependencies across different discrete cosine transform coefficients. We suggest a frequency-specific probabilistic model that tailors entropy coding to the unique distributions of different frequency bands. We also incorporate the quantization table into the entropy model using a conditional convolution to support variable-rate images efficiently. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method surpasses the previous state-of-the-art single-rate method by an average of 5.24% bitrate saving over multiple JPEG quality factors, while achieving faster encoding and decoding speeds.

title: Large Language Model Chatbot with Retrievalaugmented Generation and Function Calling for Indonesian Article 21 Withholding Tax Support
authors: Verren Angelina Saputra, Marvella Shera Devi, Ethelind Septiani Metta, Rhio Sutoyo, Mahda Karina
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) plays a crucial role in the taxation domain, in spite of their occasional tendency to provide unreliable answers. As the Indonesian income tax system is highly complicated, this study introduces BeeTax, an Indonesian Withholding Tax Article 21 (WHT Article 21) chatbot that leverages Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to support users in understanding theoretical taxation concepts, and function calling to automate tax calculations. While prior studies have shown that RAG and function calling each improve the accuracy and reliability of LLMs, they have mainly been examined separately. BeeTax bridges this gap by integrating both mechanisms within a unified architecture, demonstrating their mutual benefit in a domain-specific application. The chatbot achieved Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment (RAGAs) scores of 0.71 (faithfulness), 0.81 (answer relevancy), 0.61 (answer correctness), and 0.79 (context precision and recall), indicating reliable retrieval and high generation accuracy in a technical tax domain. The function-calling mechanism achieved 91 % accuracy, confirming its high consistency in performing tax computations. The qualitative evaluations through experts' User Acceptance Testing (UAT) yielded pass rates of 85.71 % and 95.24 %, while a user survey assessing four usability aspects reported satisfaction scores of at least 4.57 out of 5. Overall, the results confirm that combining RAG and function calling improves the user experience, making BeeTax a practical and accessible Artificial Intelligence (AI) solution for WHT Article 21 assistance.

title: Status of Current L3Harris Technologies RTG Programs
authors: Leo Gard, Andrew Lane
abstract: L3Harris Technologies (formerly Aerojet Rocketdyne) is currently working on two major flight Radioisotope Power System (RPS) contracts. This paper will discuss the current status of those two RPS programs, the MultiMission Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generator (MMRTG) and the Next Generation RTG (NGRTG or Next Gen RTG). The MMRTG has powered two different missions to Mars and will be utilized during the upcoming mission to Saturn's moon, Titan (Dragonfly Mission). The Next Gen RTG is based on the previously utilized GPHS-RTG. The MMRTG is designed to support missions in atmospheric environments and the Next Gen RTG is designed for deep space vacuum missions. The Next Gen RTG has a higher power to weight ratio than the MMRTG due to these differences in environmental requirements. The MMRTG program is in process of completing the generator to be used in the Dragonfly Mission. The Next Gen RTG program is currently testing out the power conversion unicouples and demonstrating the manufacturing and assembly operations.

title: AI-Generated Marketing Content and Consumer Trust: Examining the Role of AI Disclosure
authors: Agung Stefanus Kembau, I Gede Wisnu Satria Chandra Putra, Felliks Feiters Tampinongkol, Arief Perdana Kumaat, Julius Sutrisno, Ali Wardhana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: AI-generated marketing content is increasingly used in contemporary digital advertising, raising critical questions regarding transparency and consumer trust. This study examines how AI disclosure influences trust in AIgenerated marketing content through perceived authenticity and deception concern, while also investigating the moderating role of consumption context. Using a quantitative approach, data were collected from 189 social media users in Indonesia and analyzed using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM). The results indicate that AI disclosure positively affects perceived authenticity and negatively influences deception concern. Perceived authenticity significantly enhances trust in AI-generated content, whereas deception concern weakens it. Moreover, the relationship between perceived authenticity and trust is moderated by consumption context, with stronger effects observed in hedonic settings compared to utilitarian ones. These findings underscore the importance of transparency-by-design in AIdriven marketing and demonstrate that consumer responses to AI-generated marketing content are context-dependent. This study contributes to AI marketing literature by integrating cognitive appraisal mechanisms and contextual factors into a unified trust formation framework, while offering practical insights for designing effective and ethically grounded AIenabled marketing communications.

title: A CUDA-Based Framework for Scalable 3D Drone Swarm Collision Avoidance Using Predictive Spatial Hashing
authors: Yash Ashish Hajare, Abhishek Gaddam, Arun M.
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Scaling collision avoidance for dense drone swarms beyond a few thousand agents quickly stops being a CPU problem and becomes a GPU problem: the amount of work grows too fast for a single core to keep up in real time.[1] In this work we describe a CUDA-based framework that tackles this issue using a predictive spatial hashing scheme, where each drone is assigned to a CUDA thread and future positions are hashed into a grid instead of current ones.[1] This look-ahead hashing lets the system catch possible collisions one step before they would normally occur, while Thrust radix sort groups drones by cell in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$O((N / P) \log N)$</tex> time and each drone checks only 26 neighboring cells, keeping the effective per-frame complexity at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$O(N \cdot k / P)$</tex> with constant <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k=26$</tex>.[4] [5] [13] The framework is implemented entirely on the GPU using the CUDA Runtime API, Thrust, and cuRAND, and was evaluated on an NVIDIA Tesla T4 running CUDA 12.8.[6] [8] For <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N=10,000$</tex> drones the GPU implementation completes a full collision-avoidance step in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 2 6 4 ~ m s}$</tex>, which is roughly a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$413 \times$</tex> speedup over a single-threaded -O2 CPU baseline that takes 113 ms and already violates the 33.3 ms budget for 30 fps. At <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N=50,000$</tex> drones, the full frame time is 0.87 ms and no postavoidance collisions are observed across the tested configurations, showing that the gap between CPU and GPU performance grows with swarm size rather than closing. The results indicate that exploiting GPU parallelism is not just an optimization, but a requirement, for real-time collision avoidance in large 3D drone swarms.[4] [15]

title: A Low-Power Retrofit RFID-Vibration-GSM Security System for Traditional Motorcycles: Design, Implementation, and Experimental Evaluation
authors: Habibie Satrio Nugroho, Nila Natalia, Ady Setyo Nugroho, Aditia Putra Hamid, Rosyid Ridlo Al-Hakim, Ade Muhammad Syamkirana Putra, Christosie Immanuel Wahyudi, Muhammad Yusro, Rikawarastuti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Motorcycle theft remains a pressing issue, particularly for older, traditional motorcycles that lack built-in security features found in modern models. This study presents an advanced Arduino-based Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) security system, specifically designed to protect traditional motorcycles by integrating RFID authentication, vibration detection, and real-time alert mechanisms. The system employs an RFID recognition module (Mifare RC522) with a 4 cm detection range, ensuring secure and efficient tag authentication. A SW420 vibration sensor is incorporated to detect unauthorized movements, triggering an alarm system composed of an LED indicator and buzzer when tampering occurs. The SIM800L GSM module enables real-time notifications to the motorcycle owner via SMS in case of a security breach. Power consumption analysis shows stable operation, with 4.50 Volts in standby mode and 4.43 Volts during authentication. Experimental results confirm a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 \%}$</tex> success rate in detecting authorized tags and identifying unauthorized access, with zero false positives. By offering an affordable, retrofit security solution without requiring significant modifications to traditional motorcycles, this system provides a bridge between vintage models and modern anti-theft technology. The findings highlight the potential of this security system to enhance the safety of traditional motorcycles, supporting vehicle owners in protecting their assets against theft.

title: Automated Competency Mapping for Lecturer Recruitment Using Machine Learning and Multisource Professional Data
authors: Fransiskus Adikara, Alaniah Nisrina, Eko Wahyu Prasetyo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The digital transformation of human resource management has necessitated specialized frameworks for talent acquisition, yet academic recruitment, specifically the selection of lecturer candidates, remains largely dependent on manual screening processes. This research proposes an automated competency mapping framework designed to enhance the efficiency of lecturer recruitment by synthesizing data from professional networking platforms and traditional curriculum vitae. Utilizing the scikit-learn library, the system employs Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Term FrequencyInverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) to transform unstructured textual data into standardized competency profiles. A preliminary evaluation involving 100 candidate samples demonstrates that baseline machine learning architectures, specifically Logistic Regression and Multinomial Naive Bayes, provide high precision in classifying candidates according to academic and pedagogical requirements. Usability assessments from human resource professionals further indicate that the integration of multi-source professional data significantly improves the objectivity and scalability of the initial screening phase. These findings suggest that automated mapping provides a robust foundation for identifying highpotential talent within specialized academic hiring environments.

title: Real-Time Flood Prediction System for Ciliwung River Using XGBoost Machine Learning
authors: Christopher M. M. Gijoh, Jennifer, Yoel Soengkono, Irene A. Lazarusli, Feliks V. P. Samosir, Aditya R. Mitra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Floods in Jakarta are a persistent issue causing significant economic disruption, primarily driven by the overflow of the Ciliwung River due to upstream rainfall. A limitation in current mitigation efforts is the lack of accurate, short-term early warning systems with sufficient lead time. This study aims to develop a real-time flood prediction system for the Manggarai Water Gate, a critical downstream point, using the Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) algorithm benchmarked against a Linear Regression baseline and a persistence (naïve) baseline. The method involves processing historical time-series data from December 2023 to December 2024, integrating rainfall and water levels from the upstream Katulampa Weir and midstream Depok Monitoring Post. A key contribution of this study is the feature engineering process, utilizing cross-correlation analysis to establish an 8hour lag time for Depok and a 12-hour lag time for Katulampa. The target variable is the Manggarai water level at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t+3$</tex> hours. The results demonstrate that the Hyperparameter-Tuned XGBoost model achieved a Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (NSE) of 0.9293 and a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 6.92 cm, outperforming both the Linear Regression baseline (MAE 7.46 cm) and the persistence baseline (MAE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim$</tex> 18-22 cm estimated) on average prediction error. The study concludes that while Linear Regression captures peak trends effectively, XGBoost offers superior precision for operational early warning systems with a 3-hour lead time, and that data-driven lag feature engineering effectively encodes hydrological travel time without explicit physical modeling.

title: Simple Data Model Approach to Integrate Face® Software Applications Designed from Disparate DSDMs
authors: Travis Rogers, Ryan Wolfe, Scott Tompkins
abstract: Software integration can be difficult, costly and time-consuming. Luckily, the Future Airborne Capability Environment® or FACE® Technical Approach offers methodologies that make integration easier. However, even when developers follow this technical approach, there can still be challenges to integration. One serious challenge occurs when two or more software applications need to be integrated, but each application's supporting message set and transport services originate from a different Domain-Specific Data Model (DSDM). DSDMs are key in creating the message sets that components within an application produce and consume. The process of rectifying the differences in disparate DSDMs to ensure components within a combined application can intercommunicate is daunting. This paper presents two methodologies to integrate software applications that come from disparate DSDMs. The first methodology uses a common data model to reproject both application's message sets from a single, Simple Data Model (SiDM). This allows the software to run on a single TSS. The second methodology applies a solution where both applications integrate while maintaining their own message sets, transport services, and DSDMs allowing all semantic meaning to remain unchanged. To achieve this SiDM is used to bridge the semantically meaningful message sets between two or more applications. This allows for two or more applications with disparate DSDMs to communicate using both their native data models and across-interface with the common data model. This paper will provide the methodology to construct SiDM and explain both integration methodologies. This paper will also reference nonproprietary examples where this technique is implemented. By implementing this methodology, applications become more universally useful as “off the shelf” products that can be integrated without major reconfiguration.

title: Autonomous Tip-and-Cue Earth-Observing Constellation Tasking with Reinforcement Learning
authors: Mark Stephenson, Hanspeter Schaub
abstract: While Earth-observing constellations often collect images from an a priori request list, this paradigm greatly limits the phenomena that can be observed: Emergent and unpredictable events are often valuable imaging targets. Although tip-and-cue architectures exist to image such events—usually with a “tipping” leader satellite that cues observations by the follower satellite(s)—these lack the flexibility or capacity desired from modern Earth-observing constellations. In this work, reinforcement learning is demonstrated as a way of autonomously and scalably tasking a homogenous constellation of satellites with scanning and imaging instruments. A per-agent policy is learned that is executable onboard each satellite and able to respond to the high-uncertainty environment, solving a problem that traditional pre-planning approaches cannot handle and demonstrating collaborative behavior between agents. As satellites are added to a constellation, the performance of the satellites working together grows faster than the number of satellites. Depending on the location of a satellite within the constellation, it may assume a specific role that biases it towards scanning or imaging.

title: Development of a Force Distribution Control System for Tactile Force Reproduction Using a Fabric Actuator
authors: Ayato Kuno, Shinichi Masaoka, Yanhong Peng, Yuki Funabora
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This study presents a CNN-based control system for reproducing distributed tactile forces using a fabric-based actuator. A fabric actuator (Funabot-Sleeve) was integrated with a pressure distribution sensor to realize an open-loop tactile force control system. A dataset consisting of pneumatic input commands and corresponding tactile force distributions was collected using this setup. The learned CNN-based controller successfully reconstructed overall force distributions for unseen test data, except for minor local deviations. For humanapplied touch data, reproducibility decreased due to structural constraints in the current actuator configuration, indicating the need for design optimization. These results demonstrate the feasibility of CNN-based tactile force control and highlight the design limitations of the fabric actuator composed of pneumatic muscle threads.

title: Integrated Attitude Control Architecture for Manipulator-Equipped SmallSats
authors: Bartłomiej Koszek, Laila Kazemi, Emese Kovács
abstract: As interest in on-orbit servicing, assembly, and debris removal grows, small satellite platforms must be adapted for contactbased operations using robotic manipulators. These missions introduce strong dynamic coupling between the manipulator and the bus, often with comparable mass and inertia, which challenges conventional Attitude Determination and Control Systems (ADCS). This work presents an ADCS architecture and control algorithm tailored to small satellites equipped with manipulators. A digital twin integrates a multibody dynamics model of the satellite-manipulator system with a full ADCS suite (reaction wheels, star tracker, gyroscope, accelerometer) and a quaternion-based PD controller. To mitigate internal disturbances induced by manipulator, a feedforward control layer anticipates and compensates reaction forces and torques at the manipulator base. The approach is demonstrated during the comissioning phase of the manipulator post de-tumble. Preliminary results indicate that manipulator disturbances can dominate environmental torques, necessitating dedicated compensation. The feedforward controller method improves pointing during slow manipulator motions at the cost of higher actuator workload; controller gains must adapt in real time to manipulator pose. By iterating within the digital twin, the ADCS design is optimized with manipulator behavior and operations, enabling robust, cost-effective control for small-satellite missions with complex dynamics. The use of the digital twin can extend the use of low-cost platforms into domains traditionally reserved for larger spacecraft while preserving systemlevel performance and reliability.

title: Uncovering the Drivers of TikTok Virality: Feature Importance Analysis of Hashtags Using Machine Learning
authors: Miecel Alicia Angel J, Malvin Ferdinand Tanzil, Noviyanti Tri Maretta Sagala, Lili Ayu Wulandhari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The effectiveness of hashtag usage on TikTok remains widely debated, and existing studies rarely provide quantitative evaluation of how specific hashtag combinations contribute to video virality. This study investigates the role of hashtag <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N}$</tex>-grams in predicting TikTok video engagement using an interpretable machine learning framework. A dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5, 8 1 5}$</tex> TikTok videos was analyzed using TF-IDF representations of hashtag <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N}$</tex>-grams (unigrams to trigrams) combined with contextual control features within a Random Forest modeling framework. The Random Forest Regressor achieved an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 6 0 8 0}$</tex>, while the classifier achieved a recall of 0.81 in identifying viral content. Feature attribution using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) indicates that early engagement signals, particularly log-transformed plays and shares, are the most influential predictors of virality. While certain hashtag <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N}$</tex>-grams contribute to content categorization and discoverability, their predictive influence is weaker compared to early engagement dynamics. Temporal posting features, such as posting hour and day, were among the least influential predictors. These findings demonstrate the value of interpretable machine learning in analyzing social media engagement patterns and provide empirical insights into how hashtag usage interacts with broader engagement dynamics on TikTok.

title: A Trust-Centric and Explainable Framework for Reliable Content Recommendation Under Information Uncertainty
authors: Nivedita V S, Premalatha K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The increasing prevalent conditions of noisy, biased and subject to varying levels of trust user feedback have further complicated recommender systems to be implemented in environments where reliability and transparency are of paramount importance. The paper presents a reliable content recommendation framework that is trust-based and explainable in situations of uncertainty of information. Using the CiaoDVD data that gives explicit user to user trust relations and ratings and reviews, our framework will collaboratively learn preferences, propagate trust, and filter according to uncertainty. We propose a trust-adaptive aggregation system that dynamically gives preference to recommendations depending on the strength of social trust cues, and an explainable explanation system that generates key influencing users of trust and meaningful review data that supports each recommendation. The uncertainty of information is addressed by the framework using confidencebased scoring which reduces the effects of the sparse or contrasting feedback. Consistent improvements over strong trust-aware and explainable baselines in the accuracy of recommendations, noise resistance, and faithfulness to explanations are shown by extensive experiments on CiaoDVD. The findings underline the usefulness of combining both trust modeling and explainability in creating reliable recommender systems in the uncertain environments.

title: Foundational Analysis of Safety Engineering Requirements (SAFER)
authors: Noga Chemo, Yaniv Mordecai, Yoram Reich
abstract: We introduce a framework for Foundational Analysis of Safety Engineering Requirements (SAFER), a model-driven methodology supported by Generative AI to improve the generation and analysis of safety requirements for complex safety-critical systems. Safety requirements are often specified by multiple stakeholders with uncoordinated objectives, leading to gaps, duplications, and contradictions that jeopardize system safety and compliance. Existing approaches are largely informal and insufficient for addressing these challenges. SAFER enhances Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) by consuming requirement specification models and generating the following results: (1) mapping requirements to system functions, (2) identifying functions with insufficient requirement specifications, (3) detecting duplicate requirements, and (4) identifying contradictions within requirement sets. SAFER provides structured analysis, reporting, and decision support for safety engineers. We demonstrate SAFER on an autonomous drone system, significantly improving the detection of requirement inconsistencies, enhancing both efficiency and reliability of the safety engineering process. We show that Generative AI must be augmented by formal models and queried systematically, to provide meaningful early-stage safety requirement specifications and robust safety architectures.

title: Development of Autonomous PHM for Bearing Fault Diagnosis
authors: David He, Miao He
abstract: Most existing Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) systems are developed using supervised learning approaches, which depend heavily on labeled data. In practice, collecting extensive datasets with labels from real-world assets is expensive, risky, and often impossible, particularly for safety-critical components such as bearings, gearboxes, and engines. This limitation makes it challenging for supervised PHM systems to maintain accurate models across various operating conditions and mission profiles, as they require frequent updates with newly labeled data.
