Self-supervised learning frameworks that continuously learn from abundant unlabeled operational data, adapt to new domains, and fuse heterogeneous sensor streams without relying on labeled failures represent attractive solutions to address the challenges toward fully autonomous, on-board PHM. In this paper, we introduce the Domain-Informed Functional Guidance Network (DiFG-Net), a fault diagnosis framework that fuses self-supervised functional pretraining with domain-adaptive fine-tuning. DiFG-Net first learns the backbone Prediction of Functionals for Masked Latents with two-view consistency augmentation (PFML-TCA) to preserve task-relevant temporal structures while enhancing representation robustness. At fine-tuning, to mitigate domain shift, we add a conditional CORrelation ALignment (CORAL) mechanism that aligns class-conditional means and covariances between source and target batches using confident pseudo-labels. DiFG-Net yields faster convergence, better calibration, and higher file-level accuracy versus PFML baselines. Experiments on plastic bearing fault diagnosis datasets demonstrate consistent accuracy gains over PFML baselines, particularly in cross-domain settings, while providing actionable diagnostic cues through the learned domain-informed functionals.

title: AI IoT Based Smart Body Analyzer: Multi-Parameter Health Monitoring and Early Risk Detection for Preventive Care
authors: T. Sangeetha, Vishmaya V, Sushma Sarayu K, Adithya Sharan S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The growing need of the constant physiological measurement and the early identification of abnormalities in health has boosted the creation of smart wearable health systems. This article reflects an AI-IoT-powered Smart Body Analyzer which is aimed at preventing risk and real-time multi-parameter health control. The suggested system will combine MAX30102, GSR, and MPU6050 sensors to measure the vital parameters of the organism and motion-related body motions, such as heart rate, blood oxygen saturation (SpO2), skin conductance, and body movement patterns. IoT framework captures sensor data and transfers it to a cloudbased storage and analysis system. The machine learning system based on the Random Forest algorithm is used to carry out the regression and classification tasks to calculate the overall health indices, including balance score, gait stability, chances of falls, sleep quality estimation, recovery rate of muscles, and the likelihood of fluid retention as well as the assessment of nutritional effects. The system also uses userreported inputs that are used to augment predictive analytics that contribute to the early detection of menstrual health abnormalities and thyroid-related conditions. The proposed framework uses multi-modal data fusion and the AI-powered contextual analysis, unlike traditional threshold-based monitoring systems, which deliver personalized and adaptive health insights. Web-based dashboard provides real time visualization, trends analysis and automatic alert notification to support making medical decisions in time. The efficiency of the system in the early detection of abnormalities, continuous monitoring of health, and scalable system implementation to preventive healthcare services is proved by experimental evaluation.

title: Preliminary Investigation of a Quasi-Direct-Drive Actuation for a Biomimetic Underwater Robot
authors: Muhammad Naveed Raza, Kaisei Harada, Shuto Uchikai, Yuto Konno, Keisuke Iguchi, Yusuke Ikemoto, Jun Shintake
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This study presents a biomimetic underwater robot driven by a quasi-direct-drive (QDD) actuation system designed to overcome the continuous torque limitations of conventional direct drive (DD) configurations. The QDD system integrates a brushless DC motor with a low-ratio $4: 1$ planetary gearbox, enabling efficient torque amplification while maintaining high responsiveness and mechanical simplicity. A robotic fish prototype was developed and experimentally characterized in terms of thrust force, tail beat amplitude, and swimming speed. Results demonstrated that the QDD configuration produced a peak thrust of approximately 52 N at 10 Hz, which was 2.7 times greater than that of the DD design. The QDD robot also maintained a tail beat amplitude of about 200 mm at 7 Hz and achieved a maximum swimming speed of $175 \mathrm{~mm} / \mathrm{s}$ at 5 Hz, representing a 16% improvement over the DD robot. These findings verify that the QDD actuation effectively enhances propulsion performance by sustaining large tail kinematics at high frequencies. The proposed QDD approach combines the structural simplicity and robustness of DD systems with improved torque capacity, offering a promising framework for high-performance and agile biomimetic underwater robots.

title: Explainable AI for Regional Climate and Extreme Rainfall Analysis in Karnataka
authors: Akshatha M, Murali S, Krushna Chandra Gouda
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Karnataka exhibits one of India's steepest climatic gradients, shaped by complex interactions among topography, monsoon dynamics, and large-scale climate teleconnections. Traditional regional climate studies are constrained by sparse observations, linear assumptions, and limited interpretability. To overcome these challenges, we propose an integrated artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) framework tailored to Karnataka's climatic complexity. The framework fuses multisource datasets, including ground observations, satellite products, and reanalysis fields, and leverages unsupervised learning for climate regionalization, explainable supervised models for driver attribution, and deep learning architectures for extreme event modeling. Specifically, Random Forests, XGBoost, SHAP-based interpretability, CNN-LSTM hybrids, and physicsinformed neural networks are combined within a unified pipeline. Our approach delineates homogeneous climate zones, quantifies dominant rainfall drivers, and captures extreme events with uncertainty estimates. This interpretable, scalable AI/ML framework supports regional climate adaptation, providing actionable insights for water resource management, agriculture, and policy planning in Karnataka and other climate-sensitive regions.

title: Snapping Actuators with Asymmetric and Sequenced Motion
authors: Xin Li, Ye Jin, Mohsen Jafarpour, Hugo de Souza Oliveira, Edoardo Milana
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Snapping instabilities in soft structures offer a powerful pathway to achieve rapid and energy-efficient actuation. In this study, an eccentric dome-shaped snapping actuator is developed to generate controllable asymmetric motion through geometry-induced instability. Finite element simulations and experiments reveal consistent asymmetric deformation and the corresponding pressure characteristics. By coupling four snapping actuators in a pneumatic network, a compact quadrupedal robot achieves coordinated wavelike locomotion using only a single pressure input. The robot exhibits frequency-dependent performance with a maximum speed of $72.78 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at 7.5 Hz. These findings demonstrate the potential of asymmetric snapping mechanisms for physically controlled actuation and lay the groundwork for fully untethered and efficient soft robotic systems.

title: An Efficient Arbitration Mechanism for AXI-Based Multi-Master Shared Memory Architecture
authors: Ravi V, P. Babu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: In a Modern SOC (System-On-Chip) design, the architecture integrates many numbers of processing units (CPUs, DMA controllers, and hardware accelerators) on a single chip (One chip). These integrated units act as “masters” since they initiate memory requests and all of them connect to shared memory through the AMBA AXI interconnect, which controls and coordinates access, so that all components can use the memory efficiently. As the number of masters increases, like GPU (Graphics Processing Unit), Network Interface Controller (NIC) etc., arbitration becomes a crucial/key factor in system throughput. Latency and fairness. For high-priority masters, the Fixed-priority arbitration provides masters access to memory quickly, but all the masters' requests at the same time create heavy traffic. Low-priority masters may last forever and it can cause starvation. In round-robin, arbitration gives each master a chance to access memory and ensure fairness among masters, but increased latency due to time-sensitive masters having to wait longer. Proposed paper a threshold-based hybrid arbitration mechanism for AXI-based multi-master shared memory architectures combining deterministic priority servicing (high-priority masters get fast, predicable access to shared memory up to a certain threshold in fixed-priority) with starvation-aware round-robin scheduling (If a low-priority master waits too long (crosses a threshold), it gets a turn regardless of priority). The hybrid arbitration mechanism monitors which masters have been granted access using bounded starvation counters and if a master waits too long, its priority is temporarily elevated, so that the master gets access within a finite number of arbitration cycles. So, it prevents starvation while still giving fast service to high-priority traffic. The proposed paper develops a mathematical model to compute the following worst-case latency bounds (maximum timer a master may wait), starvation guarantees (no masters waits forever), and fairness properties (using Jain's Fairness Index, which is a standard metric to qualify fair resource sharing). This design architecture is implemented in VHDL and simulated & verified using ModelSim. This simulations results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid arbitration approach effectively balances fairness and throughput while reducing starvation and is to evaluate its performance in AMBA AXI-based multi-master SoC environments.

title: A Multi-Scale Attention-Based Temporal Network for IGBT Junction Temperature Prediction
authors: Zheng-Wei Du, Kai-Yi Yang, Zekai Bi, Rui Wu, Wen-Yan Yin
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Insulated-gate bipolar transistors (IGBTs) are critical in power converters, and junction temperature monitoring is essential for thermal management and reliability. This paper proposes a multi-scale attention-based temporal network integrating parameter and temporal attention mechanisms with long short-term memory (LSTM) units to predict junction temperatures of four chips in a single-phase IGBT leg. Results show the model outperforms LSTM-only approaches, accurately tracking temperature evolution during heating, steady-state, and cooling phases, capturing multi-chip thermal coupling, and significantly reducing prediction errors. The approach demonstrates high accuracy and reliability for IGBT module junction temperature prediction.

title: Copyright Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)

title: Low-Power ML Acceleration on FPGAs
authors: Ezekiel Nwokolo, Tahmina Akter Anondi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The rapid evolution of machine learning (ML) techniques for sensor data analytics offers significant potential for Internet-of-Things (IoT) endpoint applications. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are crucial for 2D pattern recognition tasks, yet their high computational demands often require dedicated hardware acceleration. This paper presents a design methodology for training and deploying ML models with scalable hardware acceleration, specifically targeting lowpower, resource-limited embedded Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs). A custom reduced floating-point quantization scheme and quantization-aware training method are introduced to improve the generalization and accuracy of CNNs, using less than 8-bit floating-point quantization for trainable parameters. The approach incorporates a fully customizable tensor processor (TP) implementing a pipelined vector dot-product with a hybrid of custom floating-point and logarithmic approximations, significantly reducing energy consumption and resource utilization while preserving inference accuracy. The proposed methodology is demonstrated through a CNN-based sensor analytic application for structural health monitoring (SHM) on the XC7Z007S FPGA, achieving a peak power efficiency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.5 \text{GFLOP} / \mathrm{s} / \mathrm{W}$</tex>, a runtime acceleration of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$55 \times$</tex>, and an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$808 \times$</tex> reduction in energy consumption compared to traditional embedded CPU implementations. This work underscores the potential of integrating ML acceleration on low-power embedded FPGAs, paving the way for more efficient and scalable IoT applications.

title: Addressing Extra Voices and Background Noise in Continuous Speech Monitoring: A Case Study on Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
authors: Salaar Liaqat, Daniyal Liaqat, Tatiana Son, Robert Wu, Andrea Gershon, Eyal de Lara, Alex Mariakakis
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Continuous speech monitoring using smartphones and smartwatches offers numerous opportunities to detect and predict health deterioration. However, the algorithms used to deliver these benefits must account for the realities of periodic audio recording in the wild, including other voices and overlapping noise. To address the former without clean sample voice recordings, we use speaker diarization to identify the person who speaks the most throughout a given day. To address the latter without compromising the validity of extracted speech features, we leverage the intuition that a reliable feature should remain unaltered by a noise-reduction technique when background noise is minimal. We examine both techniques in the context of a longitudinal dataset collected from 16 patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) over roughly 3 months. Our best model achieved an AUROC of 0.86 when predicting the presence of respiratory symptoms, outperforming baseline models that rely solely on voice activity detection and noise reduction.

title: Keynote - Federated AGI: A Generational Opportunity for Pervasive Computing
authors: Nicholas D. Lane
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Current scaling laws indicate that future advances in AI will hinge on access to massive amounts of compute and data. How will we obtain the computing power and data resources required to sustain continued AI progress? I believe all roads lead to federated learning, and approaches of this kind. And, in turn, this change represents the biggest opportunity for pervasive computing in a generation: with the right research priorities and execution, pervasive systems can provide the critical infrastructure for tomorrow’s frontier AI, supplying both compute and data; serving as the missing piece for the AI miracle to continue.The AI world may not realize it yet, but it is ready for this radical change. Today, frontier models are trained on essentially the same web-scraped data and rely on data centers that can only scale through unsustainable capital investment. Instead, pervasive systems of sensors, personal devices, scientific instruments, space satellites, vehicles, and embedded computers offer both a limitless stream of new data and a modular, scalable network of compute. Once resources of this scale and quality are unlocked, today’s AI infrastructure paradigm will not compete. Soon, decentralized and federated techniques built on pervasive systems will be how the strongest LLMs are trained; and in time, how aspirational capabilities like AGI will finally be achieved.In this talk, I will describe why the future of AI will be federated, and describe early solutions from Flower Labs and the Cambridge ML Systems Lab (CaMLSys), which address the technical challenges of this shift and place pervasive computing and communication at the core of how tomorrow’s AI is built.

title: Committees
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)

title: Novel Suite of Sensors for Recording Spatial, Temporal, and Spectral Optical Lightning Signals
authors: Grant Soehnel, Garrett Giddings, Steve Brown, Grace Dean
abstract: Lightning is by far the most common fast transient optical signal seen by space-based Earth observing sensors. Dedicated optical lightning sensors have historically been limited in their geographical coverage, spatial resolution, temporal resolution, and/or spectral resolution. Sandia National Laboratories has designed and built a low-cost multi-sensor payload to address the gaps in available optical lightning data. This payload includes a set of 16 spectral band-pass radiometers, a high-speed framing camera, and an event-based imager, which are all triggered by a fast-changing optical signal in a wide-band radiometer. All radiometers and cameras are synchronized to collect time-correlated data. The payload is housed inside an air-tight enclosure at 1 atm to reduce the risk to COTS components in the space environment. The first mission will be a threemonth flight on the International Space Station (ISS). The radiometer sample rate is 96 kHz with spectral bands ranging from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$400-1650 \text{nm}$</tex>. The framing camera will run at up to 1 KHz and provide 0.8 km spatial resolution on the ground. The Prophesee EVK4 event-based camera has an effective temporal resolution approaching <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 ~ k H z}$</tex> and spatial resolution of 0.25 km on the ground. The optical and opto-mechanical hardware for the radiometers consists of mostly off-the-shelf commercially available components. A unique testbed has been built to calibrate the radiometer responses to known input optical flux as a function of field angle. This mostly automated process involves holding the entire payload with a robotic arm, centering each radiometer aperture to the light source, and tilting the payload to sweep the field-of-view angle. This capability allows a payload with uncertain alignment tolerances to still record radiometrically accurate data.

title: Smartphone Authentication Using Face ID with Mask
authors: Njoud Majed Alharbi, Awny Sayed
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Face recognition has become a key technology for user authentication in modern smartphones. However, the widespread use of face masks during the COVID-19 outbreak has posed significant challenges due to facial occlusion. To address this issue, this paper proposes a hybrid face recognition framework that integrates deep learning embeddings with handcrafted texture descriptors to improve robustness under masked face conditions. Four hybrid configurations are evaluated by combining FaceNet or MobileNetV2 with either Local Binary Patterns (LBP) or Gabor wavelets. Experiments evaluate identity recognition performance under masked, unmasked, and mixed scenarios using both the Real-World Masked Face Dataset (RMFRD) and the proposed Real-World Masked and Unmasked Face Dataset (RWMUF). Notably, the FaceNet with LBP approach achieves high recognition accuracies of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$98.63 \%, 99.21 \%$</tex>, and 99.14 % for masked, unmasked, and mixed faces, respectively, on the RWMUF dataset, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$96.81 \%, 98.81 \%$</tex>, and 96.48 % on RMFRD, clearly outperforming other configurations, and is integrated into a mobile application for real-time face recognition. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining deep learning embeddings with handcrafted features for robust masked face recognition. The RWMUF dataset is available at: https://github.com/NjoudAl-harbi/A-Real-World-Masked-and-Unmasked-Face-Dataset-RWMUF-Dataset-

title: Co-Designed Multi-Objective Fault Detection Filtering for Networked Switched Positive Systems
authors: Shuo Li, Xiaodan Liu, Choon Ki Ahn, Zhengrong Xiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This study addresses the multi-objective L-/L1 fault detection filtering design for switched positive systems (SPSs) subject to a mode-dependent minimum dwell time (MD-MDT) constraint utilizing a dynamic event-triggered strategy (DETS). First, a novel linear copositive dynamic event-triggered condition is proposed for systems with positivity constraints. Next, sufficient and necessary conditions are derived to ensure that the designed fault detection filter provides a positive asymptotic estimation with an L-/L1 performance index. To address the MDMDT constraint, a novel discretized linear copositive Lyapunov function (DLCLF) approach is developed to establish sufficient conditions for ensuring the corresponding augmented system is exponentially stable. Furthermore, by employing the matrix decomposition technology to design filter matrices, an effective mode-dependent piecewise design scheme is proposed for the fault detection filtering of SPSs in a linear programming (LP) form. The developed LP rather than linear matrix inequality (LMI) based scheme features a simpler structure, lower computational demands, and less conservativeness. Finally, three examples are exhibited to validate the achieved results.

title: Estimating Series Battery Pack Capacity From Charge–Discharge Behavior at Unilateral Boundaries
authors: Songtao Ye, Dou An, Kunlei Yu, Xiaoyu Shu, Chun Wang, Tao Zhang, Zhe Shan, Huan Xi
abstract: Battery pack capacity estimation is crucial for improving the performance, safety, and reliability of energy storage systems. However, the inherent cell inconsistency and unobservable internal electrochemical reactions make this task challenging, especially when the battery behavior is only partially observed. To address this challenge, this article proposes a novel framework, UniCap, to estimate the capacity of series battery packs by utilizing partial behavior observed near the lower voltage boundary. Within this framework, the traditional estimation process is reformulated into two tractable subtasks. First, a hybrid K-nearest neighbors method is developed to nondestructively quantify unreleased capacity caused by cell inconsistency. Second, a cross-stream cell-wise transformer is proposed to predict the cell behavior and corresponding capacity at the unknown upper voltage boundary. Based on the above, the lower bound of the pack capacity can be inferred. Without requiring full charge or discharge cycles, the proposed framework provides an efficient solution for battery pack capacity estimation. Validated on a large-scale industrial dataset comprising 1757 battery packs, the framework achieves state-of-the-art performance with a mean absolute error of 0.0968 Ah. Notably, compared with existing industrial workflows, the proposed method significantly reduces test duration with only marginal accuracy degradation, thereby improving automation and production efficiency.

title: Verification of SpaceAGORA.jl 6-DoF Dynamics Using University of Michigan SmallSats Telemetry
authors: Evan Yu, James Cutler, Giusy Falcone
abstract: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{b} \boldsymbol{s} \boldsymbol{t r} \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{c} \boldsymbol{\text{-High-fidelity six-degree-of-freedom simulation is}}$</tex> a key enabler for developing and verifying autonomous guidance, navigation, and control algorithms. This paper validates SpaceAGORA.jl, an open-source Julia-based simulator that propagates fully coupled orbital and rigid-body attitude dynamics with modular environmental and actuator models. Validation is performed by comparing orbital and attitude dynamics with Basilisk simulations and flight telemetry from operational University of Michigan SmallSat missions (CYGNSS and GRIFEX). Results demonstrate a maximum position error compared to Basilisk within approximately 65 meters and attitude error on the order of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \mathrm{e}-2$</tex> radians. Comparisons with real-world CYGNSS and GRIFEX telemetry indicate that SpaceAGORA.jl accurately replicates spacecraft translational dynamics, achieving relative position differences within 1% or less, relative to the total position magnitude. In addition, a telemetry-driven replay of a CYGNSS slew demonstrates that the simulator reproduces measured attitude evolution when supplied with a wheel torque/momentum history consistent with telemetry. These validation results establish SpaceAGORA.jl as a reliable simulation environment for developing and validating autonomy and adaptive GNC algorithms under realistic operational conditions.

title: Olfactory Brain-on-Chip in Microgravity for Accelerated Aging and Drug Discovery
authors: Vivian C. Norris, Jerry Yang Olfera, Zara M. Patel, Kelvin Cooper, Sandeepa Dey, Parnian Lak
abstract: We present the first planned deployment of a patient-derived human olfactory brain-on-chip (OChip) aboard the International Space Station (ISS) to model Parkinson's disease (PD) progression and therapeutic intervention under microgravity. OChip recreates the human olfactory (smell) pathway's flow of signals and molecules- from olfactory epithelium to olfactory bulb and then to the cerebral cortex- in a tricompartmental platform with unidirectional axonal connectivity. Olfactory epithelium neurons are obtained via minimally invasive nasal swabbing from early-stage PD patients with hyposmia, or reduced sense of smell. These neurons carry endogenous pathological proteins, enabling the study of known disease hallmarks as well as novel early disease signatures absent in stem cell and organoid models previously studied on the ISS. This study will assess to what degree microgravity accelerates neuronal aging and pathological protein propagation, whether disease characteristics can be observed on OChip, and whether therapeutics such as prasinezumab (a PD drug in clinical trials) and our proprietary olfactory drug can slow the progression of pathological proteins. Parallel flight and ground OChips will be analyzed by daily brightfield imaging, compartment-resolved biochemical assays, and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry–based drug distribution tracking. Based on prior research by other groups, we hypothesize that microgravity-accelerated aging will compress disease-relevant biological processes into weeks and that our therapeutic interventions will reduce propagation of pathological PD proteins, validating OChip in space as a translational platform for drug discovery.

title: A Full Bridge Inverter for High Frequency Induction Heating of Small Rebar Workpiece
authors: Ittikorn Wongsoison, Thananop Leesupananon, Napanan Klinthoop, Juthathip Haema
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a full-bridge inverter for high-frequency induction heating of a small rebar workpiece. The proposed system comprises six main components: a step-down transformer, a full-wave bridge rectifier, a pulse-width modulation (PWM) signal generator, a full-bridge inverter utilizing power MOSFETs, a high-frequency transformer, and the induction coil with the workpiece. The rebar specimen, measuring <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.2-\text{cm}$</tex> in width and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25-\text{cm}$</tex> in length, is heated via a double 2 -turn split copper winding. Experimental results show that the workpiece reaches a temperature of at least 740° C within 60 seconds. The system delivers an output heating power of approximately 1.10 kW, demonstrating its effectiveness for small-scale induction heating applications.

title: Dual-Degree-of-Freedom DPWM Strategy for High-Power-Density VSC in Urban Power Systems
authors: Haizhou Ying, Ye Tao, Pengfei Chen
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: High-density urban power systems impose severe thermal and power quality constraints on Voltage Source Converters (VSC) where traditional modulation strategies often fail to balance switching losses and harmonic performance. To address this issue, this paper proposes an improved Discontinuous Pulse Width Modulation (DPWM) strategy utilizing dual-degree-of-freedom parametric regulation defined by clamp width ψ and phase shift δ. This approach dynamically reconstructs the zero-sequence voltage component to actively shape switching sequences. Specifically, the phase shift compensates for the power factor angle to align the clamping window with the peak current region for maximum loss suppression while the clamp width enables a flexible trade-off between efficiency and harmonic distortion. The proposed algorithm relies on simple algebraic operations to facilitate real-time implementation in digital controllers without complex look-up tables. Electro-thermal simulations using the PLECS platform demonstrate that the proposed strategy significantly reduces steady-state junction temperatures and thermal cycling amplitudes compared to traditional SVPWM. These results validate the method as an efficient software-based solution for enhancing the thermal reliability of compact power equipment in urban grids.

title: Attention-Based Graph Reinforcement Learning for Large-Scale Construction Project Scheduling
authors: Deepika Dayalan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: In essence, construction project scheduling implies coordinating a given set of interdependent tasks, satisfying precedence relationships, and resource limitations. As the size of projects increases, so does the need to identify the structural relationships among tasks within projects to make scheduling decisions. For scheduling purposes, many existing RL approaches use a flattened feature vector to represent the project state, which could fail to preserve the dependency structure between tasks. This paper introduces an attention-based graph reinforcement learning framework for large-scale scheduling of construction projects. A construction project is represented by a directed acyclic graph where nodes represent tasks and edges represent precedence constraints in the proposed approach. A graph neural network is developed to learn task embeddings through information propagation over the project structure. An attention mechanism is then deployed on the feasible tasks for structural dependencies and resource availability to select scheduling actions. The framework is tested using synthetic construction project instances for different graph sizes and resources. The empirical observations demonstrate that graph-based task representation leads to scalable scheduling and lower project makespan outcomes, compared to multilayer perceptron reinforcement learning models and classical heuristic scheduling strategies. Overall, these results show that graph neural networks and attention mechanisms can be a useful addition to reinforcement learning when dealing with complex construction scheduling problems.

title: GridML-Plus: An Improved Machine Learning Model for Predicting Grid Stabilities in Power Grids
authors: Md Ariful Islam, Zhangjie Liu, Mei Su
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Grid stability prediction uses machine learning (ML) to analyze complex power system behavior and prevent disruptions across voltage–frequency, rotor angle, transient, small-signal, thermal, and operational-contingency stability conditions. This research presents GridML-Plus, a hybrid ML framework designed for accurate and scalable prediction of seven grid stability classes, including voltage–frequency instability, rotor angle instability, thermal instability, transient instability, small-signal instability, operational instability, and contingency-related instability. The framework integrates Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for structural representation learning with a Decision Integrated Tree (DIT) classifier to enhance interpretability and robustness. A fully connected graph is constructed to model global feature interactions, while node embeddings capture relational dependencies critical for distinguishing subtle stability variations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GridML-Plus achieves 98.92% accuracy, 0.989 precision and recall, outperforming classical machine learning baselines and matching the consistency of advanced ensemble methods. The model exhibits strong generalization across the multi-class classification space and maintains stable training dynamics, with performance improving steadily across thirty epochs. Comparative evaluations confirm that GridML-Plus effectively balances computational efficiency, predictive reliability, and interpretability, making it suitable for both research and practical grid-monitoring environments. Future work will enhance GridML-Plus for real-time streaming, scalable graph construction, edge deployment, cross-grid generalization, advanced multi-label stability modeling, and practical utility integration for operational deployment.

title: Mirrored Surface Use and Measurement in Spacecraft Assembly and Test
authors: Robert Elliott
abstract: This white paper and presentation explains how flat mirrors and cubes are used on spacecraft and how they are used for subsystem alignments. It describes early measurement techniques using optical tooling and manual approaches. The advantages and disadvantages of these measurement methods will be discussed including how different measurement techniques affect the accuracy of results. Improper measurement techniques are discussed including the reasons why they are not acceptable. Instructions on how to use a modern laser tracker to measure a mirrored surface are discussed in detail including equipment setup, best practice and software commands using the Spatial Analyzer software package. Finally, a case study that demonstrates schedule savings resulting from transitioning to modern tools is given that shows the schedule and cost advantages of using a laser tracker for these measurements.

title: Spatial-Temporal Data Augmentation and Prediction in Power Systems Based on WGAN-ConvLSTM
authors: Qing Peng, Wenjun Pang, Yaodong Li, Xianfu Gong, Xian Zhang, Guibin Wang, Mahmoud Mohamed Sayed Mohamed Hemdan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Driven by global carbon neutrality targets, the widespread integration of renewable energy and electric vehicles defines modern power systems. However, intermittent renewable generation and stochastic EV charging introduce severe bidirectional uncertainty, threatening grid stability. To address this, we propose WGAN-ConvLSTM, a novel hybrid spatiotemporal forecasting framework. The WGAN module overcomes data scarcity by generating high-fidelity synthetic samples that strictly align with real-world distributions. Subsequently, the ConvLSTM network leverages this augmented data to extract complex spatial correlations and temporal evolutionary dynamics, enabling high-precision, collaborative forecasting of renewable generation and EV charging loads.

title: From Ground Tests to Orbit: ECSS Error Characterization of SentinelTRAC
authors: Laila Kazemi, Colin Huber, Vincent Yim
abstract: Redwire's SentinelTRAC star tracker has achieved significant flight heritage, with more than 100 first-generation units currently operating on orbit across a variety of missions. This large on-orbit population, combined with extensive ground qualification and simulation using a combination of a software-in-the-loop approach, SentinelTWIN, and a hardware-in-the-loop approach, SentinelSIM, has enabled a detailed assessment of performance against the ECSS-E-ST-60-20C framework. Results are presented for low-frequency spatial error (LFSE) and high-frequency spatial error (HFSE) derived from both controlled ground campaigns and on-orbit telemetry. Comparisons between expected and measured performance highlight the influence of factors such as stray light, detector readout noise, and star magnitude distribution. The analysis demonstrates how individual contributors manifest differently in ground versus flight conditions, and how blended solutions can mitigate variability between star fields and sensor heads. By aligning observed behaviors with ECSS-defined error categories, a clear quantitative baseline of SentinelTRAC's performance is established. These results not only validate the robustness of the first-generation design but also provide opportunities for further improvements. Building on this foundation, a road map is outlined for the next SentinelTRAC generation, ensuring that lessons learned from both laboratory analysis and flight operations directly inform future performance improvements and resilience in diverse mission environments.

title: Rotor Dynamics and Sprayer Efficiency in Agricultural UAVs
authors: Harrison Dean, Srikanth Bashetty
abstract: The use of rotary-wing Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) in agriculture for tasks such as pesticide spraying and field mapping has recently been increasing. Their ability to target specific areas, minimize chemical waste, and adapt to diverse field conditions positions them as invaluable tools in precision farming. However, the airflow generated by the rotors can disrupt droplet dispersion, which in turn reduces the efficacy of pesticide applications. This study examines how UAV design affects sprayer efficiency, with a particular focus on rotor dynamics and airflow interactions. Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations were employed to assess the performance of various sprayer configurations that differed in both the number and spacing of the equipped <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 1 0}$</tex>-degree fan nozzles. An initial literature review informed the setup of the simulations used to model the behaviors of the UAV rotors and the sprayer system. The actuator disk approach was employed to model rotor-induced airflow in ANSYS Fluent, with spray behavior simulated using the Discrete Phase Model. Particle tracking was used to estimate deposition area and distribution for each configuration. Results indicate that a two-nozzle setup with 0.6 -meter spacing provides the most practical balance between coverage area, particle uniformity, and ease of integration. While the actuator disk method simplifies rotor modeling, allowing the simulations to serve as a foundation for future experimental validation, this comes at the cost of bladespecific effects being omitted. Future work will involve integrating the sprayer system into a hexacopter UAV for flight testing, focusing on spray uniformity under varying flight conditions. These findings contribute to the optimization of UAV-based spraying systems and support their broader implementation in precision agriculture.

title: Rethinking Contingency in Infrastructure Cost Estimates: Using Smart Heuristics to Address Epistemic Uncertainty
authors: Peter Love, Lavagnon A. Ika, Jane Matthews, Weili Fang
abstract: When estimating cost contingency, aleatory and epistemic uncertainties are often treated probabilistically in the same manner as risk, while residual uncertainty is typically embedded implicitly within contingency allowances without explicit representation. In early project stages, assumptions are unstable, scope definition is incomplete, and small input changes can lead to large variations in estimated outcomes. Such conditions can create overconfidence in probability-based results, resulting in contingencies that underestimate epistemic effects and inadequately account for residual conditions that cannot be parameterized. The article demonstrates how epistemic uncertainty can be explicitly incorporated into pre-contract contingency estimates to improve risk management and produce more credible predictions of final construction costs. A decomposed contingency approach is adopted to separate probabilistic (aleatory), epistemic, and residual components, enabling their formalized representation and integration. Aleatory uncertainty associated with identifiable risks is treated probabilistically, while epistemic uncertainty—arising from incomplete information, scope development, and unresolved interfaces—is addressed using smart heuristics: simple, empirically grounded decision rules informed by ecological rationality. Illustrative examples show how heuristics such as “take-the-best,” tallying, and recognition can be applied at different project stages to refine epistemic allowances as information improves. Residual uncertainty is treated as a bounded, governance-managed provision.

title: vProChain: Efficient Provenance Verification in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)
authors: Jiamin Deng, Zhe Peng, Chuan Zhang, Shuhang Gu, Xin Xie, Bin Xiao
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) has been widely deployed to enable real-time monitoring and automation. Within IIoT-driven production, supply chain management plays a critical role, necessitating verifiable provenance to ensure the authenticity and traceability of goods across multi-stakeholder networks. While blockchain provides a tamper-proof foundation, traditional storage structures suffer from unsecured data integrity, poor query efficiency, and scalability over provenance data. To address these challenges, we propose vProChain, an efficient provenance verification system to support verifiable and parallel queries over graph-structured provenance data. First, we design an Adaptive DAG Verkle Tree (ADVT) that deterministically maps supply chain dependencies into a graph-native authenticated data structure, enabling constant-size proofs and low-overhead verification. Second, we introduce the Merkle Inverted Patricia Trie (MIPT) to facilitate fast, verifiable multi-dimensional Boolean queries. Third, we develop a parallel provenance query algorithm that accelerates multi-hop path retrieval via consistent hashing and weighted bipartite matching. Finally, formal security analysis and extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that vProChain can provide provable cryptographic guarantees for the soundness of provenance proofs and the completeness of query retrievals, while achieving high query efficiency in a large-scale IIoT environment.

title: An Adaptive Semantic Pattern Matching Transformer for Traffic Flow Prediction
authors: Chen-Yang Cao, Yin-Xin Bao, Weiwei Jiang, Quan Shi
abstract: Accurate traffic flow prediction is essential for intelligent transportation systems, yet most existing methods still forecast future traffic states mainly from the current historical input window and its derived representations. In real traffic systems, however, similar recent observations may correspond to different future evolutions under different latent traffic regimes, while short-term disturbances can further mislead input-local modeling. To address this issue, we propose an Adaptive Semantic Pattern Matching Transformer (ASPMformer) for traffic flow prediction. Rather than departing from the input-driven forecasting paradigm, ASPMformer complements it in three ways: a Context Embedding module enhances local semantic dependencies between adjacent time steps and reduces sensitivity to short-term fluctuations; an explicit spatiotemporal pattern repository enables the current input to be matched with reusable representative traffic patterns in both temporal and spatial dimensions; and a multi-granularity temporal modeling module enlarges the receptive field to capture long-range traffic trends. Extensive experiments on five public benchmark datasets, including PeMS03, PeMS04, PeMS07, PeMS08, and SZ-taxi, show that ASPMformer achieves competitive and consistent improvements over strong baselines. The results further suggest that the proposed pattern repository is particularly beneficial under heterogeneous and disturbance-prone traffic conditions, where relying solely on the current historical window is less sufficient. For the main content of the model, see https://github.com/ccy123q/ASPMformer.

title: EdgeSense: A Hybrid TinyML and Deep Learning Framework for SNR-Aware Adaptive Data Rate in LoRaWAN
authors: Muhammad Ali Lodhi, Xiaobing Sun, Khalid Mahmood, Anum Lodhi
abstract: In the context of Low Power Wide Area Networks (LPWANs), ensuring reliable communication in dynamic environments, especially for mobile edge devices (EDs), poses significant challenges. Traditional Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) mechanisms, while effective in static conditions, fail to adapt to the rapidly changing channel conditions that are characteristic of mobile networks. To address these limitations, we propose EdgeSense, a hybrid solution that dynamically estimates the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) with high accuracy and adapts in real-time to varying network conditions. EdgeSense combines edge intelligence with centralized temporal modeling to enable intelligent adjustment of the spreading factor (SF) and transmission power (TP). Unlike conventional rule-based approaches, which rely on static thresholds and statistical approximations, EdgeSense allows for intelligent decision-making based on real-time SNR estimations, leading to improved Packet Success Ratio (PSR) and reduced energy consumption. This solution is particularly beneficial in mobile IoT applications, where resource constraints and mobility often compromise network performance.

title: Stable Matching-Based Two-Phase Resource Allocation Framework for Multi-UAV-Assisted Covert Communications
authors: Shuang Zhang, Wen Tian, Xin Sun
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) assisted covert communication networks, a significant component of the secure Internet of Things (IoT), comprise highly maneuverable aerial relays, massive ground-based sensor nodes (GNs), legitimate receivers and a warden that integrate signal-sensing and detection capabilities. However, existing works mainly focus on energy-efficient relaying or trajectory optimization for UAV-assisted sensor networks, neglecting the joint impact of limited battery capacity of GNs and the stringent requirement for UAVs to avoid detection by the warden, which will lead to severe energy waste and a low covert rate. To tackle this issue, we propose a stable matching-based two-phase resource allocation framework for multi-UAV-assisted covert communications (STRMC) under symmetric information. Specifically, we introduce the minimum weight stable matching (MWSM) algorithm to derive the energy-optimal and covert-rate-optimal pairings in each phase, leveraging the structural properties of all stable matchings and graph-theoretic techniques. Simulation results show that the proposed STRMC significantly improves both covert throughput and energy efficiency compared with existing works.

title: On threshold policies for minimizing average age of incorrect information in IRSA for remote estimation
authors: Minha Mubarak, B. S. Vineeth
abstract: The paper considers a remote estimation problem where a large number of users transmit to a single base station using irregular repetition slotted ALOHA (IRSA). The remote estimation performance is measured using age of incorrect information (AoII), a goal-oriented information freshness metric. For a distributed implementation and to minimize signaling overhead, users implement a simple AoII-threshold policy that randomly transmits a user’s packet with a transmit probability using IRSA only if that user’s AoII is more than a common threshold. The paper considers the problem of optimizing this threshold to minimize average AoII. The characterization of average AoII (the optimization objective) as a function of the threshold is challenging and the paper proposes a novel semi-analytical approximation of the average AoII for IRSA. The approximation is used to obtain the optimal threshold and transmit probability as a function of user and IRSA parameters. Using simulations, the average AoII performance of the AoII threshold policy with optimized threshold and transmit probability is shown to be smaller than conventional IRSA and similar to that of a baseline genie policy (which assumes extra information availability at the base station). This demonstrates the utility of the approximation and subsequent optimization that enables simple threshold-based policies to have better remote estimation performance for IRSA.

title: Towards Collaborative Multi-modal Information Perception: A Hierarchical Feature Alignment Network and Acoustic-Optical Dataset for Underwater Object Detection
authors: Jing Chen, Junjie Xie, Mingxin Liu, Hao Wang, Cong Lin
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In the realm of Earth observation, underwater target detection serves as a critical component for characterizing aquatic environments. However, existing acoustic-optical collaborative methods face persistent challenges, including the high calibration difficulty across different modalities and unsatisfactory feature alignment. A core difficulty lies in enabling the network to adaptively capture intrinsic dependencies and achieve efficient fusion of multimodal information during the learning process. To address this, this paper proposes an object detection method with adaptive cross-modal feature alignment. Specifically, the method establishes automatic alignment relationships between sonar and optical images through fine-grained and instance level feature fusion. Furthermore, a novel fusion strategy is designed to enable efficient acoustic-optical information fusion via same-layer feature learning. Finally, to mitigate the issues of scarcity and low quality of multimodal underwater datasets, a data acquisition platform is constructed, and a strictly spatiotemporally synchronized sonar-optical dataset is built. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method simultaneously enhances detection performance on both sonar and optical images. On the created dataset, the proposed method achieves mAP@0.5 scores of 86.9% and 94.4% for acoustic optical collaborative detection, thereby outperforming main stream approaches. In addition, the robustness and generalization of the proposed method are verified in other multimodal object detection scenarios. The proposed AOMU dataset can be found at https://github.com/LaplaceXing/GDOU/tree/master.

title: A Quantum-Assisted Boltzmann Machine for Wind Power Probabilistic Learning
authors: Chengjun Liu, Yijun Xu, Kai Wen, Wei Gu, Shuai Lu, Lamine Mili
abstract: Accurate probabilistic modeling of renewable power is essential for the secure operation and control of modern power systems. However, learning and modeling the data structure with high accuracy remains challenging due to the inherent spatial-temporal correlations of renewables. To address this issue, we propose a novel quantum-assisted Gaussian-Bernoulli restricted Boltzmann machine (GBRBM), which is intrinsically suitable for continuous wind power modeling. Furthermore, we tailor its negative phase to be directly solvable by quantum computing, thereby accelerating the learning process. Experiments conducted on real quantum hardware validate the cost-effective performance of the proposed method.

title: An AI-Driven based Hypertension Disease Prediction Model using ML
authors: Kothandaraman D, J Bhavana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The worldwide health crisis of hypertension exists because this condition develops without any warning symptoms which results in dangerous complications that include myocardial infarction and stroke and renal failure. The research introduces Predictive Pulse as an artificial intelligence system which uses machine learning to identify hypertension at its beginning stage and monitor disease progression. The system uses real-time physiological signals from wearable devices which track heart rate and activity level and sleep duration to predict blood pressure fluctuations. The evaluation process included five machine learning models which were Logistic Regression and Random Forest and K-Nearest Neighbors KNN and Gradient Boosting and Support Vector Machine SVM. The SVM model achieved its highest accuracy of 99.9% through hyperparameter optimization performed by researchers. The trained model became available as a Flask-based web application, which allowed both people and medical staff to access it easily. The proposed platform demonstrates how AI-based solutions improve preventive healthcare through its automated data-based hypertension monitoring system which operates continuously.

title: Design and Analysis of 5-Stage Ring Oscillator using Nanosheet FET
authors: Sachin Kumar, Shreya Swaraj, Kundan Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper has designed and performance-analyzed a 5-stage ring oscillator using Nanosheet Field Effect Transistor technologies. The potential for NSFET to be a promising growth alternative to FinFET in future CMOS scaling with better gate control and reduced leakage and increased drive current make the study significant. The design here is on a 5-stage ring oscillator focusing on investigating certain key parameters like oscillation frequency, delay, and power efficiency. Simulation results show that the NSFET designs achieved better operational speed and lower power dissipation than conventional transistor technologies. The results prove that nanosheet FETs will remarkably improve the performance of circuits and therefore become a good candidate for next-generation semiconductor devices in energy-efficient high-speed VLSI applications.

title: Design and Simulation of a Pulse-Driven 1-Bit SRAM Cell Using CMOS Technology
authors: Thrishala M, Panwala Fenil Chetankumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper presents the design and simulation of a pulse-driven 1-bit SRAM subsystem using Cadence Virtuoso in CMOS technology. The proposed design integrates a ring oscillator-based clock pulse generator, a 6T SRAM cell, and a differential sense amplifier. The narrow clock pulse generated by the oscillator precisely defines the timing window for read and write operations, ensuring that these actions occur only within the intended time frame. This approach minimizes unnecessary switching activity, thereby reducing dynamic power dissipation and improving control accuracy. The read and write circuits are activated by separate pulses derived from the oscillator output, providing better synchronization and noise immunity. The sense amplifier ensures reliable data sensing by amplifying small voltage differences between the bit-lines, enabling high-speed and stable operation. Simulation results validate the functional correctness of the complete subsystem, demonstrating accurate read/write cycles and stable operation even under short pulse widths. The proposed pulse-driven control method enhances the overall timing efficiency of the SRAM cell while maintaining low power consumption. This architecture not only simplifies timing control but also offers scalability for high-speed, energy-efficient memory design. The proposed design methodology provides valuable insight into implementing compact, pulse-based memory architectures and can be further extended to multi-bit SRAM arrays or integrated into larger low-power memory systems suitable for embedded and VLSI applications.

title: Artificial Intelligence Fuzzy Approaches in History Education: A Detailed Analysis of Digital Archives, Decision Making System and Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Niharika Varshney, Jaspreet Singh, Shakhboz Meylikulov, Muyassar Kholova, Turdishov Dauletmurat Xojamuratovich, Gulomjon Bakhtiyarovich Sapaev
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper will be an empirical examination of artificial intelligent fuzzy solutions in history education based on using machine learning, deep learning, and processing the digital archive methodology. The study suggests a cohesive framework that combines fuzzy inferences systems with ML and DL algorithms to overcome uncertainty in the historical data, automate the process of classifying the archives, and improve decision-support systems in the learning process of students. A large dataset of textual documents, digitized images, and student performance indicators were assessed with the help of such models as Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, CNNs, BiLSTM, and Transformer designs. Also, Mamdani, Sugeno and neuro-fuzzy models of fuzzy logic were evaluated based on interpretability and ambiguity. The findings indicate that hybrid Fuzzy-DL models have a better level of accuracy with the transparency needed in an educational setup. The results show a great enhancement in the personalized learning recommendation, metadata quality prediction, and the ability to retrieve an archive. Altogether, this paper shows that the integration of AI-based fuzzy systems can be used to transform history education and enhance the data-driven pedagogical decision-making.

title: Crop-Weed Detection under Indian Agricultural Conditions using YOLO-based Models
authors: Kothandaraman D, Yaswanth Agraharapu, S Teja Venkata Naga Sri Sai, Kasarapu Rohan, Kappala Siddharth
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: In India farmers face challenges identifying the presence of weeds in their crop fields. The traditional method of doing this by hand requires significant labour and time, ultimately causing errors. Untreated weeds in a farm will reduce a farmer’s yield by 20-30 per cent each season. Traditionally, farmers use what they have learned to identify where to find weeds on their fields, however, the variability of the different types of soils, light intensity and crop density affect a farmer’s ability to apply their traditional weed-finding methods effectively so they will not help any farmer improve their farming effectiveness. We evaluated some of the most commonly available YOLO models to determine which model works best to identify weeds in real-time. The models were trained using a dataset that consisted of 1300 images of commonly-grown crops in India, including rice and wheat, and the most commonly-found weeds. Images were collected from various climatic conditions of the Indian monsoon season and in a dry soil environment. Minor adjustments were made to improve the outcome. I experimented with all of the adjustments in the YOLO season for all of the models to achieve the best outcome.

title: Spatio-Temporal Node Localization in Wireless Sensor Networks Using Hybrid Recurrent-Graph Neural Network
authors: Adhitiya Sharma, Abhishek S. Shamkuwar, Uddeshay Anand, Rohit Kumar, Naveen Kumar, Manjeet Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: In wireless sensor networks (WSNs), thousands of sensor nodes may be distributed across wide geographic regions. Generally, a limited number of nodes possess localization hardware. This creates a major barrier to achieving accurate network-wide node positioning. In this work, A hybrid deep learning approach is introduced, combining Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) to estimate the positions of both static and mobile nodes. The architecture integrates Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) and Graph Attention Networks (GATs) with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) models to capture spatial relationships and temporal patterns simultaneously. A custom method is also developed to generate realistic simulation data for model training and validation. Among the tested combinations—GCN-LSTM, GCN-GRU, GAT-LSTM, and GAT-GRU—the GAT-GRU configuration delivers the highest accuracy, achieving a minimum Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 0.1459 and a peak Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) of 0.9958 under clean conditions with 10% mobility. This setup also remains stable under both Line-of-Sight (LOS) and Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) high-noise environments. The findings highlight the advantage of integrating attention-based spatial learning with recurrent temporal modeling for precise and scalable node localization in dynamic wireless systems.

title: Game Coaching System Using 3D CNN and LLM
authors: Ryogo Akie, Yunhao Tu, Kazuyuki Murase, Fady Alnajjar
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: As the e-sports market has continued to expand in recent years, replay analysis has become a common method for players seeking to improve their skills. Nevertheless, novice and intermediate players often struggle to independently identify and articulate areas for improvement. This barrier to skill acquisition is a potential cause of player churn, preventing new players from fully experiencing the core appeal of a game. This paper presents an “AI coaching system” that integrates a 3D Convolutional Neural Network (3D CNN) with a Large Language Model (LLM). The proposed system first utilizes the 3D CNN to extract candidate moments for improvement from gameplay footage. An LLM then performs a secondary evaluation and generates a brief summary for each candidate. Following a user confirmation step via a web application, the LLM produces the final, detailed coaching feedback. To validate the proposed approach, we constructed a custom gameplay video dataset for training and testing purposes. Experimental results show that the developed CNN model achieved a balanced accuracy of approximately 71% on the test set. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of a novel approach that automates gameplay analysis and feedback generation, thereby enhancing player skill development and potentially improving player retention.

title: Sequential Industrial Defect Image Generation via DDPM and Real Defect Imitation
authors: Pei-Yu Huang, Ya-Hui Tsai, Bo Hsiao, Chuen-Tsai Sun
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This study proposes a sequential industrial defect image generation method based on Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM), real defect imitation, and prompt control. The system allows users to input a base image, manually define defect regions, and provide real defect samples with corresponding labels as texture and structure references. In addition to prompt-guided control over defect color and orientation, the system can automatically extract and apply real defect features for adaptive synthesis. A novel sequential defect generation mechanism is introduced, enabling each newly generated defect to be conditionally influenced by the previous one, thereby simulating realistic defect propagation or correlated formation patterns in industrial materials. The DDPM-based framework preserves the original background details while seamlessly embedding high-fidelity and diverse defects into specified regions. The proposed method supports batch generation of multiple defect images, significantly enhancing dataset diversity and improving model robustness in industrial defect detection tasks.

title: Micro-Batch Harvesting: Path Planning for Tomato Robots Using Safe-Cylinder Strategy and PC-CVRP
authors: Kazuhisa Nakasho, Hirokazu Madokoro, Stephanie Nix
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Labor shortages in agriculture have become a critical global issue, necessitating the rapid development of autonomous harvesting robots for soft fruits like tomatoes. However, optimal path planning in complex environments cluttered with stems and leaves remains computationally expensive, hindering practical deployment. Furthermore, to balance fruit quality preservation with time efficiency, it is essential to consider capacity constraints for holding a specific amount of fruit near the end-effector for batched transport. This paper proposes a high-efficiency and safe path planning framework for tomato harvesting robots. First, to bypass complex collision detection, we define a geometric “Safe-Cylinder” and introduce a strategy that ensures physical collision avoidance via rectangular trajectories. Second, we formulate the harvesting task as a Precedence and Capacity-Constrained Vehicle Routing Problem (PC-CVRP) using a “Depot Cloning” approach and derive an optimized sequence minimizing total travel cost using a mathematical optimization solver. Path simulations demonstrate that the proposed method generates batched harvesting routes that avoid physical contact with obstacles while significantly reducing travel costs compared to a simple greedy algorithm.

title: An Integrated Approach for Early Detection of Autism Spectrum Disorder using Machine Learning and Deep Learning Frameworks
authors: Rajeshwar Sharma, Harjit Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a major global health issue that calls for prompt and precise diagnostic techniques. This thorough analysis looks at machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) techniques for early ASD detection using a variety of data modalities, such as genetic profiles, electroencephalography (EEG), facial imagery, questionnaire-based screening data, and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI). We systematically assess deep neural network architectures (Convolutional Neural Networks, Recurrent Neural Networks, Autoencoders), sophisticated ensemble techniques, and conventional machine learning algorithms (Random Forest, Support Vector Machines, k-Nearest Neighbors, Logistic Regression), with a focus on hybrid frameworks combining federated learning, active learning, and explainable artificial intelligence (XAI). While ensemble methods offer balanced precision-recall tradeoffs, deep learning approaches achieve superior accuracy (95–99%), according to performance metrics across multiple datasets. Important results show that while SHAP-based explainability mechanisms improve clinical interpretability, federated learning architectures successfully handle privacy concerns in healthcare data integration. Critical research gaps are identified by this review, including the need for clinician-in-the-loop validation frameworks, dataset heterogeneity, demographic representation, and the viability of real-world deployment.

title: A Reconfigurable Multi-Mode Carry Propagation ALU with Runtime Power–Performance Adaptation
authors: Shubham Patel, Mamta Khosla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper describes a Multi-Mode Carry Propagation Arithmetic Logic Unit (MCCPA-ALU) that can be reconfigurable and has been shown that it has runtime power-performance adaption capability. The design incorporates 3 adder modes namely, Ripple-Carry Adder (RCA), Carry-Lookahead Adder (CLA) and Carry-Select Adder (CSLA) and chooses the most appropriate configuration depending on the operational needs. This allows it to operate with low power in cases where energy is limited and also allows it to compute with high speed in cases where its performance is imperative. The proposed ALU is coded on a Xilinx Zynq-7000 FPGA with the help of Verilog HDL. It has been shown by experiment that it can reduce dynamic power by up to 48 percent in low-power mode and delay in high-performance mode by 32 percent, using a smaller total number of LUTs (less than 110). These attributes render the MCCPA-ALU an ideal choice as the embedded and IoT systems with dynamic computational efficiency.

title: Exploring the Competences of Blockchain Technology and Machine Learning in Healthcare
authors: Pratik Suresh Deshmukh, Kalyani H. Deshmukh, Amit K. Gaikwad, Gajendra R. Bamnote, Kartik P. Deshmukh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: As cryptocurrencies developed block chain technology, ongoing research has been conducted to broaden its applicability to non-financial use cases. Block chain is expected to have a significant impact on the healthcare business. The research in this subject is young but quickly expanding, therefore health informatics investigators and practitioners are constantly pushed to keep up. This report provides a full overview of existing research on the usage of blockchain technology in the field of healthcare technology. The improvement in it encourages the early diagnosis of illnesses since early detection decreases the impact of diseases to a larger degree. The ease of data availability provides a wealth of information to the clinician, and thanks to recent breakthroughs, illnesses are recognized automatically using classifiers based on medical data. Data storage in IoT devices is very susceptible to assaults or unauthorized access, which may be fatal, and is prevented by using a block chain network, which stores, authorizes, and authenticates data. The illnesses are categorized using a deep CNN classifier that has been efficiently adjusted via optimization, and the performance is measured using the metrics accuracy, precision, and recall. This study also seeks to use deep learning to identify between healthy and PD patients in order to automate the PD diagnostic procedure.

title: Role of IoT & Emerging Technologies in Small Scale Dairy & Cottage Industries
authors: Ishaan Agarwal, Vanshika Mittal, Sanyam Agarwal, Vipin Mittal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid integration of Internet of Things (IoT), Artificial Intelligence (AI), and blockchain technologies is reshaping traditional rural industries by improving efficiency, productivity, and sustainability. This paper reviews the role of these emerging technologies in strengthening small-scale dairy and cottage industries, with a focus on rural India. Key applications include precision agriculture, intelligent irrigation, livestock health monitoring, automated dairy management, and blockchain-enabled supply chain traceability. By analyzing recent developments, the paper highlights how IoT sensor networks, machine learning models, and cloud-based platforms can optimize resource utilization, enhance animal welfare, reduce post-harvest losses, and enable direct market access for farmers. Challenges such as limited connectivity, high implementation costs, and resistance to digital adoption are also discussed. The study concludes by proposing feasible strategies for integrating modular, low-cost digital solutions into rural ecosystems, enabling sustainable growth while preserving traditional practices.

title: Tilted Beam Endfire Planar Patch Antenna for UHF RFID Application
authors: Vineet Kumar, Nitesh Kahsyap, Ashish Raman, Ajay Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This study presents a very little tiny antenna that is used for UHF-RFID applications. This antenna is used to propagate signal into a single direction means this antenna is a unidirectional and Endfire antenna with tilted beam make it more attractive to scan RFID tags from portable and handheld scanners. This is a very compact antenna, and the size of the antenna is 25 x 18 x 1.6 mm on FR4 substrate for this antenna is easily placed in compact devices. Proposed antenna designed by computer simulation tool (CST). Antenna works from 820 MHz to 984 MHz, and the bandwidth of this antenna is 164 MHz, and it covers the wide bandwidth for UHF RFID band applications. This antenna is used for tracking products in warehouse, monitoring medical equipment and handheld handling payments in a retail store

title: An Optimized Strassens–Karatsuba Matrix Multiplier Employing CLA using Verilog
authors: Shreya Swaraj, Sudhanshu Kumar, Kundan Kumar, Shruti Priya, Amit Kumar Rana, Ravi Ranjan, Shankar Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Efficient matrix multiplication is a crucial issue of AI, signal processing, and computing systems. This paper proposes an optimized matrix multiplication architecture, which incorporates Strassen’s algorithm and Karatsuba multipliers with carry look-ahead adders (CLA). The aim of the design is to minimize the hardware complexity and maximize the computational speed. At the integer level, Karatsuba multiplication is area and power efficient compared to conventional array multiplication and CLA based addition minimizes carry propagation delay. These optimized multipliers are implemented in a Strassen’s-based framework for matrix multiplication. Synthesis results on a Xilinx 7 series FPGA show that there are significant reductions in LUT utilization and power consumption compared to the traditional matrix multiplication along with improved critical path delay. The proposed Strassen’s-Karatsuba-CLA architecture offers an effective compromise between area efficiency and fast speed performance, and hence is suitable for FPGA-based matrix processing applications

title: Enhancing Industrial Safety Communication Over Wi-Fi Networks With Frame Replication
authors: Suneel Kumar, Daniel Camps-Mur, Zhibo Pang, Ognjen Dobrijevic, Eduard Garcia-Villegas
abstract: Industrial automation applications increasingly adopt wireless technologies and mixed wired-wireless networking. However, over a wireless channel, meeting the stringent performance requirements of such applications becomes challenging. That is especially critical for safety communication, i.e., exchange of data used to keep workers and machinery protected. This paper investigates an emerging safety solution, Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture (OPC UA) Safety, and explores how Time-Sensitive Networking’s Frame Replication and Elimination for Reliability (FRER) can enhance communication performance for OPC UA Safety over Wi-Fi networks. Using an emulation-based approach, different redundancy strategies and their impact are analyzed. The results demonstrate that, even under worst-case, high-load network conditions, applying FRER across asymmetrically loaded, independent Wi-Fi paths provides a substantial benefit. For example, round-trip time of safety messages is reduced by up to 92% as compared to single-path Wi-Fi, while communication availability is improved from near 0% to over 85%.

title: Nash Equilibria for Asynchronous Impulsive Orbital Pursuit–Evasion Games under J2 Perturbation
authors: Yue-Long Luo, Yue-He Zhu, Ya-Zhong Luo, Jia-Cheng Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Impulsive orbital pursuit–evasion (PE) games are critical for space security. The impulse timing relationship directly influences the control strategies and results. However, most existing studies assume simultaneous or sequential maneuvers, whereas real impulsive orbital games involve asynchronous maneuvers. Additionally, orbital perturbations affect game precision, particularly in low Earth orbits and long-duration scenarios. This study investigates asynchronous impulsive orbital PE games under J2 perturbation and introduces a solution method for asynchronous multi-impulse Nash equilibrium. The necessary conditions for Nash equilibrium are derived using an extended Pontryagin principle, which transforms the necessary condition into a special multipoint boundary value problem (MPBVP). A solution method combining iterative best response and segmented shooting is proposed. The first impulses of both players are modeled as a single-impulse Stackelberg game to generate an approximate Stackelberg equilibrium as the initial guess, providing physical meaning to initial guesses and avoiding the blindness of random guesses. For uneven time segments, the asynchronous Nash equilibrium is obtained through segmented shooting and interior point constraint handling. Simulations show that the proposed initial guess improvement ensures 100% convergence success, validating the algorithm's effectiveness and Nash equilibrium optimality. Comparative simulations demonstrate superior computational efficiency and broader applicability.

title: Multi-Model Local Illumination Compensation for Enhanced Compression Model
authors: Jialin Li, Zhuoyuan Li, Yao Li, Dong Liu, Li Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Inter prediction is a key technique for reducing temporal redundancy in video coding. However, irregular inter-frame illumination changes are hard to predict, yielding poorly predicted residuals that are difficult to compress. To address this, local illumination compensation (LIC) has been introduced into the next-generation exploratory coding model, which uses a linear model (LM) to approximate and compensate for inter-frame illumination variations, thereby reducing the prediction residual. Due to the simple design of LM and the least squares regression (LSR) solution in ECM, LIC suffers notable limitations, including the limited fitting capacity of the LM and the outlier sensitivity of LSR, which makes the compensation less robust to outliers. To address these limitations, we propose a multi-model local illumination compensation (MM-LIC) framework, which introduces the multi-model paradigm to optimize the model type (MMT) and model solution (MMS) jointly. In MMT, the candidate model types are extended beyond LM by incorporating a non-linear model (NLM) and a gradient linear model (GLM) to better handle diverse illumination variations.
