In MMS, ridge regression (RR) is further introduced to improve model solution accuracy and stability, especially when handling outliers. Meanwhile, in order to preserve decision accuracy without extra bit overhead, we design separable implicit decision mechanisms for the whole compensation process. The proposed method is implemented in ECM-8.0. Experimental results show that the proposed MM-LIC can achieve, on average, 0.45% and 0.23%, and achieve up to 0.68% and 0.40% BD-rate reduction under LDB and RA configurations, respectively, with only slight increases in time complexity. Furthermore, the proposed scheme also demonstrates its potential in the latest ECM version.

title: Weak Cyclostationary Target Echo Detection for Multi-UAV Enabled Passive Radar Network
authors: Long Zhang, Kui Xiong, Maosen Liao, Xianxiang Yu, Guolong Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of week cyclostationary target echo signal detection for the multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) enabled passive radar network. First, the cooperative detection system model and the signal model of the multi-UAV enabled passive radar network is constructed. Based on the cyclostationary low signal-to-noise radio characteristics of target echoes, the generalized likelihood ratio test (GLRT) method is employed to detect target echoes in complex colored noise backgrounds. Then, by applying the channel-aware Bayesian minimum risk fusion (BMRF) strategy, the local detection results from each radar receiver are optimized and fused to obtain the final decision for improving the accuracy of global detection, thus generating the cyclostationary generalized likelihood ratio test-Bayesian minimum risk fusion (CGLRT-BMRF) detection algorithm. Finally, numerical simulations are conducted to illustrate the superiority of the proposed algorithm over some typical detection algorithms.

title: Alternating Subspace Method for Sparse Recovery of Signals
authors: Xu Zhu, Yufei Ma, Xiaoguang Li, Tiejun Li
abstract: Numerous renowned algorithms for tackling the compressed sensing problem employ an alternating strategy, which typically involves data matching in one module and denoising in another. We present a novel approach, the Alternating Subspace Method (ASM), which integrates the principles of the greedy methods (e.g., the orthogonal matching pursuit type methods) and the splitting methods (e.g., the approximate message passing type methods). Crucially, ASM enhances the splitting method by achieving fidelity in a subspace-restricted fashion. We reveal that such a restriction strategy guarantees global convergence via proximal residual control and establish its local geometric convergence on the LASSO problem. Numerical experiments on the LASSO, channel estimation, and dynamic compressed sensing problems demonstrate its high convergence rate and its capacity to incorporate different prior distributions. Overall, the proposed method is promising in terms of efficiency, accuracy, and flexibility, and has the potential to be competitive in different sparse recovery applications.

title: Enhancing Cloud Network Resilience via a Robust LLM-Empowered Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Framework_supp1-3694417.pdf
authors: Yuling Liu
abstract: While virtualization and resource pooling empower cloud networks with structural flexibility and elastic scalability, they inevitably expand the attack surface and challenge cyber resilience. Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based defense strategies have been developed to optimize resource deployment and isolation policies under adversarial conditions, aiming to enhance system resilience by maintaining and restoring network availability. However, existing approaches lack robustness as they require retraining to adapt to dynamic changes in network structure, node scale, attack strategies, and attack intensity. Furthermore, the lack of Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) support limits interpretability and flexibility. To address these limitations, we propose CyberOps-Bots, a hierarchical multi agent reinforcement learning framework empowered by Large Language Models (LLMs). Inspired by MITRE ATT&CK's “Tactics-Techniques” model, CyberOps-Bots features a two-layer architecture: (1) An upper-level LLM agent with four mod ules—ReAct planning, IPDRR-based perception, long-short term memory, and action/tool integration—performs global awareness, human intent recognition, and tactical planning; (2) Lower-level RL agents, developed via heterogeneous separated pre-training, execute atomic defense actions within localized network regions. This synergy preserves LLM adaptability and interpretability while ensuring reliable RL execution. Experiments on real cloud datasets show that, compared to state-of-the-art algorithms, CyberOps-Bots maintains network availability 68.5% higher and achieves a 34.7% jumpstart performance gain when shifting the scenarios without retraining. To our knowledge, this is the first study to establish a robust LLM-RL framework with HITL support for cloud defense.

title: Online Intrusion Detection for Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems Based on Self-Supervised Predictive Model
authors: Sardar Shan Ali Naqvi, Chunjie Zhou, Peihang Xu, Yahui Li, Muhammad Uzair
abstract: Industrial cyber-physical systems (CPS) demand a reliable intrusion detection despite evolving operational conditions and a lack of labeled attack data. This article proposes a novel context–target predictive self-supervised framework for online intrusion detection in industrial CPS. The models normal behavior by predicting future sensor states from past context, allowing detection of anomalies as large prediction errors. To address concept drift, we incorporate a trust-aware online adaptation mechanism and a robust, statistics-based thresholding strategy that adjusts in real time. We validate the method on a fluid catalytic cracking unit simulation, where it achieves high precision and recall under fully online, unlabeled conditions, outperforming baseline models. The proposed framework provides an adaptive and lightweight intrusion detection systems suitable for autonomous industrial CPS monitoring without human intervention or costly labeling.

title: Front Cover
authors: N/A

title: Arrhenius Normal Life Stress Modeling for Multilayer Ceramic Capacitors Lifetime Prediction
authors: Maryam Alsawadi, Mohammed Abdulmajid, Omar Kittaneh
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper applies the Arrhenius–Normal life–stress model to predict the lifetime of multilayer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs) using thermally accelerated test data. The failure-time data at all stress levels follow a normal distribution, supporting the model assumption. A linear relationship between logarithmic lifetime and inverse temperature confirms the temperature acceleration effect. The model enables reliable extrapolation from accelerated conditions to normal operating temperature. The results demonstrate that the Arrhenius–Normal model provides a consistent and effective framework for MLCC lifetime prediction.

title: Operational Analysis of Kuwait’s Peak-Load Days Using Profiling, Clustering, and Correlation Studies
authors: Sultan Sh. Alanzi, Osama D. Alhossen, Ghada Shehada
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Electric power systems worldwide face growing stress as demand increases while aging grids struggle to maintain reliability. In Kuwait, harsh summer conditions and heavy use of air-conditioning units led to a rapid increase in electricity consumption pushing annual peaks from 11.2 GW (2011) to 17.5 GW (2024) (+56.3%). This paper examines the operational characteristics of Kuwait’s peak-load days (2011-2024) to provide useful insights for system operation and planning. Analysis of the daily load profiles shows a flat-topped shape especially in recent years. K-means clustering confirmed three operating regimes that reflect the time-associated nature of load where it mostly peaks during 14:30-15:30 window while the late-night/early-morning periods can be used as the primary maintenance window. Correlation analysis indicated a strong relationship between load and temperature/humidity both peaking at a lag of +1 hour which means their effects on the load show up around one hour later. Population also correlated strongly with peak power and daily energy, yet it proved insufficient alone. Weather and operational conditions influence the power/energy outcomes. Overall, the results pointed to tight capacity margins, prolonged near-peak operation, and limited maintenance windows indicating the need for adequate reserve, ramp capability, and operation strategies.

title: Hosting Machine Learning Algorithms in Flight Software via the F Prime Python Software Framework
authors: Matthew Alexander Mariano, Kelly Williams, Michael Pham, Zachary Gaines, Tanya Patel, Phyllis Nelson
abstract: We present a Python based API, software components, and reference system for safely running machine learning models alongside an F Prime flight software binary. F Prime is an open-source reusable component-based flight software framework developed at JPL which has been deployed on a variety of small satellites and the Mars Ingenuity Helicopter. F Prime Python was originally developed to provide a means of directly integrating rapidly prototypes Python code into the C++ F Prime binary. This link is facilitated using PyBind11 to enable users to create F Prime components that are programmed and executes entirely in Python as a part of a broader F Prime deployment. Our paper presents updated made to this F Prime Python toolchain as part of the SCALES (Spacecraft Compartmentalized Autonomous Learning and Edgecomputing System) project. These updates are compatible with F Prime v3.x.x. The full SCALES implementation includes reference hardware and software, with F Prime running on a radiation-tolerant processor and Python ML processing done on a separate NVIDIA Jetson Orin to segregate the risk of the ML software and hardware from the essential flight software functions. SCALES provides an open-source reference for machine learning manager components that can thus be used to safely host experimental machine learning models alongside a trusted flight software deployment. The SCALES reference machine learning manager component uses the Hugging Face API for executing Machine Learning (ML) models. This creates a lightweight and reusable bridge for a flight software binary to interact with the other 1 million pretrained ML models hosted on the Hugging Face Hub. The SCALES F Prime reference deployment will demonstrate a typical F Prime flight software binary operating alongside Microsoft's Phi-3-mini “Small Language Model”, depth Anything V2 Vision Transformer, and the Resnet-152 V1.5 CNN. In addition to presenting the F Prime Python-enabled machine learning manager, we also discuss analysis of the interaction between a real time F Prime environment and the asynchronous Python environment, compartmentalization of the software stack to retain core flight software safety, and initial deployments of this system on prototype edge computing hardware.

title: Dependence of optical refractive index of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength in near-infrared waveband for warm and shallow sea_supp1-3693439.xlsx
authors: Shanshan Wang
abstract: Dependence of optical refractive index (RI) of seawater on temperature, salinity, and pressure is crucial for optical fiber sensors operated in seawater. Expressions of RI of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength applicable to near-infrared waveband are developed based on a great deal of RI data measured by a semi-encapsulated microfiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer sensor. To verify the accuracy of these expressions, RI measured by infrared refractometer, theoretical calculated sensitivity and experimental measured sensitivity are used, by which the most accurate expression is determined. Based on this expression, a variable sensitivity matrix is established to demodulate temperature, salinity and pressure simultaneously with errors of 10.91 %, 6.09 % and 18.80 %, respectively. The expression proposed here will provide valuable references for theoretical and experimental study on optical fiber sensors operated in infrared waveband and push the applications of fiber optical sensors in ocean investigations.

title: A Generative Modeling Approach to Resource-Efficient Early Mission Concept Formulation
authors: Alfred Nash
abstract: Projects in NASA's Pre-Phase A aim to define a mission concept that is aligned with program goals, technically feasible, and affordable enough to merit continued development. However, this work is conducted under tight constraints-limited time, budget, and workforce-despite the need to generate, assess, and compare a wide range of mission concepts. Without structured tools to guide this process, this early concept formulation can become inefficient, increasing uncertainty and risk in later phases of mission development. Traditional concept formulation relies on discriminative tools-models that use features of a mission concept (e.g., mass, power) to predict target outcomes such as financial viability (i.e., cost). While effective for evaluating specific designs, these tools do not efficiently support the generation, assessment, nor comparison of a broad range of alternatives. Generative models are a promising alternative for overcoming these limitations. Rather than predicting outcomes from features, generative models identify the accessible design space of features from the desired target outcomes such as technical feasibility and financial viability. By guiding concept formulation to exploration within an informed population rather than by brute force or random trial-and-error search methods, generative models significantly reduce the time, labor, and finances required to identify selectable mission concepts. This paper presents the development and application of generative models at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory to support early-stage mission concept formulation. These results demonstrate the potential of generative models to transform early mission formulation into a more strategic, resource-efficient, and selection-ready process.

title: Zero Trust Architecture for Secured Energy Trading Platforms with Cryptographic Continuous Verification
authors: Remon Das, Shaira Zahin Khan, Md Sharif Robbani, Md Raisul Islam, Sajedul Islam
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Energy trading platforms have become integral components of modern smart grid infrastructures, enabling automated and decentralized energy exchange among producers, prosumers, and consumers. However, their increasing reliance on cloud services, interconnected devices, and automated decision-making mechanisms introduces significant cybersecurity challenges. Traditional perimeter-based security models are inadequate for such dynamic, distributed, and multi-stakeholder environments. This paper proposes a Zero Trust Architecture for secure energy trading platforms with cryptographic continuous verification. The proposed framework enforces strict identity authentication, least-privilege access control, and continuous risk-based authorization, while incorporating cryptographic mechanisms to ensure transaction integrity and tamper resistance. Experimental evaluation under representative attack scenarios demonstrates attack-specific detection rates ranging from 94% to 98% with acceptable performance overhead. The results indicate that the proposed approach significantly enhances protection against insider threats, transaction manipulation, and advanced cyber-attacks, making it suitable for secure and resilient energy trading systems.

title: Research on Distributed Collaborative Scheduling Strategy for Electric Vehicles Considering Dynamic Traffic Flow and Dynamic Electricity Pricing
authors: Xin Su, Qian Zhang, Tianxi Qin, Zhanhao He, Yuchen Shan, Xingchen Tan, Hui Jin, Yifan Weng
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address traffic congestion and power distribution network (PDN) voltage violations caused by uncoordinated electric vehicle (EV) charging and discharging, this paper proposes a distributed collaborative dispatch strategy considering dynamic traffic flow and dynamic pricing. First, a dynamic traffic flow model under bounded rationality is constructed to accurately capture the spatiotemporal routing and queue spillover effects of EVs. Second, a "dual-track" dynamic pricing mechanism integrating road network congestion and PDN voltage deviations is proposed to guide spatiotemporal load shifting via charging penalties and Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) rewards. Subsequently, a distributed dispatch architecture based on the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) is established to achieve the co-optimization of global power and traffic flows while preserving privacy. Finally, a coupled case study of an IEEE 33-bus PDN and a 22-node traffic network verifies the strategy's effectiveness in alleviating voltage stress, reducing congestion costs, and achieving dynamic balance across both networks.

title: Fast Thermal Overload Defense and Smooth Ride-Through Control for Converters in Resilient Urban Distribution Networks
authors: Ye Tao, Haizhou Ying, MengYa Lin, Jizeng Cai, Zichen Wang, Yingqi Wang, Chihan Zhou, Guojie Tian
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the rapid evolution of urban distribution networks towards high resilience and the increasing penetration of Distributed Energy Resources (DERs), the thermal reliability of Voltage Source Converters (VSCs) has become critical. Traditional Active Thermal Control (ATC) strategies, which typically rely on static current amplitude thresholds, often suffer from detection delays during rapid transient faults. Given the small thermal time constants of modern power devices, these delays can lead to irreversible thermal damage before protection mechanisms are triggered. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a rapid thermal overload defense strategy based on real-time transient current slope detection. The proposed algorithm utilizes moving average filtering and differential slope analysis embedded within the controller’s Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) to identify thermal threats at the incipient stage of a fault, independent of absolute current magnitude. Comprehensive simulation and experimental results demonstrate that this adaptive triggering mechanism successfully eliminates the detection latency inherent in conventional techniques—specifically avoiding a 24.4 ms delay observed in simulations. This approach allows for the immediate activation of ride-through strategies, significantly enhancing the thermal safety and operational reliability of converters under complex urban grid conditions.

title: Preliminary Design of the Cesium Oven for the ITER Neutral Beam Injector
authors: Fabio Veronese, Marco Alessandroni, Piero Agostinetti, Michele Fadone, François Geli, Diego Marcuzzi, Andrea Nazzicone, Mauro Pavei, Basile Pouradier Duteil, Andrea Rizzolo, Emanuele Sartori
abstract: The ITER experiment, currently under construction in Cadarache in southern France, will require the injection of a considerable amount of additional power from its heating and current drive (HCD) systems in order to sustain long plasma pulses. Among all of the various systems, neutral beam injectors (NBIs) will account for 50 MW of power over three devices, mediated by fast neutrals at 1 MeV in deuterium (or 870 keV in hydrogen). The required extracted negative ion current at the plasma source (PS) can only be reached through efficient negative ion generation and extraction, which in this type of NBIs is achieved by sufficient surface coating of the inner surfaces of the PS with a low work-function metal, such as cesium. This article describes the cesium oven for the ITER Heating NBI (HNBI), which is a device for the controlled evaporation of cesium inside the PS: its general structure is based on the design of the cesium oven for the Source for Production of Ion of Deuterium Extracted from Rf plasma (SPIDER) experiment, however with the necessary modifications to make it compatible with the more stringent ITER guidelines, such as radiation resistance and remote handling compatibility. A prototype has been designed at Consorzio RFX by taking into account this set of requirements, followed by extensive simulation of the thermal distribution.

title: Updated Electrical Design of the Diagnostic Neutral Beam Injector in RFX-mod2
authors: Marco Barbisan, Bruno Laterza, Luca Cinnirella, Lionello Marrelli, Federico Molon, Simone Peruzzo, Enrico Zampiva
abstract: The diagnostic neutral beam injector (DNBI) of the RFX-mod2 experiment (Consorzio RFX, Padua) is expected to provide novel and significant information about the reversed field pinch (RFP) confinement of fusion plasmas. The present DNBI, built by the Budker Institute of Plasma Physics, features an arc discharge H<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> source, coupled to a 4 grid 50 keV acceleration system, to produce a 50 ms, 5 A ion beam. This contribution presents the latest upgrades of the DNBI. The high-voltage deck (HVD) was completely restructured, and the power transfer was simplified to a single-phase insulation transformer. The 50 kV distribution circuit was modernized and made safer against breakdowns. Several custom power supplies in the HVD were designed and procured; their electronic boards were developed to be multipurpose in the DNBI, simplifying the system and improving its maintainability. The features of the magnetic insulation power supply (MIPS) and gas valve power supplies (GVPS) are presented in detail. Finally, the new PLC control system was improved for better protection of the CPU against overvoltages and for better scalability and maintainability of the system.

title: Impact of Band-to-Band Tunneling in the Charge Trap Layer of NAND Flash Memory
authors: Seongwoo Kim, Beomgeun Lee, Seungjae Baik, Myounggon Kang
publish: IEEE Journal of the Electron Devices Society
abstract: This article investigates the impact of band-to-band tunneling (BTBT) occurring in the charge trap layer (CTL) of vertical NAND (V−NAND) flash memory under excessive erasure conditions and aggressive program operation. Strong local electric fields induce BTBT within the CTL, generating electron–hole pairs that cause trap charge loss, inter-cell interference, and early threshold voltage (V<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {th}}$ </tex-math></inline-formula>) reduction. The generated holes accelerate the loss of trapped electrons near the programmed target cell, degrading incremental step pulse programming (ISPP) characteristics, whereas laterally redistributed electrons induce Vth shifts in adjacent cells. In addition, the time-dependent accumulation of BTBT-generated carriers contributes to initial threshold voltage shift (IVS) during retention. These results show that BTBT in the CTL provides reliability issues in V−NAND flash memory, such as short-term Vth instability and inter-cell interference.

title: A Method for Data Augmentation in Vertical Federated Learning Addressing Data Heterogeneity
authors: Yunpeng Xiao, Tingting Lv, Dengke Zhao, Wanjing Zhao, Tun Li, Rong Wang, Guoyin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Vertical federated learning (VFL) can aggregate data features from participating parties and is applicable to data collaboration in various fields. To address data heterogeneity in VFL, this article proposes a framework tailored for heterogeneous environments. First, to mitigate performance degradation caused by imbalanced local data across clients, we exploit conditional generative adversarial networks (CGANs) for targeted data augmentation, and propose a data balancing model named FeCWGAN-GP based on CGAN. This model pretrains a local CGAN for each client to perform local private data compensation, thereby alleviating the problem of decreased model performance. Second, to handle local model parameter distribution shifts induced by heterogeneity, leveraging both the sample size proportions and the Wasserstein distance in the model parameter space to capture parameter distribution shifts due to data heterogeneity, a parameter aggregation algorithm named WFedDA based on sample size and parameter distribution is proposed. This method calculates weights based on the sample size proportions of participants and the distribution difference between local model parameters and global model parameters, thus optimizing the global model. Finally, to address the instability of local model parameters caused by data heterogeneity, a stochastic gradient descent (SGD) method with a dual smoothing mechanism named SGD-MA is proposed. This method uses an exponential moving average (EMA) to process gradients and parameters sequentially, which reduces the fluctuation of gradients and the instability of parameter updates, thereby improving the stability of the training process. Experiments on the public datasets MNIST, CIFAR-10, Fashion-MNIST, and MIMIC-III demonstrate that the methods proposed in this article can effectively address the issues caused by data heterogeneity in multidata-source environments, significantly improving the generalization capability and stability of the global model.

title: Locally constrained non-negative matrix factorization for data clustering
authors: Xuzhu Shen, Jie Li
abstract: Semi-supervised non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) has been widely used for clustering high-dimensional data, because a small amount of label information can effectively improve clustering performance. Nevertheless, most existing semi-supervisedNMFmethods still face two important challenges: they require carefully tuned hyperparameters to control the effect of supervision, and they cannot directly produce clustering assignments from the learned factorization results. In this paper, we propose a novel semi-supervised NMF framework, called Locally Constrained NMF (LCNMF), to address both issues simultaneously. Different from existing methods that impose global penalty terms or fit predefined label codes, LCNMF injects label information by encoding locally inconsistent elements in the low-dimensional representations of labeled samples as zero, thereby introducing an explicit local structural constraint into NMF. This design eliminates the need for additional hyperparameter tuning and allows the learned representation to carry more direct clustering semantics. Furthermore, LCNMF integrates dimensionality reduction and clustering assignment into a unified framework, so that the final cluster labels can be obtained directly without an extra clustering step.We further formulate two variants of the proposed model and derive the corresponding optimization algorithms. Extensive experiments on several real-world datasets show that LCNMF consistently achieves effective and competitive clustering performance compared with state-of-the-art semi-supervised clustering methods. The code can be obtained at https://github.com/ljisxz/LCNMF.

title: DDoS Attacks Detection and Prevention on Real-time Streaming Data Using Machine Learning Methods with Kafka and Apache Spark
authors: Jameel Ahmad, Usama Ahmed, Saad Farooq, Muhammad Sarwar, Momina Shaheen, Dimitris M. Manias, Jawad Khan
abstract: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks in the constantly expanding sphere of cybersecurity are highly important to detect and respond to promptly. This paper proposes an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) that integrate Machine Learning (ML), Apache Kafka and Apache Spark to enable real-time detection and mitigation of DDoS attacks. The proposed framework collects live network traffic data, performs preprocessing and feature extraction, and applies machine learning algorithms within a streaming architecture supported by Apache Kafka and Apache Spark. Kafka enables real-time data ingestion, while Spark provides distributed processing for efficient model training and inference. The methodology supports adaptability to emerging attack patterns through training and evaluation on publicly available benchmark datasets.Machine learning models, including Random Forest and Support Vector Machine (SVM), were evaluated within the proposed streaming framework. Experimental results show that the integrated use of Apache Kafka and Apache Spark achieved an accuracy of up to 95%. The real-time streaming capability of Kafka, combined with the distributed processing power of Spark, enables efficient handling of high-volume network traffic and supports timely detection of DDoS attacks.

title: On Cycle Embedding of Enhanced Hypercubes with Scoped Vertex Faults
authors: Che-Nan Kuo, Yu-Huei Cheng
abstract: In large-scale interconnection networks, faults caused by natural disasters or human-induced incidents often occur in a localized and correlated manner rather than as isolated random events. Such behavior is frequently observed in practical systems such as data centers, multi-core processors, and network-on-chip architectures, where power failures, thermal hotspots, or regional hardware defects may simultaneously affect a group of adjacent components. This motivates the study of structured fault models that better reflect real-world failure patterns. The enhanced hypercube Qn,k is an important variant of the hypercube architecture, obtained by adding complementary edges between specific vertex pairs, and it has been widely studied due to its strong structural and communication properties. In this paper, we consider a scoped fault model in which all four vertices of an arbitrary cycle of length four, denoted by FC4 , are removed from Qn,k, representing the smallest nontrivial localized failure cluster in hypercube-based networks. We investigate the cycle embedding capability of Qn,k − FC4 and establish that it preserves a wide range of cycle lengths under this fault condition. In particular, for every n ≥ 3 and 1 ≤ k ≤ n − 1, all even cycle lengths from 4 to 2n − 4 exist in Qn,k − FC4 , and for even n ≥ 4 with n and k having different parity, all odd cycle lengths from n − k + 4 to 2n − 5 are also preserved. These results demonstrate that the enhanced hypercube maintains optimal cycle embedding capability under the considered scoped fault model and highlight its robustness against localized correlated failures.

title: A Deep Learning-Enhanced EIS Online Monitoring Framework Based on Sparse DC-DC Measurements for Vehicular Fuel Cell Systems
authors: Ganyun Zheng, Zhaoming Liu, Yuan Zhu, Haotian Wang, Zenggang Xia, Linlin Zhuang, Haifeng Dai
abstract: Fast online access to fuel cell impedance is important for health diagnosis and fine-grained management, yet existing onboard methods still struggle to balance update rate and measurement fidelity. This paper presents a hybrid monitoring framework that combines sparse physical calibration from a production DC-DC converter with continuous virtual estimation. A six-phase interleaved DC-DC converter injects dual-frequency excitation at 10 Hz and 1 kHz and, with synchronous Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) extraction under integer-period sampling, provides physically grounded impedance references while keeping current ripple below 1%. Between calibration instants, a compact recurrent estimator, FR-LSTM-lite, infers impedance continuously from electrochemistry-guided inputs. Structured pruning and 8-bit quantization reduce the model to a form suitable for automotive microcontrollers. Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) and 140-kW bench tests show inference latency below 1 ms and impedance error no greater than 1.5%, delivering milliohm-level monitoring without extra hardware or sustained excitation. Together, these capabilities make milliohm-resolution impedance available in real time on production hardware, opening a practical path toward impedance-feedback fuel cell control.

title: Design and Characterization of a Vibration-Driven Pufferfish-Inspired Inflatable Soft Robot for Pipe Inspection
authors: Mohammad Rafiee Javazm, Sonika Kiehler, Van Anh Ho, Farshid Alambeigi
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This study presents the analytical modeling, design, fabrication, and experimental evaluation of a pufferfishinspired soft robot for pipe inspection applications. This inflatable soft robot employs vibration as its sole locomotion mechanism, with internal pressure serving as a control variable to (i) tune the stiffness and natural frequency of the inflatable structure, (ii) optimize vibration transmission and locomotion performance, and (iii) enable locomotion in varying-size pipes. To investigate the influence of vibration on locomotion, we develop a vibration model and analyze the effects of internal pressure, material properties, excitation frequency, and geometric design. Locomotion performance of the proposed robot is validated experimentally using four different prototypes with varying material stiffness and geometry. Results show that the optimized design can achieve locomotion speeds exceeding $100 \mathrm{~mm} / \mathrm{s}$ in both horizontal and vertical tubes with varying diameters. This work paves the way for optimizing vibrationbased soft robot morphology to achieve adaptive locomotion in confined and dynamic environments.

title: HexSense Lunar Mapping: Deployable 360 Cameras for Panoramic Inspection & 3D Reconstruction
authors: Fangzheng Liu, Nicolas STAS, Ariel Ekblaw, Joseph A. Paradiso
abstract: The HexSense is a type of low-cost and miniature wireless sensor node that can be ballistically deployed from a rover or lander to the area of interest on the Lunar surface. Upon landing, each HexSense can automatically stand upright to realize a relatively determined orientation, enabling better wireless communication. With a modular design, each HexSense can carry different sensor payloads for different scientific applications. In this paper, we show a novel approach for in-situ panoramic inspection and 3D mapping of the area of interest on the lunar surface using HexSense equipped with custom-designed 360-degree cameras. The unique ballistic deployment mechanism of HexSense allows sensor nodes to be scattered across challenging terrain and automatically orient for optimal data collection. The captured images can be stitched together to panoramic images for in-situ omniview inspection. The images from multiple HexSenses can also be used to reconstruct a 3D model of the area of interest on the lunar surface. This method addresses the challenges of mapping in the hard-to-reach or dangerous areas for a slow-moving rover or a non-movable lander. We described the system design, time synchronization approach, localization approach, panoramic inspection, and 3D reconstruction result, highlighting the potential for scalable, distributed mapping to support future lunar exploration and scientific research.

title: Design and Characterisation of a Morphing Flexible Fin Towards Multi-Modal Locomotion
authors: Luke Johnson, Hemma Philamore, Andrew Conn
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Underwater robots are now regularly used for marine infrastructure inspection and environmental monitoring. The current state of the art underwater robots are driven by propellers, which suffer from a risk of entanglement in vegetation, or are limited to longdistance manoeuvring at intermediate depths, making it challenging to operate robots in benthic environments and in close proximity to obstacles. To address this, we present proof-of-principle for a morphing flexible fin (MFF) actuated by a single tendon driving a 3D-printed thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) 95A structure. The MFF folds between three configurations: an open state (State 1), a “three-toed” geometry (State 2), and a fully folded form (State 3). A 36.67% increase in stiffness was observed between State 1 and State 3, requiring only 2.62 N of tendon force. These results are presented with the aim of developing morphing limb structures which may enable a robot to swim, crawl, and navigate confined spaces using a single multifunctional structure.

title: Continuing Development and Enabling of Exploration Mission Systems Software
authors: Matthew Miller, David Charney, Kenneth Davis, Jacob Keller, Jackie Vu, Ben Feist, David MacKenzie, Luke McSherry, Arwa Khazali, Omar Baig, John Cox, Cameron Pittman, James Montalvo
abstract: To ensure the success of future human exploration mission such as NASA's Artemis campaign, a fully integrated environment for understanding mission operations data is critical. This paper presents an updated discussion of the suite of software tools from the Extravehicular Activity Mission Systems Software (EMSS) team, designed to enhance the “plan, train, fly, explore” workflow for both the International Space Station (ISS) and future lunar and deep-space operations. The EMSS suite of tools collectively address disparate data sources and inefficient workflows. The Collaborative Operations Data Activation (CODA) web platform temporally aligns mission data like video, audio, and telemetry, helping operators analyze past events in near real-time and reducing manual effort required for data synergy. Talky Bot is a near-real-time audio transcription application that provides written transcripts of multiple voice loops. For planetary surface operations, Artemis Extravehicular Activity (EVA) Geographic Information System (AEGIS) unites spatial and temporal data, helping to define plans that integrate and balance scientific value with mission priorities. Maestro is a platform for creating, managing, and executing EVA procedures, providing synchronized situational awareness. Finally, the EMSS Dashboard provides the broadest awareness of real-time surface EVA activities to the flight team. These tools collectively build a foundational, integrated data architecture that is essential for the safety and success of future human spaceflight missions, from lowEarth orbit (LEO) to deep space. This paper outlines the latest developments of these tools as well as their application and use in ongoing ISS and Artemis activities.

title: Numerical Investigation of a Lead-Free GaAs/$\text{CH}_{3} \text{NH}_{3} \text{SnI}_{3}$/RGO Perovskite Solar Cell Using SCAPS-1D
authors: Prasenjit Deb, Anwesha Choudhury, T Keerthi Priya
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The present work investigates and optimizes a lead-free tin-based perovskite solar cell with an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{G a A s} \boldsymbol{/} \mathbf{C H}_{\mathbf{3}} \mathbf{N H}_{\mathbf{3}} \mathbf{S n I}_{\mathbf{3}} \boldsymbol{/} \mathbf{R G O}$</tex> architecture using the SCAPS-1D numerical simulator under AM 1.5 G illumination. The study focuses on absorber layer thickness optimization (50-500 nm) and temperature-dependent device performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0-100^{\circ} \mathrm{C})$</tex>, while maintaining the reduced graphene oxide (RGO) hole transport layer and GaAs electron transport layer thickness at 150 nm each. The incorporation of RGO and GaAs provides improved energy band alignment with the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CH}_{3} \text{NH}_{3} \text{SnI}_{3}$</tex> absorber and enabling efficient charge extraction and reduced interfacial recombination. Optical absorption data of individual layers obtained from reported experimental studies were integrated into the simulation to ensure physical realism. The optimized device with a 500 nm absorber layer exhibits a high photosensitivity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.08955 \times 10^{17}$</tex> and a maximum photoresponsivity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.4597 \mathrm{A} / \mathrm{W}$</tex> under zero-bias conditions. The simulated solar cell achieves an efficiency of 21.98%, with a short-circuit current density of 26.27 mA/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, an open-circuit voltage of 0.977 V, and a fill factor of 85.62%. Temperature analysis reveals stable device operation up to ∼52°C, beyond which efficiency degradation occurs due to enhanced carrier recombination. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of GaAs and RGO as transport layers for high-performance, leadfree <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${S n}$</tex>-based perovskite solar cells.

title: A Secure Multi-Modal AI-Driven Blockchain Voting Framework with Post-Quantum Encryption and Deepfake-Resilient Authentication
authors: Thilagavathi P, Sivasankar Rajamani P, Marthandan T, Kowsalya P, Mohan K, Senthil Nathan S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The growing digitalization of the electoral system has created a serious question of security, privacy, and integrity. To address them, the following paper suggests a proposal of a Secure Multi-Modal AI-Driven Blockchain Voting Framework, which will employ artificial intelligence, block chain technology, Internet of Things (IOT), and post-quantum cryptography to develop a stable and resistant to future digital voting system. The suggested model involves the application of multi-modal biometric authentication, including the use of deep learning-based face recognition and the one-time password authentication to ensure high and deep fake-impregnable voter authentication and check against the cases of illegal and repeated voting. Voting machines that are IOT ready allow real-time voting which is secured and the votes are encrypted using post-quantum cryptographic algorithms before being stored in an indestructible block chain registry to ensure confidentiality, integrity and transparency. The experimental results indicate that the offered system allows attaining high levels of authentication between 96.85 % and 98.72 %, which is significantly higher than the current machine learning-based methods, and demonstrates the high level of protection against fraud and impersonation attacks. The results indicate that the suggested framework can be scaled, is tamper-proof, privacy secure, and has long-term durability against emerging cyber threats therefore enhancing voter trust and guaranteeing reliable digital governance.

title: Decentralized Allocation of Observation Tasks for Cooperating Agile Spacecraft Clusters
authors: Nicholas Niziolek, Nolan Ales, Eric Frew, Jonathan Chan, Michael Jacobs, Sam Packard, Moses Chan
abstract: The rapid growth in the number of large-scale Earth-observing spacecraft constellations has made autonomous task allocation and observation scheduling problems increasingly important. This work describes a decentralized approach to the task allocation and observation scheduling problem, distributing target custody among spacecraft agents to maximize total mission coverage. The approach described leverages the decentralized consensus-based bundle algorithm (CBBA), alongside a mixed integer linear program (MILP) formulation to approximate spacecraft scheduling and score target bundles with higher fidelity. Using this approach, task bundle scoring is made aware of potential multiple simultaneous target observations by the same sensor, as in the case of closely grouped targets, rather than focusing on single targets. The main drawback of this approach comes in the required edge computing capabilities, requiring a MILP to be solved many times. The resulting decentralized performance was shown to be relatively close to that of the same MILP formulation run as a centralized scheduler. It was also seen that this algorithm shows similar performance across multiple communication models, with significant impact seen only when communication was not possible.

title: Long Run Effects of Feed-In Premiums and Carbon Taxes on Generation Investment and Social Surplus
authors: Hirotaka Hiraiwa, Kazuya Ito, Ryuta Takashima
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: This study analyzes how different policy instruments, namely a feed-in premium (FIP) and a carbon tax, affect generation investment, the generation mix, and social surplus in electricity markets using a longrun equilibrium model. The results indicate that the FIP strongly promotes investment in renewable energy and increases the future share of renewables, but may raise costs for consumers. In contrast, a carbon tax effectively discourages investment in fossil fuel based generation, while its impact on renewable investment is more limited and may involve certain surplus costs. These findings suggest that relying on a single policy instrument is insufficient, and that electricity market policies should be designed using an appropriate combination of instruments to balance multiple policy objectives.

title: Stackelberg-Auction Demand Response Design to Enhance Prosumers Participation in Local Electricity Markets
authors: Arian Khaledi, Fatma Ahmed, Mumu M. Rahman, Khaled Abedrabboh, Luluwah Al Fagih
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Local Electricity Markets (LEMs) enable decentralized energy trading, reducing carbon emissions and grid costs while integrating renewable energy sources. However, barriers such as intermittent generation, fluctuating demand, and consumer uncertainty prevent effective management. This research presents an auction-based demand response mechanism that uses a Stackelberg game to increase the engagement of prosumers in LEMs. The model analyzes consumer participation, demand flexibility, and decision-making, taking into account the uncertainty surrounding willingness to participate. The grid operator serves as the leader, sending price signals to encourage flexibility, while aggregators and distributed energy resource owners participate in a uniform-price auction. The operator selects the lowest-cost proposals to ensure grid stability and reliability. The Stackelberg game model used in our research enables precise forecasting of demand response (DR) behavior and improves decision-making in a dynamic energy landscape from the operator's perspective. The outcome is a decision-making tool that enables operators to predict consumer behavior more efficiently and increase demand-side participation in LEMs. This study provides policymakers with significant information, including data-driven recommendations to enhance the adoption of renewable energy sources, improve regulatory frameworks, and promote sustainable growth in the energy market. Additionally, this study facilitates the design of efficient, flexible, and resilient energy systems.

title: Uncertainty-Calibrated Topology-Preserving Diffusion Oversampling (UTP-DO) for Imbalanced Learning
authors: R. Indhumathi, S. Moses Dian
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Class imbalance has been an inherent problem in supervised learning, and it results in biased classifiers and ineffective generalization on minorities. Current methods of oversampling, such as interpolation-based and adversarial generative models, are both ineffective at capturing the underlying geometry of minority data distributions as well as unstable and prone to mode collapse. To address these shortcomings, the current paper introduces an Uncertainty-Calibrated Topology-Preserving Diffusion Oversampling (UTP-DO) architecture for imbalanced learning. The proposed method redefines minority augmentation as conditional generative modelling issue in latent representation space. To estimate predictive uncertainty at the sample level, predictive uncertainty is first estimated through a deep classifier continuously. The scores of these uncertainties are then used as conditioning signals in a diffusion-based generative model that generates minority embeddings with better mode coverage and stability. Manifold distortion should be avoided, and a topology-respecting regularization mechanism is proposed, which is based on graph Laplacian image constraints and density-consistency alignment, and ensures that synthetic samples do not lose the local structure of the neighbourhood or the overall geometric structure of the geometry. The last classifier is trained using the augmented dataset, which consists of the real instances and the topology-consistent synthetic samples. The comprehensive testing of numerous benchmark data sets shows that the proposed framework achieves higher results than state-of-the-art SMOTE variants and GAN-based augmentation algorithms in terms of F1-score, G-mean, and balanced accuracy. The findings show that geometry-sensitive diffusion-based augmentation is a strong and scalable method of AI-based imbalanced data classification.

title: Integrating Neurosymbolic AI for Pneumonia Diagnosis Using Chest X-Rays: A Synergistic Approach
authors: Sethma N. Weerasinghe, Sampath Deegalla, Eranga Dharmarathne, Rajitha Thennakoon, Damayanthi Herath
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Pneumonia remains a major global health concern, and early diagnosis from chest X-rays is essential for timely treatment. While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have demonstrated strong performance in medical image analysis, their limited interpretability often reduces clinical trust and adoption. In this work, we propose a neurosymbolic AI framework that combines CNN based image prediction with symbolic reasoning derived from structured clinical symptoms and image based features, integrating data-driven learning with interpretable rule based reasoning to improve both diagnostic accuracy and transparency. The proposed framework was evaluated on 4,277 chest X-ray images from the PadChest dataset, containing balanced pneumonia and non pneumonia cases. Six CNN architectures were trained to classify chest X-rays, and Local Interpretable Model agnostic Explanations (LIME) and Gradient weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) were applied to identify salient regions and gradient based cues influencing model predictions. LIME provides localized, feature based explanations by approximating the model's decision boundary around individual predictions, while Grad-CAM generates class discriminative heatmaps that visualize diagnostically relevant regions in chest X-ray images. Symbolic rules reflecting clinical symptom patterns were generated using decision tree based algorithms and integrated with CNN outputs through a weighted ensemble strategy. The resulting neurosymbolic model achieved 78% accuracy, representing an 8% improvement over the EfficientNetB0 baseline, while reducing false negatives by 19%. Interpretability also improved substantially: attention accuracy reached 0.83 IoU, rule traceability remained at 100%, and clinical validation by three medical doctors using a structured 10 point Likert scale yielded an explanation plausibility score of 8.9/10 with 89% clinical agreement. These findings demonstrate that neurosymbolic AI can effectively enhance both predictive performance and interpretability in medical imaging, providing a reliable decision support tool for AI-assisted pneumonia diagnosis.

title: Multi-Stage Temporal Connection Operation Simulation Method for Electric-Thermal-Hydrogen-Ammonia Integrated Energy System
authors: Yiran Wang, Jian Chen, Yihe Zhao, Wen Zhang
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The electric-thermal-hydrogen-ammonia integrated energy system can achieve renewable energy absorption and multi-time-scale energy balance through the coordination of multiple energy storage systems. To better achieve the rapid operation simulation of integrated systems incorporating hydrogen and ammonia energy, as well as full utilization of energy, this paper proposes a multi-stage temporal connection operation simulation method for the electric-thermal-hydrogen-ammonia integrated energy system. This method consists of three stages, including data processing, annual energy optimization, and daily power optimization. The optimization problems in these stages are formulated as a mixed-integer linear programming model and solved using the Gurobi solver. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed multi-stage temporal connection operation simulation method for the electric-thermal-hydrogen-ammonia integrated energy system is feasible and superior.

title: A Low-Power VLSI Architecture for Decentralized Data Fusion in Cooperative Cognitive Radio Networks
authors: Dharshana Shri S, Mugilan D
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: As Cognitive Radio Networks (CRN) transition towards 6 G integration, the energy overhead with continuous spectrum monitoring remains a critical hardware bottleneck. This paper proposes with an high-efficiency, low-power VLSI architecture designed with decentralized data fusion in Cooperative Spectrum Sensing (CSS). Distinct conventional software defined approaches suffers from high computational latency, the proposed design utilizes with dedicated hardware-based parallel voting logics at the Register-Transfer Level (RTL). Key optimizations which includes integration of a CORDIC-based Energy Detection Unit (EDU) to eliminate power-hungry multipliers and the implementation of a fine-grained clock gating within the data fusion tree to minimize dynamic power consumption. The architecture facilitate peer-to-peer decision collection, which reducing the communication energy typically required for the centralized fusion centers. Simulation results, executed using the 45 nm CMOS standard cell library, demonstrate reduction in the Power-Delay Product (PDP) and significant area saving compared to existing serial architectures. The design are maintains a robust probability of detection <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(P_{d}>0.9)$</tex> even under the low SNR conditions, provide scalable and energy-efficient solution for the battery-constrained secondary users with next-generation networks.

title: A Focused Review of Multimodal CTR Prediction for Cold-Start Recommendation
authors: Sharm Fernando, Nethmi Wijesinghe
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Cold-start remains a persistent failure mode in clickthrough rate (CTR) prediction because newly introduced items and users lack the interaction history required by conventional ID-embedding pipelines. In these settings, multimodal content signals such as text and images often provide the only reliable priors for ranking before behavioural evidence accumulates. This paper presents a focused review of multimodal CTR prediction under cold-start constraints, synthesising the literature through a compact taxonomy spanning (i) multimodal item representation via modern encoders and pretraining, (ii) fusion architectures ranging from early and late fusion to attention-based mechanisms, and (iii) cold-start transfer techniques including graph-based relational propagation, contrastive and generative alignment, diffusion-style multimodal generation, and meta-learning for rapid adaptation. Beyond modelling advances, the review highlights evaluation as a key source of inconsistency, clarifying operational definitions of “cold” (zero-shot versus warm-up), recommending time-based and stratified protocols, and outlining minimum reproducibility requirements. The paper concludes by identifying actionable research directions around preference-semantic mismatch, robustness to modality missingness, latency-aware deployment, and faithful explainability for content-driven CTR predictions.

title: EmpathiBot: An emotion aware ChatBot for enhanced customer service using ImageBind with Cross-Modal Transformer Fusion
authors: Bharathi V, Muruganantham A, Stella Mary S, Karthick S, Sabarish G
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: The customer service chatbots struggle to understand the feelings of the people. The chatbots relying only on text sentiment analysis get things right just 60–70% of the time, often misreading emotions because they focus only on words. Thus, EmpathiBot becomes essential in the current scenario. EmpathiBot is a novel ImageBind CMF-based framework that is proposed to wholeheartedly understand customer emotions based on the textual content and voice tone as well as facial expression. ImageBind generates holistic embeddings that naturally capture emotional information across modalities, while our Cross-Modal Transformer assigns weights to input from each modality in an adaptive manner based on context. This procedure enables inferring valence (in-)congruency even from incomplete and noisy data-stimuli. Preliminary results reveal that our EmpathiBot significantly outperforms text-only systems and non-empathetic state-of-the-art multi-modal models in terms of subtle emotion including sarcasm and frustration perception. EmpatiBot increases customer satisfaction scores by 22.4% when deployed in simulation. By replying contextually relevant messages, Empathibot creates the foundation for enhanced customer service experiences with empathy and better time effectiveness. EmpathiBot also offers insights on its emotional decisions, thereby increasing trust and the usability by humans.

title: Customer Perception of Digital Banking Applications: A Sentiment Analysis and Topic Modelling Analysis of Google Play Store Reviews
authors: Sathankumar S, M. Karthikram, S. Rahini, Shwetha A, Nivedha K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: This research examines the feelings of customers regarding digital banking applications by employing the use of reviews from the Google Play Store to gain insights into people’s experiences in the emerging mobile and digital payment industry. The English reviews that were posted after January 2025 and had more than ten words been analyzed. The sentiment was categorized as positive, negative, or neutral using Naïve Bayes and Support Vector Machine (SVM) methods, and the key points of discussion were extracted using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA). Around 46% of the reviews were positive, stating that the app was user-friendly and transactions were smooth. Around 37.3% of the reviews were negative, stating app crashes, login/OTP issues, unsuccessful transactions, and poor customer service. SVM was more accurate than Naïve Bayes with an accuracy of 90.1%. The primary topics of discussion included system performance, UPI/mobile transactions, technical issues, and customer services.

title: Translive: Conceptual Design of an AI-Driven Real-Time Multilingual Speech Translation Framework
authors: Kavin R, Dinesh S, Karthick J
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Growing global needs are driving increased demand for instantaneous cross-language communication across education, healthcare, enterprise collaboration, and public interaction. Notwithstanding significant progress in artificial intelligence-driven translation, many existing solutions depend on closed cloud-based platforms that can introduce processing delays, limit customizability, and raise privacy concerns. This paper presents a conceptual framework for TransLive, a component-based frame-work for instantaneous spoken-language conversion designed to deliver scalable and privacy-aware multilingual communication on mobile devices. The proposed framework integrates Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR), Neural Machine Translation (NMT), and Text-to-Speech (TTS) modules in a streaming microservice pipeline designed to produce parallel translations across several target languages. A central concept is performing ASR once and translating the resulting text into several languages in parallel, enabling listeners to receive continuous translated audio in their preferred language. Although conceptual, this work outlines the theoretical foundation, architecture, mobile integration strategy, evaluation plan, and real-world applications for TransLive.

title: CARE: Contrastive Alignment for ADL Recognition from Event-Triggered Sensor Streams
authors: Junhao Zhao, Zishuai Liu, Ruili Fang, Jin Lu, Linghan Zhang, Fei Dou
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: The recognition of Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) from event-triggered ambient sensors is an essential task in Ambient Assisted Living, yet existing methods remain constrained by representation-level limitations. Sequence-based approaches preserve temporal order of sensor activations but are sensitive to noise and lack spatial awareness, while image-based approaches capture global patterns and implicit spatial correlations but compress fine-grained temporal dynamics and distort sensor layouts. Naïve fusion (e.g., feature concatenation) fails to enforce alignment between sequence- and image-based representation views, underutilizing their complementary strengths. We propose Contrastive Alignment for ADL Recognition from Event-Triggered Sensor Streams (CARE), an end-to-end framework that jointly optimizes representation learning via Sequence–Image Contrastive Alignment (SICA) and classification via cross-entropy, ensuring both cross-representation alignment and task-specific discriminability. CARE integrates (i) time-aware, noise-resilient sequence encoding with (ii) spatially-informed and frequency-sensitive image representations, and employs (iii) a joint contrastive-classification objective for end-to-end learning of aligned and discriminative embeddings. Evaluated on three CASAS datasets, CARE achieves state-of-the-art performance (89.8% on Milan, 88.9% on Cairo, and 73.3% on Kyoto7) and demonstrates robustness to sensor malfunctions and layout variability, highlighting its potential for reliable ADL recognition in smart homes. We release our code at https://github.com/Jhziiiig/CARE.

title: Grey Wolf Optimization Enhanced Random Forest Framework for LLM Software Defect Prediction
authors: D R Medhunhashini, B Varun, J Ramkumar, V Valarmathi, R Karthikeyan, R K Kavitha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: Software Defect Prediction in Large Language Models (LLMs) has become significant area to have an impressive ability in understanding the natural language generation. The models do give flawed results in the form of hallucinations, factual errors and logical contradictions, however, they stand to question the use of these models in critical formulations. Contrary to conventional software defect prediction, there is little data on the systematic detection of defects in LLM outputs. In this paper we present an innovative solution of the LLM Defect Prediction (GWOEnRF) framework based on the Optimization-based solution to the problem of detecting defective outputs of LLMs. The defect repository is built on benchmark data whose ground-truth solutions are known, and errors on the part of the LLM in their responses are considered defects. Prompt and response semantic embeddings are obtained and optimized though the Grey Wolf Optimization algorithm on the classifier parameter tuning problem. The experimental findings indicate that the suggested framework is much better at detecting defects than the non-optimized baselines. The research work leads to the increase of the credibility and reliability of large language models.

title: IoT and Machine Learning-Based Automated Poultry Feeding
authors: Shivathmicka. K.S, Boopathy S, Selva Divya. R, Senthil Kumar. K.S, Vidhya Dharshini. J.N, N Prakash
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Humans still play a vital role in managing and supervising most chicken farms in Brunei, where factors such as air quality, temperature, lighting, air circulation, humidity, and feeding must be carefully monitored and controlled, as they directly affect productivity. Currently, Brunei faces a high mortality rate among broiler chickens. This study aims to automate the monitoring and control of humidity, temperature, air quality, and feeding systems using Internet of Things (IoT) and Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) technologies to enhance productivity, reduce mortality, and improve flock health. It reviews the latest developments in both hardware and software for data collection and analysis in poultry farming, supported by examples from existing studies. The software components include data acquisition, analysis, and processing tools, while the hardware setup consists of cameras, lighting systems, and sensor placements for real-time monitoring. A prototype was developed integrating IoT and WSN technologies, enabling continuous evaluation of farm conditions against predefined thresholds. When key performance indicators exceed set limits, the system initiates corrective measures to prevent losses. Additionally, an attention encoder model was proposed for extracting features from chicken images to enhance detection accuracy. Experimental results indicate that incorporating this encoder with DenseNet improves the precision of identifying chicken maturity. The system also includes an SMS alert function to notify users automatically and a web-based interface for visualizing and monitoring key parameters. This study concludes by identifying future directions and challenges for large-scale implementation to ensure sustainable and efficient poultry production in Brunei.

title: Assessment of Grail Radio Science Signal Noise Patterns in Support of Lunar Radio Occultation
authors: Yu-Ming Yang, Kamal Oudrhiri, Paul Withers, Daniel Erwin, Dustin Buccino
abstract: Recently, JPL has utilized NASA's Gravity Recovery and Interior Laboratory (GRAIL) radio science as Signals of Opportunity (SoOP) to map the moon's ionosphere and investigate the measurements for the future Lunar Radio Occultation (RO) mission, which aims to study Lunar dust and surrounding plasma interactions. This research will provide an assessment of the GRAIL radio science signal noise patterns corresponding to different locations in Geocentric Solar Ecliptic (GSE) coordinate, solar plasmas conditions, and orbit dynamics of radio occultation experiments for the new scientific observations derived using GRAIL RO data; the statistical analysis of the noise patterns, estimated using the actual GRAIL RO data, will be in comparison with the uncertainties derived using analytical models. The research findings provide a reference for the scientific community to design future Lunar Radio Occultation Missions, improving the understanding of constraints and errors in these new scientific measurements made by GRAIL-like Radio Science instruments.

title: Front Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)

title: Efficient-Net and Artificial Intelligence for Precise Plant Disease Identification
authors: Neelavathi G, Venkatasalam K, Ponlatha S, Devaki K, Sri Sarvesan M G, Lakshana M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The world economy depends heavily on agriculture, and plant ailments are a major danger to harvest productivity and food security. Conventional illness recognition techniques take a lot of time and require specialized knowledge that agriculturalists may not always have. Advances in automated plant illness recognition and prevention have become more accurate and efficient with Artificial Intelligence (AI). This paper is a systematic study of convolutional neural networks, specifically the family of Efficient-Net, to detect plant diseases with high accuracy. By maximizing both accuracy and processing efficiency, the cutting-edge deep learning model Efficient-Net has demonstrated exceptional performance in image categorization tests. Large-scale agricultural datasets benefit greatly from the use of a compound scaling technique that strikes a balance between network depth, width, and resolution. The dataset of the Plant Village, which contains thousands of annotated images representing healthy and diseased flora, was used as an advantage to train the Efficient-NetB4 architecture that was used in this study. The model outperforms conventional CNN architectures like Alex Net and VGG16 in terms of accuracy while differentiating between various plant diseases. To enhance realworld applicability and integrate the trained prototypical into a mobile-based application, enabling agriculturalists to capture plant leaf images for prompt illness analysis. Besides, Grad-wCAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping) is utilized to provide visual explanations of the model inference, thus, improving the level of transparency and strengthening the belief in AI-based decision-making. The investigational outcomes indicate that Efficient-Net-B4 attains an accurateness of over 95% in classifying plant diseases, making it a reliable tool for precision agriculture. By assisting farmers in taking prompt preventive action, artificial intelligence (AI) in plant illness detection will lower yield losses and advance sustainable farming. Further investigations are needed in the framework of continuous monitoring of plant health, which should be in regard to increasing the dataset size, enhancing real-time detection, and including IoT sensors in the future.

title: Inflight Performance of Pose and Position Vision Based Sensor Enabling Submillimetre Formation Flight
authors: Mathias Benn, John L. Jørgensen, Peter S. Jørgensen, Troelz Denver, Raphael Rougeot, Daniel Serrano
abstract: ESA's mission PROBA3 consist of two space segments, the Coronagraph spacecraft and the Occulter spacecraft operated at a distance between 25m and 300m, and co-aligned to the sun direction. In addition to in-orbit Formation Flying demonstration, one science objective is to achieve science observation of the Sun's corona close to the solar rim at a specific inter-satellite distance of 150m. This configuration creates challenging illumination conditions for optical instruments which are needed to control the Formation at various operations scenarios, e.g. when cameras on the Sunwards Occulter are pointed to the Coronagraph, with the fully sunlit Earth in the background. This work presents the initial performance evaluations achieved during the commissioning phase of the PROBA3 mission. The Vision Based Sensor (VBS) extension of the micro Advanced Stellar Compass (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu$</tex> ASC) enables optical navigation capabilities, supporting formation flight, rendezvous and docking of multi-segment spacecraft - in both cooperative and non-cooperative modes. The robustness of the cooperative pose and position determination method by the VBS system rely on high accuracy synchronization between the camera system, located on the observing spacecraft, and the pulse-coded LED mires, located on the target spacecraft. Based on knowledge of the LED mires location on the target spacecraft, the VBS system provides high precision pose and position information relative to the observing spacecraft. We discuss the design aspects of this system, outlining its performance envelope, and present the inflight performance achieved of the VBS system during the commissioning phase of PROBA3.

title: Coordinated Optimization of Delivery Routes and Charging Strategies for Electric Logistics Vehicles
authors: Yulin Yang, Zilong Cai, Jian Wang, Xi Wang, Xihui Zhang, Shibin Gao
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Under the dual-carbon goals, the large-scale deployment of electric logistics vehicles imposes increasing requirements for coordinated optimization between urban transportation systems and distribution network operation. To address the strong coupling between route planning and charging decisions, as well as the tendency of time-of-use electricity pricing to induce concentrated charging loads due to inappropriate charging timing, this paper develops a coordinated optimization model for delivery routing and charging strategies with the objective of minimizing total vehicle operating cost. Within a unified framework, the proposed model explicitly characterizes route selection, dynamic battery energy evolution, and charging timing decisions under time-of-use electricity price constraints. On this basis, a dual-period energy replenishment strategy combining daytime mixed charging with optimized charging timing and nighttime orderly charging is proposed, and an improved genetic algorithm is employed to solve the resulting optimization problem. Case study results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively reduces operational costs while suppressing charging concurrency, thereby enhancing distribution-grid friendliness and overall system coordination.

title: RGB-NIR Reflectance and 3D Microtopography for Lunar Regolith Analysis Using ToF Imaging Systems
authors: Don D. Haddad, Cody Paige, Ariel Ekblaw, Joseph A. Paradiso, Dava J. Newman, Amanda Cook, Ariel N. Deutsch, Jennifer Heldmann, Anthony Colaprete
abstract: We present an integrated approach for analyzing lunar regolith using co-registered RGB—near-infrared (NIR) reflectance and 3D microtopography derived from a compact Time-of-Flight (ToF) imaging system. The method enables simultaneous surface geometry reconstruction and reflectance-based material characterization under bandwidth, illumination, and payload constraints relevant to lunar surface exploration. Controlled laboratory experiments were conducted on standardized lunar regolith simulants (NU-LHT-2M, LHT-3M, and JSC-1A), systematically varying hydration state and salinity using sodium chloride (NaCl) as a practical analog for volatile residues or permafrost-like deposits. Measurements reveal consistent, monotonic shifts in 850 nm NIR reflectance intensity correlated with increasing moisture content and salt concentration. When combined with visible-band ratios and millimeter-scale surface microtopography, these changes provide repeatable indicators of surface composition, texture, and optical maturity. Radiometric calibration using NIST-traceable photometric targets and linear statistical modeling demonstrates that single-band NIR reflectance, when fused with RGB information and local surface geometry, yields operationally useful proxies for volatile surrogates and compositional contrast. The approach builds on prior demonstrations of low-cost ToF sensing for planetary exploration and is validated in lunar regolith analog environments in support of in-situ prospecting and science operations. These results indicate that compact RGB-NIR ToF imaging systems can serve as low-SWaP, dual-use instruments for lunar science and rover prospecting, providing geometrically co-registered spectral cues that complement multispectral imaging. By enabling material-aware surface assessment directly on 3D reconstructions, the approach supports data fusion workflows and operational decision-making under bandwidth and illumination constraints.

title: The Self-Healing Software Organism: an Evolutionary Runtime Defense Framework for Autonomous Regeneration
authors: Mohammed Ishaque, Zainab Siddiqui, Mashael Hassan Alzahrani, Hanin Mohammed Almqdashi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Modern cyberattacks increasingly exploit the static and predictable structure of software systems. Traditional intrusion detection systems (IDS) detect anomalies but cannot autonomously regenerate or reconfigure software during execution, leaving long windows of exposure between detection and patch deployment. This paper introduces the Self-Healing Software Organism (SHSO), a biologically inspired, AI-driven runtime defense framework that shifts the paradigm from passive intrusion detection to autonomous regeneration. SHSO continuously generates and maintains a pool of functionally equivalent but syntactically diverse digital alleles, validated in a micro-sandbox before being deployed in a live system. Upon detecting a successful or imminent exploit, SHSO performs an online allele swap, altering the memory layout, control-flow graph, and API invocation sequences, thereby invalidating the attacker's assumptions. A reinforcement learning (RL) module learns an optimal policy for allele selection that balances security gains against performance overhead and stability constraints. Using experimental results, we illustrate that SHSO can reduce exploit success rates by up to 66 %, shorten exploit retention time by 63.4 %, and maintain runtime overhead below 4.1 %. This work opens a pathway toward evolutionary, self-adaptive software defense mechanisms suitable for smart cities, cyberphysical systems, and future cognitive infrastructures.

title: Automated Detection of Prophetic Hadith: Analyzing the Textual Content
authors: Rehab Qarout, Rahmah Alalawi, Hanen Himdi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The Qur'an and Sunnah are the primary sources of Islam. The hadith includes everything the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) said, did, or described. Hadiths help explain and clarify the teachings of the Qur'an, guiding Muslims in their daily lives. However, many fabricated hadith have spread recently. Therefore, some Muslims find it challenging to identify authentic and reliable hadiths and distinguish them from weak hadiths. For this reason, the development of automated classification systems is crucial for determining the authenticity of hadiths. The authenticity of hadiths depends on two essential parts: the chain of narrators called (Sanad or Isnsd) and the narrative called (Maten). In this study, we proposed an approach to detect Hadith authenticity using transformer-based models.
