title: Research on Oil Level Detection Technology and Application for Oil-Filled Terminations of High-Voltage Cables Based on Terahertz Echoes
authors: Zhaoyun Huang, Gang Xiong, Lige Liu, Qianglin Fu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address the challenges associated with monitoring oil levels inside the oil-filled terminations of high-voltage cables—such as the difficulty of detection, the significant influence of environmental factors on conventional methods, and the invasive nature of traditional testing—this paper proposes a non-invasive liquid level detection method based on changes in the reflected energy of ultrasonic pulse-echo signals. Through a comparative analysis of the physical boundaries of three different methods—the pulse-echo method, the impedance method and the Lamb wave method—and by constructing an acoustic propagation model for multi-layer media comprising silicone rubber, glass fibre and silicone oil, as well as establishing a finite element simulation platform for the acoustic pressure within cable terminations; the applicability advantages of the pulse-echo method in multi-layer composite media were established, the reflection mechanisms of sound waves at solid-liquid and solid-gas interfaces were clarified, and the acoustic pressure distribution characteristics of ultrasonic waves in different media environments were quantitatively analysed. Through theoretical simulation analysis, it was found that due to acoustic impedance mismatch, near-total reflection occurs at the solid-gas interface, whilst at the solid-liquid interface, the echo signal exhibits significant exponential attenuation due to the transmission of acoustic energy. This study verifies the feasibility of oil level identification based on differences in echo energy, providing both a theoretical and technical foundation for the non-destructive testing of oil levels in oil-filled cable terminations.

title: Robust and Interpretable Deep Learning for Type 2 Diabetes Prediction Using Spatial, Hybrid, and Attention-Based Models
authors: Maha Alhazmi, Hanen Himdi, Aisha Blfgeh, Reham Alhejaili
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Early prediction of type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) remains challenging owing to severe class imbalance and the requirement for high recall in screening-oriented settings. Three representative models were compared: a two-dimensional Convolutional Neural Network (CNN-2D), Convolutional Long Short-Term Memory network (CNN-LSTM), and explainable DeepNetX2/EchoceptionNet hybrid model. To better detect the minority class, imbalance-aware approaches have been adopted, including class weighting, the Adaptive Synthetic Oversampling technique (ADASYN), and a hybrid loss function combining CrossEntropy Loss (CE) and Focal Loss (FL). The CNN-2D model achieved the highest recall (0.8902), making it an ideal candidate for early screening, whereas the DeepNetX2/EchoceptionNet hybrid model demonstrated the best performance (F1 = 0.6678) with SHAP and LIME-based interpretability. Although the CNN-LSTM model achieved very high precision (0.9864), its relatively low recall indicates a trade-off between diagnostic precision and screening sensitivity. Validation on an independent holdout cohort of 72,191 samples showed strong generalization, with receiver operating characteristic area under the curve (ROC-AUC) values above 0.96 for all models. These results demonstrate that integrating spatial, sequential, and hybrid learning with imbalanceaware optimization can provide robust and interpretable AI-based diabetes risk prediction.

title: A Comparative Review of Liveness Detection Methods for Hand Vein Identification
authors: Asma Bahumayd, Sumaya Mustafa, Sumaiyah Aldhahri, Rania A. Elmanfaloty
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Hand vein identification is a biometric technique that recognizes individuals using the unique vascular patterns beneath the skin captured by near infrared imaging. Although it provides high security, it remains vulnerable to presentation attacks such as printed images, artificial hands, and replayed infrared data, making liveness detection a necessary security component as well as a practical system-level consideration. This study aims to identify the most effective liveness detection approaches by systematically reviewing and comparing reported results from prior studies. The comparison is conducted using performance metrics reported in the literature, including accuracy, error rates, and processing time, in addition to practical system considerations. Furthermore, a user questionnaire is analyzed to assess user preference and acceptance, providing insight into the trade-off between security and usability.

title: Photovoltaic Power Forecasting Based on Pretrained TimeGPT and Few-Shot Adaptation
authors: Xin Deng, Changfa Liu, Yuqi Zhou, Weijie Xu, Xuan Li, Weiyao Wu, Jiaqi Ruan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Accurate photovoltaic (PV) power forecasting is essential for the secure and economical operation of power systems. However, newly employed PV stations has no sufficient data for forecasting model training. To address this issue, this paper investigates the application of TimeGPT to single-station PV power forecasting and introduces a few-shot fine-tuning strategy to improve model performance. A TimeGPT-based multi-horizon forecasting framework is developed using a 30-day historical context window, and experiments are conducted under four forecasting horizons, i.e., 2h, 6h, 12h, and 24h. In addition, learning curve experiments are designed by gradually increasing the fine-tuning data size from 7 to 105 days to evaluate the model ’s adaptation capability and data efficiency. The results show that few-shot fine-tuning consistently reduces both MAE and RMSE on the validation and test sets compared with zero-shot forecasting, while the learning curves indicate that TimeGPT can achieve effective adaptation with limited historical data. These findings demonstrate that the proposed method provides a promising solution for PV power forecasting in data-scarce scenarios.

title: Accelerating the Pre-Silicon Functional Verification Value Ramp Using Aspect Oriented Development
authors: Hamilton Carter
abstract: In object-oriented verification methodologies like the Universal Verification Methodology, (UVM), where every checker object is run against the design for every test case-there is a high price to pay for frequent check-ins of new or modified verification code: a single flawed checker can bring down an entire regression causing 1000s of test cases to report false negative failures. These massive false-negative incidents are costly in two ways, first, we lose valuable coverage that could have been gained if the constrained random test cases had not erroneously failed, (typically, coverage is only collected from passing test cases.) Second, new, real bugs-surrounded by 1000s of false failures-may be masked or simply ignored. Therefore, the desire to simulate new verification code against a DUT is tempered by the desire not to ruin the existing verification test bench and its associated regression data. This creates a tug of war that can lead to long smoke tests and code review processes that retard the rate at which verification value can be added. We demonstrate a novel development process with three advantages: 1.It significantly reduces verification coding to regression delivery time allowing new test cases and new checkers to be released into regression rapidly. 2.It guarantees there will be no regression-wide false negative failures as a result of the newly released code. 3.It accelerates the speed at which new engineers can be productively added to a verification project. Our novel methodology leverages a programming paradigm defined in the 1970s, but seldom used: aspect-oriented programming, (AOP). We first review the concepts of AOP demonstrating that these concepts-extending multiple classes to add new or modified layers of functionality to the test bench-can be easily implemented using SystemVerilog and the UVM factory pattern. We then review the object-oriented UVM framework paying particular attention to classes that need to be created or modified to implement a checker for an unverified design feature along with the stimulus necessary to exercise that feature. We outline how to deploy aspect-oriented techniques using the UVM factory pattern to insulate the existing verification test bench from new development while allowing the newly developed code to utilize and benefit from the features of the existing test bench. Then, we discuss how the organization inherent in AOP accelerates the learning curve faced by new engineers entering a verification project. In the paper's final section, we briefly review both the implementation and benefits of the process and discuss other avenues where it can be efficiently deployed.

title: Domain-Robust AI for Dental Disease Detection: Integrating Adversarial Feature Alignment, Adaptive Attention Fusion, and Class-Balanced Loss
authors: Ahmad Alfaqehi, Hossam Meshref
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Panoramic dental radiography is essential for diagnosis, yet manual interpretation suffers from inter-observer variability and inefficiency. This research introduces a fully automated Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system combining state-of-the-art instance segmentation with advanced classification networks through a validated two-stage pipeline: (1) Detectron2 Mask R-CNN (ResNet-50-FPN) for pixel-level tooth extraction, and (2) transfer learning across six CNN architectures for classification. A large-scale dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3, 0 7 7}$</tex> panoramic images was compiled from four sources: DENTEX Challenge (705), Tufts Dental Database (1,000), Panoramic Dental Dataset (100), and Dental Radiography Dataset <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(1,272)$</tex>. Using intelligent extraction and “Split-First, Augment-Later” methodology, we generated 11,040 balanced tooth images across three pathology classes: Caries, Restoration, and Implant. In this study, a comparative analysis of deep learning architectures for dental pathology screening reveals that MobileNetV3-Large achieves a high degree of diagnostic precision without sacrificing computational efficiency. The model attained 98.7% accuracy and an F1 Score of 0.99, maintaining an inference latency of 12 ms (83 FPS). These results represent a notable improvement over the 85.6% accuracy observed with VGG16, suggesting that lightweight neural networks are a viable candidate for real-time clinical integration.

title: IoT-Integrated EEG Microstate Analytics for Ultraprecise Sleep and Cognitive Health Monitoring Using Multi-Scale Quantum-Phase and Graph-Convolutional Frameworks
authors: P. Baraneedharan, Vijay Aravind. S, Hariharan. K, A. Kumaravel
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Mental illnesses are highly related to sleep disorders, and although the brain signals are complicated, real-time monitoring poses a challenge. The paper is an IoT-enabled project of sleep state and mental health monitoring using EEG signals. EEG-based wearables continuously record the brain, which is used with four novel inferences Baptized as Quantum-Inspired Phase Pattern Extraction (QIPPE) to detect micro-state transitions, Hierarchical Temporal Attention Mapping (HTAM) to detect adaptive weightings of features, Multiscale Nonlinear Entropy Fusion (MNEF) to detect anomalies, and Graph-Convolutional Sleep State Prediction (GC-SSP) to predict inter-channel connectivity. The detection of sleep stages and mental health abnormalities proposed is accurate with low latency. The experimental findings show a higher accuracy rate of detection compared to the traditional methods and the absence of challenges when implementing the IoT to monitor remote environments. It is a framework that allows a customized mental health since EEG can be translated into actionable data, which can be used as a groundbreaking tool to conduct early intervention and ongoing monitoring.

title: An Adaptive Algorithm Selection and Load Forecasting Framework for New Power Systems Integrating Task Profiling and MATD3
authors: Dong Wang, Tianlong Wang, Taojing Zhang, Jiahao Liu, Yanyi Huang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address the challenges of high randomness and multi-temporal-spatial coupling in load forecasting with high-proportion renewable energy, this paper proposes an adaptive algorithm selection framework integrating task profiling with multi-agent reinforcement learning. A multi-dimensional task profile captures key forecasting features (data scale, temporal patterns, statistical distribution). A heterogeneous algorithm library is established, and the Multi-Agent Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MATD3) framework models each algorithm as an agent for collaborative decision-making. An innovative task-profile-guided action generation mechanism uses a lightweight encoder to dynamically adjust Actor network parameters, enabling recommendation confidences to be directly modulated by task characteristics. Experiments on real-world power load datasets show that the proposed method significantly outperforms existing approaches in forecasting accuracy (minimum MAPE of 1.36%), robustness (standard deviation of 0.27), and cross-scenario adaptability, providing an efficient and reliable solution for multi-temporal-spatial load forecasting in new power systems.

title: Development of a Ground Test Protocol for Human-in-the-Loop Evaluations of Drivable Lunar Terrain Vehicle Engineering Development Units
authors: Harry L. Litaker, Omar S. Bekdash, Tyler G. Akker, Marcum L. Reagan, Ross J. Pettinger, Jeffrey M. Royer, Christopher M. Re, Jonathan J. Lutz, Adrienne M. Ondrusek
abstract: The National Aerospace and Astronautical Administration's (NASA) Lunar Terrain Vehicle (LTV) seeks commercial contractors to develop the next generation unpressurized rover to support two crewmembers and equipment for lunar surface expeditions. NASA research engineers, in collaboration with subject matter experts, utilized NASA Johnson Space Center's Ground Test Unit (GTU) vehicle to develop a set of ground-based driving tasks and build an evaluation protocol for assessment of crewed LTV operations. The purpose of the protocol is to provide a controlled, test track-like environment to methodically evaluate the crewed capabilities of an LTV and provide recommendations for design improvements. Areas evaluated through human-in-the-loop (HITL) testing included the vehicle operations, operator workload, human machine interfaces, and seated comfort, among others. After reviewing prior HITL vehicle testing from the military, automotive, aerospace industries, and NASA, a task battery was designed that encompassed all expected driving operations and environments that the LTV will need to operate in. This included driving on flat sections, sloped surfaces, overcoming obstacles such as rocks and craters, and navigating through challenging terrain that requires precise driving. These terrain types and scenarios were demonstrated in the JSC Rock Yard, an outdoor facility which has simulated craters, a hill, and boulder field. While future testing will utilize pressurized suits to evaluate the drivable vehicles, this paper will summarize the methods, metrics, and lessons learned from initial shirtsleeve testing using the GTU.

title: Time-Domain Linear Model-based Framework for Passive Acoustic Mapping of Cavitation Activity
authors: Tatiana Gelvez-Barrera, Barbara Nicolas, Denis Kouamé, Bruno Gilles, Adrian Basarab
abstract: Passive acoustic mapping enables the spatial mapping and temporal monitoring of cavitation activity, playing a crucial role in therapeutic ultrasound applications. Most conventional beamforming methods, whether implemented in the time or frequency domains, suffer from limited axial resolution due to the absence of a reference emission onset time. While frequency-domain methods, the most efficient of which are based on the cross-spectral matrix, require long signals for accurate estimation, time-domain methods typically achieve lower spatial resolution. To address these limitations, we propose a linear model-based beamforming framework fully formulated in the time domain. The linear forward model relates a discretized spatiotemporal distribution of cavitation activity to the temporal signals recorded by a probe, explicitly accounting for time-of-flight delays dictated by the acquisition geometry. This model is then inverted using regularization techniques that exploit prior knowledge of cavitation activity in both spatial and temporal domains. Experimental results show that the proposed framework achieves enhanced or competitive cavitation map quality while using only 20% of the data typically required by frequency-domain methods. This highlights the substantial gain in data efficiency and the flexibility of our spatiotemporal regularization to adapt to diverse passive cavitation scenarios, outperforming state-of-the-art techniques.

title: Demonstrating Digital Twin Interoperability Between Heterogeneous Platforms Via Spatial Web
authors: Alicia Sanjurjo Barrio, Bingbing Li, Edward Chow, Thomas Lu
abstract: The increasing activity in lunar exploration, particularly around the Lunar South Pole, abundant in resources, highlights the urgent need for collaboration and interoperability between diverse stakeholders. Digital twins (DTs) have emerged as a powerful tool to support coordination, decision-making, and mission reliability, yet current implementations remain fragmented, relying on proprietary tools and lacking standard interfaces. The IEEE 2874-2025 Spatial Web standard addresses these gaps through protocols such as the Hyperspace Modeling Language (HSML) and Hyperspace Transaction Protocol (HSTP), which aim to enable seamless, secure, and decentralized data exchange across heterogeneous platforms. This study evaluates the effectiveness of HSML and HSTP for digital twin interoperability in a lunar rover assistance use case simulated in Unity and NVIDIA Omniverse Isaac Sim. A verification framework was developed using the DID:key method for identity management, a MySQL registry for credential validation, and a RESTful HSML API for authenticated and authorized communication over Kafka. Results show real-time synchronization with latency well below human reaction time, confirming the feasibility of trusted, crossplatform interactions. These findings demonstrate the potential of the Spatial Web as a foundation for future collaborative space missions.

title: Study on the Influence Range of DC Grounding Electrodes on DC Bias in AC Power Grids
authors: Xuefang Tong, Bo Tan, Mian Fan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To master the influence range and influencing factors of DC grounding electrodes on DC bias in AC power grids, a DC resistance network model covering substation grounding grids, transformers, and transmission lines is established. The distribution of transformer bias current at different distances was calculated, and influence of factors such as grounding current, line length, line orientation, and soil resistivity on the influence range and safety distance of DC bias are analyzed. The results show that the physical configuration of grounding electrodes exerts minimal effect on far-field surface potential distribution, allowing simplified representation as concentrated current sources. The larger the grounding current, the closer the line orientation is to the radial direction, and the higher the deep soil resistivity, the greater the influence range and safety distance. The longer the line, the greater the increase in line resistance compared to the increase in potential, resulting in a decrease in the influence range. Taking the actual power grid around the shared grounding electrode Maibu as an example, the distribution and expansion trend of substations where bias current exceeding the standard limit and influence range under different operating modes were evaluated. The research findings serve as a reference for the site selection of grounding electrode and substations, as well as the assessment of DC bias risk.

title: Analysis of Physical Transmission Delay Effect in District Heating Networks Based on Security Region
authors: Yaqian Zheng, Dan Wang, Hao Cheng, Peiyang Wu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Addressing the challenge that physical transmission delay in district heating networks(DHN) are difficult to accurately characterize within traditional steady-state models, thereby impacting system security assessments, this paper takes the DHN under the quality regulation mode (CF-VT) as the research object, proposes a security region modeling method considering the physical transmission delay. First, construct a hydraulic model and a delayed thermal flow model for the DHN. Second, establish a security region model accounting for physical transmission delay, analyzing the impact mechanism of delay on the system's security boundaries and upper supply limits. Finally, using a 32-node thermal network as a case study, we analyze the patterns of how pipeline network structural parameters affect delay effects.

title: FPGA-Based UART Temperature and Humidity Monitoring System Using Microblaze Architecture
authors: Thiruvenkadam Krishnan, Abishek J, Sridhar S, Lokesh V, Ashwell Castro A
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This project is Monitoring temperature and humidity in many places such as labs, server rooms, industries, and storage areas. In this project, we designed a temperature and humidity monitoring system using an FPGA (arty s750) board. The Arty S7-50 FPGA board is connected to the a DHT22 sensor to measure the temperature and humidity values. The microblaze processor is created inside the vivado tool and it is operated with the use of c code and this microblaze processor convert this as verilog code. The arty s7 50 board with that DHT22 sensor connect with the vivado tool. The output of this project is showed in the PuTTY sotware. The temperature goes upto the given limit then the FPGA board some LEDs are blinking.This project shows that the FPGA-based systems can be used for reliable and real-time environmental monitoring.

title: Influence of Solvents on Spray-Coated Catalyst for Direct Methanol Fuel Cells
authors: Wei Wuen Ng, Hui San Thiam, Wei-Hsin Chen, Yean Ling Pang, Yun Seng Lim, Hui Chieh Teoh
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Membrane electrode assembly (MEA) is a core component of direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC), comprising a membrane, catalyst layers, and diffusion layers, each playing a unique role in overall cell performance. The catalyst layer serves as the site for electrochemical reactions and provides pathways for the transport of reactants and products. However, this layer faces challenges such as limited durability, CO poisoning, and mass transport limitations, which reduce efficiency and long-term performance. The present study aims to optimize catalyst layer designs for improved mass transfer and structural stability by identifying the best dispersion solvent for spray coating catalyst-loaded electrodes. Three solvents under consideration are methanol, ethanol, and isopropanol. The use of methanol with higher dielectric constant results in the formation of uniformly dispersed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{PtRu} / \mathrm{C}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Pt} / \mathrm{C}$</tex> aggregate particles of approximately 2.81 nm in size. The compact and homogeneous methanol-fabricated catalyst layer effectively minimizes fuel crossover while enhancing proton transfer. As a result, the DMFC achieved a maximum power density of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9.38 \text{mW} \text{cm}^{-2}$</tex> and a current density of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$45.47 \text{mA} \text{cm}{ }^{-2}$</tex> at 8 M methanol concentration, representing a substantial improvement over the commercial electrodes. These findings highlight the significant impact of solvent selection on catalyst layer morphology and DMFC performance.

title: Analysis of the Effect of Salt Pollutants on Insulator Degradation and Evaluation of the Effectiveness of Epoxy Resin and RTV Sir Based Protective Coatings
authors: Yulianta Siregar, Tumiar Angel Tampubolon, Issarachai Ngamroo
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Power transmission and distribution system insulators function to isolate live and unlive parts. In Indonesia, outdoor insulators are generally made of ceramics and glass, which have weaknesses in humid and polluted conditions. Pollutants on the insulator surface and wet weather increase leakage current, which causes losses in the form of heating and insulation failure. The solution to insulator resistance to humidity and pollutants is hydrophobic coating. This study examines the effect of RTV silicone coating, epoxy resin, and air humidity on leakage current, voltage distribution of chain insulators in clean and polluted conditions, and the effect of pollutants on insulator performance. Laboratory experimental methods show that the leakage current of the insulator before being coated is in the range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$196.9 \mu \mathbf{A}-968.2 \mu \mathbf{A}$</tex>, after being coated with RTV silicone in the range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$92.5 \mu ~\mathrm{A}-645.3 \mu ~\mathrm{A}$</tex>, and epoxy resin in the range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$93.3~\mu \mathrm{A}-548.8 \mu ~\mathrm{A}$</tex>. The average reduction in leakage current by silicone RTV coating is 48.2%, and epoxy resin is 44%. Leakage current increases with air humidity and the weight of pollutants attached. The analysis shows that the coating worsens the voltage distribution, as evidenced by the increase in the flatness factor of the voltage distribution in polluted and humid conditions.

title: Bridging Standards and Experience: Digital Service Accessibility for People with Disabilities
authors: Muhammad Bambang Firdaus, Apol Pribadi Subriadi, Reny Nadlifatin
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Digital public services often satisfy formal accessibility standards yet fail to meet user expectations in realworld contexts. This study examines a single public digital service to assess how formal conformance aligns with lived user experience. It synthesizes recurring Standard, Bridge, and Experience patterns that connect requirements in the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG), EN 301549 (EU accessibility requirements for ICT products and services), and Section 508 of the U.S. Rehabilitation Act (Section 508) to inclusive user needs, translating them into actionable guidance for design and evaluation. Using an explanatory sequential mixed-methods approach, Phase A conducts a structured compliance audit of representative pages and flows, and Phase B evaluates robustness through scenario-based task testing with assistive technology users across four groups: visual impairment/blindness, mobility impairment, hearing impairment/deafness, and mild cognitive impairment. Measures include task performance, assistive technology compatibility (name-role-value, focus, keyboard coverage, and voice fallback), perceived usability, NASA Short TLX, and acceptability of data-intensive instrumentation. Results show high pass rates for static content but recurring failures in dynamic interactions, including unstable focus, incomplete semantic exposure, and difficult error recovery. Applying Bridge actions reduced completion time, increased task success, and improved assistive technology compatibility while preserving user agency via layered consent. Qualitative findings further identify organizational and contextual constraints shaping accessibility implementation, offering a practical blueprint to prioritize barriers limiting inclusive public digital delivery.

title: Exploring Rapid-Response Flyby Reconnaissance Mission Architectures Enabling Asteroid Mass Estimation
authors: Evan Smith, Justin Atchison, Joseph Linden, Pegah Pashai, Benjamin Villac, Elisabeth Abel, Rylie Bull, Michelle Chen, Terik Daly, Collette Gillaspie, Taejoo Lee, Matthew Rodriguez, Thomas Ruekgauer, Rachel Sholder, Ronald Smith, Connor Thompson
abstract: Flyby missions are the fastest, cheapest, and sometimes only means of obtaining critical measurements for Planetary Defense (PD) scenarios. Past missions, primarily focused on asteroid science, have measured the orbit, binarity, shape, and rotation of asteroids. Mass, one of the most important asteroid characteristics for PD scenarios, has unfortunately proven much more difficult to measure via flyby, only being possible with certain combinations of large targets and slow flybys. An adaptation of a relative tracking scheme, a measurement technique previously used to map the gravitational fields of the Earth and Moon, has the potential to substantially increase flyby mass measurement sensitivity. A relative tracking scheme adapted for a small-body flyby is envisioned to consist of two spacecraft conducting simultaneous flybys of a target. If at least one of the spacecraft passes very close to the asteroid, its change in motion induced by the small body's gravity can be observed through relative measurements of the other spacecraft. This paper begins by establishing some high-level mission objectives for a rapid reconnaissance architecture that includes a mass estimate. High-level trades pertaining to the flight system architecture, relative tracking payload selection and imaging payload configuration are then considered. In conclusion a concept of operations and flight system capable of measuring the most critical hazardous asteroid characteristics, including asteroid mass are presented.

title: A Study on Heat Generation Estimation Based on Cell Balancing Data and Resulting Cell Temperature Estimation Through Thermal Analysis
authors: Soonwon Baek, Woongchul Choi
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: With the proliferation of electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage systems (ESS), ensuring the thermal safety of lithium-ion batteries is emerging as a critical technological challenge. During actual battery operation, localized thermal abnormalities can occur in specific cells due to increased internal resistance or micro-short circuits. If left uncontrolled, these abnormalities can lead to thermal runaway. Therefore, cell-level thermal health diagnostic technology capable of precisely detecting thermal abnormalities in their early stages is essential. However, temperature sensors used in commercial battery modules can only measure surface temperatures in limited locations, limiting their ability to reflect cell temperature or thermal asymmetry. This structural limitation makes it difficult to detect localized heating early. In this study, we propose a technique to estimate cell-level heat generation using real-time voltage, current, internal resistance, and balancing count data collected from a battery management system (BMS). This data is applied to numerical heat transfer analysis to calculate cell temperatures in the module. However, uncertainties in material properties and cooling conditions can limit the reliability of absolute temperature values obtained with the numerical analysis. Therefore, a calibration procedure utilizing a module temperature sensor is introduced. The proposed compensation technique improves the relative accuracy of cell temperature estimation and more accurately reflects thermal asymmetry within the module and the temperature distributions among individual cells. This enables early detection of localized heating increases in faulty cells and is expected to complement the limitations of monitoring based on a single temperature sensor.

title: Origami-Inspired Soft Actuator Utilizing a Low-Melting-Point Alloy for Multi-Modal Motion and Shape Locking
authors: Jan Peters, Johann Licher, Liubov Marchuk, Ditzia Susana Garcia Morales, Annika Raatz
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Origami-inspired soft actuators are increasingly explored in soft robotics for their lightweight design, large deformation range, and ease of fabrication. However, achieving multiple motion modes typically requires several actuators or pressure lines, increasing system complexity and limiting practical deployment. This work presents a compact origamiinspired actuator integrating switchable strain-limiting structures (SSLS) based on a low-melting-point alloy (LMPA) to enable multi-modal motion and shape locking within a single unit. When locally heated, the LMPA melts, allowing the actuator to bend, extend, or contract. Upon cooling, it solidifies and directs the motion of the actuator or locks the configuration without additional energy input. Experimental results demonstrate axial extension of 18%, contraction of 39%, and bending angles of up to 24.6°. By solidifying all LMPA channels in the final configuration, the actuator can achieve stiffening by a factor of 11.6 and the configurations can be locked, so pressure supply is only required during reconfiguration. The proposed approach reduces the number of required pneumatic inputs, enhances structural reconfigurability, and enables energy-efficient, shapelockable designs.

title: Adaptive Shell Scripting with Quine Programs and Large Language Models
authors: Ali Mohammad Saghiri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Traditional shell scripts require manual maintenance as environments evolve, consuming a substantial fraction of scripting effort. We present an adaptive shell scripting framework that combines Quine-inspired self-modifying code with Large Language Models (LLMs) to enable autonomous script evolution. Our algorithm separates a stable core from an evolvable payload, and validates candidate updates through a multi-stage safety pipeline including syntactic checks, sandboxed semantic tests, and explicit policy validation. We evaluate the framework using Monte Carlo simulation to model realistic administrator behavior in enterprise environments across a set of representative environmental drift scenarios. The results show a clear reduction in manual intervention and high autonomous success on simple-to-moderate single-script changes, while conservatively rejecting unsafe or non-compliant patches through deterministic safety gates. Prompt-template isolation and observation sanitization are implemented; cryptographic integrity of telemetry (e.g., HMAC-signed logs) is recommended hardening for deployments where log tampering is in scope. We also identify a boundary case: complex multi-artifact reconfigurations (e.g., network changes requiring coordinated updates across multiple scripts) remain outside the current single-script scope, motivating a transactional change-set extension with integration invariants. To the best of our knowledge, prior work does not provide an end-to-end framework combining Quine-inspired self-modification, LLM patch proposal, and enforceable multi-stage safety verification for production shell scripting.

title: A Forensic-Based Prevention Framework for Web Defacement Attacks via WordPress REST API and Social Engineering
authors: Khairunnisak Nur Isnaini, Didit Suhartono, Azhari Shouni Barkah, Fadia Nur Annisa, Anisaa Qothrunnada Khoirunnita
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Website defacement is a prevalent cyberattack where an attacker alters the appearance or content of a website without authorization. This study investigates a defacement attack on a beauty startup's website, which occurred on December 3, 2024, through the exploitation of the WordPress REST API and social engineering techniques. The methodology involves digital forensic analysis, including log acquisition, server log analysis, and phishing email examination. The investigation revealed that the attacker gained unauthorized access via phishing to obtain admin credentials and subsequently exploited the WordPress REST API to publish malicious content. The results highlight the attack's impact, demonstrating how unauthorized access led to content manipulation and reputational damage. As a mitigation strategy, this study emphasizes the importance of implementing multi-factor authentication (MFA), restricting REST API access, educating users about phishing threats, and utilizing web application firewalls (WAF). These findings provide critical insights into securing web applications against defacement attacks and improving cybersecurity practices.

title: PIKAN: Physics-Inspired Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks for Explainable UAV Channel Modelling
authors: Kürşat Tekbıyık, Güneş Karabulut Kurt, Antoine Lesage-Landry
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) communications demand accurate yet interpretable air-to-ground (A2G) channel models that can adapt to non-stationary propagation environments. While deterministic models offer interpretability and deep learning (DL) models provide accuracy, both approaches suffer from either rigidity or a lack of explainability. To bridge this gap, we propose the Physics-Inspired Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (PIKAN) that embeds physical principles (e.g., free-space path loss, two-ray reflections) into the learning process. Unlike physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), PIKAN is more flexible for applying physical information because it introduces them as adaptable inductive biases. Thus, it enables a more flexible training process. Experiments on UAV A2G measurement data show that PIKAN achieves comparable accuracy to DL models while providing symbolic and explainable expressions aligned with propagation laws. Remarkably, PIKAN achieves this performance with only 232 parameters, making it up to 37 times lighter than multilayer perceptron (MLP) baselines with thousands of parameters, without sacrificing correlation with measurements and also providing symbolic expressions. These results highlight PIKAN as an efficient, interpretable, and scalable solution for UAV channel modelling in beyond-5G and 6G networks.

title: Human as an Actuator Dynamic Model Identification
authors: Harrison M. Bonner, Matthew R. Kirchner
abstract: This paper presents a method for estimating parameters that form a general model for human pilot response for specific tasks. The human model is essential for the dynamic analysis of piloted vehicles. Data are generated on a simulator with multiple trials being incorporated to find the single model that best describes the data. The model is found entirely in the time domain by constructing a constrained optimization problem. This optimization problem implicitly represents the state of the underlying system, making it robust to natural variation in human responses. It is demonstrated by estimating the human response model for a position control task with a quadcopter drone.

title: Motorcycle Parking Slot Estimation in Unmarked Environments Using YOLOv8n and Empirically Calibrated Dynamic Gap Analysis
authors: Bagus Fatkhurrohman, Rafi Muhammad Hibban, Kukuh Lintang Fajar, Astri Novianty, Umar Ali Ahmad, Wildan Panji Tresna
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The absence of physical parking markings and real-time occupancy information often leads to inefficient space utilization in motorcycle parking areas. This paper presents a vision-based motorcycle parking slot estimation system for unmarked environments using an edge computing architecture. A lightweight YOLOv8n model is employed to detect parked motorcycles from a single fixed camera, while an empirically calibrated Dynamic Gap Analysis (DGA) algorithm estimates available parking slots by analyzing horizontal gaps between detected vehicles. Unlike predefined grid-based approaches, the proposed method enables adaptive slot estimation without relying on physical parking markers. Experimental results demonstrate reliable detection performance, achieving a recall of 100 %, an F 1 -score of 94 %, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{m A P} \boldsymbol{@} \mathbf{0. 5}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 8. 5 \%}$</tex>. Quantitative evaluation of slot estimation shows a Mean Absolute Error of 0.9 slots and an accuracy of 94.7 %, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed approach under irregular parking conditions.

title: Exploring the Cinderella Complex in Digital Narratives: An Unsupervised Text Mining Study
authors: Ni Komang Gayatri Kusuma Wardhani, Audrey Theodora Phang, Lili Ayu Wulandari, Noviyanti Tri Maretta Sagala
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Gendered expectations in relationships have long been discussed in psychological and social research. One concept often associated with such expectations is the Cinderella Complex, which describes tendencies toward emotional dependence and a desire for external protection in romantic relationships. This study conducted a large-scale exploration of how such dependency-oriented narratives appeared in contemporary discourse by analyzing 1,000 Reddit posts collected through targeted scraping. After applying a standardized preprocessing pipeline to normalize and clean the text, topic-modeling techniques were employed to uncover latent thematic structures related to dependency, self-perception, and relational expectations. Multiple modeling approaches were evaluated using the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C_{v}$</tex> coherence metric to assess the interpretability and stability of extracted themes. Among the evaluated methods, Non-Negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) produced 30 interpretable topics with a coherence score of 0.4825 and was selected as the most suitable model due to its balanced topic structure and reduced risk of overfitting compared to alternative approaches. The resulting topics revealed how modern online discussions reflected forms of dependency narratives and internalized beliefs associated with the Cinderella Complex. These findings demonstrated the value of computational text analysis for examining psychological constructs at scale and highlighted the potential of topic-based modeling to understand how such narratives appear within digital communication environments.

title: Snow Accumulation Sounding Using C3 Class Hexacopter Integrated with FMCW Radar
authors: Lee Taylor, Fernando Rodríguez-Morales, Jilu Li, Carlton Leuschen
abstract: This paper presents snow sounding images produced using data collected with C3/Group 2 class Aurelia X6 Pro Hexacopter integrated with a nadir-looking <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2-8 \text{GHz}$</tex> ultra-wideband frequency modulated continuous wave (FMCW) radar. First, we apply Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) processing methods in the along-track direction to a dataset collected in Greenland during the summer of 2024, resulting in a much improved data product showing distinct snow accumulation layers to depths exceeding 30-meters below the surface. Next, the improved data processing methods were also applied to data collected during measurements of opportunity taken over repeat passes before and after a significant snowfall event in Lawrence Kansas, USA, in the winter of 2025. The before and after snowfall images are presented and successfully demonstrate snow layer interface detection over ground near the theoretical range resolution limit of the radar. Approximately 10 cm of snowfall was detected from an altitude of 75 m above ground level (AGL). The significance of this work focuses on demonstrating a cost effective small form-factor radar integrated with a commercially available small Unmanned Aerial System (sUAS) as versatile tool for snow depth retrieval compared to competing technologies, including comparable radars operating on fixed-wing aircraft, while maintaining outstanding electrical performance.

title: EduSync: DSP-Based Engagement Modeling and Vision Analytics in an Edge-Deployed LMS
authors: Joshua JJ Wonder, Rithick I, Balan M, Kingston Iraiyarasu, M.A.P Manimekalai
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This paper introduces EduSync, is an edge-deployed Learning Management System which examines learners behavior through continuous Digital Signal Processing (DSP) and vision analytics, as opposed to simple engagement metrics. The system uses FIR filtering, zero-crossing rate analysis, and spectral analysis via FFTs to create a continuous and much-interpretable signal of student scrolling behavior by modeling that behavior as a discrete-time signal. This is enhanced by a vision-based attention module with the use of real-time head pose estimations. The test scores of 21 participants show that the multimodal DSP engagement score has a significant positive Spearman correlation with quiz scores (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\rho=0.484, p=0.026$</tex>). This measure was demonstrated to be resistant to sensitivity analysis (with an optimal 60/25/15 weight combination) with performance that was significantly better than time-only and activity-only baselines. On the same note, the vision-based focused ratio had a moderate positive correlation, which is slightly less than that of the DSP-based metric, but the vision metric is still a significant predictive factor, as shown by a correlation of (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\rho=0.470, p=0.031$</tex>). In order to ensure complete privacy of data and offline capability, it is ensured that this analytical pipeline is deployed fully on edge architecture. The system manages to run these multi-dimensional analytics with a local 4-bit quantized Llama-3 (8B) model on consumer-grade hardware (RTX 3060 GPU, 6GB VRAM) which indicates that privacy-preserving, advanced AI can be viable in the current classrooms.

title: Push-Pull Class E Converter with ZVS in Wide Load Range
authors: Nian-Ci Chen, Min-Fu Hsieh
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: This paper presents a push-pull Class E converter with constant off-time control, designed to achieve wide-range zero-voltage switching (ZVS) turn-on under varying load conditions. On the primary side, the proposed converter enables ZVS turn-on across a wide load range, while on the secondary side, zero-current switching (ZCS) turn-off is achieved under light-load conditions, such as 5% of the rated output power. A novel method using simulation analysis is developed to determine the constant off-time duration ((t<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">COFF</inf>)) and to ensure reliable ZVS turn-on across different operating points. In addition, by incorporating the magnetizing inductor <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$L_{m}$</tex> into the resonant tank, its current can be utilized to adapt to different load conditions and further extend the ZVS turn-on range. Simulation results verify the effectiveness of the proposed converter in achieving wide-range ZVS turn-on behavior.

title: Mapping Political Ideologies on Social Media Using Text Mining and Deep Learning
authors: Octavius Sandriago, Jonathan Christopher Gani, Lili Ayu Wulandhari, Noviyanti Tri Maretta Sagala
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: In recent years, social media platforms have become major arenas for political discourse, offering vast and dynamic data for analyzing public ideology. This research developed an automated system that mapped political ideologies expressed in social media posts onto a twodimensional political compass, encompassing both the economic (left-right) and social (authoritarian-libertarian) dimensions. Unlike conventional studies that classify political ideology into binary labels such as “left” or “right,” this research explicitly modeled ideology as a multidimensional construct. The system utilized a synthetically adapted multilingual dataset constructed from English political datasets and transformed to reflect Indonesian social media linguistic patterns through three pipeline stages: neural machine translation (Helsinki-NLP OPUS-MT), probabilistic slang injection (50 % replacement rate, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$80+$</tex> term dictionary), and code-switching (25 % English word reinsertion). This paper is explicitly scoped to synthetically adapted multilingual data; validation on natively collected Indonesian posts is identified as essential future work. Transformer-based deep learning models were employed to predict each post's coordinates on the ideological plane. The resulting mappings visualized how discussions were distributed across ideological dimensions, revealing polarization patterns in the synthetic corpus. Experimental evaluation compared four transformerbased architectures across both ideological axes using standard performance metrics. This paper contributed a multilingual ideology mapping framework and a dual-axis transformer evaluation for Indonesian social media discourse visualized onto a two-dimensional political compass.

title: A Comparative Study of Deep Learning and Classical Image Segmentation Methods for Damaged Flower Image Analysis
authors: Shofwatul Uyun, Subhan Ghufron, Abdul Mukrim, Hanny Handayani Sucinta, Bahar Nurullah
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Damaged area detection in flower images is a crucial task for automated visual inspection and quality assessment in agricultural applications. Although numerous segmentation approaches have been proposed-ranging from classical image processing techniques to deep learning-based models-systematic and fair comparisons under identical experimental conditions, including benchmarking against previous studies, remain limited. This paper presents a comparative performance analysis between deep learningbased methods, U-Net and TransUNet, and two classical segmentation approaches, namely Otsu thresholding and Region Growing. All methods are evaluated using the same publicly available dataset with pixel-level annotations and comprehensive evaluation metrics, including Intersection over Union (IoU), Dice coefficient, Mean Square Error (MSE), and PSNR. Experimental results demonstrate that deep learningbased models significantly outperform classical methods. TransUNet achieved the highest performance with an IoU of 0.9914 and a Dice score of 0.9956, while drastically reducing the MSE to 0.0008. This represents a substantial improvement over the findings of Siswanto et al. (2023), who reported a peak accuracy of 0.8473 and an MSE of 6.35 using Region Growing. In contrast, while classical methods offer lower computational complexity and faster execution times-as low as 0.03 seconds for Region Growing in prior research-they exhibit clear performance limitations in visually complex scenarios with irregular boundaries and varying illumination. This study provides practical insights into the accuracy-complexity tradeoffs of different segmentation approaches and serves as a reference for selecting appropriate methods in agricultural image analysis applications.

title: Lightweight Deep Learning Models for Retinal Disease Classification: A Survey
authors: Ziyad Mohammed Al-johani, Hamza Ghandorh, Wael M.S. Yafooz
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Retinal disorders represent a major global health burden and remain among the predominant causes of irreversible vision loss, with more than two billion individuals affected worldwide. In Saudi Arabia, it is estimated that one in six adults suffers from visual impairment, and diabetic retinopathy has been reported to be a significant contributor to vision loss. Therefore, screening and diagnosis of this condition are of paramount importance from a public health perspective. Recent advances in deep learning have significantly improved automated retinal image analysis, enabling earlier and more reliable detection of pathological patterns; however, many state-of-the-art models remain computationally intensive, limiting their practicality in resource-constrained healthcare settings. The emergence of lightweight and efficient deep learning models has prompted increased research into their design and evaluation. This study conducts an in-depth survey of DL methods for retinal disease classification. This study conducts an in-depth survey of deep learning (DL) methods for retinal disease classification. Particular emphasis is placed on approaches leveraging lightweight architectures and efficient training strategies. The survey analyzes approximately forty research articles published between 2020 and 2025, covering studies trained and evaluated on commonly used benchmark datasets including ODIR, RFMiD, APTOS 2019, EyePACS, DR-Fundus, OCT2017, and the Bangladesh Multiclass Fundus dataset.

title: Caterpillar-Inspired Spring-Based Compressive Continuum Robot for Bristle-based Exploration
authors: Zhixian Hu, Yu She, Juan Wachs
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Exploration of confined spaces, such as pipelines and ducts, remains challenging for conventional rigid robots due to limited space, irregular geometry, and restricted access. Inspired by caterpillar locomotion and sensing, this paper presents a compact spring-based tendon-driven continuum robot that integrates with commercial robotic arms for confined-space inspection. The system combines a mechanically compliant continuum body with a tendon actuation module, enabling coupled bending and axial length change, and uses a constant-curvature kinematic model for positional control. Experiments show a mean position error of 4.32 mm under the proposed model and control pipeline. To extend the system from motion to inspection, we integrate an artificial bristle contact sensor and demonstrate surface perception and confined-space exploration through contact interactions. This compact and compliant design offers a cost-effective upgrade for commercial robots and promises effective exploration in challenging environments.

title: Real-Time Probabilistic Enemy Decision Making in Survival Games using Bayesian Networks and Behavior Trees
authors: Feby Akliji Rofiah, Hanny Haryanto
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Enemy decision making in survival games must be both readable to players and tractable for designers, yet threshold-based behavior trees can be brittle when cues conflict or fluctuate. This research investigates whether a compact, deterministic probabilistic layer can improve stability and responsiveness without violating frame-time constraints. We propose a discrete Bayesian network that fuses four readily available cues: agent health, opponent health, local enemy amount, and ally count, and drives a behavior-tree selector via deterministic maximum selection at frame rate. The approach is evaluated against a threshold-based baseline under identical content in two arenas with contrasting evidence dynamics. Using three operational metrics, mode switch rate, decision latency from evidence change to commit, and per-agent CPU cost, the probabilistic layer consistently lowers switching and reduces latency while adding negligible overhead. Because the operation is fully deterministic, traces are reproducible across runs, aiding debugging and profiling. The findings indicate that explicit probabilistic fusion provides a practical and auditable augmentation to behavior-tree controllers in survival combat, improving moment-to-moment decision quality without sacrificing reproducibility or budget discipline.

title: Agentic AI for Modern HR Operations
authors: Sudhir K. Routray, Latha Krishnan, Venkatram Narendra Puppala, Saravana Kumar G., Deepak J P
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: As organizations navigate the complexities of modern HR, Agentic AI has emerged as a transformative force. It has evolved from passive assistants into autonomous digital teammates. Unlike traditional automation, agentic systems utilize multi-step reasoning and cross-platform integration to manage end-to-end workflows. They can handle complex tasks such as predictive talent sourcing and autonomous interview scheduling to proactive benefits administration. By shifting HR from reactive task execution to strategic orchestration, these agents reduce administrative workloads by up to 80 % and accelerate hiring cycles tenfold. However, this transition necessitates a paradigm shift toward algorithmic transparency and practical flawless governance to mitigate bias and ensure data privacy. This paper explores the operational efficiencies of agentic frameworks, their impact on employee experience through hyper-personalization, and the essential shift in HR roles from process managers to architects of human-AI synergy.

title: Development of a Microgrid Simulator for Analyzing Frequency Regulation Performance of Grid-Forming Inverters
authors: Dain Kim, Taehyoung Kim, Seon Han Choi
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Renewable-rich microgrids are increasingly inverter dominated, which reduces physical inertia and makes system frequency highly sensitive to disturbances. Grid-forming (GFM) inverters can compensate by autonomously establishing voltage and frequency references and providing fast frequency support. This paper presents an agent-based microgrid simulation framework to systematically evaluate GFM impacts in microgrid networks with small synchronous generators, renewable resources, energy storage systems (ESS), and time-varying loads. The framework models heterogeneous resources and controllers as interacting agents within a network environment and couples swing-equation-based frequency dynamics with power-flow calculations and a hierarchical frequency-control loop including governors, automatic generation control, and GFM droop/virtual inertia controls. The simulator produces time-series trajectories of system frequency, bus voltages, and ESS states under realistic operating profiles and control actions. Two studies are conducted: (i) a RoCoF mitigation analysis under abrupt load changes with sensitivity to ESS rating and virtual inertia settings, and (ii) an IEEE 13-bus test feeder study that evaluates frequency response and dispatch behavior under varying ESS ratings. The results demonstrate the framework's capability to quantify frequency-support characteristics and operational trade-offs of ESS-supported GFM in inverter-dominated microgrids.

title: Smart Placement Assistance: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework with Dynamic Visual Analytics for Career Guidance
authors: Rama Krishna Peddarapu, Hemanth Perala, Rohit Mathi, Sanjay Mandan, Rohit Kumar Burugoju
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: The university recruitment process in the case of academic institutions is a critical but is always full of decentralized workflow, manual student information management, and insufficient specialist advising tools. Even though various intelligent systems have been created to address these dilemmas, the majority of them are autonomous and they exist on the single aspects. Consequently, the current solutions are expected to cope with limited actions, including simple access to data or predictive algorithms, instead of providing a user-friendly and holistic recruitment environment. This paper proposes Smart Placement Assistance, a unified web-based platform with three primary components: a Placement Dashboard, a Company Insights module, and an AI driven chatbot built on a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework. The system works cutting-edge technology, such as the Gemma Large Language Model (LLM) applied by Ollama to enable context-aware responses and semantic search, and Chroma DB for vector-based data storage. According to experimental results, the proposed architecture improves student access to relevant placement-related insights while also lowering information retrieval latency.

title: Proprioceptive Origami Sleeves Using Embedded Fold Sensors
authors: Saima Jamal, Ryan Zheng, Daniel Bruder
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Origami structures offer lightweight, compact, and highly deformable architectures that can be used for a variety of different applications. However, their adoption has been constrained by the lack of low-cost, scalable, and generalizable sensing solutions. Existing proprioceptive approaches often rely on complex fabrication or external tracking systems, limiting accessibility and integration. In this work, we present the Origami Sensing Sleeve (OSS), a low-cost, crease-embedded sensing architecture fabricated using commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) inkjet printing technology, enabling low-cost, rapid, scalable, and geometry-agnostic manufacturing. We demonstrate and validate this approach on a four-sided Yoshimura-pattern, selected as a representative origami sleeve due to its large range of motion and geometric versatility. Each crease integrates an inkjet printed fold sensor that provides low-profile feedback of local bending angles within the origami structure. A kinematic model of the OSS is coupled with sensor feedback to reconstruct its geometry and track its kinematic state in real time. The embedded sensors measure fold angles within ±5° with a 20-60 s settling time. Paired with a kinematic model, sensors estimate the position of one end of an origami sleeve relative to the other with an RMSE error of 2.32 cm for static poses and 4.06 cm for transient trajectories, given a nominal extended sleeve length of 12.18 cm. This work establishes a low-cost, manufacturable, and generalizable pathway toward scalable proprioceptive control for compliant robotic systems using origami-based designs.

title: Hybrid-Constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Collaborative Dual-Aircraft Scenarios
authors: Hao Liang, Yang Gao, Hui Chang, Zhijun Zhao, Shaojia Xi
abstract: In recent years, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has emerged as a promising approach for multi-unmanned combat aerial vehicle (UCAV) autonomous countermeasure systems. However, conventional end-to-end MARL methods often lack expert knowledge guidance, leading to low training efficiency, which poses challenges for simulation-to-reality (sim2real) transition. In this study, we focus on the scenario of cooperative beyond-visual-range (BVR) aerial engagement involving multiple unmanned combat aerial vehicles. To address these challenges, we propose the hybrid-constrained multi-agent proximal policy optimization (HC-MAPPO) algorithm. First, we design a rule filter mechanism wherein expert rules dictate agent behavior in wellunderstood states. This ensures predictable and interpretable maneuvers, while the neural policy is applied in complex, undefined scenarios. Second, we formulate the multi-agent aerial combat problem as a Constrained Markov Decision Process (CMDP) and incorporate a cost-critic network into the actor-critic architecture, which enables explicit estimation of long-term constraint costs and decouples penalty from task rewards. Third, we develop a bilevel optimization framework for constrained policy search, which provides theoretical convergence guarantees and demonstrates improved training stability over traditional Lagrangian-based methods. Empirical results demonstrate that HC-MAPPO achieves a superior success rate, improving the win rate by approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20 \%-30 \%$</tex> compared to existing MARL baselines such as MAPPO and HAPPO. Ablation studies further confirm the necessity of both constraints: removing either one leads to performance degradation.

title: Derma Nutri: An AI-Powered Multilingual Recommendation System for Affordable Skincare and Nutrition
authors: Venkata Subbaiah Desanamukula, D. V. V. Krishnaveni, M. Victor Paul, N. Raja Rajeswari
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Skincare routines play a significant role in maintaining overall health and building user self-confidence. In spite of the importance of skincare routines, many users find it difficult to look for appropriate skincare products due to lack of awareness, affordability constraints, language issues, and incorrect perception about the link between food/nutrients and skin health. Since there has been an emerging need for an encompassing and affordable solution to overcome this existing gap, this research proposes an innovative solution through the design of an artificial intelligence-powered multilingual recommendation system for skincare and food/nutrient advice by presenting “Derma-Nutri.” The proposed method uses a deep learning model designed through Vision Transformers to classify facial skin images into three types: “dry skin,” “oily skin,” and “normal skin.” The system uses a variety of image enhancement techniques for higher accuracy and better generalizability. To increase accessibility, natural language processing is also used in the system to interact in Telugu, Hindi, and English languages. It is suggested that cheap soap, lotion, and sunscreen can be opted for on the basis of the model's assessment of the skin type the individual possesses side by side with the list of food to consider on a daily basis to make sure the skin is healthy from the inside out. For the optimization process, the model uses the Adam optimizer. Additionally, the model has achieved the capability to obtain 96.43% accuracy rates in the classification. Good performance and its ability to work well in each individual's skin type is ensured by the model. The test result confirms that the aforementioned system was successful in bridging the gap between the awareness of the skin care and nutrition concepts and the linguistic simplicity.

title: DermAI: A High-Accuracy Ensemble Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Class Skin Lesion Classification
authors: Hanouf Albatati, Ahmad Alhabil, Osama Alyazidi, Rahaf Bajabaa, Lujain Mullaniazee, Moaid Abushaker, Khaled ElBahnasy
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Skin cancer remains a major global health challenge where early, accurate detection is essential for effective treatment. This paper presents DermAI, a high-accuracy ensemble deep learning framework for automated multi-class skin lesion classification from dermoscopic images. DermAI integrates a segmentation-guided preprocessing stage using DeepLabV3+ to isolate lesion regions (Dice: 0.94, IoU: 0.89) and a robust classification pipeline trained on HAM10000 and ISIC 2019 images across seven clinically significant lesion categories. Three single backbones (DenseNet, EfficientNet-B7, and Xception) achieved 98.0% test accuracy each. A weighted ensemble combining EfficientNet-B7 and Xception via softmax probability averaging improved overall performance to 99.35% on an independent test set of 6,181 images, corresponding to a 0.65% error rate (40 errors). Clinically, the ensemble achieved 100% recall for melanoma, eliminating false negatives for this life-threatening class. Four categories achieved perfect precision, recall, and F1-score (1.00). The results indicate strong robustness to class imbalance and demonstrate DermAI's potential as a reliable clinical decision-support tool for dermoscopic screening.

title: Energy-Efficient UAV Water Sampling Under Wind Uncertainty Using Reinforcement Learning
authors: Mohammad Alnakhli, Bader Fayez, Abdullah Nihad, Ahmed Abdullatif, Hadhal Ayed, Faris Abdullah
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: This paper presents a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for energy-efficient UAV-based water sampling in stochastic dam environments, accounting for environmental conditions typical of Saudi Arabia, including wind variations. The UAV is tasked with maximizing sample collection and spatial coverage while minimizing energy consumption under variable wind and temperature conditions. A Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent was trained and evaluated against deterministic baseline policies, Nearest Unvisited and Farthest Unvisited, over 500 simulation episodes. Results show that the RL agent achieves superior performance in sample collection, spatial coverage, and energy efficiency compared to baseline heuristics. The agent naturally learns strategies such as prioritizing high-value distant targets, exploiting favorable wind conditions to save energy, balancing exploration with exploitation, and making optimal return-to-home decisions. These findings demonstrate that RL provides an effective and energy-aware approach for autonomous UAV water monitoring in challenging and regionspecific environments.

title: A Frequency-Constrained AC-OPF Integrating Spinning Reserves for Generator Contingencies
authors: Capt. Surachai Waiwong, Parnjit Damrongkulkamjorn, Dulpichet Rerkpreedapong
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: An enhanced AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) framework is proposed to improve frequency security under N-1 generator outage contingencies. The formulation incorporates a Load Frequency Control (LFC) model to link generator dynamic response to frequency deviation following disturbances. In addition to power balance and system operating limits, the proposed formulation incorporates reserve adequacy and frequency constraints within the optimization model. Simulation results based on the modified PJM 5-bus system demonstrate that including frequency constraints increases the operational cost but obviously improves frequency stability and overall reliability. The study also reveals that spinning reserves alone are insufficient to guarantee secure operation when frequency limits are binding, emphasizing the importance of frequency-related reserve planning. The proposed method provides both a practical and analytical contribution toward frequency-constrained OPF modeling.
