title: An FPGA-Based Hybrid Wave Union Time-to-Digital Converter with Iterative Z-Type Code Calibration Algorithm
authors: Wenhao Duan, Changqing Feng, Junchen Wang, Zhongtao Shen, Shubin Liu
abstract: A novel FPGA-based multi-measurement scheme Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) architecture, termed Hybrid Wave Union (HWU), is proposed. By integrating the advantages of the Wave Union A and Wave Union B methods, the designed TDC launches a ripple with multiple transitions along the tapped delay line (TDL) and samples it with a D flip-flop (DFF) array across consecutive system clock cycles upon the arrival of a rising edge, yielding two complementary snapshots. Each snapshot is encoded into fine timestamps through a pipeline encoder, and the resulting timestamp pairs are processed by an iterative Z-type calibration algorithm that reconstructs accurate delay intervals without requiring any predefined knowledge of the relative ordering of delay durations. The design is implemented on a Xilinx Kintex-7 FPGA, with a measured on-chip power consumption of 42 mW per channel, including 6 mW for the TDL and DFF bank, 31 mW for the encoder and packetization logic, and 5 mW shared across all TDC channels for the coarse time counter. Experimental results demonstrate an average RMS timing precision of 4.4 ps and a dead time of only three clock cycles—two for snapshot acquisition and one for TDL reset—significantly shorter than that of conventional Wave Union B TDCs. Compared to traditional TDL TDC architectures, the proposed HWU TDC achieves high timing precision, low resource utilization, and energy-efficient operation, making it well suited for multi-channel, high-precision applications in modern high-energy physics experiments.

title: Accurate Analysis of Power Marketing Policy Risk Based on Policy Interpretation Agent
authors: Bin Liu, Yang Ji, Yao Fu, Xuan Wang, Zhihui Li, Zhenlong Li
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The power marketing business is complex and diverse, with frequent changes in rules and regulations and extensive risks. These factors lead to great challenges in compliance management of power marketing business. This paper builds a policy risk library based on policy interpretation agent. According to relevant laws and regulations, policy provisions and company rules and regulations, the possible risk points in the process of policy implementation are systematically sorted out, and the information of policy items is analyzed into structured data. Through the policy interpretation agent, the policy interpretation, risk point items and metadata information of policy documents are extracted and generated, and the risk prevention measures are continuously optimized to ensure the compliant operation of marketing business.

title: Multiphysics Analysis and Experimental Study of a Novel Waterproof Material for Distribution Cable Joints
authors: Ben Wu, Handi Weng, Jian Wang, Ziqi Lin, Zhenyu Zhang, Ting Zhu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address the insufficient waterproof performance of cable joints under moisture ingress and water immersion conditions, a novel waterproof material is proposed and applied to the cable joints. Based on a multiphysics coupling analysis method, the variations in the electric field, temperature field, and mechanical stress field distributions of the joint before and after the introduction of the waterproof material are investigated. The potential influence of the material on the original field distributions and the safe operation of the cable is evaluated. Furthermore, the effectiveness of the proposed material is verified through waterproof and electrical performance tests. Experimental results indicate that under high-water-pressure immersion conditions, the cable joint using the proposed waterproof material exhibits no partial discharge (PD) and successfully passes the and power-frequency withstand voltage tests. The overall electrical performance remains stable, providing a useful reference for the engineering application of waterproof technologies for distribution cable accessories.

title: Research on Wide-band Oscillation Suppression Strategy of LCL Three-phase Grid-connected Inverters Based on Frequency-selective Feedforward Compensation
authors: Tianhao Zhu, Hui Xiao, Jiale Lei, Yichi Zhang, Linjun Zeng
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The grid voltage proportional feedforward is a control method used in many places such as the grid-connected inverter. Grid Voltage Proportional FeedForward control has been able to suppress grid background harmonic. But under weak-grid situation, grid voltage proportional feedforward control will have different effects to grid connected inverters according to various frequency bands. When there are low-frequency oscillations that arise because of the negative resistance introduced via Phase Locked Loop (PLL), Full Feedforward can work against the negative resistance and boost stability. But due to control delay, the full feedforward introduces negative resistance into the high-frequency band which results in high-frequency oscillation from interaction between grid connected inverters and the grid impedance. To overcome the shortcoming of traditional feed forward control for keeping low -frequency and high -frequency balance under weak -grid condition, Firstly an impedance model containing PLL and control delay is created so that the mechanism of low -frequency and high -frequency oscillations can be analyzed accurately; then we present our solution. Proposed a frequency-selective feedforward compnsation link to seprte feedforward part precisely. Cutoff frequency and order of the Frequency-Selective Feedforward Link should be designed properly so that the grid-connected inverters can be stable in both low-and high-frequency regions under weak grid circumstances. Finally, this paper do some tests and make sure my theory works and is correct and all those kinds of things.

title: Hybrid and Self-Sustainable RIS for Mobile Communications: A Location-Aware Energy Scheduling and DRL-Based Optimization
authors: Junqi Liu, Yi Zhou, Gaofei Huang, Dong Tang
abstract: This work explores a self-sustainable reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted mobile communication system, where a RIS powered by the energy harvested from ambient energy sources assists the communication between a transmitter and a mobile user. Unlike the conventional passive or active RISs in existing studies, we employ a hybrid RIS which is enabled to operate in three modes (namely passive, active and idle modes) in the studied system to effectively utilize the harvested energy at the RIS. Moreover, we propose a novel protocol by which the RIS can schedule its harvested energy only based on the user's location information, which is different from the existing studies where channel state information is required, as well as the state of its harvested energy, so that we can further improve the energy efficiency at the RIS in a practical manner. Under this hybrid RIS framework and the proposed protocol, we formulate a joint optimization problem that aims to maximize the average system throughput over different user's locations by optimizing the operating mode and phase shift of each RIS element, along with the amplification factors of active elements. Owing to the stochastic energy arrivals at the RIS and the discrete nature of mode and phase-shift variables, the problem is challenging to solve directly. To address this, we develop a deep reinforcement learning based approach and design a proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithm to obtain a near-optimal solution. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed PPO algorithm.

title: Dual-Mode Multiplexed Micro-Ring Resonator on Thin-Film Lithium Tantalate with High Tuning Efficiency and Large Free Spectral Range
authors: Hao Zhang, Hao Yao, Jiayao Deng, Yuzhe Sun, Mengke Wang, Xiaoxia Ma, Jieyun Wu, Kaixin Chen
abstract: Simultaneous achievement of a large free spectral range (FSR) and high tuning efficiency in a single micro-ring resonator (MRR) remains a significant challenge for integrated photonic applications such as optical filters, on-chip spectrometers, and tunable external cavity lasers. In this work, we address this challenge by leveraging, for the first time, an intermode Vernier effect implemented within a single MRR on a thin-film lithium tantalate platform. Our proposed device integrates a multimode racetrack resonator with shared tuning electrodes and two pairs of optimized mode-selective couplers for the TE0 and TE1 modes. The slight difference in the effective refractive indices between these two modes generates a Vernier envelope, which simultaneously extends the effective FSR and amplifies the tuning efficiency. Our proof-of-concept device, with a 5.2-mm electro-optic interaction length, achieves an expanded effective Vernier-envelope FSR of 18.7 nm, alongside electro-optic and thermo-optic tuning efficiencies as high as 0.43 nm/V and –0.76 nm/°C, respectively. The device also exhibits excellent stability, with a central wavelength drift of only 0.02 nm over 30 minutes. Benefiting from a large FSR, high tuning efficiency, and stable operation, our proposed MRR holds strong promise for use in optical information processing, sensing, external cavity laser, and beyond.

title: Safe from Prying Eyes
authors: Scott Davidson

title: Uncertainty-Aware State Estimation: A Multi-Modal Signal Fusion Framework
authors: Muhammad Babar Rasheed, Inam Ullah, Xiandong Ma
abstract: Despite significant advances in data-driven battery modeling, no existing framework simultaneously guarantees estimation accuracy, well-calibrated predictive uncertainty, and computational tractability for real-time battery management systems. This letter addresses this gap by proposing an uncertainty-aware multi-modal signal fusion framework that hierarchically extracts complementary features from voltage, current, and temperature measurements and adaptively combines them through linear synthesis, polynomial basis expansion, and kernel mapping. The fusion mechanism is underpinned by formal convergence guarantees, with weights converging at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(\log k/k)$</tex-math></inline-formula>, ensuring provable robustness alongside computational efficiency. Experimental validation across three real-world electric vehicle datasets yields SOC RMSE of 0.07% and voltage RMSE of 0.1 p.u. (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V_{\rm{p.u.}}=V_{\rm{measured}}/V_{\rm{nom}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, corresponding to 0.36-0.42 V absolute error), and coverage deviation within 0.2 percentage points of the nominal 95% prediction interval, all within millisecond-scale cycle times. These results establish a new state-of-the-art in the joint optimisation of estimation accuracy, uncertainty calibration, and real-time BMS deployment.

title: Regime-Conditioned Horizon-aware Mixture-of-Experts for Spatio-temporal Wind Forecasting
authors: Bowen Zou, Ming Li, Laeeq Aslam
abstract: Accurate short-term wind speed prediction (WSP) reduces operational risk in wind-integrated grids because turbine power amplifies wind-speed errors through a cubic dependence on velocity. Improved WSP therefore supports tighter reserve scheduling and more reliable dispatch under regime-dependent variability. Existing deep learning forecasters often rely on a single backbone or step-invariant fusion, which enforces uniform computation across forecast steps and can reduce robustness when dominant dynamics shift across regimes. Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) offers capacity allocation, yet many recent hybrids keep inference-time fusion fixed or use MoE mainly for offline optimization rather than step-wise, regime-conditioned routing. This work proposes Regime-Conditioned Horizon-aware Mixture-of-Experts (RCHA-MOE) for spatio-temporal forecasting. RCHA-MOE fuses three complementary experts that encode persistence, dilated multi-scale temporal structure, and locality-preserving recurrent dynamics, and it learns a Horizon-Conditioned Regime Gate (HCRG) that predicts step-wise mixture weights from the input window to enable regime-adaptive fusion at inference. Experiments on three hourly NASA POWER datasets under a fixed 3 × 3 mesoscale sampling design show that RCHA-MOE achieves the best mean accuracy within this controlled benchmark, improving RMSE by 6.44%–6.93% and MAE by 7.49%–7.65% over the strongest baseline while increasing R2 by 0.77%–1.02%. Moreover, after power-curve mapping, RCHA-MOE reduces power and energy proxy errors, including in the ramp region where the power curve is most sensitive to wind-speed deviations.

title: Improved Finite-Time Speed-Current Single-Loop Control of PMSM Considering the Limitations of High-Order State Observer
authors: Yixuan Gao, Zhonggang Yin, Yanping Zhang, Yanqing Zhang, Hui Yang, Dongsheng Yuan
abstract: High dynamic response is a key performance indicator of permanent magnet synchronous motor. In the classic dual-loop structure, the inner loop limits the response speed of the outer-loop. Therefore, an improved finite-time composite controller is proposed to be applied in the speed-current single-loop architecture to enhance the dynamic performance of the system target loop. This paper aims to enhance the performance of the outer loop, i.e., the target loop, directly in scenarios where precise control of the inner loop is not strictly necessary. First, after merging the speed and current loops, the order of the controlled objects increases. Compared with the low-order observer designed with the dual-loop structure, the estimated bandwidth of the high-order state observer decreases and the phase delay increases under the same design bandwidth. To this end, a finite-time state observer incorporating proportional differential terms is proposed to overcome the adverse effects of high-order systems. Second, in response to the problem of chattering near the convergence point caused by the inappropriate selection of exponential powers for finite-time functions, a speed-error and torque-current adaptive finite-time control strategy is proposed based on the characteristics of finite-time function and motor control requirements to effectively suppress chattering phenomena. Finally, the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method are verified on the constructed experimental platform.

title: Modeling and Evaluation of Single Event Effects on Hafnia-Based FeRAM
authors: Stuart E. Wodzro, Shimeng Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: In this work, we employ 3D technology computer-aided design (TCAD) mixed-mode simulations to investigate single-event effects (SEE) in FeRAM across three distinct access-transistor geometries, prioritizing heavy-ion impingement on the access transistor.We evaluate write, hold, and read operations to elucidate the device response under varying bias configurations. The simulations are coupled with a regenerative voltage-latch sense amplifier implemented using 32 nm PTM low-power (LP) transistor models to realize the logical manifestation of the single-event effects. Under heavy-ion irradiation, the internal polarization dynamics exhibit a state-dependent response. For the Data 1 state, heavy-ion–induced charge deposition produces an electric field that degrades polarization, whereas in the Data 0 state, the induced field is constructive. This asymmetry arises from state-dependent electrostatics at the storage node. The degradation in the Data 1 state is further amplified by residual charge due to the parasitic bipolar effect present in bulk transistors. Additionally, the sense amplifier topology, whether single-ended or double-ended/differential, strongly influences the available sensing margin and thus the read robustness under radiation events.

title: Cancellable Iris Recognition Scheme Based on Positive-Negative Mapping and Negative Selection
authors: Qianrong Zheng, Baotong Ren, Jianwen Xiang, Rui Hao, Songsong Liao, Dongdong Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Iris recognition technology is widely used in identity verification and other fields due to its high accuracy and strong security. However, with the popularization of the technology, the privacy protection of iris data has become increasingly prominent. Existing systems are often difficult to protect personal data privacy while guaranteeing recognition accuracy, especially when facing data leakage and attacks, iris features cannot be modified once leaked, which is extremely risky. Therefore, proposing an efficient and accurate protection scheme that can ensure the security of iris data has become an important issue that needs to be solved urgently. In this paper, we propose an iris template protection framework based on positive-negative mapping and the negative selective algorithm, called the IrisPN-NS. The framework effectively protects iris data by constructing a complex system of inequality equations and combining it with the negative selection algorithm, thus increasing the difficulty for attackers to crack. Based on this framework, we propose two iris template protection methods, IrisPNM-NS and IrisPNS-NS. We conducted experiments on the CASIA-IrisV3-Interval, CASIA-IrisV4-Lamp, and IIT Delhi Iris datasets. The experimental results show that the scheme achieves high performance and effectively resists the attack of the greedy algorithm, while satisfying the requirements of irreversibility, revocability, and unlinkability.

title: Kinematically-Constrained Marching for Optimal Reeds-Shepp Nonholonomic Path Planning on 2D Cartesian Grids
authors: Ibrahim Ibrahim, Wilm Decré, Jan Swevers
abstract: We present an effective solution for computing locally optimal Reeds-Shepp distances and paths for kinematically-constrained vehicles in environments represented as obstacle-rich 2D Cartesian occupancy grids, addressing the reliance of current methods on discretization and approximation techniques. Our solution leverages a visibility-based marching architecture with continuous analytical expressions for propagating the Reeds-Shepp distance function. We introduce a model to identify reachable and unreachable regions for Reeds-Shepp vehicles, accompanied by a comprehensive representation of the Reeds-Shepp distance function in both cases. Unlike existing approaches, our method computes locally optimal distances and smooth paths globally without discretizing vehicle orientations, motion primitives, or the PDE, and without gradient descent (GD) backtracking, ensuring both accuracy and computational efficiency. Extensive simulations in various environments demonstrate effective improvements over state-of-the-art methods, particularly in complex obstacle-rich scenarios. To facilitate adoption, we provide an open-source solver implemented in C++.

title: Supervised Feature Selection Guided by Granular-Ball Computing: A Two-Stage Geometry-Driven Framework
authors: Huakang Xu, Jin Qian, Shaowei Yan, TingFeng Wen, Duoqian Miao
abstract: Feature selection is a crucial data preprocessing technique aimed at enhancing robustness against boundary uncertainty and complex geometric distributions and improving model interpretability by removing irrelevant or redundant features. Most filter-based feature selection methods employ a single evaluation metric and often fail to explore the underlying geometric distribution structure of the data, which holds significant guiding information for feature selection. In view of this, this paper proposes a Supervised Feature Selection framework guided by granular-ball computing (SFS-GBC). The framework aims to identify superior feature subset candidates for downstream classification tasks by leveraging geometric insights derived from granular balls. Meanwhile, the proposed algorithm introduces a unique two-stage evaluation process designed to synergize coarse-grained efficiency with fine-grained geometric precision, adapting to data of varying dimensionality. Spectifically First, Granularity Consistency (GC) is employed to rapidly rank all features, effectively pre-screening for candidates highly relevant to the decision classes. More innovatively, the subsequent stage departs from traditional statistical metrics by proposing a novel evaluation measure based on the geometric distribution of granular balls, combining Intra-class Compactness (ICC) and Inter-class Discrimination (ICD) to accurately assess feature subsets within a forward search procedure. Furthermore, by adjusting a granulation parameter, the algorithm can explore the data structure from a multi-granularity perspective, potentially yielding superior feature subsets. Experimental results on public benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority and effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: On the Impossibility of Lossless Waveform Rank Reduction for Certain Redundant Arrays
authors: Robin Rajamäki, Piya Pal
abstract: Efficient use of spatio-temporal resources, including sensor arrays and transmit waveforms, is a key challenge in modern MIMO active sensing systems. This paper studies the impact of array redundancy and WR on active sensing performance. Specifically, we show that parameter identifiability at reduced WR critically depends on subspace properties of the so-called array redundancy pattern. We show that array geometries with identical sum co-arrays can exhibit markedly different identifiability properties at low WR. We derive a novel necessary condition for maximizing identifiability at reduced WR, which reveals that the unfavorable redundancy patterns of certain redundant arrays fundamentally limits their performance. The results yield new insights into resource-efficient sensing systems, motivating redundancy-aware array and waveform design.

title: When AI Becomes Its Own Biggest Fan: Self-Preference Bias in AI-Assisted Peer Review_supp1-3691360.docx
authors: Terence T. Ow
abstract: This study examined whether large language models exhibit self-preference bias, a tendency to favor texts generated by themselves over those produced by other models, in AI-assisted peer review. Using a controlled review simulation, GPT-4, GPT-3, and LLaMA were used to generate and evaluate 60 academic manuscripts under a common rubric covering clarity, relevance, originality, and methodological appropriateness. This design enabled direct comparison between self-reviews (a model evaluates its own generated manuscript) and cross-reviews (a model evaluates manuscripts generated by other models) under identical evaluation conditions. In addition, BERT was used to compute embedding-based semantic similarity as a robustness measure. The results indicated that GPT-4 and GPT-3 exhibited strong self-preference bias, assigning significantly higher scores to their own outputs than to manuscripts generated by other models. Human review scores were more closely aligned with AI cross-reviews than AI self-reviews, whereas LLaMA showed weaker and less consistent self-preference patterns, corresponding to its higher rejection rate during quality screening. These findings remained robust across prompt-sensitivity, resampling, alternative aggregation, subsample, and model-specification checks. Taken together, the findings point to a structural source of distortion in AI-assisted evaluation, suggesting that bias may arise not only from training data but also from how generation and evaluation roles are configured within a workflow. The study contributes to research on AI-enabled evaluation systems by extending current discussions of algorithmic bias toward workflow design, and it offers practical guidance on cross-model evaluation, calibration, and human-in-the-loop safeguards in high-stakes evaluative settings.

title: Passenger Flow and Dwell Behavior Analysis in Station Plazas Using Vision-Based Deep Learning
authors: Chun-Chen Chou, Yui Onishi, Miyu Matsuoka, Masanobu Kii, Satoru Sugita
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Station plazas function as key nodes for the convergence and dispersion of passengers in stations. Beyond passenger flow management, they offer significant commercial potential, especially in Japan's transit-oriented developments. While previous studies using vision-based AI have focused on bottleneck detection and crowd control, dwell behavior analyses have relied mostly on subjective methods like surveys and observations. This study introduces a framework using visionbased deep learning to analyze passenger flow and dwell behaviors. Utilizing YOLO and BoT-SORT, it maps behavioral patterns across spatial mesh units, capturing variations in movement and dwell time. The study was conducted at Osaka Umeda Station plazas, a major interchange with a symmetrical layout but distinct behavioral differences across zones. The findings reveal clear spatial and temporal patterns in user behavior, offering actionable insights for station operators. This research underscores how AI-based behavioral analytics can support intelligent space management in transit hubs and contribute to data-driven, human-centered smart city initiatives.

title: Bridging the Scale Gap: High-Resolution Mapping of Seagrass Biomass and Sediment Carbon Using UAV Imagery and Random Forest Classification
authors: Xuan Truong Trinh, Masahiko Nagai
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Seagrass meadows are globally significant “blue carbon” sinks, yet they remain poorly quantified in Southeast Asia, particularly in Vietnam where nearly 50% of the national extent has been lost in recent decades. This study addresses a critical data gap by providing the first robust, spatially explicit assessment of seagrass biomass and sediment organic carbon stocks in the coastal waters of Phu Quoc Island. Adopting a multi-scale approach, we integrated ultra-high-resolution Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) imagery with standardized field sampling and laboratory analysis following IPCC wetlands guidelines. UAV orthomosaics were processed using Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry and classified via a Random Forest algorithm, achieving an overall accuracy of 83.75% (Kappa =0.72). These high-resolution habitat maps were subsequently downscaled to integrate with broader satellite grids for carbon stock estimation. Our results reveal a strong positive gradient between seagrass density and carbon storage. The mean total organic carbon (TOC) tock across the study area was 159.1 ± 61.6 MgC ha<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>, with biomass pools (117.3 MgC ha<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>) contributing nearly three times more than sediment reserves. Notably, dense seagrass meadows were identified as significant carbon hotspots, storing up to 3 2 8. 6 MgC ha - i - m o r e than seven times the capacity of bare sediments. This research establishes a fundamental scientific baseline for blue carbon accounting in Phu Quoc, validating a scalable framework for integrating drone technology with field inventories. The findings provide essential data to support Vietnam's conservation policies and commitments to the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs 13 and 14), facilitating the future inclusion of these ecosystems in national carbon finance mechanisms.

title: Trend-Aware Proactive Defense Using Dynamic Antibody Deployment and Updates
authors: Masato Okawa, Shingo Yamaguchi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In recent years, with the spread of IoT devices, the threat posed by botnets such as Mirai has expanded. Conventional immune-based botnet defense systems (iBDS) are reactive systems that inject antibodies after infection is detected, and the problem was that infection spread due to delays in initial response. In this research, we propose a preventive defense method that dynamically deploys and updates antibodies according to attack trends. The proposed method is characterized by dynamically placing an optimal set of antibodies calculated based on attack trends on the network even before the threat becomes apparent. Deployed antibodies have a set lifespan, and their composition is updated in line with attack trends and the passage of time. This prevents unnecessary resource consumption while ensuring that the system is always ready to respond to the latest threats. As a result of evaluation experiments using simulations, we confirmed that the proposed method can significantly reduce the maximum number of infections by bad worms and shorten the time to complete eradication by 50% compared to the conventional post-injection method. These results demonstrate that a preventive approach that involves advance deployment and dynamic updating of antibodies is extremely effective in a dynamically changing attack environment.

title: Optimizing Human-Machine Interaction in BDA-AI Systems
authors: Chia-Hung Hsieh, Yu-Chun Ma, Asif Khan
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Despite growing scholarly interest in Big Data Analytics and Artificial Intelligence (BDA-AI) systems, empirical research lacks standardized measurement tools to assess their environmental impact. This research addresses the gap by developing an instrument that examines the influence of BDA-AI on eco-innovation (EIN) and ecological performance (EP). Building on the Technology-Organization-Environment (TOE) framework, this study introduces employee digital skills (EDS) and top management support (TMS) as key determinants of technology adoption. Following an extensive review of the literature from 2015 to 2025, a theoretical framework was constructed that encompasses six hypothesized relationships. The proposed measurement instrument comprises 37 items that measure BDA-AI, TOE framework, EP, EIN, TMS, and EDS. This research advances the environmental sustainability and technology management literature by offering researchers a robust tool for investigating how BDA-AI shapes organizational environmental practices.

title: Performance Fairness in Hybrid Memory Architecture
authors: Hsung-Pin Chang, Qing-Xuan Su
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In the cloud environment, the memory cache are shared between users to improve resource utilization, but it also leads to unfair performance due to competition for the memory cache. To address this issue, this paper proposes a memory allocation scheme to achieve performance fairness in DRAM- and NVM-based hybrid memory architecture. To copy with the dynamic access characteristics of each application and the diverse access performance of all user applications, we dynamically adjust the size of the DRAM and NVM cache partitions regularly. This approach not only ensures performance fairness but also improves overall system performance.

title: Detecting Events and Groups at a Campus by Using WLAN Connection Log
authors: Ryoya Makida, Hinako Baba, Teruaki Kitasuka, Toru Nakanishi, Zhuotao Lian
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: By detecting events occurring in specific areas, benefits can be provided to people who use or stay near those areas. In this study, event detection on the Hiroshima University campus is conducted using wireless LAN access logs. From data aggregated at regular intervals, five methods Z-Score, MedAD, IQR, Prophet, and Isolation Forest are used to identify anomalies. As a result, most of these methods were able to detect both the events that were expected to occur and those that were not anticipated. In addition, the study analyzes specific connection patterns to estimate groups of users who are likely attending the same lectures that is, groups that are presumed to belong to the same faculty. While the estimation was successful for in-person lectures, it was not possible for classes that were not conducted face-to-face.

title: CLO-Driven Personalized Remediation Framework for Post-Requisite Course Success in Higher Education
authors: Wagdi Alrawagfeh, Robert Ford, Abdulhalim Dandoush
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: This paper introduces and implements a novel approach for a smart course enrolling system that generates remedial courses within higher education, aiming to address gaps in student learning associated with course learning outcomes (CLOs) and post-requisite courses. While most existing research on remedial education concentrates on preparing high school students for college by addressing issues such as educational inequality, student preparedness, limited attention has been given to remedial interventions through the higher education journey of students. In higer institutions, students may successfully complete courses without fully mastering all their CLOs, resulting in knowledge deficiencies that limit their performance in subsequent advanced courses. Such gaps can also foster a false sense of achievement, thereby undermining students' academic progression and the value of institutional credentials. To mitigate these challenges, this study proposes smart remedial courses that leverage technology to deliver targeted, personalized materials for specific learning deficiencies. Enrolling in advanced courses will be conditionned by passing self-evaluations (e.g., quizzes) simplifying the integration of the approach into the used ERP and avoiding a new load on the academic faculty. The approach seeks to strengthen student success, support academic progression, and enhance the overall quality of higher education. This work represents a significant step toward integrating intelligent, technology-driven solutions for personalized remedial learning at the university level.

title: Design and Optimization of Vertical TFET For Low Power Applications
authors: Chalumuri Raviteja, Ramesh Kumar Sunkaria, Anil Kumar Yadav
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This research examines a vertical tunneling field-effect transistor (VTFET) on a SiGe/Si heterojunction for low power applications. We model the double-gate vertical tunneling FET with a p<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup>-SiGe source, an intrinsic-Si channel, and an n<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup>-Si drain. Device parameters are evaluated using TCAD simulation model. The SiGe/Si heterojunction properties substantially narrow the tunneling barrier, thereby achieving steep subthreshold swing (SS) 26mV/dec, high Ion/Ioff ratio 3.0×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">15</sup> and high I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</inf> current 7.7×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup>A/µm. The analysis of the prospective performance of SiGe, as well as Si channel materials, shows that SiGe provides much superior characteristics for the device. The three different materials were studied for the gate dielectric: HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, Si<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>N<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>, and SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, with HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> showing the best results overall. Therefore, it was concluded that use of Hafnium Oxide (HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) is the most suitable gate dielectric for the optimized VTFET structure. title: AI-Driven Endangered Species Detection and Conservation Using Deep Learning
authors: Vadthyavath Nikshitha, Akali Venkata Atchutarao, Ashish Ranjan, Aviral Awasthi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The accelerating decline of global biodiversity requires innovative tools that not only monitor wildlife but also educate the public. However, existing identification systems are often inaccessible to non-experts or lack integrated conservation context. This paper proposes a web-based "Endangered Species Detection And Prevention Using Technology" framework that leverages the YOLO (You Only Look Once) deep learning algorithm for robust object detection. The system architecture utilizes a React.js frontend for user interaction and a Flask-based backend to handle inference pipelines. Upon processing user-uploaded images, the model identifies species and dynamically maps detections to a database of conservation metrics, including IUCN Red List categories, habitat descriptions, and population threats. Experimental results shows that the integrated model perform a detection mAp of 87.6% successfully identifying endangered species across varied environments. The system effectively combines real-time computer vision with educational retrieval, providing users with immediate, actionable biodiversity knowledge. This study confirms that integrating lightweight deep learning models with modern web technologies offers a scalable solution for environmental awareness

title: Performance Trade-Offs in Dynamic Comparator Architectures: Offset, Speed, and Energy Perspectives
authors: Akrosh Vikram Shukla, Vimla Bharti, Dhandapani Vaithiyanathan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Dynamic comparators play a crucial role in modern high-speed and low-power analog-to-digital converters (ADCs), where parameters such as offset voltage, propagation delay, energy consumption, and noise significantly influence the achievable resolution and sampling performance of the overall system. This work presents a comparative study of various dynamic comparator architectures in wide usage, such as the conventional dynamic comparator, double-tail comparator, enhanced StrongARM with MOS capacitors, MOSCAP-free enhanced StrongARM, and an enhanced StrongARM architecture with reduced tail capacitance. The circuits were implemented on 180nm technology scale with the clock frequency of 1GHz. Comparison is carried out based on the basis of input-referred offset voltage, decision delay, power and energy consumption, slew rate, and metastability behaviour. Architectural trade-offs among gain before regeneration, tail-node dynamics, and charge redistribution are analysed to explain performance variations across designs. Results show that conventional StrongARM comparators offer fast regeneration with minimal complexity. However, enhanced architectures significantly improve offset, noise robustness, and energy efficiency, while reduced tail-capacitance designs offer the best balance between speed and power. This comparative study gives clear design insights into choosing dynamic comparator architectures for low-voltage, high-speed ADC applications.

title: Design and Implementation of a Area Efficient Logic BIST Architecture for 4-bit ALU using Verilog and Cadence Genus
authors: Akash Kumar Prajapati, Bodile Roshan Mukindrao, Suraj Prajapati, Rohit Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper represents the design and the implementation of area efficient Logic Built-in Self-Test (LBIST) architecture for a 4-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) using Verilog and Cadence Genus. To minimize the hardware overhead, the proposed method uses only two small components like a Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) for pseudo-random test pattern generation and a Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR) for output response compression. Testing activation and pattern generation are controlled externally through a simple mode-select signals which eliminates the need for a control FSM and reduces the additional logic block. The LBIST enable ALU was synthesized under area optimization constraints, and the results show that the inclusion of LFSR and MISR introduces only the minimal area overhead while maintaining correct functionality and reliable faulty detection. The final MISR signature effectively distinguish fault free and faulty behavior, demonstrating that an efficient and compact self-test mechanism can be achieved without a BIST controller. This approach is well-suited for small data paths unit, educational testability demonstrations, and the low-power SoCs modules where area is a primary constraints of the blocks.

title: Abstract vs. Layman Summary Classification Using Machine Learning and Transformer Models
authors: Banala Saritha, Arundhuti Sengupta, G Purnachandrarao, Sainadh Singh Kshatri, B Girirajan, Vasu Koduri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Scientific articles now often have several summaries aimed at different types of audience, most often expert summaries and simplified layman summaries. Automatically identifying these types of summary is crucial for advances in improving science communication, content organization and information retrieval systems. This study consists of the comparative evaluation of traditional embedding-based models of machine learning and transformers-based architectures for the task of abstract versus layman summary classification. A task of supervised learning approach is followed using balanced dataset of 1,000 scientific summaries; one-half abstract and one half of layman summaries. The dataset is separated into training, validation, and test groups so that a sound evaluation of model performance could be conducted. Traditional machine learning techniques of Word2Vec and GloVe word embedding with Logistic Regression are used as baseline models, and BERT and RoBert are fine-tuned end-to-end in order to use contextualized language representations. The models performance is measured based on traditional execution measures that are accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 -score. Experimental results show that it can correctly extract fundamental semantics differences between summary types with an accuracy rate of about 86%, which can be regarded as the effectiveness of embedding-based models. However, we can prominently see the power of transformer based models compared with the traditional ones, indicating the importance of context understanding in audience awareness text classification. BERT is accurate to 90% whereas RoBERTa gives the finest performance all around 93% accuracy as well as balanced values of precision, recall, and F1 score. The results validate the idea of using contextualized representations of the transformer to understand why they're better than static embeddings when it comes to distinguishing between expert-oriented and layman summaries. This work makes RoBERTa the most reliable model among the models under evaluation and highlights the potential of the transformer-based approaches in improving automated science communication and content classification systems.

title: Designing of an AHB Protocol Using Single Master, Multiple Slaves and Power Optimization Using Clock Gating
authors: Tanya Singh, Neetu Sood
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The interconnection of buses in a computer system has a significant impact on its implementation. The complexity of the system buses will affect the transfer of data and instructions between the processor and memory or between peripheral devices and memory. The Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA) offers a wide standard for connecting and controlling multiple functional blocks in SoCs to overcome these issues. The AHB architecture ensures that the system is designed properly with the multi-processors, controllers and peripherals implemented in it.The motive of this paper is to offer a system-on-chip design summary and an RTL view of the AMBA AHB module. Since, AHB is one of the important protocol in the AMBA family. It is the backbone bus of the system because of its high performance and high-clock system modules. It allows high power peripheral devices to be directly connected to CPUs, on chip memories, and external off-chip memory interfaces. The paper emphasizes the Verilog-based AHB design and implementation, with the Xilinx simulator used for read/write operations.

title: Design of a Modulated Metasurface Reflector for Anomalous Reflection
authors: Ratna Priya, Anjali Kumari, Gaurav Sharma, Rahul Sharma, Neha Ahuja, Jagannath Malik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper introduces a modulated metasurface design to achieve anomalous reflection where unit cell consists of circular patch with alternate patch having slot to provide capacitive discontinuity and suppress surface waves resulting in a non-uniform design, on a circular substrate and ground. Horn antenna is used to incident normal waves and metasurface is working on far field region over a frequency range of 4.68 GHz to 5.32 GHz. The design is simulated, optimized in CST studio suite which validates strong directivity beam steering of 13 ° at 5 GHz and narrow beam width giving better results than conventional metasurface by tuning phase, amplitude and polarization. The proposed design can be used in beam steering metasurface and high gain reflector designs.

title: Design and Implementation of an FSM-Based DDR Memory Controller on FPGA
authors: Simran, Khushi Gupta, Kunal Gupta, Nitesh Kashyap
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Double Data Rate (DDR) memory is widely used in modern digital systems due to its high bandwidth and efficient data transfer capability. However, reliable communication with DDR SDRAM requires strict adherence to timing constraints, refresh mechanisms, and command sequencing. This paper presents the design and FPGA implementation of an FSM-based DDR memory controller capable of handling read/write operations, refresh cycles, and DDR timing requirements. The controller translates processor-level requests into DDR commands such as ACTIVATE, READ, WRITE, PRECHARGE, and REFRESH while maintaining JEDEC-compliant timing parameters. The design was synthesized and validated on an FPGA platform using Xilinx Vivado, demonstrating correct and stable operation without timing violations.

title: A Novel Chaotic Log-Tangent Map with Bit-Level Permutation and Diffusion for Secure Image Encryption
authors: Prasun Sarkar, Mohit Dua
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid development of digital imaging applications has created an ever-increasing demand for security mechanisms that could resist attacks using modern cryptanalytic techniques. This paper proposes an image encryption approach utilizing a Log–Tangent chaotic map that is designed as a two-dimensional nonlinear system in order to introduce a high sensitivity with respect to initial states and control parameters. The whole bit-level [9] strategy adopted by the proposed cipher combines a permutation stage, aimed at rearranging bit-plane positions, with a proper diffusion process sustained by a chaos-derived keystream. The integration of both operations into a single chaotic domain ensures a high degree of pixel randomness and structural disruption. In order to investigate the dynamical properties of the Log–Tangent map, Lyapunov exponent profiles, Shannon entropy measurements, and bifurcation characteristics are conducted. These analyses show that the system has extensive chaotic intervals and produces statistically uniform sequences, which are adequate for cryptographic applications. Experimental results on images indicate that the cipher produces encrypted outputs with negligible correlation among neighboring pixels and high entropy values, and visual indistinguishability from noise. The proposed scheme is resilient to brute-force attacks because of its large key space and its sensitivity regarding the variation of the parameters. The work demonstrates that the Log–Tangent chaotic map with bit-level permutation and diffusion forms an efficient, lightweight methodology for image data protection in security-sensitive applications.

title: Vista: Verifier-in-the-Loop Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Quantum Program Synthesis
authors: Cong Yu, Tuo Shi, Valter Uotila, Shilong Deng, Lei You, Bo Zhao
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems
abstract: None

title: Universal Time-Series Representation Learning: A Survey
authors: Patara Trirat, Yooju Shin, Junhyeok Kang, Youngeun Nam, Jihye Na, Minyoung Bae, Joeun Kim, Byunghyun Kim, Jae-Gil Lee
abstract: None

title: Multi-Agent Systems in Education: A Survey from the Trustworthiness Perspective
authors: Chahana Dahal, Jinming Chen, Muchao Ye, Zuobin Xiong
abstract: Multi-agent systems are becoming an important part of educational technology by helping students learn through collaboration, feedback, and adaptive support. This paper reviews previous and recent research on multi-agent learning systems and explores the roles agents can take, such as tutors, peers, or facilitators. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first comprehensive survey of multi-agent learning systems that introduces a pedagogy-based role and interaction taxonomy, compares system architectures beyond LLMs, and links design to learning theory. The fields are grouped on the basis of system architecture, learning scenarios, and connections to learning theories such as constructivism and the Zone of Proximal Development. The survey examines how these systems are evaluated and the challenges of measuring learning, engagement, and collaboration. In addition, this work provides a comprehensive study of emerging trustworthiness frameworks that address safety, privacy, fairness, and transparency in multi-agent learning systems. Finally, the paper concludes with open challenges and future directions in using the multi-agent learning system in education scenarios, including the need for better simulation environments, stronger coordination between humans and AI agents, etc.

title: Quantifying the Benefits of Securities Regulation
authors: Naveen Durvasula, Tim Roughgarden

authors: Aanjhan Ranganathan

title: Algorithmic UDAP
authors: Talia Gillis, Riley Stacy, Sam Brumer, Emily Black

title: A Stochastic Approach to Terrain Maps for Safe Lunar Landing
authors: Anja Sheppard, Chris Reale, Katherine A. Skinner
abstract: Safely landing on the lunar surface is a challenging task, especially in the heavily-shadowed South Pole region where traditional vision-based hazard detection methods are not reliable. The potential existence of valuable resources at the lunar South Pole has made landing in that region a high priority for many space agencies and commercial companies. However, relying on a LiDAR for hazard detection during descent is risky, as this technology is fairly untested in the lunar environment. There exists a rich log of lunar surface data from the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter (LRO), which could be used to create informative prior maps of the surface before descent. In this work, we propose a method for generating stochastic elevation maps from LRO data using Gaussian processes (GPs), which are a powerful Bayesian framework for non-parametric modeling that produce accompanying uncertainty estimates. In high-risk environments such as autonomous spaceflight, interpretable estimates of terrain uncertainty are critical. However, no previous approaches to stochastic elevation mapping have taken LRO Digital Elevation Model (DEM) confidence maps into account, despite this data containing key information about the quality of the DEM in different areas. To address this gap, we introduce a two-stage GP model in which a secondary GP learns spatially varying noise characteristics from DEM confidence data. This heteroscedastic information is then used to inform the noise parameters for the primary GP, which models the lunar terrain. Additionally, we use stochastic variational GPs to enable scalable training. By leveraging GPs, we are able to more accurately model the impact of heteroscedastic sensor noise on the resulting elevation map. As a result, our method produces more informative terrain uncertainty, which can be used for downstream tasks such as hazard detection and safe landing site selection. We compare against several stochastic mapping baselines using both simulated DEMs and real-world LRO Narrow Angle Camera data at the lunar South Pole.

title: A Prototype Low-Cost Hybrid-Actuated Mobile Spherical Terrain Exploration Rover
authors: Winnie Gao, Youyou Luo, Drew Schlauch, Stephen T. Kwok-Choon
abstract: This paper presents the design, development, and testing of a low-cost Hybrid-Actuated Mobile Spherical Terrain Exploration Rover (HAMSTER) intended for use on a lunar surface exploration mission. As current technologies develop and push boundaries of space exploration, the need for robotic systems capable of maneuvering across various obstacles and terrains is expected to increase. This paper explores the design considerations, manufacturing, and testing completed. HAMSTER uses a pendulum-based actuation method for steering, and an actuated internal shaft that propels the vehicle forward. The internal structure includes a two-tiered central case comprised of: the navigation control module, accelerometer, motor controller, battery, voltage regulators, Raspberry Pi, pendulum servo for steering, and a motor shaft. A camera is being considered to aid in navigation that shall be internally mounted; however, this has presented challenges that will be discussed. Additive manufacturing through 3D printing was selected for rapid prototyping fabrication of the HAMSTER chassis and exterior shell. This has allowed for the proof-of-concept phase of this design sequence, where the shape, form, and function of the vehicle are to be refined through iteration. In conclusion, this paper outlines the considerations found in the design, prototyping, fabrication, and assembly with a discussion on proposed work to be completed that includes integration and preliminary testing of the HAMSTER vehicle.

title: From Twitter to Reddit: Cross-Domain Indonesian Sarcasm Detection with Pretrained Transformers
authors: Auryn Larissa Sabrina, Darren Cornelius Citra Wijaya, Meiliana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Sarcasm is a complex linguistic phenomenon that challenges natural language processing (NLP) systems, especially when models are transferred across online platforms with different linguistic styles. This study examines the cross-domain generalization ability of transformer-based models for Indonesian sarcasm detection, focusing on how a Twitter-trained sarcasm model performs on Reddit data. Using the w11wo/reddit_indonesia_sarcastic dataset containing 14,116 annotated comments, three pretrained models were fine-tuned and evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and macro-F1 metrics: XLM-RoBERTa-base, IndoBERT-base, and Multilingual BERT. Experimental results show that XLMRoBERTa achieves 0.8052 accuracy and 0.7162 macro-F1, IndoBERT attains the best performance with 0.8067 accuracy and 0.7224 macro-F1, while Multilingual BERT records the lowest scores with 0.7766 accuracy and 0.6883 macro-F1. These findings indicate that transformer-based can capture sarcasm cues across Indonesian social media platforms, while also emphasizing the need for domain adaptation to improve robustness in handling subtle sarcastic expressions.

title: A New Geometric Configuration of Metallic Terminals in On-board Components for Terahertz-Era EMC
authors: Hiroshi Kamiya, Satoru Honda, Hiroyuki Sato, Toshiki Kanamoto
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: With the rapid growth of high-frequency applications such as high-speed wireless communications, on-board components are increasingly required to operate beyond the conventional GHz range. In this range, chip-level terminals can act as unintended radiating structures, leading to electromagnetic compatibility issues. This paper investigates the influence of two terminal geometries, a rectangular terminal with sharp edges and a hemispherical terminal with a smooth contour, using finiteelement method simulations. The results show that rectangular terminals exhibit a pronounced resonance at 912 GHz, and demonstrate broadband resonances with an upper-bound radiation efficiency two to three times higher than that of hemispherical terminals in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{G H z}-\mathbf{T H z}$</tex> range. These findings demonstrate that terminal geometry provides a systematic simulation-based design guideline for mitigating broadband resonances, with direct relevance to sub-THz EMC-conscious design.

title: FlexSeal: Passive Pinhole Valve Suction for PneuNet Gripper with One-way Flow Object Attachment and Release
authors: Nam Phuong Dam, Jiyeop Kim, Amy Kyungwon Han, Van Anh Ho
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: We introduce FlexSeal, a hybrid soft gripper that integrates a suction cup and a PneuNet actuator, both powered through a single pneumatic line. At the core of FlexSeal is a stomata-inspired pinhole valve that enables one-way air flow, automatically coordinating suction-based attachment and pressure triggered release without any electronic or active control. Unlike conventional hybrid grippers requiring multiple pneumatic channels or continuous vacuum, FlexSeal employs a pressure-threshold mechanism that autonomously switches between grasping and releasing phases. Experiments confirm stable and repeatable operation across dry, wet, low-friction, and underwater environments, requiring air supply only during transition phases. This passive one-way pneumatic architecture achieves high reliability and energy efficiency, offering a promising solution for compact robotic manipulation.

title: Surface-based Manipulation Using Tunable Compliant Porous-Elastic Soft Sensing
authors: Gayatri Indukumar, Muhammad Awais, Diana Cafiso, Matteo Lo Preti, Lucia Beccai
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: There is a growing need for soft robotic platforms that perform gentle, precise handling of a wide variety of objects. Existing surface-based manipulation systems, however, lack the compliance and tactile feedback needed for delicate handling. This work introduces the COmpliant Porous-Elastic Soft Sensing (COPESS) integrated with inductive sensors for adaptive object manipulation and localised sensing. The design features a tunable lattice layer that simultaneously modulates mechanical compliance and sensing performance. By adjusting lattice geometry, both stiffness and sensor response can be tailored to handle objects with varying mechanical properties. Experiments demonstrate that by easily adjusting one parameter, the lattice density, from 7% to 20%, it is possible to significantly alter the sensitivity and operational force range (about $-23 \times$ and $9 \times$ respectively). This approach establishes a blueprint for creating adaptive, sensorized surfaces where mechanical and sensory properties are co-optimized, enabling passive, yet programmable, delicate manipulation.

title: Evaluating AI-Powered Chatbot Acceptance in Indonesian Skincare E-Commerce: An Extended Technology Acceptance Model Approach
authors: Brianna Sanjaya, Mikael Bryan Ciputra, Joseph Setiawan, Antonius Felix, Julius Sutrisno, Johanes Fernandes Andry
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: This study investigates the acceptance and implementation of AI-powered chatbots for customer service in Indonesian skincare e-commerce using an extended Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) framework. The research develops and evaluates an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{A I}$</tex> chatbot prototype for L'Essaine, a pre-launch skincare brand, combining heuristic usability evaluation with TAM-based surveys. Data were collected from 10 participants through usability testing and surveys measuring perceived usefulness, perceived ease of use, attitude toward use, and behavioral intention. Results indicate high acceptance levels with perceived usefulness scoring 4.4/5 and perceived ease of use <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.5 / 5$</tex>. The chatbot successfully handled 45 customer consultations with a 92 % resolution rate. This research integrates chatbot-specific factors including response speed, natural language processing capability, knowledge base comprehensiveness, and interface design into the traditional TAM model. Findings contribute practical insights for skincare brands implementing AI chatbots for prelaunch customer engagement and extend theoretical understanding of conversational AI technology adoption in Indonesian e-commerce contexts. The study recommends phased implementation strategies focusing on domain-specific knowledge base training and continuous learning mechanisms to enhance service quality. As an exploratory study with a limited sample of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 0}$</tex> participants, findings are indicative rather than statistically generalizable and are intended to inform future large-scale validation research.

title: A multi-Degrees-of-Freedom Soft Robotic Chamber that Mimics Muscles to Stimulate Myoblasts
authors: Atsushi Takata, Yuta Kurashina
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Mechanical stimulus is a critical factor in myoblast maturation. The effect of one degree of freedom (1DoF) stimulus on myoblasts has been previously investigated by medical engineering researchers; however, mechanical stimuli should be applied in multi-DoF in vivo. This is because a single bone is driven by many muscles, and each muscle actuates each other. In this study, we propose a multi-DoF soft robotic chamber (multi-DoF SRC) to stretch cells with multiple actuators. To this end, we designed and fabricated the chamber composed of a poly-dimethylsiloxane flexible cell carrier and three pneumatic actuators. The collagen-based scaffolds in the carriers were able to transmit actuation to the cells. Next, the actuation characteristics were measured by image processing. Finally, we conducted a preliminary experiment in which myoblasts were cultured for two days while applying 1-DoF and 2-DoF stretches ($\mathbf{n} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{1}$). As a result, fluorescence imaging visualized the orientation of actin filaments in each sample. This result demonstrates the ability of the proposed chamber to culture myoblasts with multi-DoF stimulation, allowing for the observation of immunostaining in cells. Future evaluations will be conducted with a larger sample size.

title: Dimensions of the E-Commerce System Success (ESS) Model and Augmented Reality (AR) That Affect User Satisfaction and Repeat Usage Intent on the Shopee App in Indonesia
authors: Rebekka Ledyana, Celia Marleen Kardijaya, Winna Michelle, Arta Moro Sundjaja
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Rapid growth in e-commerce industry in Indonesia, particularly on mobile apps like Shopee, raises the need to understand factors that foster user loyalty. This study integrates Augmented Reality (AR) characteristics to E-Commerce Quality (information Quality, System Quality, Service Quality) variable to examine their impact on Perceived Value, User Satisfaction, and Reuse Intention alongside Hedonic & Utilitarian Value. Data were collected from 555 female Shopee users who had used Virtual Try-On as AR feature in Shopee Mobile. Data was analyzed using statistical software (PLS-SEM). The results show that E-Commerce quality significantly enhances Perceived Value and User Satisfaction. Perceived Value as the strongest predictor of Reuse Intention, exceeding the influence of User Satisfaction. Meanwhile, Hedonic Benefit does not significantly influence Perceived Value, suggesting that users prioritize functional and economic benefits over experiential enjoyment when evaluating AR-enabled shopping features. These findings highlight the importance of e-commerce mobile apps developers to strengthen their technological system quality and functional value to foster the existing user (loyal user).

title: TATERS (Tool for Autonomous Terrain Exploration of Remote Spaces)
authors: Richard Chase, Tyler Doiron, Ryan Navarre, Meryl Spencer, Zane Almquist, Nathan Lemus
abstract: This paper reports on the development of TATERS (Tool for Autonomous Terrain Exploration of Remote Spaces), a software framework for planning and risk analysis of single and multirobot traversals. TATERS is designed to aid in mission planning workflows by merging planning methodologies with execution of autonomy on board robotic systems. This is done by developing coarse and high-fidelity simulations as well as tying in features of autonomy stacks into Monte Carlo simulations during pre-mission studies. Further, we investigate and report on methods for curating live data, incorporating into the planning activities, and representing and updating optimal paths on autonomous systems without the need for updating large amounts of data. This is achieved by a data reduction scheme called skeletonization which compresses spatio-temporal cost cube data derived from geographic information system (GIS) data layer sets. The cost cube derivation strategy is designed using cost metrics that can be adjusted and guided by intuition. We identify and focus on three key metrics: scientific merit, traversability, and survivability.

title: Torsion Resistant Strain Limiting Layers Enable High Grip Strength of Electrically-Driven Handed Shearing Auxetic Grippers
authors: Ian Good, Srivatsan Balaji, Jacob N. Miske, Jeffrey I. Lipton
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Soft grippers made from handed shearing auxetics (HSAs) have demonstrated a strong ability to successfully pick and manipulate many objects. A key limitation to their wider adoption is their inability to grasp larger payloads due to objects slipping out of grasps. We have overcome this limitation by integrating a torsion resistant strain limiting layer (TRSLL) with Handed Shearing Auxetics. This reduces out-of-plane bending while maintaining the gripper’s softness and in-plane flexibility. We characterized the design space of the strain limiting layer and HSA actuators for a soft gripper through simulation and experimentation. The inclusion of the TR-SLL with HSAs enables HSA grippers to be made with a single digit. We found that the use of our TR-SLL HSA gripper enabled pinch grasping of payloads over 1 kg. We demonstrate a lifting capacity of 5 kg when loading an HSA using the TR-SLL. We also demonstrate a peak pinch grasp force of 5.8 N and a peak planar caging force of 14.5 N. Finally, we test the TR-SLL gripper on a suite of 43 YCB objects. We show success on 37 objects demonstrating significant capabilities.

title: Real-Time Simulation of Deformable Tactile Sensors and Objects in Robotic Grasping using Graph Neural Networks with Inductive Biases
authors: Guillaume Duret, Frederik Heller, Danylo Mazurak, Tim Schneider, Alap Kshirsagar, Florence Zara, Jan Peters, Liming Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Physical simulation of deformable bodies is crucial for robotic manipulation, particularly for applications involving deformable objects and deformable tactile sensors. While Finite Element Method (FEM) simulations provide high accuracy for modeling deformable objects and tactile sensors, they are prohibitively expensive for real-time applications, with simulation times often exceeding practical limits for robotic control and learning. This paper presents a novel Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework that accelerates the simulation of tactile sensors by factors of $10^{2}-10^{4}$ compared to FEM, while maintaining high physical accuracy. Our approach addresses limitations in existing GNN-based physics learning through inductive biases. The key contributions include: (1) extending FEM simulation to deformable tactile sensors in grasping scenarios, (2) incorporating novel inductive biases through tetrahedral features and global graph features to improve information propagation and training stability, and (3) demonstrating the first successful application of GNN simulation for tactile sensors with generalization to unseen objects. Additionally, the inductive biases reduce prediction errors by up to 45% compared to baseline approaches. This work enables real-time simulation of soft tactile sensors and deformable objects for robotic applications, including deformation and stress prediction. All simulation and training code can be found at https://tacgraspnets.github.io/.

title: BOOST Converter Performance Optimization for Microsatellite Power Systems Using MPPT Fuzzy Control
authors: Abdelkader Hadj-Dida
abstract: In space technology, microsatellites have emerged as key platforms due to their flexibility, low cost, and wide range of applications, from Earth observation to telecommunications. One of the major challenges to ensure the reliable operation of these spacecraft is the efficient management of their Electrical Power System (EPS), mainly based on solar energy. Future space missions require high-performance power systems that operate under strict mass and volume constraints in harsh space environments. To supply, store, and distribute electrical energy to various microsatellite subsystems, a variety of DC-DC converters are used; The design specifications and simulation parameters of a Boost converter for microsatellite applications are presented in this work. Achieving optimal power conversion efficiency to enhance the microsatellite's EPS performance is a critical objective during its design, due to the limited energy availability and stringent weight constraints of space systems. Maximizing the energy extracted from the microsatellite's solar panels under varying environmental conditions depends on advanced Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) techniques. These techniques are therefore essential to optimize microsatellite performance and ensure its autonomy throughout the mission. This paper presents the performance optimization of a DC-DC boost converter designed for microsatellite power systems using a Fuzzy-Logic based MPPT Controller. The proposed approach enhances the efficiency and reliability of the EPS, contributing to the successful accomplishment of the microsatellite mission in orbit. Power analysis and control strategies of the proposed converter were carried out and simulated within the MATLAB/Simulink environment. All design objectives, analytical calculations, and simulation results of the Boost converter are thoroughly analyzed, verified, and validated in this paper.

title: Surface-Tension-Controlled Molding of Large Suction Cup Arrays for Adaptive Adhesion
authors: Qifan Yu, Kaitlyn Becker
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: In this paper, we present a scalable and low-cost fabrication process that uses surface-tension-controlled (STC) molding to generate suction cups, enabling adaptive adhesion of soft robotic surfaces through large suction cup arrays. The STC molding process leverages the surface tension of liquid rubbers to form fillets, or menisci, around open molds and fixtures. The menisci create smooth and curved surfaces that are used as both the molding surfaces and the bodies of suction cups, allowing us to generate both push-on and vacuum-powered suction cups in large arrays. This STC molding process allows us to create larger numbers of suction cups in a single array, supported by a soft underlying body without complex or manually intensive molding processes. Direct integration of suction cups into a soft underlying body and vacuum manifold can allow the arrays to adapt to irregular, compliant, and deforming surfaces. We explore the potential uses of STC molding for applications like bio tagging, manipulation, and locomotion by prototyping a sheet with 70 push-on suction cups, a 19 -cup vacuum-powered grasper, and a tube feet array with 20 suction cups on their tips. The fully soft underlying body and vacuum manifold allows the large arrays of suction cups to conform and thus adapt to irregular, compliant, and deforming surfaces.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)

title: Linearized UC1875 PWM Controller in Phase-Shifted Full-Bridge Current-Doubler-Output Converters
authors: Natan Ranjbar, Kasemsan Siri
abstract: Stability analysis of space-class phase-shifted-full-bridge (PSFB) converters is hindered by the absence of a linear dynamic macro-model for the UC1875 pulse-widthmodulated (PWM) controllers, as the UC1875-SP variant is the only qualified manufacturers list class-V (QML-V) radiation-hardened phase-shifted controller available for use in space qualified PSFB power topologies. A frequencydomain macro-model suitable for loop-stability analysis is, however, absent. Developed using SPICE is a linearized model of the UC1875 PWM controller, that can be embedded directly in SPICE as a subcircuit model. Implemented with the phase-shifted macro-model is a large-signal averaged model of the PSFB's power stage utilizing a current-doubleroutput (CDO). Performance of the developed averaged macro-model was validated against a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.2-\text{kW}$</tex> rated closedloop pulse-by-pulse switching model operating in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM), enabling a Middlebrook loop-break AC injection into the outer voltagecontrol feedback loop in order to obtain direct Bode-plot extraction for loop-stability analysis. The result is a reusable schematic form model that closes a critical modeling gasp for electrical design engineers designing radiation-hardened PSFB systems driven by the UC1875-family controllers for high-power spaceborne power-conditioning and RF payload applications.

title: A Machine-Learning-Based Thermal Run Risk Assessment Model for Transformer Under Distribution Systems
authors: Chengjun Xie, Chenhao Sun, Haochen Li, Yating Qiu
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: As critical nodes in distributed energy systems, distribution transformers support secure and efficient power delivery. Yet the integration of distributed energy resources and energy storage introduces pronounced load volatility, heightening overload risk, accelerating insulation degradation, and driving thermal stress that undermines operational reliability. Under limited maintenance staff and diagnostic equipment, it is impractical to perform continuous, network-wide inspections for latent faults, so accurate spatiotemporal Thermal Run prediction is needed to prioritize interventions and allocate inspection and repair resources more effectively. With such motivations, this paper proposes a Transformer Thermal Run Risk Assessment (TTRRA) Model to achieve more robust spatiotemporal Thermal Run prediction in transformers under diverse and non-stationary load variations. First, a feature matrix that integrates static features capturing long-term drift and dynamic features reflecting short-term fluctuations is constructed. Second, static and dynamic feature risks are assessed with the Static Feature Risk Assessment (SFRA) model and the Dynamic Feature Risk Assessment (DFRA) model, respectively, each adapted to its feature distribution. Finally, the Risk Score Fusion (RSF) model is employed to fuse the static and dynamic risk scores, cooperatively integrating the complementary strengths of the cloud and the edge. An empirical case study demonstrates the feasibility and efficiency of the strategy. Overload events in operation logs are used as proxy labels of overload driven thermal stress to train and evaluate the model.

title: An Edge-Enabled Deep Learning Framework for PV Generation and Load Forecasting in Microgrids
authors: Ruonan Chen, Guoxi Shen
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The intermittent nature of high-penetration solar photovoltaic (PV) systems poses a challenge to the global energy transition, especially in areas like Australia, which has the highest per capita rooftop PV rate in the world and aims to have 82 % renewable energy by 2030. Due to this volatility, grid stability requires extremely accurate short-term forecasting, which is difficult for traditional centralized statistical and physics-based methods to provide. As a result, there is a known research-to-practice gap. This study presents a framework for decentralized predictive intelligence (Edge AI) in photovoltaic microgrids, employing two innovative hybrid deep learning architectures validated on comprehensive Australian datasets. A hybrid Convolutional Neural Network-Long Short-Term Memory (CNN-LSTM) model was employed for photovoltaic generation forecasting to capture cloud-induced ramp events and long-term temporal dependencies. A Complete Ensemble Empirical Mode Decomposition-Gated Recurrent Unit-Attention (CEEMDAN-GRU-Attention) framework was utilized for short-term load demand forecasting, employing CEEMDAN to address signal non-stationarity and an Attention mechanism to emphasize significant load peaks. Both models employed the Huber Loss function to increase robustness against outliers and enhance generalizability.
