Empirical validation established the enhanced predictive efficacy necessary for PV microgrid operation. The CNN-LSTM model for photovoltaic forecasting exhibited remarkable goodness-of-fit, attaining a Coefficient of Determination (R2) of 0.8922 and a negligible Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.0419 in the normalized power domain. The CEEMDAN-GRU-Attention model for load forecasting concurrently attained a high proportional accuracy, evidenced by a Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1. 1 1 \%}$</tex> in half-hourly demand prediction. This framework offers a technological blueprint for the deployment of Edge AI, crucial for improving operational grid stability, optimizing the management of distributed energy resources, and strengthening energy resilience within Australia's high-renewable grid environment.

title: Overcoming Challenges of Realism in Competitive Space-Based Reinforcement Learning with AstroCraft
authors: Loren James Anderson, R. Scott Erwin, Moses Hansen, Victoria Outkin, Rehman S. Qureshi, Anthony Aborizk, Rohan Kulkarni, Elyssa Dennery, Sahitya Senapathy, Srija Makkapati, William Flathmann, Kathryn Alcalde, Berkan Dokmeci, Shahin Firouzbakht, Edfil Basan
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a popular choice for training artificial intelligence algorithms in competitive scenarios due to recent successes of achieving superhuman performance in board and video game environments. However, there is currently a lack of open-source, competitive environments designed to exhibit challenges of realism in the domain of spacecraft control to assist in the transfer of RL algorithms to physical systems. We present AstroCraft, a spacebased capture-the-flag environment comprised of two opposing teams, each containing multiple maneuverable satellites and a space station in geosynchronous equatorial orbit. The primary goal of each team is to maneuver a satellite to capture a flag at the opposing player's station and return the flag to its own station without being tagged-out by opposing satellites. We first perform an experimental study on AstroCraft that elicits challenges from realisms such as long time horizons, complex dynamics, and stringent fuel constraints. Throughout, we conduct experiments on similar environments from the literature indicating that many of these realisms are not significantly present and do not degrade performance. Finally, we design an RL algorithm which first gathers data from a heuristic opponent competing against itself; constructing this dataset enables the application of Conservative Q-learning for offline pretraining before further online finetuning. This algorithm produces a model that is superior to the original heuristic opponent. We believe that the lessons learned from our experiments on AstroCraft provide promising avenues for constructing RL algorithms that overcome challenges of realism in simulation and physical systems alike.

title: Classification of Wheat Disease Using Model-Level Graph Network with Parrot Optimizer
authors: P. Yogashree, M. Sangeetha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: One of the most vital staple crops in the world, wheat plays a major role in both economic stability and human sustenance. However, a number of diseases pose a threat to wheat productivity, which can severely reduce yield and quality. Existing detection methods face limitations such as imbalanced datasets, noise in images, and high computational requirements, restricting timely and accurate disease identification. To overcome these challenges, the research presents a Model-Level Graph Network with Parrot Optimizer (MoLGNNet-ParO) for wheat disease classification. The approach begins with data collection from the wheat leaf disease dataset, ensuring representation of both healthy and diseased samples. Collected images undergo preprocessing using a Modified Square Root Sage-Husa Adaptive Kalman Filter (MSSHAKF) to reduce noise and enhance image quality. Feature extraction is performed using a Modified ResNet-152 with Hinge Attention Network (MR-152-HiAN), capturing discriminative and hierarchical disease features. Then, the Model-Level Graph Network (MoLGNNet) leverages structural relationships between features to classify wheat leaf diseases accurately, while the Parrot Optimizer (ParO) optimizes the loss function, improving convergence and overall model performance. The experiment yields the better results, which are out of the F1-score (99.79%), precision (99.62%), accuracy (99.84%), specificity (99.57%), and recall (99.45%); the proposal framework performs better than the traditional models. The suggested methodology offers an effective, highly accurate and efficient method of detecting early wheat diseases, promoting sustainable crop yield, and efficient management of the disease.

title: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) and Human Cognitive Factors: A Systematic Literature Review of Domains, Techniques, and Impacts
authors: Zulfian Rahmadiansyah, Ahmad Ataka Awwalur Rizqi, Sunu Wibirama
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The rapid use of AI in critical fields requires explainable AI (XAI) to make systems transparent. However, providing too much information can overwhelm the user's cognitive capacity. To address this, this systematic literature review (SLR) analyzed 35 studies published between 2020 and 2026 using the PRISMA protocol. The review examined XAI applications, techniques, and their impact on the human mind. The results show that most research focused on cognitive foundations, using physiological signals to test systems before applying them to healthcare or aviation. Technically, Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) is the most common tool used to validate these signals. A key finding is the “transparency-workload trade-off”: while XAI builds trust and improves accuracy, it also increases mental effort and can cause users to rely on AI too much. The study concludes that future XAI must achieve “cognitive fit” by adapting the amount of information in real-time to match the user's mental state.

title: Phasor Measurement Unit with Particle Filtering Estimation
authors: Rithvik Reddy Adapa, Xin Wang
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) are very sophisticated measuring devices that find amplitude, phase and frequency of various voltages and currents in a power system. Particle filter is a state estimation technique that uses Bayesian inference. Particle filters are widely used in pose estimation and indoor navigation and are very reliable. This paper studies and compares four different particle filters as PMUs namely, generic particle filter (GPF), genetic algorithm particle filter (GAPF), particle swarm optimization particle filter (PSOPF) and adaptive particle filter (APF). Two different test signals are used to test the performance of the filters in terms of responsiveness and correctness of the estimates.

title: Synthetic Data Generation with GANs to Boost CNN Accuracy in Plasmodium Species and Lifecycle Stage Detection
authors: Galih Bagaskoro, Rujianto Eko Saputro, Azhari Shouni Barkah
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Classification of Plasmodium species and phases is important in the process of malaria diagnosis. However, the limited amount of microscopic image data often becomes an obstacle in training optimal machine learning models. This study focuses on optimizing the performance of the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model by increasing the amount and diversity of data using augmentation techniques, especially the Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). The initial dataset consisted of 134 images which were expanded to 665 through augmentation, then further developed into 6665 synthetic images using GAN. The CNN model was trained on two data scenarios to determine the effect of the amount and quality of data on classification performance. Evaluation was carried out using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, which are important metrics in the biomedical context. The experimental results showed a significant increase in performance, from 55.64% accuracy on the original data to 94.15% after augmentation with GAN. These findings indicate that GAN-based augmentation is associated with improved CNN performance in Plasmodium classification under the conditions of this study.

title: DRESS: Distributed Robotic Enhanced Soft System for Non-Uniform Object Transportation
authors: Silvia Terrile, Razanne Abu-Aisheh, Oleksii Kulykov, Tom Didiot-Cook, Youssef Alboraei, Richard Suphapol Diteesawat, Henry Hickson, Sabine Hauert, Jonathan Rossiter
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: While robots excel at transporting small, uniform, and well-structured items, irregular or delicate objects remain challenging, and moving large objects typically requires lots of infrastructure. This limits their application in domains such as logistics, manufacturing, agriculture, and disaster response, where such requirements are common. To address these limitations, we propose combining the advantages of soft and swarm robotics inside a unified system: swarm cooperation enables the transport of large objects without heavy infrastructure, while soft actuators enable the safe handling of irregular and delicate items. This paper presents the Distributed Robotic Enhanced Soft System (DRESS), a proof-of-concept platform that integrates soft inflatable actuation with a mobile groundbased swarm to explore cooperative transport of varied object types. DRESS enhances the existing DOTS swarm platform by incorporating three soft actuator modules: a top pillow that secures items, a bottom doughnut that mitigates collision impact, and a soft lifter that provides compliant lifting and adapts to the shape of the object being carried. Preliminary experiments with diverse payloads demonstrate the system’s potential for safe manipulation and decentralised coordination. The results demonstrate the feasibility of hybrid soft-swarm systems and highlight future directions for adaptive, infrastructure-free object transport.

title: A Multi-Objective Optimization Framework for Chemical Synthesis Planning Using a Retrievalaugmented Language Model
authors: Priyanka More, Srushti Ghogare, Samiksha Surwase, Aditya Sonone, Piyush Waghjale, Ayush Kale
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Chemical synthesis planning is a complex and multi-dimensional decision-making process that goes beyond identifying chemically feasible series of reaction. Traditional Computer-Aided Synthesis Planning (CASP) systems consider feasibility as the main center of focus, often overlooking critical real-world considerations such as economic cost and environmental sustainability. This paper proposes a hybrid decision-support framework that integrates a RetrievalAugmented Generation (RAG)-enabled Large Language Model (LLM) with a Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO) engine to explicitly simulate trade-offs in synthesis planning. The framework generates candidate retrosynthetic routes and evaluates them across three competing objectives: (i) feasibility, (ii) sustainability, quantified using Process Mass Intensity (PMI), and (iii) economic cost. A Pareto front of synthesis pathways is built with an NSGA-II-based optimization module; this module allows chemists to search trade-offs in a systematic manner or building a Pareto front instead of depending on single-objective feasibility. To validate the optimization capability of the proposed architecture, we conduct a proof-ofconcept study using a simulated route-generation agent that generates a realistic distribution of candidate pathways for a target molecule (Ibuprofen). Results demonstrate that the optimization framework can successfully determine the nondominated synthesis paths and can indicate the significant trade-offs between feasibility, cost, and sustainability. The paper presents the prospect of integrating generative AI and formal optimization to convert CASP systems from systems that are based on feasibility planning into systems that undertake decision-based synthesis design.

title: CROP: Conservative Reward for Model-Based Offline Policy Optimization
authors: Hao Li, Xiao-Hu Zhou, Shu-Hai Li, Mei-Jiang Gui, Xiao-Liang Xie, Shi-Qi Liu, Shuang-Yi Wang, Zhen-Qiu Feng, Zeng-Guang Hou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) aims to optimize a policy using collected data without online interactions. Model-based approaches are particularly appealing for addressing offline RL challenges because of their capability to mitigate the limitations of data coverage through data generation using models. Nonetheless, a prevalent issue in offline RL is the overestimation caused by distribution shift. This study proposes a novel model-based offline RL algorithm named <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">C</u>onservative <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">R</u>eward for model-based <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">O</u>ffline <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">P</u>olicy optimization (CROP). CROP introduces a streamlined objective that concurrently minimizes estimation error and the rewards of random actions, thereby yielding a robustly conservative reward estimator. Theoretical analysis shows that the designed conservative reward mechanism leads to a conservative policy evaluation and mitigates distribution shift. Experiments showcase that with the simple modification to reward estimation, CROP can conservatively estimate the reward and achieve competitive performance with existing methods. The source code will be available after acceptance.

title: Simpler is Better: Feature Guard and Interaction for Semantic Correspondence_supp3-3693144.mp4
authors: Songlin Du
abstract: Semantic correspondence establishes keypoint correspondences between different instances of the same category. Fusing texture and semantic features from vision foundation models like stable diffusion (SD) and DINO significantly improves matching performance. However, we found an unnoticed yet essential problem: current feature fusion enhances the edge and semantic information in SD features with fine textures and DINOv2 features with fine semantics, but it destroys the semantic and structural information in SD features with weak and coarse semantics. We propose guard features (GuFT), a simple yet efficient method, to prevent feature degradation. Moreover, matching methods designed for traditional deep neural networks can be simplified based on two key insights: 1) vision foundation models provide rich visual knowledge; and 2) GuFT yields high-quality feature descriptors. We propose a bottleneck-style non-shared aggregation and backward interaction (NABI) module to efficiently capture intra- and inter-feature relationships, instead of common self- and cross-attention. The resulting framework, SimBetter, embodies a “simpler is better” design philosophy. It achieves state-of-the-art results with lower computation on SPair-71k, AP-10K, and PF-PASCAL, excelling in geometry-aware, cross-species, cross-family, and cross-dataset tasks. SimBetter also shows excellent potential in the applications of image-video semantic correspondence and sticker editing. Code is available at https://github.com/wzhlearning/SimBetter.

title: From Simulation to Production: Few-Shot Learning for ID Card Presentation Attack Detection
authors: Alvaro S. Rocamora, Juan E. Tapia, Juan M. Espín
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: This work focuses on proposing a presentation attack detection (PAD) system for ID cards based on a meta-learning approach, such as Few-Shot Learning (FSL), to determine whether an image is bona fide, printed, or displayed on a screen with only a few samples (50). This approach involves a commercial PAD system trained in one country, such as Chile, that extends its capabilities to other countries with fewer available images, such as Nicaragua, Honduras, and El Salvador. We demonstrate that Prototypical Networks generalise effectively to new bona fide and attack with an average EER of 3,10% and BPCER20 of 2,80%. Our experiments validate FSL as a comprehensive solution for diverse presentation attack modalities in mobile production environments where acquiring large datasets is impractical<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: Pseudo Sentences Evaluation and Quality-aware Robust Learning for Unsupervised Text-based Person Search
authors: Kai Niu, Jiahui Chen, Ke Han, Xinyue Song, Yanning Zhang
abstract: Unsupervised Text-Based Person Search (TBPS) eliminates the need for costly manual sentence annotations by generating pseudo sentences via Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, these pseudo sentences often face the quality defect issues, resulting in semantic misalignment across modalities, which will hinder discriminative representation learning. To address this problem, we propose the PSE-QRL (Pseudo Sentences Evaluation and Quality-aware Robust Learning), a unified framework that enhances robustness to pseudo sentences for unsupervised TBPS. The PSE-QRL dynamically couples an evolving TBPS model with MLLMs to assess pseudo sentences’ reliability, and adaptively leverages high-quality ones during training. It consists of three key components: 1) Multi-granularity Sentence Augmentation, for enriching pseudo sentences with multiple granularities to broaden the diversity of image-sentence pairs; 2) Hybrid Quality Evaluation, to combine MLLM’s cross-modal reasoning knowledge with TBPS model’s person-specific distinguishing capabilities for effective sentence quality assessment; and 3) Quality-aware Robust Learning, for selecting and re-weighting samples based on quality scores to emphasize reliable sentence annotations while suppressing low-quality ones. Extensive experiments on CUHK-PEDES, ICFG-PEDES, and RSTPReid benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PSE-QRL for improving learning robustness, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) retrieval performance for unsupervised TBPS.

title: CAMformer: Binary Associative Memory Is All You Need
authors: Tergel Molom-Ochir, Benjamin F. Morris, Mark Horton, Chiyue Wei, Cong Guo, Brady Taylor, Peter Liu, Shan X. Wang, Deliang Fan, Hai Li, Yiran Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Transformer attention mechanisms pose significant scalability challenges due to quadratic complexity in sequence length, and existing accelerators remain bottlenecked by dense arithmetic and data movement. This paper proposes CAMformer, a hardware accelerator that reinterprets attention as an associative memory operation, contributing at three levels. At the circuit level, a voltage-domain Binary Attention CAM (BA-CAM) computes Hamming similarity through analog charge sharing, achieving 1.12% mean error under PVT variation—<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$7\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> lower than time-domain approaches. At the architecture level, a three-stage pipeline with hierarchical two-stage top-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math> </inline-formula> filtering reduces score storage by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> while hiding DRAM latency. At the algorithm level, this top-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math> </inline-formula> mechanism, co-designed with Hamming Attention Distillation (HAD), maintains <0.4% accuracy degradation on GLUE benchmarks. Implemented in 65 nm CMOS and evaluated on BERT-Large, Vision Transformer, and GPT-2 decoder workloads via HSPICE simulation and Design Compiler synthesis, CAMformer achieves 9,045 queries/mJ (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>), 191 queries/ms (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>), and 0.26 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> (6–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> reduction) for attention computation compared to state-of-the-art accelerators. These results demonstrate that reconceptualizing attention as associative memory retrieval enables order-of-magnitude efficiency gains for edge Transformer inference.

title: A 100–140-GHz Differential LNA Using Stacked-Transistor Neutralized Cascode Topology
authors: Volkan Erturk, Seyyid Dilek, Aniello Franzese, Batuhan Sutbas, Corrado Carta
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a 100–140-GHz low-noise amplifier (LNA) implemented in a 130-nm SiGe BiCMOS technology using a neutralization technique based on stacked transistors. High gain, low noise, and wide bandwidth (BW) are achieved by applying the proposed neutralization technique, which consists of stacked transistors to precisely attain the low capacitance required at F-band. The fabricated two-stage LNA is based on differential cascode topology and achieves a peak gain of 28.7dB with a 3-dB BW ranging from 100 to 140GHz while consuming 30.7 mW from a 1.8-V supply. The measured noise figure (NF) is 3.3dB at 132GHz, while the fit NF curve reaches 4.5dB. An input 1-dB compression point (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\textbf {IP}_{\textbf {1 dB}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm {-19.8~dBm}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and an input third-order intercept point (IIP3) of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm {-14.1~dBm}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> are measured. The LNA is evaluated under modulated signals using a 128-QAM scheme at a symbol rate of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm {2~\text {G}baud}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, resulting in an error vector magnitude (EVM) of −30.46dB at −26-dBm peak input power. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this work achieves the lowest measured NF and the highest reported figure of merit (FoM) among silicon-based F- and D-band LNAs.

title: Resonance Stability Analysis Method for Power Systems with High Proportion of Renewable Energy
authors: Yifeng Chen, Huangqing Xiao, Huichen Gan
abstract: As inverter-based resources (IBRs) are increasingly integrated into power systems, network resonance instability has become more common in recent years, primarily caused by the interaction between IBR power-electronic controllers and passive network elements. The existing s-domain nodal admittance matrix-based method provides a useful framework for large-scale resonance analysis and can deliver comprehensive modal information. However, when applied to power systems with a high proportion of renewable energy, the physical conditions associated with resonance instability driven by the negative-resistance effect of IBRs are not explicitly incorporated, and singular behavior in the nodal susceptance matrix may lead to misidentification of resonance points. To address these limitations, this paper proposes an improved s-domain nodal admittance matrix-based method. Specifically, the relationship between the negative-resistance effect of IBRs and modal impedance is analyzed to establish a modal-parameter criterion for unstable resonant modes, and the first-stage resonance screening is reformulated using the nodal reactance matrix to avoid singularity-induced misidentification. In addition, a method for establishing the s-domain impedance model of renewable energy stations is presented, and the theoretical basis and implementation procedure of the improved method are detailed. Finally, a modified IEEE 9-bus system is built in PSCAD/EMTDC. Comparing results with the existing method validates the improved method’s effectiveness.

title: A Fully Integrated Dual-Mode Control Switched-Capacitor DC-DC Converter With Fast Transient Response
authors: Cheng Liu, Meng Wang, Yanqi Zheng
abstract: This paper proposed a fully integrated dual-mode control switched-capacitor (SC) dc-dc converter. The proposed dual-mode control combines linear frequency modulation (LFM) and pulse skipping modulation (PSM) to achieve wider load range and better load regulation. A mode switching mechanism and transient-enhancement techniques are proposed to achieve small output voltage droop and optimal transient recovery time. Moreover, the dynamic sampling frequency (DSF) technique, which can dynamically adjust the sampling frequency of PSM according to output loading current, further improves the efficiency and the response speed under ultra-light loads. The dual-mode SC converter was fabricated in a 0.18-μm CMOS technology and measured within load of 20μA to 20mA (1000X) , which showed that it could achieve a peak efficiency of 80.3% and maintain efficiency over 50% within loading ranges between 100μA and 20 mA. Due to the DSF technique in PSM mode, the efficiency is improved by 4%~20% in the range of loads below 1mA. Under a load transient step of 20mA, the output voltage undershoot is about 200mV and the response time is about 80ns.

title: Intent-Aware Contrastive Learning for Cross-Domain Recommendation
authors: Lei Sang, Yiwei Shen, Lin Mu, Yiwen Zhang, Xindong Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Accurately capturing user preferences across diverse domains is a fundamental challenge in cross-domain recommendation (CDR) systems. Recent literature has established that disentangling user preferences into global and domain-specific components significantly enhances recommendation performance. However, existing CDR systems are hindered by two critical challenges: 1) how to align user representations across domains to accommodate the discrepancy of user intents in different domains; and 2) how to mitigate data conflicts caused by cross-domain heterogeneity. To address these limitations, we propose intent-aware contrastive learning for cross-domain recommendation (ICCDR). This framework learns unified user interests across domains while mitigating data conflicts from heterogeneity. The ICCDR framework consists of the following core components: 1) a cross-intent module that dynamically identifies users’ consistent intents across domains to guide the transfer of knowledge; and 2) an information bottleneck-based aggregation module designed to mitigate data conflicts by filtering out task-irrelevant information, thereby retaining only the essential latent features required for effective knowledge transfer. Extensive experiments conducted on Amazon datasets demonstrate that the ICCDR framework outperforms traditional baseline methods.

title: Cooperative Dynamic Positioning for Multiple Uncrewed Surface Vehicles Under Switching-Edge Deception Attacks
authors: Lili Li, Mengjie Li, Hong Sang, Bo Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: This paper investigates cooperative dynamic positioning (CDP) for multiple uncrewed surface vehicles (USVs) under switching-edge deception attacks (SEDAs). The SEDAs can not only disconnect connected links but also fabricate malicious connections where no physical link exists. Existing secure control schemes, which predominantly concentrate on data tampering and disruptions in communication edges, become ineffectual for this new SEDAs. These existing control schemes lack the ability to detect and respond to this attack pattern that manipulates the communication edge. To address SEDAs, an injected edge energy function is used to construct a model that can accurately describe the changes in communication edges during this attack. Then, a detection mechanism is developed to effectively monitor the reliability of communication edges and data. Finally, based on this detection mechanism, the cooperative controller is designed to ensure CDP of multiple USVs. The validity and outperformance of the secure control scheme are confirmed by an example simulation.

title: Ride-Hailing Assignment in Heterogeneous Networks Based on Graph Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Baozhen Yao, Dongxuan Bai, Shaohua Cui, Zhihao Qi, Ankun Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The rapid growth of online ride hailing services has greatly improved passenger convenience. Existing methods that combine travel time prediction with order matching mainly focus on interactions between adjacent road segments, while ignoring latent relations between non-adjacent segments. In addition, global matching for mixed orders wastes computation on invalid and low-quality solutions. To address these issues, this paper proposes an online assignment framework for mixed ride hailing orders. First, a Graph Convolutional Neural Network with physical and virtual graphs is developed to extract heterogeneous road network features and predict travel time. Second, graph clustering and bipartite matching are combined to group and match mixed orders. Experiments on the urban road network within Beijing’s Fifth Ring Road show that, compared with baseline methods, the proposed method achieves higher travel time prediction accuracy and improves both the feasibility of matching results and online solving efficiency.

title: Selective Deployment of Post-Quantum Edge–Device Authentication for IIoT: A Lifecycle-Oriented SDAKA Protocol
authors: Hao Tang, Hui He, Weizhe Zhang, Hongwei Yang, Qingyang Fan
abstract: Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) deployments increasingly rely on cloud–edge collaboration while operating with long-lived field devices, heterogeneous hardware, deterministic control requirements, and bandwidth-constrained links. These properties make “PQC everywhere” upgrades costly and operationally risky, yet the edge–device access boundary remains a critical trust bottleneck and an attractive target for impersonation, replay, and key-compromise threats. This paper proposes a selective-deployment approach for post-quantum authentication in industrial systems: rather than enforcing uniform migration, we identify security-critical boundaries and map protocol changes to lifecycle safe points to enable incremental, deployable protection. Building on this framework, we design SDAKA, a lifecycle-oriented post-quantum authentication and key agreement protocol spanning onboarding/registration, runtime access, maintenance (key evolution), and decommissioning/ revocation. SDAKA combines the standardized post-quantum signature primitive ML-DSA with an ML-KEM-inspired Module-LWE key-establishment component to establish session keys and authenticate lifecycle operations, while supporting operational needs such as pseudonymous access with accountable traceability. We validate the protocol via machine-checked symbolic analysis (ProVerif) and reduction-style security arguments in the Real-or-Random (RoR) model. A heterogeneous edge–device testbed evaluation further demonstrates that SDAKA’s computational latency and communication overhead remain practical for industrial links, and that different security configurations provide flexible trade-offs between protection strength and deployment cost.

title: Dual-Trust Graph Attention Network for Malicious Device Detection in Internet of Things
authors: Jun Wang, Weijie Tan, Zhi Ouyang, Xiuzhang Yang, Yuling Chen, Zhen Li, Gang Xu
abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) systems face escalating security threats as the number of connected devices surpassed 18.5 billion in 2024 and is projected to reach 39 billion by 2030. The dynamic and heterogeneous characteristics of IoT networks create vulnerabilities to sophisticated attacks. Existing trust management approaches struggle with these threats due to static graph construction, feature redundancy between direct and indirect trust, and inflexible fusion strategies. This paper proposes a Dual-Trust graph attention network-based malicious device detection method for IoT environments, named DTEM. The proposed scheme constructs dynamic temporal graphs based on real interaction history. Then it designs a completely decoupled dual-trust learning architecture. Finally it introduces a hybrid fusion mechanism. Overall, the improvements significantly enhance the detection accuracy of malicious devices in complex environments and adaptability to heterogeneous scenarios. Experimental results on the UNSW-NB15 dataset demonstrate that DTEM achieves an average F1-Score of 0.973 and ROC-AUC of 0.994 across three threat scenarios, outperforming traditional methods.

title: Development of an Embedded System for Agricultural Sensors Based on IoT Technology: A Simulated Electronic Corn Ear
authors: Shulun Xing, Tao Cui, Dongxing Zhang, Li Yang, Xiantao He, Jiaqi Dong
abstract: To investigate the causes of grain breakage during the corn harvesting, this study designed an electronic corn ear based on internet of things (IoT) technology. The electronic corn ear integrates an ESP32 module, an ultra-wideband (UWB) module, an inertial measurement unit (IMU) module, and a flexible film pressure sensor. By utilizing IoT technology, it can detect various kinematic and dynamic parameters of the corn ears during harvesting. To improve detection accuracy, the working parameters of the electronic corn ear were calibrated one by one. The results show that the average detection error of the static compressive force is ≤ 0.93 N, whit the maximum detection error ≤ 6.49 N, and the average relative error ≤ 3.90%. The average detection error of the dynamic impact force is ≤ 0.72 N, with the maximum detection error ≤ 1.92 N, and the average relative error ≤ 2.80%. The spatial positioning error is < 10 cm, and the average positioning errors along the X-axis, Y-axis, and Z-axis are 4.12 cm, 8.44 cm, and 8.18 cm respectively. Using the calibrated electronic corn ear, a corn threshing experiment was conducted. The kinematic and dynamic parameters such as the motion trajectory, the average dynamic impact force, the average impact frequency, the average static compressive force, and the average threshing time of the electronic corn ear during threshing process were detected. The force of the corn ears at different positions in the threshing device and the variation patterns of the grain breakage rate were also identified. This study provides an application scenario of IoT technology in the field of corn harvest damage detection. It can provide data support for elucidating the mechanisms of corn threshing damage and offer technical support for the detection of damage sources during the harvesting process.

title: Small Signal Stability Analysis of Grid-Connected New Energy System with High Inertia Energy Storage Type Doubly-Fed Condenser
authors: Yang Bai, Qingsong Zhang, Hua Ye
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Due to the high penetration of renewable energy sources interfaced via power electronic converters, the inertia level of modern power systems continues to decline, bringing the high-inertia energy-storage-equipped doubly-fed synchronous condenser (DFSC) into increasing focus. To date, small-signal stability analysis of such DFSC-integrated systems remains scarce. This paper investigates the impact of DFSC control parameters on system stability in renewable-dominated grids. First, a state-space model of the grid-connected system is developed and transformed into a dq-frame impedance model. Subsequently, an equivalent impedance-based criterion is employed to establish a quantitative monotonicity metric that correlates with system stability margins. Finally, leveraging this metric, the influence of DFSC control parameters on system stability is analyzed, and the analytical findings are validated through time-domain simulations.

title: Onboard Transformer-Based Lossless Neural Compression on Satellite Imagery
authors: Jefferson Boothe, Ian Peitzsch, Evan W. Gretok, Alan D. George
abstract: Communication bandwidth for data transfer between and downlink from space platforms is and always has been exceptionally limited. Despite this limitation, the capabilities of these platforms and similarly the amount of data collected have greatly increased in recent years, with more capable high-resolution imagers and other sensors becoming commonplace on most missions. This combination of large amounts of data but limited bandwidth drives a need for data compression. Thankfully, many compression methods, both lossy and lossless, are commonplace and have even been used in the space domain. Recent research has leveraged transformers as general-purpose compressors when paired with arithmetic encoding, due to their strong predictive power. Predictive power has been established to be directly correlated with compression performance. This prior research, though, primarily focuses on large language models running on server-grade hardware. This research seeks to expand upon these findings and apply transformers for lossless satellite image compression on edge devices. We use images from the RapidEye satellites to both train and test varying scales of transformer models. We compare the compression ratio with those of other compression algorithms, including general-purpose and domain-specific options. We benchmark all compression methods on various NVIDIA Jetson Orin devices. The transformer compression methods achieved a compression ratio upwards of 3.0, which is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 2.2 \times$</tex> higher than PNG, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 1.9 \times$</tex> higher than JPEG 2000, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 1.9 \times$</tex> higher than gzip. The transformer compression methods achieve a throughput of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>2.15 k B p s$</tex>, which is comparable to other research running on more performant hardware. We also propose further runtime optimizations which could increase throughput.

title: Mission-Specific Trajectory Dispersions for T-SCVx Landing Guidance
authors: Waylon Lee, Carson Capps, Jack Goldhammer, Gregory Chamitoff
abstract: With multiple missions planned to target the Moon's south pole, robust and high-performance powered descent guidance algorithms are critical to ensuring safe and autonomous landings. One leading approach is the six degree-of-freedom (6DOF) Successive Convexification (SCVx) algorithm [1], currently utilized in vehicles such as the Falcon 9. SCVx works by iteratively solving a non-convex optimal control problem by approximating it as a sequence of convex subproblems, efficiently generating realtime, constraint optimized trajectories. To further enhance SCVx's robustness and convergence speed, recent work by Briden [2] introduced a transformer-based warm-starting method known as TSCVx. T-SCVx maintains the rigor of SCVx's convex optimization framework while enhancing its responsiveness in dynamic environments, especially where onboard computational resources or time are constrained. In this work, we train a transformer neural network with mission-specific trajectory dispersions to increase safety and convergence reliability of T-SCVx, extending on previous efforts of training a dataset for landing. By providing feasible initial trajectory generations for preliminary targeting activities during the coasting phase of flight prior to powered descent guidance, under performing burns may be effectively accounted for by the trained neural networks, increasing convergence robustness. This methodology of customized neural network training with mission specific dispersions data can be useful for similar initial conditioned/mass properties vehicles landing at or near the same landing site, such as multiple Starship landings. In this work, the simulation is of a transfer from the Artemis Gateway proposed 9:2 Near Rectilinear Halo Orbit (NRHO) around the Moon to a low-lunar oribit (LLO) transitioning to T-SCVx powered terminal descent. During training, the initial trajectory leg between NRHO-LLO is simulated in NASA Goddard's GMAT with an underperforming engine modeled to produce a diverse set of initial conditions for training terminal descent conditions. After the transformer has been trained and tuned, the simulation is run in the Space Teams PRO environment, starting at a variety of terminal descent points derived from the simulated dispersion. This simulation is used to validate the method's ability to maintain accuracy and safety in scenarios with significant trajectory variation. Our mission-informed dispersion-based training outperformed our spherical distribution trained implementation in convergence speed for the case where initial conditions live within both spherical distribution and mission-informed trained transformer (average 8.18 seconds ± 5.16 seconds at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \sigma$</tex>). The mission-informed trained transformer also shows consistently smaller variance in solve times for all cases while reliably converging on all test cases.

title: Atmospheric Corrosion-induced Interturn Short Circuit in a 21 kV Dry-type Air-core Shunt Reactor
authors: Liu Lingxia, Wang Yang, Francisco Chiuminatto, Ramon Andrade
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Dry-type reactors have advantages of maintenance-free and low operating costs. When installed outdoors, environmental factors and other stresses induce insulation failures individually or synergistically. This study clarifies the root causes and progression of an interturn short circuit insulation failure of a 21 kV dry-type air-core shunt reactor operating under long-term exposure to the polluted environment containing multiple metallic and acidic contaminants, with annual relative humidity maintained at approximately 80%. Alongside disassembly observation and fault oscillography analysis, material characterization techniques are applied to identify main fault types and evidence of environmental impact. Microscopies are employed to examine the macro- and micro-morphologies of materials, while the scanning electron microscopy - energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS) is utilized to determine elemental compositions of attachments on windings. Electrical tracking occurred in weak points of electrical insulating materials (EIMs) particularly in hidden joints duct-sticks, or heavily polluted insulating surfaces triggering the insulation failure, inducing a 5-10 times current rise in branch current, leading to interturn short circuit, and eventual breakdown.

title: Load Characteristic Analysis of eVTOL Propulsion Motors Based on Data-Model Hybrid-Driven Approach
authors: Zhu Chen, Wang Huaji, Wang Huaizhi
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To ensure the high-reliability operation of electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft power systems, this paper proposes a data-model hybrid-driven approach for analyzing motor load characteristics. A multiphysics coupling mechanism model integrating aerodynamics, flight dynamics, and motor dynamics is established to accurately characterize the generation mechanisms of axial tension, radial unbalanced forces, and transient impact loads. Based on flight test and bench test data, variational mode decomposition (VMD) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks are employed to identify and compensate for high-frequency random load components that are difficult to model accurately in the mechanistic model, thereby constructing a mechanism-data fused load model. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively reveals the complex dynamic load characteristics of eVTOL motors during multi-mode flight, with composite load peaks reaching up to 130% of the rated value and impact dominant frequencies extending beyond 200 Hz. Furthermore, applying this high-fidelity load spectrum to structural health assessment reduces the prediction error of bearing remaining useful life (RUL) by approximately 25%. This study provides an effective analytical tool for the reliability design and airworthiness verification of eVTOL propulsion motors.

title: Federated Reinforcement Learning for PV Inverters Enabled Voltage Regulation of Medium-Low Voltage Unbalanced Distribution Grids
authors: Xiao Liu, Youbo Liu, Yongdong Chen, Zhiyuan Tang, Hongjun Gao, Zhengbo Li
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To guarantee operational security in distribution grids by PV inverter scheduling, conventional cloud-based methods require massive data transmission, causing high bandwidth consumption and privacy issues. This study develops an federated reinforcement learning (FRL)-based approach for PV power regulation to enhance distribution grid security. Each low-voltage (LV) station acts as an agent that coordinates local PV inverters via reinforcement learning. A decentralized training framework is developed using edge computing and federated learning, where each LV station is equivalent to a load node via a linear power flow model. Agents learn voltage control strategies using local data and shared model parameters without raw data exchange. This structure preserves data privacy, reduces communication overhead, and weakens the data-sharing dependence of traditional RL. Simulation results validate the superior voltage control performance of FRL.

title: Low-Complexity Hybrid-Field Channel Estimation for XL-MIMO Systems With Asymmetrical Uplink and Downlink Transceivers
authors: Zhaoang Luo, Kaizhe Xu, Ting Liu, Xi Yang, Shaodan Ma
abstract: Asymmetrical transceiver architectures in extremely large-scale multiple-input multiple-output systems employ fewer receive than transmit radio frequency chains to reduce hardware costs. However, the resulting small-dimensional uplink observations pose significant challenges for hybrid-field channel estimation. To address these challenges with low computational complexity, this letter proposes a discrete fractional Fourier transform (DFrFT)-based two-stage Newtonized orthogonal matching pursuit algorithm. By exploiting channel sparsity in the DFrFT domain, the proposed algorithm formulates channel estimation as a peak detection problem in DFrFT spectra. Through spectral peak analysis, the relationship between peak positions and channel parameters is established, from which the DFrFT rotation angle range where spectral peaks concentrate is derived, thereby significantly reducing the search space. Within this range, a two-stage detection strategy is developed to achieve low computational complexity, while Newton-based refinement ensures gridless accuracy. Without requiring prior knowledge of path numbers, the proposed algorithm efficiently reconstructs complete downlink channels from partial uplink observations.

title: Online Fault Diagnosis of Dual Active Bridge Based on R-RVFL Neural Network
authors: Hanhui Che, Xiangyang Xing, Xiangjun Li, Yan Li, Yang Liu, Rui Zhang, Frede Blaabjerg
abstract: Dual active bridge (DAB) converters are key components in modern power electronic systems. However, they are vulnerable to open-circuit faults (OCFs) in their insulated gate bipolar transistors (IGBTs) under high-frequency switching conditions, which can significantly impair the operational reliability. Although data-driven intelligent diagnostic methods have demonstrated improved diagnostic capabilities in recent years, they still encounter critical challenges, such as high computational complexity and low training efficiency. To achieve rapid and accurate detection of OCFs in DAB converters, this article proposes an online fault diagnosis method based on regularized random vector functional link (R-RVFL) neural networks. Specifically, multiple electrical signals are first processed through Gaussian filtering and then fused using a multifeature strategy to construct informative feature sets for fault classification. Subsequently, the fast-learning algorithm of the R-RVFL neural network is utilized to extract a nonlinear mapping between selected features and fault patterns. To balance the testing accuracy and computational time of the diagnostic algorithm, R-RVFL parameters are adjusted to reduce the computational burden while regularization coefficients are fine-tuned to enhance generalization and mitigate overfitting. Comprehensive comparisons with state-of-the-art techniques demonstrate the superiority of the proposed approach in terms of diagnostic accuracy, computational efficiency, and response time.

title: Towards miniaturisation of dielectrophoretic liquid zipping actuators with series-elastic fabric electrodes
authors: Reece White, Jonathan Rossiter, Martin Garrad
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Smaller scale artificial muscles could greatly benefit many applications, including wearable technologies, smallscale autonomous robots and prosthetic devices. However, this vision is held back by difficulties in creating artificial muscles of the required size without greatly diminishing strain, stress, work density, and/or power density. This is because actuator performance is intrinsically linked to scale. For example, dielectrophoretic liquid zipping actuators (DLZs) are electrostatic actuators that can deliver high performance at centimetre length scales but rapidly lose performance when reduced in size. In this work, we overcome this limitation by introducing a Series-elastic Liquid Zipping Actuator (SEZA), which combines fabric electrodes with a series-elastic element, removing the need for a single material to provide both the stiffness and conductivity required for the electrodes. We show that SEZAs are capable of producing 70% of the specific power of a steel DLZ, a 419% improvement over using fabric electrodes without series-elasticity. Additionally, SEZAs were shown to exceed the maximum isotonic load and work output of a steel electrode DLZ by 16% and 30%, respectively. Overall, SEZA demonstrates high-performance at the meso-scale and a route towards further miniaturisation of DLZs.

title: Comparative Evaluation of SARIMA, FB Prophet, and LSTM for Hourly Demand Forecasting in Saudagar Kopi
authors: Kevina Syahla Aqila, Revaldy Arya Kusuma, Muhamad Reynaldi AliSafitrah, Rahma James Galliano Setiardi, Tuga Mauritsius, Eka Miranda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Demand forecasting plays an important role in the food and beverage (FnB) industry by helping businesses anticipate customer demand and support operational planning during peak periods. This study conducts a comparative empirical evaluation of three forecasting models, Seasonal Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (SARIMA), FB Prophet, and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) to predict hourly demand patterns in a coffee shop environment. The models were evaluated using hourly demand patterns in a coffee shop environment. The models were evaluated using hourly sales data collected from Saudagar Kopi in Jakarta over a threemonth period (April-June 2025). Forecasting performance was assessed using Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) as the primary evaluation metric. The results indicate that the LSTM model achieves the lowest forecasting error and is better able to capture abrupt demand spikes and high-frequency fluctuations compared with SARIMA and FB Prophet, which perform well in modeling trend and seasonality but tend to underestimate sudden peak-hour demand. These findings highlight the potential of deep learning approaches for modeling complex demand patterns in high-frequency time-series data. However, the results should be interpreted within the context of a limited dataset and a single business environment. Future research may explore alternative models that address zero-inflated demand patterns and evaluate forecast performance across multiple retail locations.

title: Electromagnetic Field and Mechanical Forces Analysis of a Direct-Drive Generator in Wind Turbines
authors: Marjan Noroozi, Reza Yazdanpanah, Abbas Mehrad Kazemi Amiri, Ziad Azar, Alasdair McDonald
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Wind energy is a widely commercialised, affordable, and reliable renewable energy source. Direct-drive permanent magnet synchronous generators (DD-PMSGs) enable efficient, reliable wind power generation while minimising downtime, noise, vibrations, and maintenance. By integrating the winding function approach with an improved equivalent magnetic circuit and employing an enhanced analytical model of the airgap electromagnetic field in DD-PMSGs, this paper presents an efficient computational model to accurately capture key electromagnetic phenomena, including slotting effects, winding distribution, flux leakages, and higher-order harmonics. Not only does the formulated approach increase the accuracy, but it also facilitates sensitivity studies, design optimisation, and vibration analysis. This study considers the slotting effect by applying the real inverse airgap function Fourier series and investigates the effect of the tangential component of the magnetic flux density. The total airgap magnetic flux density and radial stress under the loading condition are calculated and compared to the finite element analysis results, showing a good agreement. Spatial harmonic analysis is conducted for both analytical and numerical results to identify the main harmonics.

title: Case Studies Surveying with Augmented Reality Adopting Technical and Employer Based Data for Maintenance and Performance at Diversified Areas
authors: Krishna Priya M, Angeline Prasanna G, Sangeetha M, Gayathri R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: Augmented reality (AR) is a visual based interactive technology which is helping people from kids to employees with their respective learning and working areas. This paper discusses the role of augmented reality is in supporting people geographical boundaries. It also highlights how augmented reality is applied with various domains such as educational, medical, industrial, sales, product launching with automobile and other electronic deliverables. The augmented reality area is empowering the work force with better visual learning methods during training. The case study on automobile industry is done, from which the results are analyzed and derived. It shows the better executed data results in number, less manual work involved, easy documentation and practicing training sessions in real world scenario. The best results of implementing and using augmented reality in many application areas shows the better return on investment (ROI).

title: Development of an Agile Mission Concept Formulation Framework
authors: Kinga Sylwia Wrobel
abstract: The non-recurring engineering (NRE) cycle time for space missions remains misaligned with the accelerated timelines dictated by emerging threats, rapid scientific discovery, and evolving commercial data demands. To address this, a systematic re-architecting of mission formulation methodologies and toolchains is essential. Integrating artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) within human-in-the-loop frameworks enables real-time parameter consistency checks, generative design, and automated data transfer between tools, substantially reducing design cycle duration. Empirical demonstrations in generative structural design at NASA have shown order-of-magnitude improvements in development speed and mass optimization, validating the feasibility of a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 10 \times$</tex> reduction in formulation time. Concurrently, digital engineering paradigms—particularly model-based systems engineering (MBSE), digital threads, and mission digital twins—establish an authoritative source of truth that supports concurrent workflows, automatic requirements verification, and high-fidelity trade space exploration. These frameworks mitigate late-stage design inconsistencies, reduce rework, and enable rapid iteration across subsystems. The incorporation of Industry 5.0 concepts further advances this capability by coupling intelligent automation with human expertise, fostering agile design cycles that preserve innovation while increasing throughput. When coupled with cost- and value-driven optimization methods, these techniques can establish a closed-loop feedback system in which mission utility, affordability, and user-driven requirements that are continuously evaluated during formulation. This will result in a technically rigorous, economically optimized, and operationally responsive framework capable of delivering scientifically and strategically relevant missions on compressed timelines without compromising system reliability.

title: TAM, Memorable Tourism Experience, Museum Virtual Tour, Visit Intention Cultural Heritage Virtual Tour (CVHT): Evaluating the Impact of Technology Acceptance on Intention to Visit in Museum
authors: Titis Shinta Dhewi, Dediek Tri Kurniawan, Ita Prihatining Wilujeng, Yesiana Ihda Kusnayain, Abdul Rahim Ridzuan, Erika Febianti Puspitasari
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Cultural heritage virtual tours (CHVT) become an interesting concept in increasing prospective visitors before they visit the museum. A cultural heritage virtual tour defines the use of digital technologies to create a new experience that enables users to explore and interact with cultural and historical sites remotely. This study aims to examine the impact of CVHT on museum visit intention. This study used a platform with the CVHT concept, called inmuse.id. The platform provides a virtual tour and e-ticketing for the Vredeburg Museum in Yogyakarta, Indonesia. This study uses the Technology Acceptance Model as a theoretical framework to analyze the factors that affect users' intentions to visit (visit intention) a technology or application of a virtual tour museum. This study aims to examine the interrelationships among perceived usefulness (PU), perceived ease of use (PEU), memorable tourism experience (MTE), and tourist visit intention (VI) in museum cultural heritage tourism in Indonesia. This research successfully collected 400 respondents. SEM-PLS is an analysis technique used to test the relationship between variables in a conceptual model simultaneously, both for measurement models and structural models. These findings confirm the positive influence of perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use on the memorable tourism experience (MTE) of virtual museum tours. This study also confirms the positive influence of perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use on visit intention in physical museums. In addition, MTE in virtual museum tours has been shown to affect the desire to visit physical museums.

title: Enhancing Subway Waterlogging Management: An Iot-Based Solution with AI-Assisted Weather Forecasting
authors: Rajeshwaran K, Janani V, Kamalesh S, Madhankumar V, Jeevith Pranav P, Charu M G
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: During heavy rainfall events, rapid water accumulation occurs in urban road subways and underpasses leading to traffic disruptions, infrastructure degradation, and safety hazards. Traditional monitoring methods lack predictive actions and intelligence since they rely on manual inspection or fixed threshold mechanisms. This paper proposes an IoT-based architecture for an automated drainage control system and road subway waterlogging monitoring with cloud synchronization, rainfall forecasting, and remote actuation. Water depth is continuously measured using an ESP32 microcontroller interfaced with an ultrasonic time-of-flight sensor. Structured JSON packets are used to transmit filtered water level data over a reliable network to Firebase Realtime Database for secure storage and visualization. A mobile application developed using Flutter enables authenticated users to visualize live depth levels, receive alerts, zone classifications (Safe/Warning/Critical), and remote actuation of the pump motor. A GPT-assisted rainfall risk prediction module processes meteorological inputs retrieved from the OpenWeather API to produce short-term forecasts and generate risk prediction index values. Experimental results demonstrate a responsive remote operation, providing a scalable and cost-effective innovative waterlogging management solution for urban subway infrastructures.

title: Impact of Artificial Intelligence on Faculty Performance in Delhi-NCR
authors: Deepti Kuhar, Sushma Sharma, Pawan Kumar, Anju Rohilla
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is a relatively recent phenomenon to be introduced to daily pedagogical activities in educational institutions across the Delhi-NCR region, which needs appropriate scholarly examination. Since the performance of the faculty is a determinant factor in the quality of higher education as well as organisational productivity then an argument about AI needs to be concerned with its implications on academic staff. Specifically, how AI increases creativity, innovation, and restructures a typical teaching organization should be the focus in terms of how faculty-related outcomes are to be explored systematically. Even though the AI in education is the topic that thrives in extant literature, the quantitative studies and empirical data on the effects of AI on faculty performance are few, and most studies only cover the firstorder and second-order outcomes of AI with implications on students or operational efficiencies instead of the second- and third-order impacts on teachers. This research paper aims to address such a gap. It aims to state the means through which AI influences the creativity, motivation, stress, flexibility, and job security of faculty members working in the institutions located in Delhi-NCR. To this effect, the population of interest in the study will be 514 full-time educators, both in government and privately owned unaided colleges, who shall be involved in a survey that will assess the correlation between AI and these elements of performance. The researchers aim to determine paramount factors of performance, which encompass creativity, innovation, motivation at the workplace, burdens and pressure, and freedom of choice and independence granted to faculty by implementing exploratory factor analysis, paired-sample T-tests, one-way ANOVA, and multiple regression. The survey also examines the emergence of an interest in AI-fueled pedagogy will spur educators, as it relieves the burden of administrative work and allows time to devote to teaching practice. Nonetheless, the results also point to the fact that there is still a fear of loss of jobs and even an increase in work harassment due to dependence on technology. Increased scheduling flexibility and pedagogical autonomous become other benefits that are associated with AI. All together, these findings provide a foundation to modern-day arguments in the effects of the dual-edged sword of AI on the efficiency of the faculty and provide clues as to the implications of some policies in education practice. The identification of the benefits as well as the disadvantages of AI can present institutions with a positive model of introducing these technologies to their functioning in a sustainable way. An essential line of training of the faculty that has been prepared to use AI effectively and, at the same time, faces the problem of workload and employment security becomes a necessity. Simultaneously, it will be vital to create and maintain an atmosphere enabling creativity and innovation when it comes to the full potential of AI in the academic realm.

title: A Closed-Loop System for Measuring Music Therapy Effectiveness Through Longitudinal Emotion Tracking
authors: Aniket Pai, Surendra Shetty, A. Anish
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This work puts forward a feedback-driven framework designed to gauge the impact of therapeutic music sessions through facial expression analysis conducted both prior to and following each playback event. Emotion classification is handled by a convolutional neural network (CNN) built on the FER2013 corpus, achieving 63.0% test accuracy across seven affective states (weighted F1-score: 0.62, macro F1: 0.55). A valence translation scheme, grounded in Russell's circumplex model of affect, converts these categorical labels into numeric scores, and the gap between the post-session and pre-session values yields a per-session impact metric. The implementation stack consists of a React-based user interface, a FastAPI service layer, SQLite for persistent storage, and the Spotify Web API for music delivery. In a pilot study of 45 sessions, 68% resulted in measurable mood elevation, with the mean pre-to-post session valence improvement reaching <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.2 \pm 1.8$</tex> valence units (95% CI: [0.22, 2.18]).

title: Mechanism-Driven Surrogate-Assisted Dynamic Reactive Power Capacity Optimization for Converter-Dominated Systems
authors: Ziqian Yang, Wangqianyun Tang, Ye Zhang, Baorong Zhou, Zihan Cai, Lin Guan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address transient voltage instability in converter-dominated power systems, this paper proposes a mechanism-driven dynamic reactive power planning framework that integrates risk-oriented scenario screening with surrogate-assisted capacity optimization. Operating-condition risk indices are first constructed to characterize transfer stress, structural vulnerability, and converter saturation, forming a multi-dimensional risk feature space. Based on this space, high-risk operating conditions are identified through clustering and hierarchical, mechanism-aware screening, enabling the decoupling of scenarios dominated by different transient instability mechanisms. To reduce the computational burden associated with time-domain simulations in dynamic reactive power sizing, a transient stability surrogate model is developed for the identified critical scenarios. By leveraging high-dimensional model representation (HDMR), the nonlinear mapping between reactive power capacities and transient voltage performance indices is decomposed into low-order interaction components and efficiently approximated using Kriging models. On this basis, a minimum-cost dynamic reactive power sizing problem is formulated subject to transient stability constraints. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly reduces computational complexity while achieving a favorable balance between robustness and economic efficiency.
